Global Trusted CA



Post Quantam Technology Adoption





Inclusion in Global Trusted Root Certificate List(s)





100% e-Service Integration














NATIONAL DIGITAL

TRANSFORMATION STRATEGY (2025-30)







Special Assistant

to the Chief Adviser

Ministry of Posts, Telecommunications and Information Technology



It is with a profound sense of responsibility and a clear vision for the future that I present the National Digital Transformation Strategy (2025–2030).

My tenure as the Special Assistant to the Chief Adviser was defined by a singular mission: to dismantle a legacy of systemic fragmentation and siloed decision-making that had long stifled innovation and resulted in redundant infrastructure. This was no easy task and I am greatly thankful to my technical and support team for their hard work as they went above and beyond with minimal support. We prioritized digital sovereignty, data rights, and institutional interoperability. We inherited an ecosystem characterized by fragmented digital assets and overlapping mandates. This strategy represents our collective response to those systemic challenges, providing a roadmap to transition Bangladesh from a consumer of technology into a regional digital leader.

The philosophy underpinning this strategy is anchored in the vision of a "Connected State" where every citizen is empowered. We have enacted a foundational legal triad—the Cyber Safety, Personal Data Protection, and National Data Governance Ordinances of 2025, which anchor the digital ecosystem in a robust legal perimeter. These reforms shift our focus from state control to citizen protection, granting individuals ownership of their data and ensuring digital rights are enforceable. Most significantly, through the National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO), we have established the National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) as an independent body to oversee data privacy, AI ethics, and interoperability across all government agencies.

We believe that institutional rebirth is the prerequisite for operational excellence. We have initiated a strategic turnaround for our agencies, shifting the focus from "hard concrete" to "soft infrastructure" and business-led facilitation. We have transitioned a2i into a dedicated GovTech Agency and restructured the Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC) to serve as the nation’s digital infrastructure backbone. Through the journey from MESH to STAR to DSTAR, we are ensuring that government data is collected once and used many times, eliminating the bureaucratic friction that has long burdened our citizens.

Our vision for technical sovereignty is embodied in our strategy. We have moved decisively to end the era of vendor lock-in by establishing the National Source Code Repository, ensuring the state owns and controls the code essential for public services. By operationalizing the National Fiber Bank and achieving 100% fiber-to-tower connectivity, we are building the "digital rails" required for a truly inclusive economy. This strategy is ultimately about trust. By launching a National Digital Transformation Dashboard for real-time progress tracking and independent third-party audits, we have made the government directly accountable for its digital promises.

To our successor and the dedicated public servants of the division, we leave behind a framework designed to outlast political cycles. The responsibility now lies in the discipline of implementation. The journey from a captured past to a sovereign digital future has begun. Let us ensure that no community is left behind in the march toward a knowledge-based economy.



Acknowledgement



We extend our deepest gratitude to Faiz Ahmed Taiyeb, Special Assistant to the Chief Advisor, for his visionary leadership and strategic guidance, which were instrumental in shaping the direction of this roadmap.

We also convey our sincere thanks to Shish Haider Chowdhury, NDC, MCIPS, Secretary of the Information and Communication Technology (ICT) Division, for his unwavering support and leadership in facilitating this initiative.

A special note of appreciation goes to Md. Azaher Uddin Anik and Arefin Mizan for their meticulous research, writing, and editorial contributions that brought this comprehensive strategy to life. We also acknowledge the significant efforts of ASM Ashraf, Asif Shahriyar Sushmit, Md. Zamilur Reza and Partha Pratim Roy for their dedicated documentary assistance and coordination throughout the development process.

We are particularly grateful to the core institutions of the ICT Division - Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC), Department of ICT (DoICT), National Cyber Security Agency (NCSA), Aspire to Innovate (a2i), Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority (BHTPA), Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA), Bangladesh Data Center Company Ltd (BDCCL), and the Software and Hardware Quality Testing Center (SHQTC); for providing the valuable data, insights, and institutional support necessary to formulate this strategy.

Finally, we thank the subject matter experts, stakeholders, and team members who shared their time, knowledge, and ideas that informed this compendium.

Disclaimer

This document, the National Digital Transformation Strategy (2025-2030), is published by the Information and Communication Technology (ICT) Division, Ministry of Posts, Telecommunications and Information Technology, Government of the People's Republic of Bangladesh.

The information, data, and projections contained herein are based on currently available statistics, research, and strategic assessments as of the date of publication. While every effort has been made to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the content, the ICT Division makes no representations or warranties of any kind, express or implied, regarding the completeness, accuracy, or suitability of the information for any particular purpose.

The strategies, timelines, and key performance indicators (KPIs) outlined in this document are subject to periodic review and adjustment based on evolving technological landscapes, economic conditions, and government priorities. This document serves as a strategic roadmap and does not constitute a legally binding contract or statutory obligation unless otherwise enacted through specific legislative instruments or official gazette notifications.

The ICT Division assumes no liability for any errors, omissions, or discrepancies in the data provided, nor for any actions taken in reliance thereon. References to specific private sector entities, technologies, or platforms are for illustrative purposes only and do not constitute an endorsement.

It is hereby declared that no public funds were utilized in the drafting, design, or production of this document. Due to time constraints and the urgent need for strategic direction, Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools were extensively utilized to assist in the generation of graphics and textual content. However, all AI-generated material has undergone rigorous human review and extensive error-checking to ensure accuracy, contextual relevance, and alignment with national interests.

Executive Summary

National Digital Transformation Strategy (2025-2030)

Bangladesh stands at a defining moment in its digital evolution. The National Digital Transformation Strategy (2025-2030) serves as the comprehensive roadmap to transition the nation from a consumer of technology to a regional digital leader. Anchored in the vision of a "Connected State," this strategy addresses systemic fragmentation, strengthens governance, and lays the foundation for a secure, inclusive, and innovation-driven economy.

Strategic Vision & The Nine Pillars

The strategy is built upon nine core pillars designed to modernize governance, empower citizens, and catalyze economic growth:

  1. Unified Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI): Establishing the "National Digital Rail" through the National Responsible Data Exchange (NRDEX) to enable secure, interoperable data sharing across all agencies.

  2. Citizen-Centric Governance: Ensuring 95% of government services are delivered end-to-end digitally by 2030, enforcing a "Zero-Visit" mandate for service verification.

  3. Data Sovereignty & Legal Certainty: Anchoring the ecosystem in a robust legal triad—the National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO), Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO), and Cyber Safety Ordinance (CSO)—to protect citizen rights and national data assets.

  4. National Connectivity Backbone: Guaranteeing high-speed access by operationalizing the National Fiber Bank and achieving 100% fiber-to-tower connectivity.

  5. Autonomous Regulatory Oversight: Empowering the National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) as an independent constitutional body to oversee data privacy, AI ethics, and interoperability.

  6. Economic Resilience & Innovation: Targeting $10 billion in ICT exports by streamlining cross-border digital trade, establishing a Universal Payment Gateway, and modernizing tax collection via the NBR API Connectivity Hub.

  7. Future-Ready Human Capital: Building a workforce of 8 million professionals skilled in Deep Tech (AI, Cybersecurity, Semiconductors) under the BEAR framework (Biotech, Electronics, AI, Robotics).

  8. Institutional Interoperability: Enforcing the "Collect Once, Use Many Times" principle to eliminate redundant data collection and bureaucratic friction.

  9. Proactive Security & Resilience: Transitioning from reactive defense to Active Threat Intelligence through the National Security Operations Center (NSOC) and the Cyber Safety Ordinance.



The Legal & Governance Foundation

The strategy is underpinned by a transformative legal architecture enacted in 2025:

  • The Cyber Safety Ordinance (CSO) shifts focus from state control to citizen protection, criminalizing modern cybercrimes (e.g., deepfakes, sextortion) while safeguarding freedom of expression.

  • The Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO) grants citizens ownership of their data, establishing rights to access, correction, and erasure.

  • The National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO) mandates interoperability, creates the NDGA, and establishes the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDIA) as the technical standard for all government systems.

DPI as the Delivery Backbone (Interoperability-by-Design)

A central thrust is the shift from point-to-point integration to standardized, governed exchange across agencies—implemented through DPI components and mandatory technical requirements (e.g., API-based exchange, metadata standards, complete audit trails, and zero-trust patterns). The document explicitly enumerates key components in the national data exchange and trust stack (e.g., NRDEX, NSOC, TRUST-CCA) and sets a direction for standardized APIs and interoperable data flows across government services.

Digital Identity as a Foundational Layer

The strategy defines a Digital Identity Framework (OneID) to ensure clarity, interoperability, and trust across the identity lifecycle and across agencies. It notes that Bangladesh’s identity ecosystem is anchored in the National Identity Registration Act, 2010, and highlights legal ambiguity created after the Unique Identification Number (UIN) concept introduced in a later Act was repealed—therefore recommending consolidation/clarification through legal review (e.g., amend the 2010 Act or create a new consolidated instrument).

It also specifies the high-level identity design: a 17-digit opaque identifier designed for governance and scalability, intentionally not encoding personal attributes such as DOB/gender to reduce privacy risk.

Sectoral Transformation and Citizen Outcomes

Beyond core platforms, the strategy emphasizes sector-level transformation where DPI and identity enable practical outcomes. Examples include:

  • Digital Social Protection: addresses fragmentation, duplication/leakage and transparency gaps; proposes a Unified Beneficiary Database under a national authority linkage and digitized G2P payments, plus analytics-driven targeting and ministry dashboards, with explicit 2030 outcomes such as full digitization and leakage reduction targets.


  • Justice & Judiciary digitization: targets the large case backlog with e-courts and end-to-end digital case management, with outcomes including digitization of new filings and backlog reduction targets by 2030.


  • Resilience & Public Safety: integrates AI/IoT/satellite/drone-enabled early warning, pipeline/grid monitoring, emergency alerting and intelligent transport safety, backed by a KPI framework over 2025–2030 (coverage expansion and response-time reduction goals).


Human Capital: A Multi-Track National Skills Program

The strategy treats capacity building as a “make-or-break” constraint: growth in ICT exports and freelancing is acknowledged, but current training programs are described as fragmented, mismatched with industry demand, and unevenly distributed geographically—requiring institutionalized, outcome-focused reform.

It then proposes a lifecycle-based, multi-track skills framework, including foundational digital skills through schooling, plus scaled infrastructure such as digital classrooms and a national education cloud, supported by KPI tables that define annual scale-up targets through 2030 (e.g., coverage of digital classrooms, student e-learning usage, teacher ICT training volumes).

Institutional and Governance Reforms

The document includes a substantial reform agenda across institutions and governance mechanisms, emphasizing that digital transformation requires enforceable coordination, standards, procurement reform, cybersecurity capability, and data-center/cloud operating reforms. The table of contents indicates detailed reform sections for multiple institutions and cross-cutting governance topics (coordination, PPP, procurement, funding mechanisms, M&E).

Monitoring, Evaluation, and Accountability

A defined M&E framework is included with KPIs, real-time dashboarding, independent audits/third-party evaluation, and feedback loops—positioning performance management as an integral control layer, not an afterthought.

Phased Roadmap to 2030 and Beyond

The strategy uses phased implementation planning through 2030 and identifies some initiatives with longer time horizons beyond 2030. The roadmap section explicitly lays out multi-phase sequencing (foundation, scaling/optimization, consolidation/global integration) and includes “long-term vision” items beyond 2030 such as semiconductor industry development and national drone coverage, as well as a deep-tech identity framing (BEAR: Biotech, Electronics, AI, Robotics).

Conclusion

The National Digital Transformation Strategy 2025-2030 is not just a plan for digitization but a blueprint for a unified, citizen-centric Bangladesh. By harmonizing legal mandates with technical infrastructure and human capital development, it aims to build a state that is efficient, transparent, and equitable—where technology serves the citizen, and the digital economy drives national prosperity.





1. Strategic Vision & Context

1.1 Background

The National Digital Transformation Strategy provides a comprehensive framework for 2025-2030, addressing systemic challenges while fostering socio-economic growth and building a secure, inclusive, and technologically literate society.

The strategy is anchored in nine core pillars:

  1. Unified Digital Public Infrastructure

  2. Citizen-Centric Governance

  3. Data Sovereignty & Legal Certainty

  4. National Connectivity Backbone

  5. Autonomous Regulatory Oversight Over Data and Privacy

  6. Economic Resilience & Innovation

  7. Future-Ready Human Capital

  8. Institutional Interoperability

  9. Proactive Security & Resilience

The overarching vision is to secure a resilient, sustainable, and future-ready economy that is technologically advanced yet equitable and human-centric.

With over 120 million internet users and a rapidly growing digital economy, Bangladesh must act decisively. Outdated legal frameworks, siloed systems, and vendor lock-ins risk impeding innovation and increasing inequality. This strategy provides the roadmap for swift reforms that will protect sovereignty, empower citizens, and establish Bangladesh as one of South Asia's premier digital hubs.

Comprehensive Digital Governance and Reform for Bangladesh

Bangladesh faces enormous opportunities as well as significant challenges at this critical juncture in its digital transformation journey. The government has implemented a thorough digital transformation plan with a planned timeline from 2025 to 2030 in order to traverse this terrain. The goal of this roadmap is to go beyond simple digitization and create a strong, secure, and inclusive digital ecosystem that will promote economic expansion, improve the effectiveness of governance, and guarantee sustainable development. Building a robust, just, and future-ready economy that can take advantage of cutting-edge technologies like blockchain and artificial intelligence for the good of all citizens is the fundamental tenet of the strategy.

The following major goals form the basis of the strategy, which aims to modernize the country's digital policies and infrastructure:

1. Create Interoperable Digital Infrastructure: Dismantling the institutional silos that presently impede digital advancement is one of the main objectives. The nation's data systems are disjointed and do not have secure interoperability, including those for tax records, health information, and National ID (NID). The roadmap suggests establishing a National Data Exchange (NRDEX) and a national digital architecture to enable safe, instantaneous data sharing between government organizations. The quality and security of citizen data will be enhanced, operational expenses will be decreased, and redundant data collection will be eliminated. More effective and citizen-focused services will result from the guiding principle of "collect once, use many times."

2. Strengthen Cybersecurity & Data Governance: To guard against the increasing risk of cyberattacks and data breaches, the strategy gives cybersecurity top priority. This entails implementing the Personal Data Protection Ordinance, 2025 and its 2026 Amendment, which strengthens data sovereignty. In support of these legislative efforts, Bangladesh has set ambitious targets to train 20,000 cybersecurity experts by 2027 and increase this to 50,000 by 2030. Building a robust national defense against cyber threats and creating a workforce with the skills to meet demands worldwide depend heavily on this massive upskilling program.

3. Reform the ICT Division for Efficient Service Delivery: The ICT Division will experience substantial reform in order to guarantee the strategy's successful execution. The objective is to make it a more effective and flexible organization that can spearhead digital transformation in all government agencies. This will entail improving technical capability, simplifying procedures, and revising the guidelines for dealing with suppliers and other governmental bodies.

4. Expand Digital Services for Businesses & Citizens: The roadmap calls for a comprehensive transformation of the way government services are provided. Moving past partial digitization to a point where all services can be accessed and completed fully online is the goal of expanding end-to-end digital services. By eliminating the need for in-person meetings and bureaucracy, this will significantly increase efficiency and convenience for citizens.

5. Facilitate a thriving innovation ecosystem and digital economy: The strategy is intended to stimulate economic expansion. This includes modernizing tax collection via the NBR API Connectivity Hub, establishing a Universal Payment Gateway to unify disparate payment systems, and improving the regulatory climate to attract foreign direct investment and startups. Fostering an innovation-driven ecosystem that can advance the country's digital economy is the aim.

6. By 2030, increase the number of ICT professionals to 7-8 million by upskilling them: Investing in human capital is a fundamental component of the strategy. The government has set the ambitious goal of increasing the number of ICT professionals from the current number to 7-8 million by 2030. This will be accomplished by a number of measures, such as updated university curricula and collaborations with foreign organizations to offer top-notch instruction and certifications.

7. Citizen-Centric "Paperless" Services: The roadmap calls for a comprehensive transformation toward 95% End-to-End Digital Services. The goal is to enforce a "Zero-Visit" mandate, where citizens can Apply, Pay, Verify, and Receive services (such as Land Mutation or Passport renewal) fully online via mobile devices, eliminating the need for physical lobbying and intermediary bureaucracy.

7. Future-Ready Human Capital (The BEAR Framework): Investing in human capital is a fundamental component. The government has set the ambitious goal of building a workforce of 8 million professionals by 2030. The focus shifts from basic literacy to Deep-Tech mastery under the BEAR framework (Biotech, Electronics, AI, and Robotics), supported by updated university curricula and international certification partnerships.

8. AI-Resilient Labor Market : The strategy incorporates a proactive plan to upskill blue collar workers and migrant workforce. Recognizing the threat of automation, the government aims to preserve jobs by implementing "Human-in-the-Loop" training programs, and just transition for sustainable job creation, ensuring workers are equipped with digital skills to manage AI-assisted manufacturing rather than being replaced by it.

8. Create NDGA: The roadmap suggests creating an independent Data & AI Authority to guarantee strong and objective governance. Operating under the Supreme Court's supervision, this body will be immune to administrative or governmental meddling. In order to uphold national data sovereignty and foster public trust, its mandate will include monitoring AI policies, enforcing data protection laws, and serving as an impartial arbiter in disputes involving data.

To sum up, the National Digital Transformation Strategy is a thorough and progressive road map for Bangladesh's future. By giving institutional reform, cybersecurity, workforce development, and ethical governance top priority, it tackles fundamental issues head-on. In addition to updating the nation's digital infrastructure, this roadmap aims to empower its people, spur economic expansion, and establish Bangladesh as a leader in the world's digital arena.

1.2 Current Digital Landscape & Challenges

Bangladesh's ICT ecosystem faces significant systemic challenges that this strategy aims to address comprehensively:

1.2.1 Fragmented Digital Ecosystem

Major data repositories including the National ID (NID), Birth and Death Registration System (BDRIS), Civil Registration and Vital Statistics (CRVS), Bangladesh Bank, Bangladesh Bureau of Statistics (BBS), National Board of Revenue (NBR), Health MIS, and BANBAIS operate in isolation without secure interoperable mechanisms. This fragmentation results in:

  • Redundant infrastructure and increased operational costs across ministries

  • Lack of coordination among government agencies leading to duplicated efforts

  • Citizen data vulnerabilities from inconsistent security protocols

  • Ministries procuring standalone assets that fail to serve end-users effectively

  • Data-informed policymaking and decision making is difficult when data is siloed

1.2.2 Data Governance & Security Risks

Bangladesh has experienced serious data breaches, with sensitive citizen information including NID data, birth and death registrations, financial transactions, and mobile banking details exposed on the dark web. The absence of a structured data protection policy and robust cybersecurity framework has led to:

  • Unauthorized access and misuse of citizen data undermining public trust

  • Cybersecurity vulnerabilities including weak firewalls and absence of a fully operational National CERT

  • Mismanagement of government digital infrastructure leading to systemic inefficiencies

  • Citizens more vulnerable to cyber-threats etc. due to data leakage



1.2.3 Incomplete Digital Service Delivery

While over 350 services have been digitized, most remain partially digital, requiring physical visits for verification and approvals. The MyGov and D-Nothi platforms have not achieved full adoption across ministries. Key gaps include:

  • Continued dependency on manual verification that bottlenecks service delivery

  • Inconsistent user experience across different government portals

  • Poor service and interface designs which lead to increased reliance on middlemen for service delivery.

  • Limited mobile optimization despite a comparatively higher number of mobile users.

1.2.4 Digital Economy Bottlenecks

Despite hosting over 100 million digital financial accounts, Bangladesh lacks:

  • A universal payment ecosystem integrating banks, Mobile Financial Services (MFS), and government transactions

  • Interoperable taxation, VAT, and financial reporting mechanisms

  • A startup-friendly regulatory environment, limiting FDI inflows

1.2.5 Skills Gap

While Bangladesh has a large youth demographic, the workforce suffers from a critical "skills mismatch" that threatens future competitiveness. There is a profound gap between the output of the traditional education system and the demands of the global digital economy.

  • There is currently no master plan identifying high-demand competencies. Training programs are often static, failing to adapt to rapid shifts in technology (e.g., Generative AI, Quantum Computing).

  • While entry-level freelancers exist, there is a severe shortage of mid-level technical managers and solution architects capable of leading complex projects.

  • Current initiatives struggle to reach key demographics—specifically rural women, madrasa students, and marginalized communities—leaving a significant portion of the potential workforce untapped.

  • While basic digital literacy is improving, deep expertise in cybersecurity, cloud architecture, and data science remains scarce, forcing reliance on foreign consultants for critical infrastructure.

1.2.6 Policy Paralysis & Regulatory Gaps

The current legal and regulatory framework is reactive rather than proactive, creating friction that stifles innovation and export growth.

  • Outdated foreign exchange regulations make it difficult for IT firms to pay for essential global services (cloud hosting, software licenses), forcing many to operate through informal channels or register offshore.

  • The absence of mandated data protection laws leaves citizen data exposed to abuse by both state and non-state actors. There is no legal recourse for data breaches or unauthorized surveillance.

  • Without a mandatory "Once-Only" data policy, government agencies continue to operate in silos, leading to massive duplication of efforts and citizen harassment.

  • Policies fail to address emerging technologies. There is no clear framework for AI ethics, blockchain assets, or drone usage, creating uncertainty that deters foreign direct investment (FDI).

  • The lack of a robust Cyber Safety Ordinance has left citizens vulnerable to cyber-harassment and financial fraud, eroding trust in digital platforms.

1.3 Vision Statement

"Establishing Bangladesh as a trusted, interoperable, and resilient nation by leveraging Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) to drive inclusive growth, ensure data sovereignty, and empower every citizen through a connected governance ecosystem."

This vision captures Bangladesh's aspiration to leverage information and communication technology as the cornerstone of national development. It recognizes that digital transformation must serve all citizens equitably, driving economic prosperity while ensuring no community is left behind in the march toward a knowledge-based economy.

1.4 Mission Statement

"To build a secure and unified digital backbone by operationalizing the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDIA), mandating interoperable data exchange, and deploying citizen-centric DPI that eliminates fragmentation, ensures transparency, and catalyzes a future-ready digital economy."

The mission translates the vision into actionable mandates, emphasizing three interconnected objectives: modernizing governance structures, revolutionizing public service delivery, and catalyzing economic growth through targeted ICT investments. This tripartite approach ensures that digital transformation benefits both the state apparatus and the citizens it serves.

1.5 Guiding Principles

The strategy is built upon foundational principles that guide all reform initiatives and policy decisions:

GUIDING PRINCIPLES

Principle

Description

Citizen-Centric Design

All digital services and infrastructure are designed with the end-user in mind, prioritizing accessibility, simplicity, and inclusivity across all demographics and regions.

Interoperability First

Breaking down institutional silos through mandatory data exchange standards, unified APIs, and the "collect once, use many times" philosophy to eliminate redundancy.

Digital Sovereignty

Ensuring government ownership of critical source code, preventing vendor lock-in through open standards, and maintaining control over national data assets.

Transparency & Accountability

Publishing service metrics in real-time, conducting public audits of digital systems, and establishing clear KPIs for all reform initiatives.

AI-Driven Efficiency

Leveraging artificial intelligence and data analytics for evidence-based policymaking, predictive governance, and automated service delivery where appropriate.

Collaborative Ecosystem

Fostering partnerships between government, industry, academia, and civil society to drive innovation and ensure sustainable implementation.

Localized Innovation

Adopting global best practices while tailoring solutions to Bangladesh's unique socioeconomic context, with Bangla-first interfaces and local language accessibility.



1.6 Nine Strategic Pillars

The roadmap is structured around nine interconnected pillars that collectively address Bangladesh's digital transformation needs:

  1. Unified Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI): Establishing the Digital Public Infrastructure. This pillar shifts focus from individual systems to a unified framework for Digital Identity, Payment Gateways, and Secure Data Exchange.

  2. Citizen-Centric Governance: Operationalizing the "Digital by Default" and "Bangla First" mandates. It ensures that all public services are inclusive, mobile-optimized, and end-to-end digital by 2030.

  3. Data Sovereignty & Legal Certainty: Anchoring the digital ecosystem in a robust legal perimeter. This pillar ensures that national data assets are protected, individual privacy is sacrosanct, and digital rights are enforceable through the PDPO, CSO, and NDGO.

  1. National Connectivity Backbone: Treating high-speed connectivity as a fundamental right. This involves transitioning to a National Fiber Bank and ensuring 100% fiber-to-tower connectivity to eliminate the rural-urban digital divide.

  2. Autonomous Regulatory Oversight: Empowering the National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) as an independent constitutional body. The NDGA serves as the guardian of the national digital architecture, ensuring interoperability across all 100+ ministries.

  3. Economic Resilience & Innovation: Fostering a $10 billion digital economy. This pillar streamlines the startup ecosystem, enables cross-border digital trade, and modernizes fiscal systems like the Tax-VAT API Hub.

  4. Future-Ready Human Capital: Moving from general literacy to Deep-Tech mastery. The goal is to build a workforce of 8 million professionals skilled in AI, Cybersecurity, and the BEAR framework (Biotech, Electronics, AI, Robotics).

  5. Institutional Interoperability: Enforcing the "Collect Once, Use Many Times" principle. This eliminates bureaucratic friction by mandating that government agencies share data through secure, real-time APIs instead of manual transfers.

  6. Proactive Security & Resilience: Transitioning from passive defense to Active Threat Intelligence. This pillar operationalizes the National Security Operations Center (NSOC) to protect critical information infrastructure in real-time.

1.7 Global Benchmarks

Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) models pioneered by leading digital nations provide valuable reference points for Bangladesh's transformation:

Country/Region

Key Achievements

Lessons for Bangladesh

Estonia

99% of services online; X-Road data exchange; blockchain-secured identity; digital residency program

Federated architecture model; decentralized yet secure systems; annual savings equivalent to 2% of GDP

Singapore

GovTech Agency centralizes 1,300+ applications; rapid prototyping; citizen-focused innovation

Centralized tech procurement; service design standards; AI integration in healthcare and governance

India

Aadhaar digital identity for 1.4B citizens; UPI payment platform processing billions monthly; clear regulatory frameworks

Scale-appropriate DPI; economic inclusion through digital payments; population-scale identity management

South Korea

Data-driven policymaking; advanced 5G infrastructure; comprehensive e-government services

Analytics-based governance; high-speed connectivity as enabler; private sector collaboration models

Denmark/EU

GDPR compliance; green ICT practices; citizen privacy protection; DANIDA funding model

Strong privacy frameworks; sustainable digital practices; milestone-based funding for talent development

UK

Government Digital Service (GDS); agile procurement; open standards; user-centric design methodology

Skill-based recruitment; agile project management; value-driven procurement over lowest cost





Bangladesh's roadmap aligns with these established international models while placing strong emphasis on affordability, inclusivity, and regional leadership to ensure that digital transformation reflects the nation's socioeconomic priorities while maintaining global competitiveness.

1.8 Reform Urgency

With more than 120 million internet users and a rapidly expanding digital economy, Bangladesh stands at a critical juncture. The urgency for reform stems from multiple converging factors:

Risk of Missed Opportunities: Global industries are rapidly adopting IoT, AI-driven automation, and green ICT practices. Without swift reforms, Bangladesh risks losing opportunities to attract foreign investment and create millions of high-skilled jobs.

Competitive Pressure: Regional competitors are advancing rapidly. Bangladesh must act decisively to establish itself as one of South Asia's premier digital hubs.

Security Imperatives: Past data breaches and security vulnerabilities demand immediate strengthening of cybersecurity frameworks and data protection mechanisms.

Citizen Expectations: A digitally aware population increasingly expects seamless, efficient, and transparent government services.

Bridging the Digital Divide: Despite high internet penetration, a stark disparity remains between urban centers and rural peripheries. Services such as telemedicine, e-learning, and digital commerce have to be scaled up to reach marginalized communities. Failing to bridge this gap risks creating a "two-tier" society where digital exclusion perpetuates economic poverty.

The Youth Employment Crisis & Skills Mismatch: Bangladesh faces a paradox of high graduate unemployment alongside a shortage of skilled ICT professionals. The traditional education system is struggling to keep pace with industry demands. Immediate reforms are essential to convert the country's "demographic dividend" into a "digital dividend" by upskilling millions of unemployed graduates in deep-tech fields (AI, Cybersecurity, Blockchain), ensuring they are employable in the global market.

Safeguarding Civil Liberties & Data Privacy: As the government collects unprecedented amounts of personal data (NID, Health, Financial), the risk of misuse, whether by state actors or private corporations has grown exponentially. Reform is urgent to establish a rights-based framework (via the PDPO) that protects citizens from mass surveillance, unauthorized profiling, and data abuse, thereby restoring public trust in digital governance.

Mitigating AI-Induced Labor Displacement: The unregulated adoption of Artificial Intelligence and automation poses a significant threat to labor-intensive sectors like the Ready-Made Garments (RMG) industry and traditional BPOs. There is a critical need for proactive policy planning to address potential mass layoffs. The strategy must prioritize "Human-in-the-Loop" economic models and safety nets to ensure that AI serves as a tool for augmentation and productivity rather than a driver of sudden unemployment.

This urgency necessitates quick policy updates, robust regulatory organizations like NDGA, and strategic investments in digital infrastructure. Prompt reforms will protect sovereignty, empower citizens, and position Bangladesh for sustainable digital growth.

1.9 Strategic Objectives (2025-2030)

The following major objectives form the basis of the strategy, aimed at modernizing the country's digital policies and infrastructure:



Strategic Objective

Description & Target

Unified Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI)

Operationalize the "National Digital Rail" via the NRDEX platform. Mandate the "Collect Once, Use Many Times" principle to eliminate data silos and ensure seamless, secure API-based exchange across all agencies.

Proactive Cyber Resilience

Operationalize the Cyber Safety Ordinance (CSO). Transition from reactive defense to Active Threat Intelligence via the NSOC. Train 50,000 cybersecurity professionals to protect Critical Information Infrastructure (CII).

Institutional Restructuring

Redefine ICT Division as the "Policy Architect" and DoICT as the "Grassroots Bridge." Enforce Vendor Neutrality and open standards in procurement to break vendor lock-in and ensure competitive pricing.

Citizen-Centric "Paperless" Services

Achieve 95% End-to-End Digital Services by 2030. Mandate a "Zero-Visit" policy for verification, ensuring all services (Apply, Pay, Verify, Receive) are accessible via mobile without physical office visits.

Digital Economy & Fiscal Integration

Deploy the NBR API Connectivity Hub for real-time tax transparency and the Universal Payment Gateway for cashless transactions. Foster a startup-friendly climate to achieve $10 Billion in ICT exports by 2030.

Future-Ready Human Capital

Build a workforce of 8 million professionals focused on Deep-Tech mastery. Shift curriculum focus to the BEAR Framework (Biotech, Electronics, AI, Robotics) to move beyond basic data entry to high-value engineering.

AI-Resilient Labor Market

Proactively upskill the RMG and Migrant workforce to mitigate automation risks. Implement "Human-in-the-Loop" training programs to ensure technology augments rather than replaces labor during the Industry 4.0 transition.

Autonomous Regulatory Oversight

Operationalize the National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) as an independent constitutional body. Empower it to enforce the PDPO, audit algorithms for bias, and serve as the supreme guardian of citizen data rights.

Data Sovereignty & Legal Certainty

Enforce the "Public Money, Public Code" policy to ensure state ownership of source code. Establish a robust legal perimeter that guarantees Data Sovereignty, protecting national data assets from unauthorized foreign jurisdiction.



---

End of Chapter 1



2. Legal Landscape

2.1 Why Legal Reform Matters

Bangladesh’s digital transformation agenda requires more than new platforms and digitized services; it requires legal certainty that enables trust, safeguards citizen rights, and creates enforceable rules for data and cyber risk management. The strategy therefore treats the legal landscape as a foundational layer for the entire reform program, particularly for:

  • Trust and legitimacy: citizen confidence in digital services depends on privacy protection, transparent enforcement, and meaningful redress.

  • Interoperability at scale: secure data exchange across agencies requires clear legal authority, consistent standards, and defined accountability.

  • Future-readiness: emerging technologies (AI, data exchange infrastructure, identity systems, cloud) require modern terminology, governance models, and compliance duties.

In 2025, the Government enacted three landmark ordinances that collectively establish the legal foundation for a secure, interoperable, and citizen-centric digital ecosystem:

  1. Cyber Safety Ordinance, 2025 (CSO) – Addressing cybercrime, online safety, and digital security

  2. Personal Data Protection Ordinance, 2025 (PDPO) – Protecting citizens' privacy and data rights

  3. National Data Governance Ordinance, 2025 (NDGO) – Enabling secure data sharing and interoperability

These three ordinances work in concert to create a comprehensive legal architecture. The CSO establishes the security perimeter, the PDPO protects individual rights within that perimeter, and the NDGO enables productive and secure use of data across government and the private sector. Together, they align Bangladesh with international standards such as GDPR while addressing the nation's unique socioeconomic context.

2.2 Cyber Safety Ordinance, 2025 (CSO)

2.2.1 Background and Purpose

The Cyber Safety Ordinance, 2025 has been enacted to update and replace previous legislation, including the Digital Security Act, 2018 and the Cyber Security Act, 2023. This new legal framework modernizes terminology, protects citizens' rights, and addresses emerging cyber threats while balancing security needs with freedom of expression.


Addressing Past Misuse

A core driver for this reform was the documented misuse of previous legislation for politically motivated harassment. For example, under the DSA and CSA, hundreds of cases were filed against journalists, activists, and ordinary citizens for "tarnishing the image of the state" or "hurting religious sentiments". Notable instances of misuse included:

  • Arbitrary Arrests: Numerous individuals were detained for social media posts, often without clear evidence of a crime, leading to prolonged pretrial detentions.

  • Effect on Expression: Broadly defined sections regarding "defamation" and "propaganda" were used to suppress legitimate dissent and investigative journalism.

The CSO decisively breaks this cycle by repealing the CSA, 2023 and annulling all pending cases under its most controversial sections (including sections 21, 25, 28, and 29).

A Shield for the Citizens, not Government

The new ordinance pivots focus toward protecting citizens—especially women and children—from modern, non-political cybercrimes that were previously neglected. It introduces explicit protections against:

  • Gender-Based Crimes: Criminalizing Non-Consensual Sexual Imagery (NCSI), "revenge porn," AI-generated deepfake pornography, and sextortion.

  • Protection of Minors: Dedicated provisions for Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM).

  • Procedural Guardrails: To prevent harassment, only the police, the victim, or their legal representatives are now permitted to file a case, effectively ending the practice of third-party politically motivated suits.

By including domain experts and civil society in the judiciary process and the National Cyber Security Council, the CSO ensures that technical and human rights perspectives guide the law's application, moving Bangladesh toward a trusted and secure digital perimeter.

2.2.2 Key Features and Provisions

Recognition of Rights

For the first time in Bangladesh's legal framework, the ordinance recognizes citizens' right to continuous internet access as part of their cyber protection. This landmark provision acknowledges the internet as essential infrastructure for modern life and economic participation.

Modernized Definitions and Terminology

Section 2 introduces and modernizes several critical definitions to address contemporary digital challenges:

  • Digital Identity – Formal recognition of citizens' online identities

  • National Security Operations Center (NSOC) – Central hub for cyber threat monitoring

  • Cyber Terrorism – Attacks on digital infrastructure with intent to cause mass harm, loss of life

  • Pornography Harassment – Non-consensual sharing of intimate content

  • Child Sexual Abuse Material – Specific provisions for protecting minors

  • Disinformation Campaigns – Coordinated efforts to spread false information

Proactive Threat Intelligence and Response

The ordinance establishes a robust institutional framework for real-time cyber defense, transitioning from reactive recovery to proactive, intelligence-led protection.

  • NCERT and NSOC Operations: It mandates the creation of the National Cyber Emergency Response Team (NCERT) to coordinate national incident response and the National Security Operations Center (NSOC) to serve as the apex monitoring hub for the state’s digital perimeter.

  • Mandatory Reporting for CII: Entities designated as Critical Information Infrastructure (CII)—including banks, power grids, and telecommunication hubs—are now legally required to report cyber incidents to the NCERT and maintain secure connectivity with the NSOC for real-time threat monitoring.

  • Active Intelligence Sharing: The framework moves beyond passive defense to active intelligence sharing. The National Cyber Security Agency (NCSA) is empowered to analyze telemetry data and disseminate actionable threat intelligence across domestic sectors.

  • International Cooperation and Threat Intel: Recognizing that cyber threats are borderless, the CSO specifically mandates International Threat Intelligence Sharing. The NCSA is authorized to act as the national point of contact for global cybersecurity alliances, enabling Bangladesh to exchange real-time threat data, malware signatures, and vulnerability research with international CERTs, foreign security agencies, and global technology partners. This ensures the national defense is fortified by global early-warning systems against emerging zero-day exploits and state-sponsored actors.

Criminal Offenses

The ordinance establishes clear categories of punishable offenses:

Offense Category

Description & Provisions

Crimes with AI

Crimes committed using artificial intelligence or AI-powered tools are explicitly punishable, addressing emerging technological threats.

Online Gambling

Section 20 addresses and criminalizes cyber gambling operations.

Violence & Harassment

Section 25 addresses pornography harassment, blackmailing, and sextortion. Section 25(3) includes special provisions for offenses against women and children. "Revenge porn" is defined as non-consensual sharing of intimate images.

Identity Forgery

Section 23(4) categorizes forgery of citizen's national identity as a punishable crime.

Instigating Violence

Section 26 addresses publication of content that clearly instigates religious or communal hatred and violence, while preserving freedom of expression.



Content Blocking and Removal

  • Section 8(1) grants the National Cybersecurity Agency (NCSA) power to remove or block digital content posing threats to national security or public safety

  • Section 8(3) mandates the government to maintain full transparency regarding all blocked or removed digital content. Any content removed must be unblocked if tribunal permission is not obtained within three days of the action. Furthermore, the relevant authority is obligated to periodically release a comprehensive list of all blocked and removed content to ensure public transparency and institutional accountability.

Justification: The three-day tribunal requirement balances security needs with due process, preventing arbitrary censorship while allowing rapid response to genuine threats.

National Council Restructuring

  • Section 12 details the restructuring of the National Cyber Security Council

  • Section 12(1) provides for expert individuals or civil society representatives as council members

  • The Chief Adviser or Prime Minister serves as Chairman of the Council

Victim Protection and Discharge

  • Section 30 provides for monetary compensation to victims, ordered by the tribunal

  • Section 31(4) allows immediate release of individuals if no evidence is found for offenses like cyber terrorism, pornography harassment, and blackmailing

Repeal of Previous Laws

The ordinance repeals the Cyber Security Act, 2023 and annuls all pending cases under specific sections of that act, including sections 21, 24, 25, 26, 27, 28, 29, 31, and 34. Section 46 specifies which offenses are bailable, non-bailable, or compoundable, providing clarity for law enforcement and judiciary.



2.2.3 Implementation Framework

To ensure effective implementation of the CSO, the following supporting measures are being developed:

  • Bangladesh Cybersecurity Strategy 2026-2030: National-level framework for building a resilient and secure digital ecosystem

  • Cyber Safety Policy: Supplementary guidance for public and private sectors on secure digital operations

  • NSOC Establishment: $5 million World Bank proposal through EDGE project for National Security Operations Center

  • Specialized Training: Programs for judiciary and law enforcement on cybercrime investigation and digital forensics

  • Public Awareness Campaigns: Multimedia content in Bengali and English focusing on digital hygiene and social engineering awareness



2.3 Personal Data Protection Ordinance, 2025 (PDPO)

2.3.1 Background and Purpose

The Personal Data Protection Ordinance, 2025 establishes a comprehensive legal framework for protecting personal data and privacy rights of citizens in Bangladesh. This ordinance introduces modern data protection standards aligned with international best practices, including GDPR principles, while accounting for Bangladesh's developmental context.

2.3.2 Recognition of Data Subject Rights

The ordinance recognizes citizens' fundamental rights regarding their personal data, establishing unprecedented control over how personal information is collected, processed, and shared:

Right

Description (Section)

Right to Access

Data subjects can access their personal data held by data-fiduciaries (Section 10)

Right to Data Portability

Right to receive personal data in structured, commonly used format and transfer to another data-fiduciary through Federated Interoperable Ecosystems (Section 11)

Right to Correction & Erasure

Data subjects can request correction of inaccurate data and erasure under specified conditions (Section 12)

Right to Consent Withdrawal

Data subjects can withdraw consent and request deletion of their personal data (Section 13)

System-wide Propagation

Updates, corrections, or erasures must be propagated across all systems where the data is stored (Section 14)



2.3.3 Data Processing Requirements

Lawful Basis for Processing

Section 5 requires data-fiduciaries to obtain explicit consent from data subjects before processing personal data, with specific conditions for lawful processing. This consent-first approach ensures citizens maintain control over their information.

Sensitive Personal Data

Section 6 provides special protections for sensitive categories including:

  • Genetic data and biometr ic data

  • Health data and medical records

  • Sexual orientation information

  • Financial data

  • Children's data (requiring verifiable parental consent under Section 8 for individuals under 18)

2.3.4 Data-Fiduciary Obligations

  • Transparency (Section 15): Data-fiduciaries must maintain transparent practices and inform data subjects about data processing

  • Security Measures (Section 17): Implementation of technical and organizational security measures to prevent data breaches

  • Data Breach Notification (Section 20): Mandatory notification to the Authority and affected data subjects in case of personal data breaches

  • Data Protection Impact Assessment (Section 21): Required for significant data-fiduciaries processing sensitive data

  • Chief Data Officer (Section 23): Significant data-fiduciaries must appoint a Chief Data Officer

2.3.5 Cross-Border Data Transfer

  • Section 29 establishes conditions for transferring personal data outside Bangladesh, requiring adequate protection levels in recipient jurisdictions. Pursuant to the 2026 Amendment to Section 29, any cloud-based processing of Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) or Restricted Personal Data requires the maintenance of at least one synchronized real-time copy within Bangladesh.

  • Section 30 provides for anonymization, pseudonymization, and de-identification of data for cross-border transfers

2.3.6 Offenses and Penalties

Offense

Penalty

Non-compliance with consent requirements (Section 36)

Up to 3 years imprisonment or fine up to 10 lakh taka

Unauthorized processing of sensitive personal data (Section 37)

Up to 5 years imprisonment or fine up to 20 lakh taka

Unauthorized disclosure of children's personal data (Section 38)

Up to 3 years imprisonment or fine up to 5 lakh taka

Unauthorized interception or extraction of personal data (Section 39)

Up to 5 years imprisonment or fine up to 10 lakh taka

Unauthorized disclosure of personally identifiable information (Section 41)

Up to 5 years imprisonment or fine up to 15 lakh taka

Corporate liability for company violations (Section 44)

Officers and company held jointly liable

Violations by government officials (Section 47)

Government departments and officers held accountable





2.4 National Data Governance Ordinance, 2025 (NDGO)

2.4.1 Background and Purpose

The National Data Governance Ordinance, 2025 establishes a comprehensive framework for data governance, interoperability, and management across Bangladesh. This ordinance creates the National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) and sets standards for data sharing, protection, and utilization in both public and private sectors. It represents the enabling infrastructure for Bangladesh's transition from fragmented "data islands" to an integrated, trusted digital state.

2.4.2 Key Definitions and Concepts

Section 2 introduces comprehensive definitions critical to understanding the framework:

  • Interoperability: The ability of different systems to exchange and use information through technological, semantic, legal, and organizational dimensions

  • Zero-Trust Architecture: Security model requiring verification for every access request regardless of network location

  • Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI): Shared, secure, interoperable digital systems including identity, payments, and data exchange

  • NRDEX Platform: National Responsible Data Exchange platform for trusted API-based data sharing

  • BNDIA: Bangladesh National Data Governance and Interoperability Architecture

2.4.3 National Data Governance Policy Board

Establishment and Structure

  • Section 5 creates the National Data Governance Policy Board with 15 members including government officials, technical experts, private sector representatives, and civil society members

  • Section 8 establishes the Chief Adviser as Chair

  • Section 10 establishes a Secretariat for administrative support

  • The NDGA is an independent statutory body with perpetual succession and its own fund

2.4.4 Organizational Structure of the NDGA

NDGA will be headed by a Chairperson and up to six Members, supported by six specialized divisions:

  1. Data Protection & Management Division – data classification, privacy, and consent management

  2. Technical Standards & Interoperability Division – API certification, metadata standards, and NRDEX operations

  3. Legal & Compliance Division – PDPO, cybersecurity, and enforcement

  4. Administration & Finance Division – budgeting, staffing, and fund oversight

  5. Coordination & Capacity Building Division – sectoral data offices, training, and awareness

  6. Citizen Data & AI Governance Division – unified digital ID, AI registry, algorithmic ethics, and innovation sandbox

2.4.5 Mandate and Core Functions of the NDGA

Domain

Key Responsibilities

Data Governance & Interoperability

Implement BNDIA; operate NRDEX; standardize APIs; ensure metadata and audit trails; maintain the National Citizen Data Repository

Privacy & Security

Enforce PDPO and Cybersecurity Ordinance; monitor breaches; accredit data centers; maintain encryption and TRUST-CCA standards

AI Ethics & Model Oversight

Certify AI algorithms used by public agencies; manage national AI registry; audit datasets for bias and transparency; maintain AI innovation sandbox

Compliance & Enforcement

Conduct inspections and VAPT audits; impose penalties for misuse; coordinate with the Grievance Redress Council

Capacity Building

Train 5,000 officials in data stewardship and AI ethics; publish open data handbooks and interoperability toolkits



Powers and Functions (Section 23)

  • Enforce ordinance provisions and ensure compliance across government

  • Manage BNDIA and NRDEX Platform operations

  • Ensure data interoperability across government and private sectors

  • Facilitate data sharing through Data Resharing Agreements (DRA)

  • Oversee digital infrastructure development

  • Manage electronic identity (e-ID) systems with unified identity management

  • Establish National Datacentre Ecosystem (NDE) with disaster recovery

  • Provide technical certifications and compliance oversight

  • Issue sector-specific guidelines for banking, MFS, and digital payments

2.4.6 Chief Data Officers (CDOs)

Each ministry will appoint a Chief Data Officer (CDO) responsible for data inventory, interoperability compliance, and coordination with NDGA. This ensures institutional accountability and creates a network of data stewards across government.

2.4.7 Data Classification Framework

Section 31 establishes a four-tier classification system for data repositories:

Data Category

Description

Core Citizen Data

11-digit citizen identification, Tax Identification Number (TIN), birth/death registration, land records, judicial system repositories

Government Policy & Administration

Internal government data used for policy formulation and administrative decisions

Public Sector Service Delivery

Data generated through public service delivery requiring interoperability across agencies

Private Sector Service Delivery

Data from private sector entities regulated under NDGA guidelines for banking, MFS, and digital payments



2.4.8 Organizational Responsibilities (Section 34)

All organizations must comply with the following requirements:

  • Compliance with ordinance and BNDIA guidelines

  • Purpose-based data processing and interoperability

  • Use of NRDEX Platform for all data exchange

  • Technology infrastructure modernization with zero-trust architecture

  • Cloud-first policies and API-based integration

  • Open-source platform adoption where applicable

  • Support for Digital Public Infrastructure development

2.4.9 Cross-Border Data Transfer and Penalties

Section 43 requires Authority approval for international data transfers. Violations are punishable by up to 25 years imprisonment or fine up to 25 lakh taka, reflecting the seriousness with which Bangladesh views data sovereignty.

Section 60 establishes a three-year transitional period for organizations to achieve full compliance.

2.4.10 Dispute Resolution Mechanism

  • Grievance Redressal Committee (Sections 46-48): Handles citizen complaints with decisions within 30 days (extendable to 45 days)

  • Appeals (Section 49): Appeals to designated tribunal within 30 days

  • Tribunal Powers (Section 50): Can summon witnesses and examine evidence



2.5 Legal Framework Integration

2.5.1 Complementary Roles

The three ordinances form an integrated legal architecture where each addresses a distinct but interconnected domain:

Aspect

CSO

PDPO

NDGO

Primary Focus

Cybersecurity & Crime Prevention

Individual Privacy Rights

Data Sharing & Interoperability

Key Institution

NCSA, NSOC

Data Protection Authority

NDGA

Primary Beneficiary

Society & State

Individual Citizens

Government & Economy

Core Platform

NSOC

Consent Management

NRDEX, BNDIA



2.5.2 Cross-References and Dependencies

The ordinances explicitly reference each other to ensure coherent implementation:

  • NDGO Section 42 requires organizations to establish consent management systems per the PDPO

  • NDGA's Legal & Compliance Division is responsible for enforcing both PDPO and the Cybersecurity Ordinance

  • Data breach notifications under PDPO are coordinated with NCSA under CSO provisions

  • AI governance under the National AI Policy is supported by the data governance framework of PDPO and NDGO

2.6 Strategic Impact of Legal Reforms

Strategic Impact of the Legal Trinity

The simultaneous enactment of the Cyber Safety Ordinance (CSO), Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO), and National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO) creates a synergistic "Legal Trinity" that fundamentally reshapes Bangladesh's digital ecosystem. This framework moves beyond isolated regulation to create a cohesive environment for trust, innovation, and security.

Impact on Citizen Trust & Rights

  • From Fear to Empowerment: By repealing the Digital Security Act and introducing the CSO, the state shifts from a posture of surveillance to one of protection. Citizens regain the confidence to participate in the digital economy without fear of arbitrary harassment, fostering a vibrant online civil society.

  • Data Ownership: The PDPO grants citizens legal ownership of their digital footprint. The "Right to Forget" and "Consent Management" provisions empower individuals to control how their personal data is used by both state and private entities, building profound trust in digital services.

Impact on Digital Economy & Innovation

  • Market Certainty for Investors: The clear data classification and cross-border transfer rules in the PDPO and NDGO provide international investors with regulatory certainty. This predictability is crucial for attracting FDI into the high-tech sector, as global firms require GDPR-aligned frameworks to operate.

  • Unlocking the API Economy: The NDGO’s mandate for interoperability and the establishment of the NRDEX platform eliminates the "data silos" that stifle innovation. Startups can now build value-added services on top of government data (e.g., credit scoring using utility payment history), catalysing a new wave of fintech and govtech solutions.

Impact on National Security & Sovereignty

  • Proactive Cyber Resilience: The CSO's institutionalization of the NSOC and NCERT transitions national defense from reactive firefighting to proactive threat intelligence. Mandatory reporting for Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) ensures that the state has real-time visibility into systemic risks.

  • Data Sovereignty: By enforcing data localization for critical sectors while allowing controlled cross-border flows for commerce, Bangladesh asserts sovereign control over its most valuable national asset—its data—without isolating itself from the global internet.

2.7 Key Highlights Summary

  1. Centralized Governance: NDGA created with broad regulatory powers over data, privacy, and AI

  2. Interoperability Platform: NRDEX and BNDIA ensure seamless data exchange across sectors

  3. Digital Infrastructure: Framework for secure, interoperable systems including unified identity management

  4. Rights Protection: Comprehensive data subject rights aligned with GDPR principles

  5. Strict Cross-border Controls: Authority approval required for international data transfers

  6. Significant Penalties: Heavy fines and imprisonment for violations ensure compliance

  7. Three-Year Transition: Phased implementation approach allowing organizations to adapt



---

End of Chapter 2



3. Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI)

3.1 Introduction and Definition

Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) refers to the set of shared, secure, and interoperable digital systems that form the foundation for delivering public and private services. These core systems typically include national digital identity platforms, payment gateways, data exchange frameworks, and the technical standards that allow different services to work together seamlessly.

A clear and strategic definition of DPI is essential for guiding Bangladesh's digital transformation. It provides a common framework for ministries, agencies, and stakeholders; ensures investments are inclusive, citizen-focused, and secure; and supports alignment with international standards. Without a shared definition, efforts risk becoming fragmented, leading to duplication, inefficiency, and weaker governance.

3.1.1 Bangladesh DPI Definition

As defined in the National Data Governance Ordinance 2025 (NDGO), Digital Public Infrastructure encompasses:

  • National Digital Identity Platforms: Systems enabling citizen identification, verification, and authentication

  • Payment Gateways: Universal payment systems integrating banks, MFS, and government platforms

  • Data Exchange Frameworks: Secure, API-based platforms for cross-agency data sharing (NRDEX, BNDIA)

  • Technical Standards: Zero-trust architecture, pseudonymized identity tokens, data minimization principles, Data Sovereignty & Mirroring

3.1.2 Implementation Approach

Bangladesh will formally adopt a national DPI definition through policy that:

  1. Clearly defines the scope — including identity, payments, data exchange, and interoperability tools

  2. Establishes governance principles — covering data privacy, cybersecurity, accessibility, and accountability

  3. Adopts a hybrid model — with government managing core infrastructure while enabling private sector, academia, and civil society to build interoperable services

  4. Integrates the DPI definition into the ICT Strategic Roadmap and relevant sectoral strategies

3.2 The Interoperability Journey: MESH → STAR → DSTAR

To move toward an integrated, trusted, and intelligent digital state, the Government of Bangladesh has adopted a phased interoperability roadmap—MESH → STAR → DSTAR—anchored in the National Data Governance Ordinance 2025 (NDGO). This ordinance legally establishes an autonomous National Data Governance & AI Authority (NDGA) under the Prime Minister's Office to regulate, facilitate, and secure cross-agency data exchange and responsible AI use.

The journey represents a systemic transition from isolated data systems to a cohesive digital public infrastructure. Each stage builds upon the previous, progressively introducing new architectural components, governance mechanisms, and technical capabilities that transform how government agencies interact with each other and serve citizens.

3.3 Stage 0: MESH to STAR Transition

Figure 3.1: Stage 0 – MESH to STAR

Architectural Overview

Stage 0 represents the foundational architecture implementation. At this baseline, the system exhibits the following characteristics:

Horizontal Identity Layer: At the top of the architecture, identity registries remain siloed. The National ID (NID) database contains 184 million records, the Birth and Death Registration (BDR) system holds 124 million records, and Passport maintains 1 million records. Additional identity sources include Health, Education, Address, Driving, TIN, Social Welfare, and AChalaan—each operating independently without a unified identity management framework.

SITA: The SITA (Strengthening Institutions for Transparency and Accountability), a World Bank project which aims to connect key agencies including NBR (National Board of Revenue), BBS (Bangladesh Bureau of Statistics), BPPA (Bangladesh Public Procurement Authority), CAGR (Comptroller and Auditor General), and PC (Planning Commission) through an integration point. This gateway represents the first attempt at cross-agency data exchange but operates with limited scope.

Fragmented Service Connections: Multiple service agencies are connected through point-to-point integrations rather than through a centralized gateway. These include the Election Commission, Birth/Death Register General, Statistics Department, Export Processing Bureau, Chittagong Port Authority, BEPZA, A2i, Credit Rating agencies, SIM/Telco providers, RMG Association, Large Taxpayers Unit, ASYCUDA, Micro Credit Regulatory (MRA), BRTA (Driving License), BWTA (Water Vehicles), Roads & Highway (eTicket), Rail Sheba, iBAS+, Ministry of Land, and Private Organizations. Each connection creates a mesh-like network with inherent inefficiencies.

Trust Framework (TRUST-CCA): A preliminary trust and certificate authority framework exists to ensure authenticity and integrity of data transactions, but it is not yet universally enforced across all connected systems.

Key Actions in MESH to STAR Transition

  1. Establishing Connectivity: Creating the initial network links between previously isolated systems

  2. Introducing API Gateway (Sectoral): Deploying sector-specific API gateways to enable structured data exchange

Challenges Addressed

The MESH stage presented significant challenges: duplicated citizen data across registries, inconsistent data formats, no standardized APIs, security vulnerabilities from multiple point-to-point connections, and the inability to implement cohesive governance policies. Stage 0 marks the beginning of addressing these issues through the SITA project while acknowledging that substantial architectural improvements are still required.



3.4 Stage 1: The Foundation of NDGO (STAR to DSTAR Transition)

Stage 1 represents a transformational leap in Bangladesh's digital architecture. This phase introduces the foundational elements required for the National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO) implementation, establishing the critical infrastructure components that will enable true interoperability.

Figure 3.2: Stage 1 – Foundation of NDGO (Initial Phase)

Key Architectural Enhancements

1. Introduction of Virtual ID (VID) and Sectoral ID (SID): A critical enhancement at this stage is the introduction of derived identity tokens. The Virtual ID (VID) provides citizens with a privacy-preserving identifier that can be used instead of their actual NID for service transactions, reducing the risk of identity theft. The Sectoral ID (SID) enables sector-specific identification without exposing the core national identity, allowing for controlled data access based on purpose limitation principles enshrined in the Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO).

2. NDGA (National Data Governance Authority): The establishment of NDGA marks the institutional foundation for data governance. Operating under the Unified Identity Management framework, NDGA serves as the central authority responsible for implementing the Bangladesh National Data Governance and Interoperability Architecture (BNDIA), operating the National Responsible Data Exchange (NRDEX) platform, certifying and monitoring digital platforms handling citizen data, and coordinating Chief Data Officers across ministries.

3. Enhanced Compliance and Enforcement Layer: The architecture introduces a robust compliance framework including Trust Certificate and Single Sign-On (SSO) capabilities for secure authentication, PDPO enforcement mechanisms ensuring privacy protection, and API Gateway with mediation services for standardized data exchange.

4. Supporting Infrastructure Components: New components include Model AI Data Security frameworks, Chief Data Officers at institutional level, and NSOC (National Security Operations Center) with API Monitoring capabilities for real-time surveillance of data anomalies.

Figure 3.3: Stage 1 – Introduction of Sectoral Gateway and Proxy Layer

Sectoral Gateway Architecture

The progression within Stage 1 introduces the Sectoral Gateway (and Proxy) layer—a critical architectural component that fundamentally changes how services connect to the national data infrastructure. This gateway serves as an intermediary layer between individual service agencies and the core NDGIA infrastructure.

The Sectoral Gateway provides protocol translation and format standardization across different legacy systems, request routing and load balancing for efficient resource utilization, security proxy services including authentication, authorization, and audit logging, and data transformation services to ensure compatibility with BNDIA standards.

STAR to DSTAR Transition Actions

  1. Introducing NDGIA: Establishing the central governance authority for data and AI

  2. Enhanced API Gateway (National): Scaling from sectoral to national-level API management

  3. Connecting All Sectors: Bringing all government agencies under unified architecture

  4. Introduce Unified Identity: Implementing VID and SID for privacy-preserving identity management

  5. Introduce Sectoral Gateway: Deploying proxy and mediation services

  6. Apply Legal Aspects on Data Hosting: Enforcing PDPO and requirements for mandatory domestic mirroring of CII data and other data localization requirements.



3.5 Stage 2: Shaping the DPI (Full DSTAR Implementation)

Figure 3.4: Stage 2 – Shaping the DPI (Full DSTAR Architecture)

Stage 2 represents the culmination of Bangladesh's DPI vision—a fully integrated, intelligent, and citizen-centric digital public infrastructure. This stage introduces advanced technological capabilities while consolidating the governance frameworks established in earlier phases.

National Data Center Ecosystem

The most significant architectural addition in Stage 2 is the fully operationalized National Data Center Ecosystem. This ecosystem comprises four integrated components:

NDC (National Data Center): The primary sovereign infrastructure for hosting critical government data and services. The NDC serves as the anchor for all government data operations, ensuring data sovereignty and security compliance.

DR (Disaster Recovery): Geographically distributed disaster recovery facilities ensuring business continuity and data resilience. This component safeguards against data loss and ensures service availability during emergencies.

BDCCL (Bangladesh Data Centre Company Limited): The commercial arm providing scalable cloud services for government and enterprise clients. BDCCL enables value-added services for freelancers, the gaming industry, and the broader digital services ecosystem.

Pvt. DC (Private Data Centers): Accredited private sector facilities operating under NDGA certification, expanding capacity while maintaining compliance with national standards.

Advanced Processing Capabilities

Stage 2 introduces sophisticated processing infrastructure that enables Bangladesh to leverage emerging technologies:

TPS Exchange: High-throughput transaction processing services capable of handling the scale required for national-level digital services, from tax settlements to citizen service requests.

Blockchain RPC (Remote Procedure Call): Distributed ledger capabilities enabling transparent, immutable record-keeping for critical government transactions. This technology supports applications ranging from land registration to educational credential verification.

AI Engine (GPU): Dedicated GPU-powered artificial intelligence processing capabilities supporting predictive analytics for governance, AI-based anomaly detection for security, natural language processing for citizen services, and machine learning models for policy optimization. This infrastructure positions Bangladesh to deploy AI-powered predictive governance for real-time decision-making.

STAR to DSTAR Final Transition Actions

  1. Structuring National Data Center: Integrating NDC, DR, BDCCL, and private facilities into a cohesive ecosystem

  2. Migration of Data: Systematically moving legacy data to sovereign infrastructure

  3. Enable AI Engine: Deploying GPU-powered processing for intelligent governance

  4. Enable Blockchain RPC: Implementing distributed ledger for transparency and trust

  5. Introduce Citizen Wallet: Launching the National Document Wallet for secure citizen credential management

3.5.1 Interoperability Phases

Phase

Characteristics

Key Challenges/Actions

MESH

(Current State)

Fragmented "data islands"—isolated registries for NID, taxation, land, education, health without real-time coordination

• Duplication of digital assets

• Increased operational costs

• Data breaches exposing citizen information

• Lack of end-to-end digital services

STAR

(2025-2026)

Secure hub-and-spoke model connecting priority ministries through SITA project and standardized APIs

• Deploy SITA project across 5-10 ministries

• Appoint Chief Data Officers

• Begin PDPO compliance audits

• Establish NDGA Secretariat

DSTAR

(2027-2030)

Data-driven STAR: Full interoperability with AI-enabled analytics, unified identity, and zero manual data exchange

• Operationalize NRDEX nationwide

• Integrate 100+ ministries and agencies

• Launch AI registry and certification

• Zero manual data transfers



3.5.2 Justification for Phased Approach

The phased MESH → STAR → DSTAR approach is justified by:

  • Risk Mitigation: Gradual deployment allows identification and correction of issues before nationwide rollout

  • Capacity Building: Time for training officials, appointing CDOs, and developing institutional capability

  • Legal Compliance: Three-year PDPO transitional period allows organizations to adapt to new requirements

  • Stakeholder Buy-in: Phased integration demonstrates value to hesitant ministries through early wins

3.6 Bangladesh National Data Governance and Interoperability Architecture (BNDIA)

The Bangladesh National Data Governance and Interoperability Architecture (BNDIA) is the comprehensive technical and governance framework that enables secure, standardized data exchange across all government entities. Legally established under NDGO Section 21, BNDIA provides the architectural backbone for Bangladesh's DPI.

3.6.1 Core Components

Component

Description

NRDEX Platform

National Responsible Data Exchange—secure API-based platform for trusted cross-agency data sharing with purpose-based access controls and audit trails

SITA Project

Strengthening Institutions for Transparency and Accountability—secure API exchange connecting NBR, BBS, BPPA, Planning Commission, and OCAG during STAR phase

Unified Identity Layer

Interoperability between NID, Birth Registration, Passport, and Business ID with system-wide propagation of updates

NSOC

National Security Operations Center—real-time monitoring of network and data anomalies across all DPI components

TRUST-CCA Chain

Trust and Certificate Chain Authority—ensures authenticity and integrity of data transactions through PKI infrastructure

National Data Exchange Layer (NDEL)

Scalable platform for all cross-agency data flows with standardized APIs, metadata management, and audit capabilities



3.6.2 Technical Standards and Requirements

BNDIA mandates the following technical requirements for all connected systems:

  • Zero-Trust Architecture: Every access request must be verified regardless of network location

  • API-Based Integration: All data exchange through standardized RESTful APIs with proper authentication

  • Cloud-First Policies: Government agencies required to adopt cloud-first but hybrid-friendly architecture

  • Open-Source Adoption: Preference for open-source platforms where applicable to prevent vendor lock-in
