  • Metadata Standards: Consistent metadata tagging for all datasets to enable discovery and governance

  • Audit Trails: Complete logging of all data access and modifications for accountability

3.7 Digital Identity Framework (OneID)

To ensure clarity, interoperability, and trust, Bangladesh requires a structured framework for digital identity covering legal foundations, lifecycle inclusion, standards, and governance. The Unified Identity Layer (oneID) concept represents Bangladesh's approach to creating a single, authoritative identity that follows citizens throughout their lives.

3.7.1 Legal Basis

Currently, Bangladesh's digital identity ecosystem is anchored in the National Identity Registration Act, 2010. The later 2023 Act introduced the Unique Identification Number (UIN) but has since been repealed, creating ambiguity about the legal foundation for UIN as a universal identifier.

  • The strategy recommends a policy review to clarify and consolidate the legal framework for digital identity

  • Options include amending the 2010 Act or considering a new consolidated legal instrument

  • Interoperability and trust will be guided by existing legal provisions, supplemented by PDPO and NDGA oversight

3.7.2 The 17-Digit OneID Architecture

OneID introduces a standardized 17-digit decimal identifier designed for governance, provenance, and scalability:

  • Position 1 (Fixed Prefix): '1' identifies the Bangladesh national identity space.

  • Positions 2-3 (Reserved): '00' reserved for future expansion.

  • Position 4 (Producer Agency): Identifies the issuing authority (e.g., '1' for Election Commission, '2' for Registrar General).

  • Positions 5-6 (Sub-Agency): Identifies the specific service point type (e.g., Union Parishad, Hospital).

  • Positions 7-17 (Citizen Number): Unique 11-digit random number assigned to the individual.

Note: The identifier is opaque and non-predictable; it does not encode personal attributes like date of birth or gender to protect privacy.

3.7.3 Lifecycle Coverage (0-18 Years)

The strategy emphasizes a life-cycle approach to ensure no citizen is excluded:

Life Stage

Identity Foundation

Associated Credentials

Birth (0 years)

Birth Registration as the root identity

Birth Registration Number (BRN)

Childhood (0-17)

Progressive Digital Credentials linked to BRN

Health records, education credentials, social protection IDs

Adulthood (18+)

Automatic transition to full NID-based digital identity

NID, Passport, TIN, Business ID, driving license



Justification: This lifecycle approach prevents fragmentation (separate education IDs, health IDs, etc.) and ensures continuity. Citizens maintain a single identity thread from birth through adulthood.

3.7.4 Standards and Interoperability

Bangladesh will adopt internationally recognized standards to guarantee interoperability and vendor neutrality:

  • ISO/IEC 19794 series for biometric formats

  • ICAO standards for travel and identity credentials

  • NIST Digital Identity Guidelines for assurance levels

  • FIDO2/WebAuthn for passwordless authentication

  • GDPR/eIDAS principles for alignment with global data protection norms

3.7.5 PKI, Digital Signatures, and Trust Framework

A robust Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) is the bedrock of secure digital transactions. The digital identity system will integrate with an enhanced PKI framework to enable:

  • Secure Authentication & Legally Binding E-Signatures: Ensuring non-repudiation and legal validity for all digital government and financial transactions.

  • Consent-Based Data Sharing: Facilitating auditable, citizen-controlled data exchange.

  • Offline Verification: Supporting QR codes and credential wallets for low-connectivity areas.

Reform of the Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA): To operationalize this trust framework, the CCA is undergoing strategic reform to address current limitations in adoption, legal recognition, and international interoperability. See chapter 6.6 for further details.

  • Legal Harmonization: The CCA will lead the amendment of the Information and Communication Technology Act, 2006 to modernize provisions for digital signatures. This includes removing requirements for "blue-ink" signatures and physical presence in service delivery, ensuring that electronic records and signatures have absolute legal equivalence in courts.

  • Mandatory E-Signatures in Government: A circular from the Cabinet Division will mandate the use of digital signatures in all critical G2G and G2C services (e.g., e-Nothi, e-GP, iBAS++), moving away from unsecured "system-generated" documents.

  • Financial Integration: The Ministry of Finance will introduce specific economic codes to allocate budgets for digital signature procurement across government offices, removing a key barrier to adoption.

  • Global Trust & Recognition: The CCA will pursue WebTrust audits to include Bangladesh's Root CA certificate in international trusted stores (Microsoft, Mozilla, Adobe). Furthermore, Mutual Recognition Arrangements (MRA) will be established to facilitate cross-border digital trade and legally binding international contracts.

  • Future-Ready PKI: Investment in research for Post-Quantum Cryptography to future-proof national encryption standards against emerging threats.

These components will evolve into a National Trust Framework, drawing from global models such as EU eIDAS 2.0 and NIST standards, ensuring that Bangladesh's digital ecosystem is secure, legally robust, and globally connected.

3.7.6 Identity Wallet Governance

Bangladesh already has an NID Wallet operated by the Election Commission. The strategy clarifies that:

  • The wallet should be consolidated and expanded, not duplicated

  • Its custodian authority (EC or another designated body) should be clearly defined through policy review

  • Core functions should include credential storage, e-signatures, consent management, offline verification, and regulated integration with private-sector apps (banks, fintechs, telcos)

3.7.7 Privacy & Trust Architecture

OneID operates under the strict legal regime of the National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO) and Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO), implementing a multi-layered trust framework that balances verification utility with citizen privacy.

  • Sectoral Identifiers (SIDs): To mitigate the risk of mass surveillance and cross-sector profiling, OneID employs a system of Sectoral Identifiers. Relying parties in different domains (e.g., a healthcare provider vs. a financial institution) receive distinct, derived identifiers for the same individual. These SIDs cannot be correlated without specific legal authorization or the citizen's explicit consent, effectively isolating data silos and preserving privacy.

  • Tokenization for Verification: To minimize data exposure, verification services return cryptographically signed "Yes/No" tokens or attribute assertions (e.g., "Is Age > 18?" or "Address Match: True") rather than revealing raw personal data. This "Zero-Knowledge" approach allows service providers to verify eligibility without accessing sensitive underlying records.

  • Citizen Wallet & Selective Disclosure: The architecture introduces a secure Citizen Wallet mobile application that empowers individuals to hold and manage their own verifiable credentials (e.g., Driving License, Education Certificates, Professional Licenses). The wallet supports Selective Disclosure, enabling citizens to share only the specific data points required for a transaction (e.g., proving age without revealing name or address) without handing over physical documents.

  • Trust & Certificate Chain Authority (TRUST-CCA): All credential issuance, digital signatures, and authentication events are anchored in the national PKI framework administered by TRUST-CCA. This ensures that every digital interaction is cryptographically verifiable, non-repudiable, and tamper-evident.

3.7.8 Integration & Interoperability

OneID serves as the foundational "Trust Anchor" for the entire Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) ecosystem, facilitating seamless integration across disparate sectors through the National Responsible Data Exchange (NRDEX).

  • Digital Social Protection: OneID integration eliminates "ghost beneficiaries" and duplicate payments by linking beneficiary registries directly to the unique biometric identity. It enables the Integrated Beneficiary Registry (IBR) to perform cross-program deduplication, ensuring that social safety net funds reach only eligible, unique individuals.

  • National Address Management System (NAMS): OneID integrates with NAMS using a "Pointer Model," where the identity record stores a reference (National Address Identifier - NAID) rather than raw address text. This ensures that when an address is updated in NAMS, the change is automatically reflected for all services relying on that identity, enabling reliable service delivery and geographic targeting.

  • Payments Ecosystem: The framework supports secure Account Binding, linking a beneficiary's OneID to their preferred financial address (Bank Account or MFS Wallet). This enables frictionless Government-to-Person (G2P) payments and supports FIDO-based biometric authentication at agent points, allowing secure cash-out even for citizens without smartphones.

  • Health Sector: OneID enables the creation of a portable Longitudinal Health Record. By uniquely identifying patients across different facilities (hospitals, clinics, diagnostic centers), it ensures continuity of care, prevents duplicate medical records, and supports national immunization registries.

  • CRVS Integration: The system maintains a real-time synchronization link with the Civil Registration and Vital Statistics (CRVS) system. Birth registration automatically triggers OneID creation, while death registration triggers immediate identity deactivation and event propagation to subscribed systems (e.g., stopping pension payments), ensuring the national identity database remains perpetually accurate and current.

3.7.9 Implementation Governance

  • Regulator: National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) oversees compliance, sets standards, and accredits enrolling agencies.

  • Producer Agencies: Designated bodies like the Election Commission (NID Wing) and Office of the Registrar General (ORG) responsible for data capture.

  • Technology: Built on Open Standards (MOSIP-aligned principles) to avoid vendor lock-in, with data hosted exclusively in sovereign government data centers.

3.7.10 Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • 100% of citizens (birth to death) covered by OneID.

  • Zero manual data entry for government service applications (Once-Only Principle).

  • 99.9% accuracy in beneficiary targeting for social safety nets.

  • Frictionless eKYC for banking and mobile payments nationwide.



3.8 National Address Management System (NAMS)

The National Address Management System (NAMS) establishes the definitive technical and governance framework for a unified, geocoded national address registry. It solves the critical "Address Ambiguity" problem that currently hampers service delivery, e-commerce logistics, and emergency response in Bangladesh. NAMS transforms addresses from unstructured text into precise, machine-readable geospatial coordinates linked to a unique National Address Identifier (NAID).

Vision and Core Objectives

Vision: To provide every physical location in Bangladesh—residential, commercial, or institutional—with a unique, verified digital address that enables precision service delivery, seamless logistics, and effective emergency response.

Strategic Objectives:

  • Standardization: Replace unstructured address text with a standardized schema aligned with ISO 19160 and UPU S42.

  • Geocoding: Link every address to precise geospatial coordinates (Latitude/Longitude), enabling "Dot-on-Map" navigation.

  • Uniqueness: Assign a persistent, immutable National Address Identifier (NAID) to every addressable object.

  • Interoperability: Serve as the "Single Source of Truth" for address data across all government agencies via OneID and NRDEX integration.

The National Address Identifier (NAID) Architecture

NAMS introduces the NAID, a 12-character alphanumeric code that serves as the permanent digital key for any location.

  • Structure: The NAID is opaque and non-semantic (e.g., BD-7834-K92M), designed to remain valid even if administrative boundaries (Ward, Upazila) change.

  • Persistence: Unlike street names or holding numbers which may change, the NAID remains constant for the lifecycle of the building or property.

  • Pointer Model: Government databases (NID, Passport, Utility) will store the NAID as a "Pointer" rather than duplicating full address text. This ensures that when an address is updated in NAMS (e.g., a street is renamed), all linked services automatically reflect the change without manual data entry.

Core Components and Integration

NAMS operates as a foundational layer of the Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI):

  1. Central Address Registry (CAR): The authoritative database hosting verified address records, geocodes, and NAID mappings.

  2. Digital Addressing Portal: A citizen-facing platform for self-service address registration, verification, and NAID generation.

  3. OneID Integration: NAMS provides the "Where" to OneID's "Who."

    • Address Verification: OneID verification services check the NAID against the NAMS registry to confirm address validity during eKYC.

    • Service Delivery: Social protection benefits and emergency services are routed using the precise geocodes linked to the beneficiary's NAID.

  4. Logistics & E-Commerce Integration: NAMS provides open APIs for logistics companies to resolve NAIDs into precise delivery coordinates, reducing "Last Mile" failure rates and delivery costs.

Implementation and Governance

  • Custodian Agency: Bangladesh Post serves as the primary custodian of the NAMS registry, leveraging its nationwide field force for verification and data maintenance.

  • Technical Operator: ICT Division / BCC manages the digital infrastructure and API gateway.

  • Local Government Role: City Corporations and Union Parishads are responsible for assigning holding numbers and validating new address creation requests at the source.

Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • 100% of households and businesses assigned a NAID.

  • Zero ambiguity in emergency response routing (Ambulance/Fire/Police) due to precise geocoding.

  • 30% reduction in e-commerce logistics costs due to elimination of address verification calls and failed deliveries.

  • Seamless utility connection applications using NAID for instant premise verification.



3.9 Digitally Integrated Tax-VAT Ecosystem: NBR API Connectivity Hub

To modernize fiscal administration and strengthen domestic revenue collection, a national NBR API Connectivity Hub will be deployed—enabling secure, real-time, system-to-system linkage between the National Board of Revenue (NBR) and public/private ERP systems, payment service providers, banks, and digital financial platforms.

This reform shifts NBR from relying on delayed, secondary reporting toward automated access to primary financial transactions—transforming Bangladesh's tax lifecycle into a real-time, transparent, and data-driven system anchored in Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI).

3.9.1 Four Pillars of Reform

Pillar 1: ERP Integration & Instant (T+0) Tax Settlement

All major public and private ERP platforms—handling salary disbursement, procurement, contractor payments, vendor management, etc.—will be connected to the NBR hub. Once a payment is initiated within an ERP, a digital payment slip will automatically trigger advance income tax/withholding tax deduction and reporting to NBR. This realizes a T+0 tax settlement model, minimizes leakage, and eliminates manual compliance burdens.

Pillar 2: VAT Net Expansion via Payment & ERP Connectivity

Simultaneously, all payment gateways, MFS/PSP platforms, wallets, and VAT-enabled ERPs will be integrated through a dedicated VAT API, enabling automated VAT capture at the exact point of transaction. This approach expands the VAT base without increasing rates—particularly across growing segments such as e-commerce, services, logistics, and SME supply chains.

Pillar 3: Secure API-Based Architecture & Cyber Protection

NBR will establish a cyber-secure API Gateway & Connectivity Hub to govern all integrations. Embedded within this will be a new Tax-VAT Cybersecurity Operations Unit, responsible for endpoint protection, encrypted data pipelines, zero-trust access control, monitoring, and regulatory coordination with N-CERT and the proposed National Data Governance & Interoperability Authority (NDGA).

Pillar 4: Real-Time Reporting & Predictive Monitoring

A national analytics dashboard will offer MoF and NBR live insight into revenue flow, taxpayer behaviour, risk patterns, and compliance hotspots. AI-based anomaly detection systems will guide enforcement and forecasting. This same data backbone will feed into planning, budgeting, and public service delivery reforms across government.

3.9.2 Technical Architecture

Component

Function

Integration Points

NBR API Gateway

Central hub for all tax/VAT data exchange

ERPs, banks, MFS, PSPs, customs

Tax-VAT Cybersecurity Unit

Endpoint protection, zero-trust access

N-CERT, NDGA, NSOC

Real-Time Analytics Dashboard

Live revenue monitoring, anomaly detection

MoF, Planning Commission, BBS

ERP Integration Layer

Automated tax deduction at source

Public/private ERP platforms

VAT API Module

Point-of-transaction VAT capture

Payment gateways, wallets, e-commerce

3.9.3 Expected Outcomes

  • Transparent, corruption-free tax administration through automated compliance

  • Real-time revenue visibility for fiscal planning and policy decisions

  • Expanded VAT base without rate increases through comprehensive transaction capture

  • Reduced compliance burden for businesses through automated reporting

  • Enhanced enforcement capabilities through AI-powered anomaly detection

  • Integration with national DPI backbone for seamless cross-government data sharing



3.10 Expanded Role of BBS and Cross-Government Data Ecosystem

A key design principle of the DPI framework is "collect once, use many times"—especially for the integration of Bangladesh Bureau of Statistics (BBS) into a dynamic, real-time data ecosystem. This represents a fundamental transformation in how national statistics are generated and utilized for evidence-based governance.

3.10.1 From Periodic Surveys to Real-Time Intelligence

Instead of receiving periodic, siloed, survey-based inputs, BBS will be granted API-level access to anonymized primary transactional data (e.g., salaries, business payments, sector-wise VAT/tax flows, digital commerce activity, service-based revenue streams).

This will allow BBS to:

Generate High-Frequency Economic Indicators:

Produce employment, consumption, and informal-to-formal transition indicators without waiting for quarterly or annual surveys.

Improve National Accounts:

Enhance price indices, input-output tables, and GDP forecasting models using real-time datasets instead of historical survey data.

Provide Dynamic Sectoral Statistics:

Deliver timely statistics to ministries (e.g., commerce, planning, industries, agriculture) for evidence-based policymaking.

3.10.2 Institutional Transformation

This transition will also reduce reliance on manual Excel/email reporting, enabling BBS to evolve from being a census-survey heavy institution toward a digitally enabled national data intelligence agency. Key transformations include:

  • Automated data pipelines replacing manual data collection and entry

  • Real-time dashboards for policymakers instead of periodic reports

  • API-based data sharing with authorized government agencies and researchers

  • Integration with international statistical frameworks for comparability

3.10.3 Cross-Government Integration

Beyond BBS, live data streams from the NBR hub will automatically connect to:

iBAS++ (Integrated Budget and Accounting System):

Real-time budget execution monitoring and fiscal management

Planning Commission's MIS:

Project monitoring and development planning informed by actual economic data

Budgetary Systems:

  • Revenue forecasting and expenditure planning based on real transaction flows

  • This integration removes the operational burden of data submission by ministries, officials, and SOEs—thereby dramatically improving public financial management and planning efficiency.

3.10.4 Data Governance and Privacy

  • All cross-government data sharing will operate under strict governance frameworks:

  • Anonymization and pseudonymization of individual-level data

  • Purpose and role based access controls aligned with PDPO requirements

  • Audit trails for all data access and usage

  • NDGA oversight and certification of data-sharing arrangements

3.11 Future-Ready Payment Ecosystem

Bangladesh's payment ecosystem must evolve into a seamless, interoperable, and inclusive infrastructure to support the nation's transition toward a digital economy. The existing system is fragmented, dominated by a few players, and constrained by regulatory bottlenecks. Reform is essential to unlock the potential of Payment System Operators (PSOs), Payment Service Providers (PSPs), FinTech innovators, and digital commerce actors.

A future-ready payments framework must not only enable secure digital financial services for citizens but also integrate with national transport, utility, and commerce systems, ensuring inclusivity, accessibility, and competitiveness.

3.11.1 Breaking Silos and Ensuring Security

The Challenge: The "Silo over Silo" Landscape

The current payment ecosystem in Bangladesh is characterized by fragmented operating silos that hinder true financial inclusion and efficiency.

  • Banking Silos: The VISA card network is currently limited effectively to only four major banks, creating a restricted "Silo Banking Network."

  • MFS Fragmentation: Major Mobile Financial Service (MFS) providers—including bKash, Nagad, Sonali Pay, and Upay—have constructed proprietary networks, creating a "silo over silo" situation across the merchant and payment layers.

  • Capacity Constraints: The limitations of independent infrastructure were highlighted when Nagad, operating on its own system, faced significant challenges handling approximately 80 million transactions during the Eid festival, exposing critical gaps in Transaction per Second (TPS) capacity planning.

  • Interoperability Gaps: The National Payment Switch Bangladesh (NPSB) has historically been commercial bank-centric. Consequently, effective MFS-to-MFS interoperability remains unrealized, and MFS providers continue to operate within closed loops for merchant and government services.

The Solution: A Universal Interoperable Framework

To transition from fragmentation to a unified digital economy, the following structural changes are mandated:

  • Universal Payment Gateway: A transition from mandatory NPSB connectivity to a truly Interoperable Universal Payment Gateway. This will enable critical national functions including Automated e-Invoicing, Pattern Recognition, Capacity Dimensioning, and Financial Intelligence.

  • Central Clearing & NRDEx: The NPSB must evolve into a Central Clearing System under Bangladesh Bank. High-capacity transactions will be routed through the National Responsible Data Exchange (NRDEx) to ensure API-based end-to-end connectivity.

  • Marketplace Guidelines: New guidelines will enforce that MFS operators cannot directly enter marketplaces without routing through the national switching infrastructure (NPSB/Universal Gateway) to ensure transparency.

Cross-Border Security and API Monitoring

The digital payment landscape faces emerging threats from unregulated cross-border flows:

  • Crypto-Exchange Risks: Despite cryptocurrency being unauthorized, platforms like Binance have previously connected via API to local banks for NID verification. This allows external applications to penetrate the internal banking system, posing severe security risks. Strict monitoring of all Cross-Border API communications is required to prevent unauthorized financial flows.

  • Gambling Control via DPI: To curb online gambling, the Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission (BTRC) will utilize Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) technology to identify gambling app traffic and introduce latency, effectively slowing down and discouraging illicit usage.

Integration with Taxation and Data Governance

  • Automated Revenue Collection: By integrating Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems (e.g., corporate salary disbursements) directly with the National Board of Revenue (NBR) via APIs, the ecosystem will enable automatic tax calculation, collection, and the generation of e-Invoices.

  • Data Protection: Recognizing payment data as sensitive personal information, all stakeholders must strictly adhere to the Personal Data Protection Ordinance 2025.

Advanced Intelligence: AI, NEIR, and BFIU

A robust ecosystem requires advanced surveillance and intelligence capabilities:

  • SIM-Device Tagging: Leveraging BTRC’s National Equipment Identity Register (NEIR), MFS accounts will be strictly tagged to a specific SIM and Device. This creates a "Mobile-SIM-Device" triangle, ensuring a complete and accurate e-KYC process.

  • Geo-Fencing: MFS Agent accounts will be subject to SIM-based geo-fencing to prevent unauthorized remote transactions.

  • Centralized Intelligence (BFIU): Moving beyond partial local processing, data processing must be centralized through the Bangladesh Financial Intelligence Unit (BFIU).

  • AI & Machine Learning: Centralized data will be analyzed using AI/ML to enable:

    • Real-time Financial Modeling and Fraud Detection.

    • Automated Suspicious Transaction Reporting (STR).

    • Digital Transaction Forensics.

    • Revenue collection from global platforms (e.g., Meta, Google).

User Experience and Security Enhancements

  • Biometric Authentication: Reducing reliance on OTPs by shifting toward app-based biometric verification.

  • Incentives: Providing incentives for digital payments in public services.

  • Charge Control: Rationalizing and controlling additional charges at Points of Sale (POS).

By implementing this interoperable network and establishing effective coordination between agencies (Bangladesh Bank, BTRC, NBR, BFIU), Bangladesh will build a secure, efficient, and data-driven national payment ecosystem.

3.11.2 Ensuring Market Competitiveness & Fair Access

  • Declare bKash as Significant Market Player (SMP) to prevent monopolistic dominance and create a level playing field

  • Expand PSO licensing scope to allow QR merchant acquisition, PSP top-ups, account-to-account (A2A) transfers, and settlement for Personal Retail Accounts (PRAs)

  • Mandate connectivity to the National Payments Switch of Bangladesh (NPSB) for all PSOs to enable interoperability across banks, MFS, and PSPs

3.11.3 Financial Inclusion & CMSME Empowerment

  • Leverage PSOs to onboard micro, small, and ultra-micro entrepreneurs under the PRA framework in partnership with banks, MFS, and PSPs

  • Recognize transactional data as collateral to facilitate CMSME access to credit

  • Enable crowdfunding and peer-to-peer lending through regulated PSOs to diversify financing options for entrepreneurs

3.11.4 Remittance & Cross-Border Transactions

  • Empower PSOs to enhance remittances by enabling real-time transfers, account validation, and standardized APIs

  • Reduce remittance costs through competition and interoperability while ensuring AML/CTF compliance

  • Permit PSOs to facilitate small-value cross-border transactions, particularly for SMEs in regional trade corridors (e.g., China–Bangladesh, SAARC)

3.11.5 Government-Led Innovation & Sovereign Investment

  • Instruct Startup Bangladesh Ltd (SBL) to co-invest in FinTech, InsurTech, and PSO-led innovations that directly support financial inclusion and digital commerce

  • Establish a National Data & Payment Analytics Platform to provide regulators and businesses with insights into digital commerce flows, risks, and opportunities

  • Position PSOs as true FinTechs by granting them authority to provide embedded financial services for CMSMEs

3.11.6 Unified Ticketing & Transport Payment Integration

  • Introduce a Metro Travel Pass / Unified Transport Pass (single card/app) that enables seamless payment across metro rail, buses, tolls, ferries, and future transport systems

  • Ensure interoperability with the national payments backbone, allowing users to fund transport passes via banks, MFS, and PSOs

  • Integrate with a Super App to unify transport payments, bill pay, fuel charges, plane ticketing, and government fees

  • Develop a real-time transaction backbone for instant settlement across all transport operators and financial intermediaries

3.11.7 Policy Actions

Area

Action

Regulatory Reform

Amend PSO/PSP guidelines to expand permissible services (QR, A2A, PRA integration, PSP top-ups)

Competition Framework

Introduce anti-trust regulations for dominant MFS players to encourage fair pricing and service innovation

Financial Inclusion

Expand PRA framework to authorize PSOs as enablers of CMSME merchant settlement and financing

Cross-Sector Integration

Build interoperability between payment systems and public infrastructure services

Innovation & VC

Allocate a FinTech Innovation Fund (via SBL) to invest in PSOs, PSPs, and transport-integrated payment startups

Internationalization

Support PSOs in enabling remittance modernization and cross-border small-value trade payments

3.11.8 Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • Inclusive digital finance: 20M+ PRA accounts linked with PSO-enabled settlements

  • Expanded IT & transport payments: Single pass usable across metro, bus, ferry, and toll payments nationwide

  • Remittance efficiency: >70% of inward remittance via digital channels with near-real-time settlement

  • CMSME financing: At least $1B in credit facilitated via transactional data–driven lending

  • Global competitiveness: Interoperable PSO ecosystem aligned with regional trade and global standards



3.12 National Fiber Bank

Bangladesh currently has approximately 78,400 km of government-owned fiber-optic networks across agencies like BTCL, BCC, Power Grid (PGC), and Bangladesh Railway; however, about 40% of this fiber remains unused. Further, only approximately 25% of mobile towers are currently connected via fiber—limiting the ability to roll out high-speed services like 5G and causing duplication of new deployments.

3.12.1 Current State Analysis

Underutilized fiber cores represent a significant national asset:

BCC using just 2 cores out of 48 in some segments (46 unused) for services like Summit and Fiber@Home

BTCL has approximately 12 fiber cores unused nationwide

These inefficiencies lead to higher costs, slower project delivery, and under-realized potential for national backbone capacity, redundancy, and broadband expansion.

3.12.2 Strategic Objective

To optimize the use of existing fiber infrastructure across government agencies, accelerate high-speed broadband deployment, reduce rollout costs, and enable resilient digital services including 5G, IoT, and data interconnection.

3.12.3 Key Initiatives & Implementation Priorities

Governance & Institutional Setup:

  • Establish a BTCL-led National Fiber Management Team responsible for oversight, leasing policies, pricing, and technical monitoring

  • Define stakeholder roles: ICT Division, BCC, BTCL, PGC, Railway, BTRC, and private sector operators

Centralized Inventory & Digital Platform:

  • Develop a GIS-based platform cataloguing all government fiber routes: location, number of fiber cores, usage status, underground/overhead, condition, etc.

  • Enable online lease applications, monitoring, and reporting

Policy & Regulatory Measures:

  • Mandate inclusion of unused government fiber in the Fiber Bank

  • Implement fair, subsidised leasing for government/public service use; competitive leasing rates for private operators

  • Use DWDM (Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing) on leased fibers to enhance capacity. BTRC has recently lifted restrictions on mobile operators using DWDM on leased fibers

Pilot Implementation (2025):

By end-2025: complete planning, data collection, platform development, pilot leasing initiatives, and test deployment in a few divisions or regions.

Integration & Upgrades:

  • Use underutilized fiber cores to connect more mobile towers (increase fiber-to-tower coverage)

  • Encourage public/private collaboration in network sharing and maintenance

3.12.4 KPI Framework (2025-2030)

3.12.5 Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • Full inventory of fiber assets across government with GIS visibility

  • All mobile towers served by fiber, enabling significantly higher capacity, lower latency services

  • Major reduction in redundant new fiber deployments; costs and time for broadband rollout lowered substantially

  • Strengthened backbone for 5G, IoT, data centres, and other high-capacity services

  • Transparent, fair leasing model generating measurable revenue to maintain fiber infrastructure

3.13 Implementation Roadmap (2025-2030)

• Enact NDGA Rules & Regulations

• Establish NDGA Secretariat and Policy Board

• Deploy SITA project across 5-10 ministries

3.15 Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • Full legal and operational establishment of an independent NDGA regulating data, privacy, and AI

  • 100+ ministries and agencies interoperable via NRDEX and unified identity

  • Zero manual data transfers; all exchanges through secure APIs

  • AI governance regime ensuring fairness, transparency, and accountability in public-sector AI

  • National Data Exchange Layer recognized as a regional model for secure and ethical digital governance

  • Enhanced citizen trust through robust privacy protection, grievance redress, and transparent public-service delivery

End of Chapter 3



4. Sectoral Impacts Outside ICTD

Foundational Digital Systems, Service Improvement, AI Integration, Citizen-Centric Design & Nagorik Sheba

4.1 Introduction: Digital Transformation Beyond ICT Division

While the ICT Division serves as the anchor institution for Bangladesh's digital transformation, the true measure of success lies in the transformative impact across all sectors of government and society. This chapter examines how the Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) established through the MESH → STAR → DSTAR journey translates into tangible improvements across foundational digital systems, service delivery, AI-enabled governance, and the flagship Nagorik Sheba initiative.

The sectoral impacts documented in this chapter demonstrate that digital transformation is not merely about technology adoption—it is fundamentally about improving citizens' lives through better education, healthcare, agriculture, land administration, law enforcement, justice delivery, social protection, and fiscal administration. Each sector benefits from the interoperability frameworks, unified identity systems, and secure data exchange platforms that form the core of Bangladesh's DPI.

4.2 Citizen-Centric Service Design Framework

Citizen-centric digital service delivery will be at the heart of Bangladesh's digital transformation. All public services will follow a common national standard that prioritises simplicity, accessibility and inclusion, ensuring that citizens—regardless of location, gender, language, or digital ability—can complete essential services with confidence.

Government agencies will adopt user-journey mapping, service-design guidelines, mobile-first interfaces and accessibility requirements so that services reflect real needs and reduce the time, cost and number of visits required. To uphold the Once-Only Principle, agencies will reuse verified data from trusted government sources instead of repeatedly asking citizens for the same information. Digital services will progressively move toward end-to-end delivery, supported by secure data exchange, interoperable systems, and reusable building blocks of a future BanglaGovStack.

Nine Strategic Actions for the Digital Operating System

#

Strategic Action

Description & Rationale

1

National Definition of Digital Public Service

Adopt definition aligned with IMAPS/European Interoperability Framework: service produces clear administrative outcome, has designated owner, primary end-user group, and digital interface

2

Annual Digital Maturity Assessment

0-4 scale: Analogue → Informational → Semi-digital → Digital with incomplete integration → End-to-End Digital. Ministries submit annual reports with bottlenecks and required support

3

National Service Design & Portal Standards

Mobile-first design, plain-language labels, accessibility for PWDs, multilingual readiness, status tracking, common authentication, uniform UI/UX components, registry of all services

4

Once-Only Principle for Data Reuse

Agencies must reuse verified data from authoritative registers (NID, Birth, Address, Land, Tax, Marriage). Citizens cannot be required to re-submit existing data. Requires secure, logged exchange mechanisms

5

Procurement Reform for Outcomes

Mandatory user testing with women, rural residents, PWDs. Service-level KPIs (completion rate, satisfaction, processing time). Reusable components over custom builds. Government ownership of data/systems

6

Digital-First Legislative Reform

Conduct a comprehensive legal review led by the CCA to amend the ICT Act 2006 and repeal analog provisions requiring physical presence or "blue-ink" signatures. Introduce mandatory Digital Signatures for G2G/G2C services, economic codes for PKI procurement, and legal recognition for Cross-Border Mutual Recognition Arrangements (MRA) to facilitate digital trade.

7

Digital Service Workforce Framework

Specialized roles: Service owners, UX designers, Product managers, Data engineers, Cybersecurity specialists. Competitive pay, career progression, rotational assignments. Training for frontline staff

8

BanglaGovStack: Reusable Building Blocks

Centrally maintained modules: Authentication, Payments, Notifications (SMS/email/WhatsApp), Document storage, Case management, Grievance handling, Multilingual content, Accessibility—aligning with India's GovStack, Estonia's X-Road, UK GDS

9

Public Digital Literacy Programmes

Ensure both service providers and users are equipped to benefit from digital systems. Capacity building for civil servants, clear procurement standards requiring user testing, equitable access initiatives



Why Citizen-Centric Digital Services should be prioritised in the Strategy

Digital transformation succeeds only when services are designed around the real needs, constraints, and expectations of citizens. Despite significant progress, evidence from field consultations and service audits shows that many services remain difficult to navigate, inconsistently designed, or reliant on manual steps. This increases the burden on both citizens and frontline officials and weakens public trust in digital government.

A citizen-centric approach ensures that every public service—digital or analogue—delivers value through simplicity, dignity, accessibility, and predictable outcomes. This approach reduces administrative workload, improves compliance, and strengthens accountability across the public sector.

The need for a clear national definition of a Public Digital Service

At present there is no agreed national definition of what counts as a “digital public service” (DPS) or a “citizen-centric service.” This leads to inconsistent design, mislabeling of portals as digital services, and difficulty for ministries in planning or prioritising improvements.

Recommendation:

The Strategy should adopt a national definition aligned with international standards (e.g., IMAPS, European Interoperability Framework):

A digital public service is a public service that:

  1. Produces a clear administrative outcome (certificate, approval, registration, entitlement).

  2. Has a designated service owner within the government.

  3. Has a primary end-user group (citizens, businesses, agencies).

  4. Includes a user centric digital interface or machine-to-machine process through which the outcome is delivered.

This will allow government to clearly distinguish:

  • End-to-end digital services,

  • Semi-digital services,

  • Application builders,

  • Informational portals.

Such classification is essential for prioritisation, budgeting, and accountability.

Annual Digital Maturity Assessment of all public services

Without a systematic way to measure service maturity, it is impossible to understand progress, identify bottlenecks, or direct resources where they are most needed.









Recommendation

Introduce an Annual Digital Maturity Assessment for all public services in Bangladesh, using a simple scale (e.g., 0 to 4):

Level

Classification

Description

0

Analogue Service

No digital pathway exists

1

Informational Presence

Instructions available online, but no digital transaction capability

2

Semi-Digital

Some steps online, but manual verification still required

3

Digital with Incomplete Integration

Online application with partial back-office automation

4

End-to-End Digital Public Service

Full digital lifecycle, automated data verification, no manual steps

Each ministry would submit an annual maturity report with:

  • Current level of each service,

  • Bottlenecks,

  • Planned improvements,

  • Required support (skills, budget, data access).

This would provide the Strategy with a baseline and a tool for monitoring implementation.

National Standards for Service Design and Government Portals

At present, different ministries and vendors use different layouts, labels, authentication methods, languages, and processes. Citizens must learn each service from scratch, leading to confusion and dependency on intermediaries.

Recommendation

Adopt national Service Design Standards covering:

  • Mobile-first design

  • Clear process steps and plain-language labels

  • Accessibility for persons with disabilities

  • Prioritize ease of access for users instead of highlighting ministry officials or activities

  • Multilingual readiness

  • Status tracking and notifications

  • Integration of feedback and grievance redress

  • Mandatory user testing with target groups

  • Privacy, data minimisation, and transparency principles

Adopt national Portal (there could be thematic, geographic or other portals) Standards covering:

  • Single search interface

  • Uniform navigation patterns

  • Common authentication

  • Common UI/UX components

  • Consistent accessibility rules

  • A maintained registry of all government services

These standards will reduce duplication, improve usability, and create a predictable user experience no matter which service a citizen accesses.

Once-Only Principle and Data Reuse Across the Government

Citizens repeatedly enter information that government already holds—NID details, birth date, parent names, land records, addresses, tax IDs, etc. This violates the Once-Only Principle, increases errors, and creates unnecessary burden on both citizens and staff.

Recommendation

Codify the Once-Only Principle:

  • Public agencies must reuse verified data held by any other government agency.

  • Citizens cannot be required to re-submit or re-enter information already present in a trusted registry.

  • Any exception must be justified on legal or security grounds.

This requires:

  • A catalogue of authoritative registers (NID, Birth Registration, Address, Land, Tax, Marriage, etc.).

  • Secure, logged data exchange mechanisms.

  • Clear institutional agreements between agencies.

The impact will be immediate: shorter applications, fewer errors, faster decisions, and reduced reliance on middlemen.

Reforming Procurement for Citizen-Centric Outcomes

Most tenders today focus on software delivery, infrastructure, and the number of services digitised—not on usability, adoption, or outcomes. Vendors are not required to conduct user testing, support inclusive design, or demonstrate service impact.

Recommendation

Revise procurement rules to require:

  • Mandatory user testing with specific groups (women, rural residents, PWDs).

  • Service-level KPIs (completion rate, drop-off rate, user satisfaction, processing time).

  • Compliance with national service design and interoperability standards.

  • Reusable components rather than custom builds.

  • Clear ownership of data and systems by the government.

This aligns incentives with real-world outcomes rather than technical outputs.

Digital-First Legislative and Regulatory Reform

Many laws still require physical signatures, stamps, or in-person verification, which blocks end-to-end digitisation.

Recommendation

Many laws still require physical signatures, stamps, or in-person verification, which blocks end-to-end digitization. Furthermore, a prevailing misconception that "system-generated documents do not require signatures" undermines legal validity in courts. To address this, the Office of the Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA) must play a central role in harmonization.

Recommendation Conduct a Digital-First Legal Review, led by the CCA and ICT Division, across ministries to:

  • Amend the Information and Communication Technology Act, 2006: Update the act to reflect modern digital realities, ensuring the legal equivalence of electronic records and signatures is absolute.

  • Identify and Repeal Analog Provisions: Remove requirements for "blue-ink" signatures, physical stamps, and mandatory physical presence in service delivery.

  • Establish Cross-Border Recognition: Empower the CCA to facilitate Mutual Recognition Arrangements (MRA) for digital signatures, enabling cross-border digital trade and legally binding international electronic contracts.

  • Global Trust Integration: Mandate necessary audits (e.g., WebTrust) to ensure Bangladesh’s Root CA certificate is included in international trusted stores (Microsoft, Mozilla, Adobe), preventing browser security warnings that erode user trust.

Introduce:

  • Legally Recognized Digital Signatures: Enforce the use of digital signatures in all G2G and G2C documents, moving away from unsecured "system-generated" footers.

  • Economic Codes for PKI: The Ministry of Finance must introduce specific economic codes to allocate budgets for digital signature expenses in government offices.

  • Evidence-Based Risk Assessments: Guidelines for when physical verification is strictly necessary versus when e-KYC suffices.

  • Future-Ready Clauses: Enabling legal provisions for Post-Quantum Cryptography, digital wallets, and verifiable credentials.

This will allow services like birth registration, licensing, pensions, and trade certificates to become fully digital, legally admissible, and globally recognized.

Capacity and Staffing for Digital Service Delivery

Digital services cannot be delivered or maintained without the right skills. Today’s public service is structurally designed for analogue processes, not continuous delivery of digital products.

Recommendation 

Introduce a Digital Service Workforce Framework:

Create specialised roles inside ministries:

  • Service owners

  • UX designers (staff, not contractors)

  • Product managers

  • Data engineers

  • Cybersecurity specialists

Professional pathways:

  • Competitive pay scales

  • Career progression for digital roles

  • Rotational assignments between ministries

Frontline support:

  • Training programmes for registrars, UDC operators, hospital staff, and others who deliver digital services daily.

This strengthens sustainability and reduces reliance on vendors.

Reusable Building Blocks: Toward a BanglaGovStack

Almost all ministry and divisions and agencies under them builds their own login system, payment module, document upload tool, notification system, and ERP solutions. This leads to the "reinventing the wheel" syndrome, where public money is wasted procuring the same software repeatedly (e.g., Inventory Management, Payroll, or File Tracking systems) for different departments. It also increases the burden on vendors to build basic infrastructure from scratch for every tender.

Recommendation: Develop BanglaGovStack, a set of centrally maintained, open-standard digital building blocks that agencies can combine to build services instantly. This includes:

  • National Source Code Repository (NSCR): A centralized, secure repository (based on the National Source Code Policy) hosting the source code of all government-owned software.

    • Mandate for Reuse: Agencies must check the NSCR for existing modules (e.g., an ERP module used by the Roads Department) before tendering for a new one.

    • Public Money, Public Code: Software developed with public funds must be treated as a national asset, available for reuse and customization by other agencies, drastically reducing procurement costs and time.

  • Authentication and Identity (OneID): Single sign-on for all government portals.

  • Payments: Unified gateway integration.

  • Notifications: Centralized SMS, email, and WhatsApp API gateways.

  • Document Storage: A secure digital locker for citizen credentials.

  • Case Management & Grievance Redress: Standardized ticketing systems.

  • Multilingual Content Libraries: Pre-approved Bangla terminology and UI kits.

Government would maintain these centrally, and agencies would assemble them when creating new services. This reduces cost, ensures interoperability, reduces vendor burden, and improves the user experience. This approach aligns with global models such as India’s GovStack, Estonia’s X-Road, and Singapore’s CODEX, tailored to Bangladesh’s context.

How These Measures Support Officials and Frontline Staff

This policy note is not about imposing additional burdens on ministries. Rather, it aims to:

  • Reduce workload by eliminating repetitive manual verification.

  • Improve reliability so frontline staff face fewer system failures.

  • Simplify responsibilities through clear standards and shared infrastructure.

  • Strengthen trust in public institutions.

  • Direct funding to practical reforms with measurable impact.

The Strategy can position these measures as tools that empower ministries, not constrain them.





4.3 Sector-Wide Digital Transformation Impacts

4.3.1 Integrated E-Commerce and Logistics Supply Chain Modernization:

E-commerce in Bangladesh has grown rapidly in recent years but remains heavily concentrated in metropolitan centers. Rural producers and entrepreneurs—particularly women and small enterprises—have limited access to digital marketplaces, restricting inclusive growth. At the same time, logistics networks suffer from addressing gaps, inefficiencies, and fragmented last-mile delivery systems.

A next-generation strategy is required to bridge the rural–urban divide, modernize logistics infrastructure, and enhance Bangladesh’s competitiveness in regional and global trade.

Strategic Objectives

  1. Expand nationwide access to e-commerce platforms, enabling rural entrepreneurs to directly reach urban and international markets.

  2. Establish a National Address Management System to standardize delivery locations.

  3. Modernize logistics infrastructure with digital, geotagged, and interoperable systems.

  4. Leverage AI-driven forecasting to improve national logistics performance.



Strategic Interventions

1. Nationwide E-Commerce Access & Rural Integration

  • Onboard rural producers, women entrepreneurs, and marginalized groups onto national e-commerce platforms.

  • Create local digital commerce hubs offering training, mobile-first inventory tools, and financial literacy.

2. National Address Management System (NAMS)

  • Divide Bangladesh into structured micro-regions with unique identifiers.

  • Introduce GPS-based geofencing for accurate deliveries.

  • Align with international postal standards to facilitate cross-border trade.

3. Smarter Logistics Infrastructure

  • Integrate geotagged address systems into delivery operations for last-mile optimization.

  • Ensure interoperability between logistics firms, platforms, and payment systems under the BNDA framework.

4. AI-Driven National E-Logistics Dashboard

  • Monitor real-time flows of goods across national networks.

  • Use predictive analytics for demand forecasting, route planning, and bottleneck identification.

  • Provide government and private operators with decision-support tools for efficiency and cost reduction.

Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • 1.5 million rural entrepreneurs directly engaged in digital commerce, with women playing a central role.

  • 100% standardized addressing system nationwide, ensuring delivery reliability and global trade compatibility.

  • Seamless integration of logistics, marketplaces, and payment systems.

  • AI-driven national dashboard providing real-time visibility of supply chains.

  • Bangladesh positioned as a regional leader in inclusive e-commerce and smart logistics.

4.3.2 National Land Automation Initiatives:

The transformation of Bangladesh’s land administration is pivoting from fragmented digital services to a unified, fully interoperable ecosystem. While previous initiatives laid the groundwork, the current strategy focuses on eliminating the persistent "hybrid" processing gaps that force citizens into physical bureaucracy.

  • Vumisheba and Service Gaps: The Ministry of Land launched the Vumisheba platform as a single-window entry point for digital services. However, critical assessments reveal that the system is not yet truly end-to-end digital or interoperable. Citizens often face a disjointed experience where they can apply online but must still navigate highly complicated manual processes and make time-consuming physical visits to local land offices for final verifications, corrections, and hard-copy submissions.

  • Integrated Land Management Software (land.gov.bd): To rectify these inefficiencies, the strategy mandates the full deployment and integration of the Automated Land Management Integrated Software hosted at land.gov.bd. This centralized platform will replace standalone "silos" with a unified system that seamlessly connects:

    • E-Mutation: Fully automated mutation workflows that do not require physical hearings for standard cases.

    • Land Development Tax (LD Tax): A cashless, transparent tax collection system integrated with the national treasury.

    • Digital Record Room (DRR): A repository for certified copies of Khatians and Mouza maps, accessible remotely.

  • Interoperability & Data Integrity: A core objective is to enforce the "Once-Only" principle by linking land records with the National ID (NID) database and the Registration Directorate. This ensures that a single change in ownership is automatically reflected across all government registries—preventing the forgery and dual-ownership disputes that currently clog the judicial system.

4.3.3 Police Automation Program:

The Police Automation Program is a cornerstone of the country's move toward smart Policing, transitioning law enforcement from manual, station-centric operations to a data-driven, citizen-first service model.

  • Nationwide Online GD Adoption: Building on successful metropolitan pilots, the strategy enforces the nationwide adoption of the Online General Diary (GD) system. This platform allows citizens across all 64 districts to file non-emergency reports (such as for lost documents, phones, or vehicles) via a dedicated mobile app or web portal. This initiative eliminates the harassment and delays often associated with physically visiting a police station for routine matters.

  • Integrated Case Management System (CDMS): Beyond simple reporting, the program is digitizing the core investigative lifecycle. This includes:

    • Crime Data Management System (CDMS): A centralized database for tracking First Information Reports (FIRs), case evidence, and investigation updates in real-time.

    • Forensic & Criminal Records: Digitizing criminal profiles to enable instant biometric verification and background checks nationwide.

  • Transparency & Accountability: The platform introduces a "Track My Case" feature, providing complainants with real-time SMS and dashboard updates on their case status. By removing the opacity of manual filing, the system fosters greater accountability and public trust in the police force.

  • Predictive Policing Analytics: The integration of traffic, crime, and complaint data into a central Personnel Information Management System enables the use of AI-driven analytics. This allows for predictive resource allocation—deploying patrols to high-risk zones before crimes occur—and ensuring data-backed decision-making for public safety.

4.3.4 Strengthening National Helplines 333 and 999:

The National Helpline 333 serves as Bangladesh’s inclusive, voice-based citizen service platform, providing information on government services, complaint management, and non-emergency support across ministries and agencies. In parallel, the National Emergency Service 999 addresses critical, life-threatening situations by connecting citizens to the Police, Fire Service, and Ambulance. However, a considerable number of non-emergency calls are directed to 999 every day, creating operational pressure and reducing efficiency in handling genuine emergencies. Furthermore, public awareness of the distinct role of 333 remains low, limiting its potential as a primary service channel.

To address these challenges, the technical and operational integration of 333 and 999 is proposed. Through this integration, non-emergency calls received by 999 will be automatically redirected to 333, while genuine emergencies reported to 333 can be seamlessly escalated to 999. This model will reduce congestion on emergency lines while strengthening 333 as the central hub for citizen service information, complaints, and election support.

Objectives

  • Centralized Platform: Establish 333 as the primary gateway for citizen information and complaint management.

  • Seamless Integration: Implement technical call integration for automatic redirection between 999 (emergency) and 333 (service).

  • Enhanced Awareness: Launch targeted campaigns to educate citizens on the specific utility of 333 versus 999.

  • Election Support: Provide special election-period support for voter information and reporting irregularities.

  • 24/7 Trust: Ensure a reliable, inclusive service platform that enhances public trust in government institutions.

Proposed Scope

  1. Integration & Smart Redirection

    • Automatic Redirection: Implement protocols to forward non-emergency calls from 999 to 333.

    • Emergency Escalation: Enable immediate transfer of critical cases from 333 to 999 dispatchers.

    • AI-Powered Efficiency: Introduce AI-driven voice assistants and analytics to record caller details, categorize complaints automatically, and transcribe calls in real-time. This will significantly reduce agent workload and enable faster response times.

    • Shared Analytics: Develop a unified dashboard for call data, reporting, and performance metrics across both services.

  2. Government Service Information & Complaints

    • Comprehensive Database: Maintain up-to-date information on services from 50+ ministries and agencies.

    • Process Guidance: Guide citizens through complex processes, documentation requirements, and service delivery steps.

    • Complaint Escalation: Register grievances and escalate them to the relevant authorities with tracking numbers.

  3. Strategic Awareness Campaign

    • Public Education: Launch nationwide awareness initiatives to clarify the distinction between 999 (Emergencies) and 333 (Services/Information), targeting rural and less digitally literate populations.

  4. Election Period Support

    • Voter Assistance: Provide information on polling stations, schedules, and voting rules.

    • Complaint Reporting: Receive reports of election irregularities, violence, or harassment and coordinate with the Election Commission and law enforcement.

    • Inclusive Support: Offer dedicated assistance for vulnerable groups, including women, the elderly, and persons with disabilities.

Expected Outcomes

  • Optimized Emergency Response: Reduced congestion at 999 allows faster response to life-threatening situations.

  • Improved Service Access: Faster complaint handling and transparent escalation for non-emergency issues.

  • Operational Efficiency: AI integration reduces manual data entry and speeds up call processing.

  • Increased Utilization: Higher public awareness leads to correct usage of 333 for administrative needs.

  • Institutional Trust: A responsive, integrated platform fosters greater citizen satisfaction and trust in public services.



4.3.5 Disaster Recovery and Smart National Infrastructure Safety:

As Bangladesh accelerates its digital transformation, resilience and public safety must remain at the core of national planning. With rising climate risks, rapid urbanization, and expanding critical infrastructure, a forward-looking framework is essential to:

  • Protect citizens during emergencies.

  • Safeguard national pipelines, grids, and highways.

  • Enable rapid response through data-driven technologies.

This strategy integrates AI, IoT, satellite, and drone systems to ensure resilience, early warning, and coordinated national responses.

Strategic Interventions

1. AI-Powered Public Safety Surveillance

  • Upgrade urban CCTV systems with AI-based hazard detection (gas leaks, smoke, structural collapse, suspicious activity). Proper standards and regulations will be introduced to ensure PDPO and NDGO compliant data collection and AI training from CCTV footage.

  • Establish a centralized Emergency Alert Platform, linked to 999 and other national call centers.

  • Enable automated alerts from surveillance feeds for faster dispatch of responders.



2. National Pipeline and Grid Safety Monitoring

  • Deploy sensor networks across gas and electricity pipelines to monitor leakage, pressure, vibration, and flow.

  • Build a central Infrastructure Safety Monitoring System to aggregate data, allow predictive maintenance, and enable rapid fault isolation.

  • Ensure coordination with BAPEX, PGCB, DESCO, DPDC, Titas Gas and other relevant agencies.

3. Satellite and Drone-Based Disaster Response

  • Use satellite imaging for large-scale disaster forecasting (cyclones, floods, landslides).

  • Deploy drones for post-disaster mapping, enabling targeted relief, damage assessment, and infrastructure checks.

  • Archive and analyze all imagery within the National Image Repository (under NDGA).

4. Smart Highway and Transport Infrastructure

  • Introduce embedded sensors and AI systems on highways to detect accidents, congestion, and environmental risks.

  • Integrate toll automation, BRTA vehicle data, and emergency services into a National Intelligent Transport Safety Platform.

  • Apply GPU-powered data analytics for real-time traffic and hazard management.

Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • 50% faster response times during national emergencies.

  • Nationwide AI-enabled surveillance, integrated with 999 and emergency services.

  • Full pipeline and grid coverage with predictive maintenance capability.

  • National archive of disaster imagery (satellite + drone).

  • Smart transport network covering 1,500 km of highways with real-time safety monitoring.





4.3.6 Digital Accessibility for Persons with Disabilities:

To ensure inclusive, barrier-free access to all public and private digital services for persons with disabilities (PwDs), enabling equal participation in Bangladesh’s digital economy, education, and public service ecosystem.

Why This Is Needed

  • According to a2i estimates and national data, over 9% of Bangladesh’s population (~15 million people) live with some form of disability.

  • Yet fewer than 20% of government websites are fully accessible.

  • Accessibility is not just a social obligation — it is a driver of economic inclusion, as digitally empowered PwDs can participate in freelancing, e-commerce, and digital jobs.



Strategic Components

1. Mandatory Digital Accessibility Standards

  • National guidelines aligned with W3C Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG 2.1).

  • Enforcement for all government websites, apps, and digital public services by 2030.

2. Inclusive Public Platforms (e.g., Nagorik Seba)

  • Assistive Technology Integration: Screen-reader compatibility, audio navigation, voice guidance, subtitles, and sign language in video content. Simplified layouts for cognitive, motor, and visual impairments.

  • Human-Assisted Digital Access: Recognizing that not all citizens, particularly those with disabilities or low literacy, can navigate digital interfaces independently, Nagorik Sheba centers operate as human-agent-based digital service hubs. Trained entrepreneurs at these centers provide direct assistance, helping PwDs and illiterate citizens apply for services, access benefits, and complete transactions, ensuring no one is left behind due to a lack of digital skills.

3. Accessible Social Security & Benefits Platforms

  • Voice-assisted navigation.

  • Alternative authentication (fingerprint, OTP, face ID).

  • Accessible tracking of social protection payments.

4. Accessible Education Tools

  • Multimedia Talking Books, e-learning with Bangla screen readers.

  • Text-to-speech integration across education platforms.

  • Interactive content for visually and hearing-impaired learners.

5. Braille-Compatible Services

  • E-documents, digital libraries, device integration for blind and visually impaired users.

6. Accessible Emergency & Health Services

  • 999 and 333 helplines with text-based chat, video relay for the hearing impaired.

  • Priority tagging for persons with disabilities.

7. Training & Capacity Building

  • Specialized training for 5,000+ developers, designers, and service staff on inclusive digital design.

8. Assistive Technology Innovation

  • Incentives for local startups to build AI-powered Bangla screen readers, voice-command systems, and affordable assistive devices.

Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • 100% accessibility compliance across all government websites and major private digital platforms.

  • 2M+ persons with disabilities directly benefiting from accessible social security, education, and health platforms.

  • 5,000 developers trained to design inclusive systems.

  • A vibrant assistive technology ecosystem, creating export-ready products and services.

  • Bangladesh recognized as a regional leader in digital inclusion.

4.3.7 Digital Social Protection Framework:

Bangladesh has a wide range of social safety net programs (SSNPs) — including allowances for the elderly, persons with disabilities, widows, and marginalized groups — serving over 9 million beneficiaries. However, program delivery remains fragmented, with challenges such as duplication, leakage, and lack of transparency.

A Digital Social Protection Framework will ensure targeted, efficient, and inclusive benefits delivery using ICT tools.

Key Provisions

  • Unified Beneficiary Database (UBD): Consolidate all SSNP data under NDGA, linked to NID and Digital ID Wallet.

  • Targeted E-Cash Transfers: Social protection payments delivered directly into mobile wallets and bank accounts, reducing leakage.

  • Smart Eligibility & Monitoring: Use AI/data analytics to eliminate duplication, track poverty dynamics, and identify new beneficiaries.

  • Accessible Platforms: Enable voice-assisted and disability-friendly systems for citizens to check benefits status.

  • Cross-Agency Integration: Connect MoSW, MoWCA, and Finance Division through APIs for coordinated benefits delivery.

Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • 100% of social protection payments digitized.

  • Leakage reduced by 50% through data-driven targeting.

  • Real-time monitoring dashboards operational at ministry level.

  • Enhanced financial inclusion of marginalized groups (women, PwDs, rural poor).


4.3.8 ICT in Justice & Judiciary:

Bangladesh’s judiciary faces a backlog of over 3.7 million pending cases, leading to delays in justice delivery. Manual processes, paper records, and lack of integrated case management slow proceedings. To address this, the government is scaling up a2i’s E-Court Initiative (labeled MyCourt or Amar Adalat in pilot phases) to introduce a fully digital judicial ecosystem.

Strategic Objective The primary goal is to transform the traditional courtroom into a "Virtual Court" environment that ensures speedy trials, transparent case tracking, and remote accessibility for all citizens, reducing the time and cost of litigation.

Key Provisions

  • Virtual Courtrooms: Leveraging the success of virtual courts during the pandemic, the initiative standardizes remote hearings for bail, witness testimonies, and civil motions. This reduces the need for physical transport of prisoners and witnesses, enhancing safety and efficiency.

  • Judicial Dashboard & Cause List: A unified digital platform where lawyers and litigants can check the Daily Cause List, case status, and next hearing dates in real-time via mobile apps, eliminating the need for physical lobbying.

  • Digital Evidence Management: Implementing a secure repository for uploading and storing digital evidence (audio, video, documents), ensuring chain-of-custody integrity and preventing evidence tampering.

  • e-Filing & e-Summons: Enabling lawyers to file cases electronically and receive digitally signed summons/notices via SMS and email, drastically reducing procedural delays caused by manual dispatch.

  • AI-Assisted Judgment: Piloting Artificial Intelligence tools to assist judges in legal research, case summarization, and translating judgments from English to Bangla (and vice versa) to make legal outcomes accessible to the general public.

Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • Case backlog reduced by 30% through faster e-filing and hearings.

  • 100% digitization of new case filings.

  • Establishment of specialized e-courts for commercial disputes.

  • Increased citizen trust through transparent, trackable legal processes.

4.4 National E-SERVICE Guideline



Bangladesh has made significant progress in digitalization, with over 350+ services digitized, MyGov platform expansion, and Union Digital Centers providing access across rural areas. However, most services remain partially digital, often requiring manual verification and physical presence. To achieve a fully inclusive and efficient digital governance system by 2030, a structured e-Service Guideline is essential to:

  • Mandate end-to-end digital services (apply, pay, verify, receive online).

  • Ensure equity and inclusion, with Bangla-first interfaces and accessibility standards.

  • Build citizen trust through secure, transparent, and efficient service delivery.

  • Drive cost savings and efficiency in government operations.

  • Align Bangladesh with global digital governance benchmarks (e.g., Estonia, Singapore, India).

Guiding Principles

  1. Digital by Default – Every new government service must be designed as fully digital from inception.

  2. Bangla First – All services must provide Bangla-enabled interfaces, ensuring inclusivity.

  3. Mobile-Centric Access – Given Bangladesh’s high mobile penetration, services must be optimized for mobile devices.

  4. End-to-End Digitalization – No mandatory physical visits; all steps (application, payment, verification, delivery) must be digital.

  5. Interoperability – All e-services must ensure interoperability following the NDGA guidelines.

  6. Trust and Security – Services must comply with PDPO 2025 (as amended 2026) and NDGA data protection standards.

  7. Citizen-Centric Design – Services should be user-tested for accessibility, clarity, and speed.

  8. Accessibility & Inclusion – Services must meet digital accessibility standards for persons with disabilities.

  9. Transparency & Accountability – Service metrics (processing time, approvals, complaints) must be published in real time.

  10. Continuous Improvement – Feedback loops and user surveys should inform service redesigns.

Institutional Roles

  • ICT Division (Lead): Policy oversight, digital standards, interoperability enforcement.

  • DoICT: Capacity building, grassroots training, district and upazila service hubs.

  • BCC: Certification of e-services for compliance, infrastructure hosting.

  • NDGA: Data protection, interoperability, trust compliance.

  • Line Ministries: Service design, ownership, and integration with core platforms.

  • a2i: Innovation, rapid prototyping, and user-centered design pilots.

Implementation Framework

Phase 1 (2025–2026): Foundation

  • Digitize 800+ services end-to-end.

  • Enforce common design standards (Bangla, mobile, PDPA-aligned).

  • Launch National e-Service Dashboard to track KPIs.

Phase 2 (2027–2028): Expansion

  • Achieve 75% of all services fully digital.

  • Integrate all services into MyGov super-app.

  • Introduce real-time grievance redressal across ministries.

  • Implement AI-based chatbots for citizen interaction.

  • Expand the Nagorik Sheba agent network to 2,000 centers, ensuring that every Union has a physical touchpoint where illiterate citizens can receive digital support for land, passport, and NID services.

Phase 3 (2029–2030): Smart Service Delivery

  • Reach 95% end-to-end digital service coverage.

  • Enable predictive governance: AI models anticipate citizen needs (e.g., automatic benefit eligibility).

  • Integrate digital ID & document wallet for instant verification.

  • Bangladesh enters UN E-Gov Index Top 15.

Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • 95% of government services delivered digitally end-to-end.

  • 110 million citizens actively using digital services.

  • $850M in cost savings from reduced paper, travel, and manual processes.

  • Universal Bangla digital access with inclusive service design.

  • Enhanced citizen trust and transparency in governance.

Bangladesh recognized as a regional leader in e-governance.

4.5 Flagship Initiative: Nagorik Sheba Bangladesh

Nagorik Sheba Bangladesh is a nationwide initiative to bring government e-services closer to citizens through community-based service centers operated by trained local entrepreneurs. Unused government premises such as post offices and union facilities are being transformed into modern co-working service hubs, providing citizens with convenient access to services like NID, passport, tax filing, and more. Nagorik Sheba is fundamentally a Human-Assisted Digital Service model. It bridges the digital divide not just by providing internet access, but by providing a trained human intermediary who navigates the complex digital ecosystem on behalf of citizens with low literacy, disabilities, or limited technical skills.

Zero-Cost Interoperability Hub Model

A critical innovation of Nagorik Sheba is its financial model—it operates as an interoperability hub built with zero cost to public money. The public-private-community partnership model ensures centers are managed by licensed entrepreneurs under a sustainable social business framework, with private financing from banks, NGOs, and donors replacing direct government subsidies.

Impact on Interoperability and Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI): Nagorik Sheba serves as the "last-mile" physical manifestation of the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDIA). By acting as a decentralized interoperability hub, it directly supports the transition from fragmented "data islands" to the DSTAR (Distributed Secure Trusted Architecture for Resilience) ecosystem.

Current Status (2025 Pilot)

  • Three pilot centers launched in Gulshan, Uttara, and Nilkhet

  • 60 entrepreneurs trained and certified (25 women, 35 men), with 6 operating the first centers

  • Each center delivers 500+ government services digitally

  • Officially inaugurated on 26 May 2025 by Chief Advisor Dr. Muhammad Yunus
