12,000

50%

1,200+

50M

2030

8,000+

20,000+

50%

1,500+

80M+



Strategic Role in e-Service Ecosystem

  • Accessibility: Ensures "walk-to-distance" availability of digital services nationwide

  • Inclusion: Creates jobs for youth and women while bridging the rural-urban divide

  • Trust & Transparency: Centers use standardized SOPs and digital monitoring for accountability

  • Scalability: Provides a replicable model to expand e-services sustainably without heavy fiscal burden

Integration with the National e-Service Ecosystem

Nagorik Sheba is not a stand-alone program but a practical expression of the National e-Service Guideline. Its rollout directly operationalizes the core principles of Bangla-first service delivery, citizen-centric design, inclusion, and end-to-end digitalization. Each Nagorik Sheba center acts as a local access point to the government’s wider digital ecosystem, ensuring that citizens can reach services without long travel or administrative barriers.

The platform will be fully integrated with MyGov to allow seamless digital applications and service tracking. It will also complement Union Digital Centers (UDCs) by standardizing service quality and expanding coverage beyond existing rural hubs. In addition, the National Portal will serve as the central directory of all services available at Nagorik Sheba centers. The initiative is designed to interlink with helpline services (333 for citizen queries and 999 for emergencies), ensuring citizens have a consistent, trusted, and accessible gateway for all government services.

---

End of Chapter 4

5. Strategic Policies for Digital Transformation

This chapter outlines the strategic policies essential for Bangladesh's digital transformation journey. These policies establish the regulatory frameworks, governance structures, and operational guidelines necessary to ensure secure, ethical, and sustainable digital development aligned with the UN Sustainable Development Goals.

5.1 Guiding Policy Principles

Before detailing individual policies, this section establishes the cross-cutting principles that govern all policy formulation and implementation within the digital transformation agenda. These principles, while not formally codified in separate policy documents, represent binding guidance that all agencies and implementing bodies must observe.

5.1.1 Vendor Neutrality and Digital Sovereignty

A critical pillar of Bangladesh's digital transformation is the assurance that public digital infrastructure and services remain under national control, free from dependency on any single vendor, technology provider, or proprietary system. Over the past decade, certain government ministries, agencies, and state-owned enterprises have procured "vendor-locked" or "closed" software and hardware systems under the banner of automation. While these systems have enabled short-term operational gains, many have created long-term strategic vulnerabilities.

Such systems often come with restrictive licensing models, lack of interoperability, and prohibitively high renewal or change fees. Even minor modifications—such as profile changes, configuration updates, or integration of new features—may require substantial additional payments to vendors, particularly foreign suppliers. In some cases, critical public sector platforms are contractually bound to a vendor's proprietary ecosystem until as far ahead as 2030 or beyond. This dependency limits adaptability, delays service improvements, and imposes excessive financial burdens on the state.

Policy Directives

Ban on Vendor-Locked Systems:

Public funds, whether from national revenue or foreign loans, must not be used to procure software or hardware that creates dependency on a single vendor for its operation, modification, or maintenance. All newly procured systems must support open standards, interoperability, and integration with the National Digital Architecture.

Mandatory Source Code Ownership:

Government agencies must ensure full ownership of the source code for all software they procure or develop. Contracts must include provisions granting the government the legal right to modify, update, and redeploy the software without additional vendor authorization.

Institutional Capacity for Code Maintenance:

Ministries and agencies procuring software must either maintain their own secure Git-based code repository or partner with another government entity that has such capacity (e.g., National Source Code Repository under ICT Division). No automation project should proceed without an identified technical team capable of maintaining and evolving the software post-deployment.

Interoperability and Security by Design:

All new systems must be designed with interoperability, API integration, redundancy, and cybersecurity considerations from inception. Regular code audits and testing must be conducted through the Software and Hardware Quality Testing and Certification (SHQTC) Center.

Phased Transition Away from Existing Vendor-Locked Systems:

For current locked systems, agencies must develop phased migration plans toward open, interoperable solutions. Where legal or contractual barriers exist, renegotiations should be initiated to recover control over system architecture and code.

Strategic Outcomes

Implementing this policy will reduce long-term operational costs by eliminating excessive licensing and change fees, increase agility in adapting systems to evolving national needs, strengthen cybersecurity by allowing local oversight of all source code, prevent strategic infrastructure from being controlled by a small number of vendors, and promote innovation by enabling multiple domestic and international suppliers to compete on a level playing field.

5.1.2 Open Source for Trust and Transparency

Bangladesh's digital transformation depends on sovereign, interoperable, and cost-effective digital infrastructure. Reliance on vendor-locked proprietary systems creates financial burdens, dependency, and security risks. Inspired by the UN Open Source Principles (endorsed by France in 2025), Bangladesh will adopt an "Open Source by Default" posture for all digital policies to ensure resilience, innovation, and citizen trust.

Strategic Objectives

Mandate open by default for public sector digital systems. Strengthen sovereignty by retaining full source code ownership. Enhance trust and transparency via open repositories and public audits. Promote reuse and interoperability across ministries through the National Source Code Policy. Foster a domestic open source innovation ecosystem (universities, startups, freelancers). Support Bangla-first tools to ensure local language access and inclusivity.

Strategic Pillars (aligned with UN Open Source Principles)

Open by Default: All government-funded software developed under open licenses unless exceptions approved.

Contribute Upstream: Public agencies must contribute fixes, translations, and enhancements to global projects.

Trust and Security: Code published in auditable repositories; regular vulnerability assessments.

Bangla Inclusivity: Prioritize open source platforms that support Bangla interfaces, fonts, and NLP datasets.

Reuse and Interoperability: Mandatory use of the National Source Code Repository for all agencies.

Recognition and Capacity: Civil service engineers' contributions count as KPIs; community developers incentivized.

Sustainability: Dedicated annual budget line for open source maintenance and scaling.



Open Source Implementation Targets (2025-2030)

Year

% New Gov Systems Open Source

Developers Trained

Repositories

Cost Savings (USD)

Bangla Tools

Expected Outcomes by 2030

80% of new government software will be open source. 50,000 developers will be certified in open source skills. 2,000+ public repositories will be in the National Source Code Repository. $200M in annual savings will be realized from reduced vendor dependency. 50 Bangla-enabled open source platforms will support e-gov and citizen services. Bangladesh will be recognized as a regional leader in open government technology.

5.2 Draft Technology Policies

5.2.1 National AI Policy

Bangladesh has developed a draft National AI Policy (Draft V1.1: 2026-2030) which establishes a strategic roadmap to leverage Artificial Intelligence as a fundamental driver for sustainable economic growth, governance modernization, and regional leadership. The policy recognizes that Bangladesh, like most of the world, is currently a consumer of AI products rather than a producer, with production concentrated primarily in the US, China, and some European and Asian countries. With over half the population having internet access through smartphones and strengthening connectivity infrastructure, people in Bangladesh are already exposed to and using AI products, mainly Generative AI chatbots like ChatGPT.

The policy is designed to be flexible and adaptable to the accelerated growth and unknowns of AI, prioritizing the regulation of AI consumption, public safety from potential harms, and the creation of an ethical foundation for future domestic innovation.

Core Objectives and Vision

Vision: The development of an ethical and innovative environment for the use and development of AI to accelerate Bangladesh's path towards a middle-income country and aligned with the UN's Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

Timeline

Objectives

Short-Term (3-5 Years)

Establish core government offices for AI implementation and monitoring under NDGA; regulate ethical use through risk-based classification; build public awareness; develop regulatory sandboxes for safe experimentation

Medium-Term (5-10 Years)

Fully incorporate AI into public service delivery; foster local AI entrepreneurship through targeted incentives; develop a sustainable, skilled national workforce; establish AI Data Exchange platform

Long-Term (10-20 Years)

Achieve regional leadership in South Asia by building a sovereign AI ecosystem encompassing quality data, compute infrastructure, and talent; position Bangladesh as a competitive player in global AI governance



Bangladesh AI Principles

The Principles of AI in Bangladesh are rooted in the national and cultural context, in alignment with commitments to international norms and standards including UN, OECD, and UNESCO frameworks:

Ethical and Safe: The government shall govern the use and development of AI to improve quality of life and distribute benefits equitably across all segments of society, ensuring AI consumption and production are aligned with ethical standards, human rights, rule of law, and democratic values.

Pro-Innovation and Sustainable Development: The government shall promote innovation through an enabling legal, regulatory, and fiscal environment that supports research and development, startups, and responsible experimentation, including targeted tax incentives, public funding mechanisms, and pilot and sandbox frameworks.

Human-Centric: The government shall prioritize AI systems aligned with human rights, rule of law, and democratic values, requiring mandatory explainability and accountability mechanisms for high-impact decisions.

Inclusive, Equitable, and Non-Discriminatory: The government shall govern AI use to avoid discrimination that can emerge through algorithmic bias, flawed data structures, and human errors.

Subject to Oversight and Accountability: The government shall enforce strict human accountability measures for AI benefits and harms, establishing continuous evaluation, feedback, and learning mechanisms to adapt AI governance frameworks over time.

Transparent and Responsible: The government shall establish transparency measures in AI use, decision-making processes, and mechanisms to build public trust in AI consumption.

Regulatory Framework and Risk Classification

The government shall adopt a risk-based classification framework for AI systems, with regulatory obligations calibrated to the level of risk posed to individuals, society, and the State. The framework is supported by the Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO) 2025 (as amended 2026), the National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO) 2025, and the Cyber Safety Ordinance 2025.

Risk Category

Description

Requirements

Prohibited (Unacceptable)

AI-enabled social scoring, indiscriminate biometric mass surveillance, manipulative systems exploiting vulnerabilities

Banned entirely; explicit legal prohibition

High-Risk

Law enforcement, biometric ID, creditworthiness, welfare allocation, employment, healthcare decisions

Prior approval, documentation, oversight, mandatory Algorithmic Impact Assessments (AIA)

Limited-Risk

Content generation, chatbots, customer service AI, recommendation systems

Transparency requirements, user disclosure, content labeling

Low-Risk

Spam filters, inventory management, general productivity tools

Minimal compliance; baseline safety standards



Institutional Framework: National Data Governance Authority (NDGA)

The government shall designate the National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) as the central coordinating and regulatory body for AI governance across government. Key responsibilities include:

Issue Technical Standards: Develop and maintain technical standards for AI systems, data formats, and interoperability requirements.

Certify Compliance: Establish certification programs for high-risk AI systems and AI vendors, incorporating both technical standards and ethical considerations.

Oversee Algorithmic Impact Assessments: Mandate and review AIAs for all significant AI systems deployed by public authorities and for high-risk AI systems deployed by private actors.

Manage AI Data Exchange: Establish and manage a dedicated AI Data Exchange to facilitate responsible access to high-quality datasets for AI research, innovation, and public-interest applications.

Data Governance and Sovereignty

Without data, there is no AI. The National Data Governance Ordinance provides a landmark foundation for AI development in Bangladesh. Key provisions include:

Data Sovereignty: Sensitive and critical datasets-including health, biometric, financial, children's, and national security-related data-shall be stored and processed within Bangladesh or in jurisdictions explicitly designated as trusted. For Critical Information Infrastructure (CII), any offshore processing is strictly conditional upon the maintenance of a synchronized real-time domestic copy.

Right to Erasure: Extends to AI systems trained on personal data. Where an AI system has been trained using data that was unlawfully obtained or processed without valid consent, the deploying entity shall implement algorithmic disgorgement measures.

Open Standards: All public-sector AI systems shall adopt open standards, interoperable data formats, and standardized APIs to prevent vendor lock-in and strengthen digital sovereignty.

Children's Data Protection: Prohibit AI companies from collecting data of children under 16 without parental consent. AI-generated targeted advertising directed at minors shall be prohibited.

UNESCO AI Readiness Assessment Methodology (RAM)

Bangladesh has undertaken a comprehensive AI readiness assessment in partnership with UNESCO to evaluate its preparedness for ethical and inclusive AI adoption. This assessment follows UNESCO's Readiness Assessment Methodology (RAM), a systematic framework designed to help countries assess their capacity to develop, deploy, and govern AI systems in alignment with UNESCO's Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence.

Assessment Governance Structure

The ICT Division, under the Ministry of Posts, Telecommunications and Information Technology, leads Bangladesh's AI governance efforts. A National Steering Committee, co-chaired by the ICT Division and UNESCO, has been established to oversee the assessment process. Key collaborating ministries include the Cabinet Division, Law Ministry, Ministry of Public Administration, and Home Ministry. Aspire to Innovate (a2i), launched in 2007 with support from UNDP, the Gates Foundation, and the Government of Bangladesh, provides technical expertise and coordinates innovation initiatives.

Key Assessment Dimensions and Findings

Dimension

Current Status

Key Gaps Identified

Legal & Regulatory

Emerging framework with PDPO 2025, NDGO 2025, Cyber Safety Ordinance 2025

National AI Policy pending finalization; AI-specific procurement policies needed

Institutional Capacity

ICT Division leads; a2i provides innovation support; NDGA proposed

Specialized AI institutions needed; multi-stakeholder governance body required

Infrastructure

National Data Center operational; 54.8% household internet access

GPU compute resources limited; cloud infrastructure fragmented; urban-rural digital divide

Data Ecosystem

NRDEX platform under development; data sharing frameworks emerging

Bengali language datasets inadequate; data governance frameworks need strengthening

Human Capital

Limited AI awareness; skills concentrated in urban areas

Comprehensive AI literacy programs needed; workforce reskilling urgent

Research & Innovation

University research ongoing; startup ecosystem nascent

R&D funding inadequate; industry-academia collaboration limited



Strategic Opportunities

The assessment identified significant opportunities for Bangladesh to leverage AI for inclusive growth:

Ethical Framework Alignment: Establish robust AI frameworks aligned with UNESCO's ethical AI principles, building public trust and international credibility.

Institutional Development: Create specialized AI institutions including research centers, certification bodies, and a multi-stakeholder AI governance body under NDGA coordination.

Language Localization: Build comprehensive Bengali and indigenous language datasets to localize AI applications, ensuring accessibility for all citizens.

Talent Development: Invest in AI research infrastructure and talent development through curriculum updates, vocational training, and international partnerships.

Inclusive Access: Focus on gender equity and digital inclusion to ensure equitable access to AI benefits, particularly for women, rural populations, and marginalized communities.





5.2.2 National Source Code Policy

The National Source Code Policy 2025 ("Public Money, Public Code") establishes a unified, transparent, and secure framework for the development, management, reuse, and governance of all government-funded or government-controlled software in Bangladesh. The policy recognizes source code as a national asset and positions it at the heart of secure, interoperable, and sustainable digital transformation. This policy is issued under the National Data Governance and Interoperability Ordinance 2025, Section 8, and administered by Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC) under NDGA oversight.

Purpose and Vision

Strengthen Digital Sovereignty: All government-funded software is public property unless exceptions apply. Centralized governance ensures long-term control, security, and sustainability.

Reduce Vendor Lock-in: By mandating open-source licensing and reuse-first approaches, agencies are protected from dependency on single vendors.

Increase Software Reuse: A "Reuse First" mandate ensures ministries must search and evaluate existing government-developed software before starting new development.

Improve Transparency and Security: All source code, artifacts, licensing, and metadata must be stored in a national repository with full audit logs, version history, and CI/CD integrity.

Modernize Development Practices: Promote the adoption of modern software development lifecycles (SDLC) and modular architectures (e.g., microservices) to enhance reusability, accelerate deployment, and facilitate continuous upgradation of government digital services.

Promote innovation, skill development, and local industry growth: Open-source code and transparent standards help build local capacity, strengthen trust, and encourage industry–government collaboration.

Scope of the Policy

The policy applies to all ministries, divisions, departments, attached/special/autonomous bodies, and all software, APIs, and digital services developed through national budget, foreign loans or grants, and government-led or government-funded projects. It also applies to public–private projects using government funds, vendor-developed software delivered to the government, and mixed financing models where the government has partial ownership. No software developed with public funds may be deployed to production without being registered in the National Source Code Repository (NSCR).

Key Provisions and Mechanisms

A. National Source Code Repository (NSCR):

A centralized repository operated under BCC/BGD e-Gov (KoUdiL) and governed by the National Interoperability and Asset Management Authority. Every project must upload source code, documentation, build instructions, release tags and commit history, metadata, and SBOM (Software Bill of Materials). No deployment is allowed until the repository entry is complete.

B. Reuse-First Mandate:

Before starting new development, agencies must search the national repository, evaluate existing software or modules, document findings, justify non-reuse with strong technical/legal reasons, and obtain approval from the authority before proceeding. This reduces duplication, cost, and development time.

C. Open Licensing:

Government software must use approved open-source licenses. Non-open or ambiguous licenses are prohibited. Any third-party intellectual property issues must be cleared before publication. The default principle is: "Public money, public code." Exceptions (with strict approval) include national security, confidentiality, and sensitive IP restrictions.

D. Secure Development and Deployment:

A Coding Standards Committee serves as a national body that maintains coding standards, reviews critical components, issues security guidance, and produces boilerplate and reusable templates. A mandatory CI/CD pipeline requires every government software system to use an approved pipeline that performs automated testing, vulnerability scanning, license verification, and manual approval before production deployment. Mandatory security patching ensures deployed software receives regular security updates, vulnerability fixes, and patch release logs.

E. Role-Based Access Governance:

The policy mandates strict access control in the repository with roles including Viewer (can view code), Contributor (can submit code), Maintainer (manages codebase), Approver (authorizes deployments), and Auditor (performs reviews and compliance checks). All contributors must sign NDAs.

F. Data and Metadata Governance:

All software-related datasets are classified as Open, Restricted, or Regulated. Metadata must be recorded in the national data catalog. Sensitive datasets require NDAs, legal compliance, and access controls.



Compliance, Monitoring and Audit

The policy requires independent and automated audits, self-assessment by each agency, regular compliance reporting, and mandatory focal points in each ministry. Enforcement actions include administrative measures, contract penalties, and legal consequences. The policy must be reviewed every three years to remain aligned with evolving technology and national priorities.

Additional Recommendations

Government Code Exchange Portal: A public portal showcasing government open-source projects, similar to India's OpenForge and UK's GitHub Government initiative.

Mandatory SBOM for all vendors: This improves supply-chain security and traceability.

National Training Program on Secure DevOps: To build capacity across ministries.

Mandatory Open API Registry: Ensures interoperability and prevents API fragmentation.

Integration with National Cybersecurity Strategy: Shared logs, SIEM integration, and secure build pipelines.



Role-Based Access Control

Role

Permissions

Viewer

View repository contents and documentation

Contributor

Push code to repository; NDA signature required

Maintainer

Manage codebase, handle merge requests, manage CI/CD integration; NDA required

Approver

Authorize production deployments; NDA required

Auditor

Analyze logs, conduct compliance reviews, generate audit reports; NDA required



Each agency must designate at least one Maintainer, Approver, and Auditor. Vendors are not granted independent repository access; agencies work with their vendors to manage source code. All user activities are logged for audit purposes, and NDAs remain effective even after role termination.

NDGA and BCC jointly monitor compliance through repository analytics, random audits, and annual agency reports. Each agency must designate a Focal Point for policy compliance. The policy is reviewed every three years to ensure alignment with evolving legal, technical, and administrative requirements.



5.2.3 National Drone Policy

Bangladesh's National Drone Policy treats drones (UAS) as shared public infrastructure to accelerate digital transformation across land, river, environment, agriculture, cities, infrastructure, disaster response, and public safety. The policy's core principle is "collect once, use many times": any publicly funded drone data is standardized and deposited into a National Aerial Imagery Repository (NAIR) within the National Spatial Data Infrastructure (NSDI), and all publicly procured drones are tracked in a National Drone Inventory (NDI).

Governance Framework

Institution

Responsibilities

NDGA

Lead coordinating body for drone data governance; data standards, interoperability, sharing protocols, repository management, and compliance audits

CAAB

Regulate flight safety, licensing, registration, geozones, and operational authorizations; implement UTM system

Survey of Bangladesh

Receive and validate authoritative survey deliverables; maintain mapping standards

BCC

Operate secure cloud infrastructure, APIs, and host UTM systems at National Data Center

SPARRSO

Technical advisor on sensors, remote sensing methods, and data analysis techniques



Strategic Sectors for Drone Use

Agriculture: Crop health monitoring using RGB and multispectral imagery, soil moisture assessment, targeted input application, damage assessment, and crop spraying pilots.

Urban Planning: 3D city models, traffic monitoring, infrastructure inspection, and informal settlement mapping.

Land and Rivers: Cadastral mapping with centimeter-level accuracy, erosion monitoring, char mapping, and identification of illegal structures.

Environment: Forest patrols with infrared cameras for nighttime surveillance, biodiversity monitoring, pollution monitoring with mounted sensors.

Infrastructure: Bridge, rail, and runway inspections; construction progress monitoring; earthwork volume calculations.

Disaster Response: Rapid damage assessment maps, search and rescue with thermal cameras, live feeds to emergency coordination centers.

Law Enforcement: Crowd monitoring, crime scene mapping with 3D models, search and rescue operations, border patrol, and traffic enforcement.

Regulatory and Safety Framework

Category

Description

Requirements

Open

Low-risk operations in designated green zones, below certain altitudes, away from sensitive areas

Minimal paperwork; auto-approval or blanket permission; flight logging required

Specific

Operations in yellow zones; flights near critical infrastructure, at night, or BVLOS

CAAB permission required; SORA-based risk assessment; additional safety measures

Certified

High-risk operations in red zones (airports, military, sensitive facilities)

Both CAAB and High Security clearances required; case-by-case approval



Data Sharing and Repository Access Tiers

The National Drone Image Repository operates under NSDI with a five-tier access model aligned with PDPO data protection requirements:

Access Tier

Description and Examples

Open Public

Unrestricted access; generalized land cover maps, agricultural surveys, post-disaster damage maps; published under open licenses

Public with Registration

Requires user registration; detailed environmental and infrastructure data; academic research applications

Gov-to-Gov

Internal government data; high-resolution urban imagery, preliminary survey data; authenticated government users only

Restricted

Tightly controlled access; detailed critical infrastructure imagery, law enforcement footage; 2FA required, usage audited

Confidential

National security data; encrypted storage only; secure offline access; metadata visible but files in separate secure systems



Satellite-Drone Complementarity:

The repository includes satellite imagery for comprehensive coverage. However, satellite imagery is significantly more expensive. Drone images compensate for satellite costs while offering superior precision for detailed applications - maximizing cost-effectiveness while ensuring comprehensive spatial coverage at multiple resolution levels.

Coverage

Outcomes

5.2.4 National Cloud Policy

Strategic Context and Rationale

The growth of e-services, digital identity systems, financial technology, and artificial intelligence demands secure, sovereign, and scalable data storage capabilities. Bangladesh's cloud adoption has historically been fragmented, governed by ad-hoc guidelines that created risks of data breaches, vendor lock-in, and non-compliance with emerging global standards. The National Cloud Policy addresses these challenges by establishing clear rules for cloud adoption across government while enabling the innovation necessary for digital transformation.

The policy recognizes that cloud computing is not merely a technology choice but a strategic capability that underpins virtually every digital initiative. From citizen-facing services to internal government operations, from AI-powered analytics to disaster recovery, cloud infrastructure provides the scalable, flexible foundation that modern digital government requires.

Vision and Guiding Principles

Vision: Establish a secure, sovereign, and scalable Government Cloud ecosystem that accelerates digital transformation while maintaining the trust and protection that citizens deserve.

• Sovereign-by-Design: Sensitive government data and critical infrastructure remain under Bangladesh legal control at all times.

• Risk-Based Placement: Workload placement decisions balance innovation needs with appropriate protection based on data sensitivity.

• Security Baseline Compliance: All cloud implementations meet mandatory security, privacy, and resilience controls aligned with PDPO, NDGO, and CSO.

• Vendor Neutrality: Procurement ensures interoperability and prevents single-provider dependency through open standards.

• FinOps Governance: Cost optimization through centralized financial operations practices including automated anomaly detection and transparent chargeback models.

Data Classification Framework

The policy establishes a five-tier data classification framework that determines permissible cloud deployment models, security controls, and residency requirements:

Level

Category

Description

Residency Requirement

D0

Open

Publicly available information

No restriction

D1

Internal

Internal government use, limited harm if disclosed

Preferred domestic

D2

Confidential

Personal data, operational data requiring protection

Mandatory domestic

D3

Restricted

Sensitive personal data, law enforcement, financial

Sovereign zone only

D4

National Critical

National security, CII-related, diplomatic

NDC sovereign zone only

This classification system directly aligns with NDGO data governance requirements and determines workload placement: D4 workloads are hosted exclusively in National Data Center sovereign environments; D3 workloads require accredited Tier 4 sovereign cloud zones; D2 workloads require domestic data residency with Tier 3+ accredited providers.

Hybrid Multi-Provider Approach

The policy mandates a hybrid multi-provider approach that balances sovereignty with flexibility. Rather than mandating a single cloud provider or model, the policy enables agencies to select the most appropriate deployment pattern for each workload based on data classification, performance requirements, and cost considerations.

The National Data Center (NDC) serves as the primary Cloud Governance and Implementation Authority, exercising regulatory oversight and maintaining the Government Cloud Technical Baseline. Bangladesh Data Centre Company Limited (BDCCL) operates as the primary Government Cloud service provider, offering IaaS and PaaS capabilities with sovereignty guarantees.

Accreditation Framework

Any Cloud Service Provider supporting Government workloads must be accredited against the Government Cloud Control Baseline:

Tier

Permitted Data Classes

Accreditation Requirements

Tier 1

D0 (Open) only

Self-attestation + registry listing

Tier 2

D0-D1 (Open, Internal)

Documented controls, NDC review

Tier 3

D0-D2 (including Confidential)

Independent audit (ISO 27001), penetration testing, DPIA

Tier 4

D0-D4 (including Restricted, Critical)

Enhanced audit, sovereign controls, NCSA approval, CII compliance

Zero Trust Architecture

The policy mandates Zero Trust Architecture for all cloud implementations, implementing the principle that network location shall not confer trust:

• No Implicit Trust: All access requests authenticated and authorized regardless of origin

• Continuous Verification: Context-aware access policies evaluating risk signals for each decision

• Least Privilege: Elimination of standing privileged access in favor of just-in-time elevation

• Micro-Segmentation: Networks segmented to limit lateral movement with deny-by-default policies

• Assume Breach: Security architecture designed for detection, containment, and recovery

Implementation KPIs

KPI Metric

2026

2028

2030

Critical data in sovereign cloud

40%

80%

100%

National green data centers

1

2

3-4

ISO/IEC 27001 compliance

50%

80%

100%

Agency cloud migration

30%

60%

100%



5.2.5 National Blockchain Policy

Introduction and Strategic Rationale

As Bangladesh advances toward a fully digital economy, the need for a trusted, immutable, and transparent data layer has become critical. The National Blockchain Policy 2026 establishes the foundational framework for adopting Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT) not merely as a financial instrument, but as a critical component of the nation's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI).

This policy addresses the "Trust Deficit" in digital governance by replacing manual verification with cryptographic certainty. It aligns strictly with the legal triad of 2025: the National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO), the Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO), and the Cyber Safety Ordinance (CSO). The policy explicitly pivots away from speculative cryptocurrency trading, focusing instead on sovereign, permissioned blockchain networks that enhance public service delivery, secure land records, and validate citizen identity.

Vision and Mission

Vision: To transform Bangladesh into a blockchain-enabled nation where DLT serves as a sovereign, trusted infrastructure layer enhancing government transparency, citizen empowerment, and economic efficiency while strictly adhering to data sovereignty and privacy rights.

Mission: To establish a sovereign, permissioned blockchain ecosystem that integrates seamlessly with the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDIA) and the National Responsible Data Exchange (NRDEX), enabling secure and verifiable data sharing across government agencies while protecting citizen rights.

Core Policy Provisions

A) Priority application domains (illustrative, policy-specified examples)

The Policy specifies concrete public-sector and regulated-industry use cases and assigns lead stakeholders. Examples include:

  • Public finance & procurement: recording tender announcements, bids, evaluation, awards; anchoring budget and expenditure records for real-time tracking; integrating subsidy distribution with MFS for last-mile transparency (with Finance Division, iBAS++/CGA, CPTU e-GP, OCAG roles).


  • Supply chain traceability: pharmaceuticals “lab-to-patient,” agriculture “farm-to-fork,” RMG traceability for buyer compliance, and export documentation as Verifiable Credentials with TradeTrust compatibility.


  • Healthcare data exchange: verifiable vaccination/fitness/screening credentials, consent-based sharing with on-chain consent records, and PDPO compliance for sensitive personal data (MoH/DGHS and private ecosystem roles).


B) Legal and regulatory positioning

The Policy establishes how blockchain artifacts interact with Bangladesh’s legal system:

  • Evidentiary status: blockchain records are treated as electronic records under the ICT Act 2006 and Evidence Act 1872 (as amended); cryptographically secure chains may receive a rebuttable presumption of integrity; timestamps evidence existence at a point in time.


  • Smart contracts: recognized as evidence of contract formation when standard elements exist; natural language generally prevails if code conflicts; courts retain authority for corrective actions when code behaves unintentionally.


  • Crypto-asset regulatory perimeter:

    • Bangladesh Bank retains authority over cryptocurrency trading, VASPs, payment services, CBDC, and FX matters;

    • BSEC governs security tokens and related market infrastructure;

    • Ministry of Commerce governs consumer protection and trade/e-commerce aspects for blockchain applications.


  • Liability and market integrity controls: validator operators are accountable for secure, honest participation; the policy prohibits predatory MEV behaviors (front-running, sandwich attacks, arbitrary reordering) with revocation and penalties; smart contract deployers carry code-quality, upgrade governance, and PDPO “data fiduciary” obligations; NDGA is not liable for third-party application losses on the NBI.


  • Dispute resolution: may use on-chain mechanisms, ADR, or courts; government blockchain services must maintain grievance redress with defined timelines.


The "Immutability Paradox" and Privacy Solutions

A critical challenge in blockchain adoption is the tension between the technology's inherent immutability (once written, data cannot be deleted) and the Right to Erasure mandated by the Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO). To resolve this "Immutability Paradox," the policy mandates a Privacy by Design architecture for all public sector blockchain implementations:

  • Off-Chain Data Storage: Personal or sensitive data shall never be stored directly on-chain. Instead, data resides in secured, off-chain encrypted vaults (e.g., within the National Data Center), while only the cryptographic hash (a digital fingerprint) of the data is recorded on the blockchain.

  • Crypto-Shredding for Right to Erasure: When a citizen exercises their Right to Erasure, the system does not attempt to edit the immutable ledger. Instead, it destroys the specific encryption keys associated with that user's off-chain data. This renders the data mathematically inaccessible and unrecoverable, effectively satisfying the legal requirement for deletion without compromising the blockchain's integrity.

  • Pseudonymization: To prevent user profiling, the system mandates the use of rotating Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs). This ensures that a user's transaction history cannot be easily correlated to their real-world identity by unauthorized parties.

Technical Architecture: National Blockchain Infrastructure (NBI)

The policy establishes the National Blockchain Infrastructure (NBI) as the single, sovereign platform for all government blockchain applications. This approach avoids the risks associated with public, permissionless networks (like Ethereum or Bitcoin) where data governance is impossible.

1. Infrastructure Layer (Sovereignty & Localization)

  • Hosting: The NBI will be hosted exclusively within Bangladesh's sovereign territory, primarily at the National Data Center (NDC) in Kaliakair and Bangladesh Data Center Company Limited (BDCCL) facilities. This ensures compliance with Data Localization laws.

  • Disaster Recovery: A geographically separated Disaster Recovery (DR) site (minimum 200km distance) will maintain a real-time synchronized copy of the ledger with a Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of 4 hours.

2. Network and Consensus Layer

  • Permissioned Network: The NBI will operate as a consortium blockchain where only authorized government entities can validate transactions.

  • Validator Nodes: Validator nodes will be operated by trusted ministries including the ICT Division, National Board of Revenue (NBR), Ministry of Land, Bangladesh Bank, and Ministry of Health.

  • Consensus Mechanism: To ensure environmental sustainability, the network will use energy-efficient consensus protocols such as Proof of Authority (PoA) or Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT). Energy-intensive Proof of Work (PoW) mechanisms are explicitly prohibited for government deployments.

3. Interoperability & Integration

  • NRDEX as Oracle: The National Responsible Data Exchange (NRDEX) will serve as the trusted "Oracle," feeding authenticated off-chain data (e.g., NID verification results) into smart contracts.

  • BNDIA Alignment: All blockchain applications must expose data through standardized RESTful APIs compliant with the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDIA), ensuring legacy systems can interact with the ledger without needing specialized blockchain knowledge.

Priority Application Domains

The policy identifies six high-impact domains where blockchain will be deployed immediately to solve systemic governance challenges:

A. Digital Identity & Credential Verification (The "OneID" Trust Anchor)

  • Objective: To eliminate fake certificates and identity fraud.

  • Mechanism: A national DID registry on the NBI will issue Verifiable Credentials (VCs). Citizens can hold these credentials (birth certificates, driving licenses) in a mobile wallet. Verifiers (e.g., banks, employers) can instantly check the validity of a credential against the blockchain without contacting the issuing authority.

B. Land Records Management (The "Property Chain")

  • Objective: To stop land forgery and reduce litigation.

  • Mechanism: The "Property Chain" will record the complete history of a land parcel—from original survey to current mutation. Every transfer of ownership, mortgage, or lien will be an immutable transaction. This creates a single source of truth that prevents the "double-selling" of land.

C. Educational Credential Verification (The "Academic Chain")

  • Objective: To automate degree verification and combat counterfeit diplomas.

  • Mechanism: Universities and education boards will issue degrees as digital assets on the NBI. Employers can verify a candidate's degree in milliseconds by scanning a QR code, which checks the cryptographic signature on the blockchain.

D. Public Finance & Procurement (Smart Audit)

  • Objective: To prevent corruption in public spending.

  • Mechanism: The e-GP (Electronic Government Procurement) system will anchor tender opening logs and bid evaluation scores on the blockchain. This ensures that bid documents cannot be tampered with retroactively. Budget releases and expenditure tracking will also be tokenized to ensure funds reach their intended destination.

E. Supply Chain Traceability

  • Objective: To ensure food and drug safety.

  • Mechanism: "Farm-to-Fork" and "Lab-to-Patient" traceability systems will track products through the supply chain. Consumers can scan a medicine packet to verify its origin and ensure it is not counterfeit.

F. Healthcare Data Exchange

  • Objective: Secure, patient-controlled medical records.

  • Mechanism: Patients will grant doctors temporary access to their medical history via smart contracts. The blockchain records the "consent" transaction, ensuring an immutable audit trail of who accessed the patient's data and when.

Regulatory and Governance Framework

To ensure this powerful technology is managed responsibly, a robust governance structure is established:

1. National Data Governance Authority (NDGA)

  • Role: The primary regulator for the NBI. The NDGA is responsible for approving blockchain platforms, designating node operators, and enforcing data classification rules.

  • Oversight: It will operate a dedicated Blockchain Division to monitor compliance with the NDGO and PDPO.

2. National Blockchain Technical Committee (NBTC)

  • Role: A specialized technical body comprising experts from academia, industry, and government.

  • Functions: The NBTC will define technical standards (e.g., cryptographic algorithms), conduct smart contract security audits, and approve upgrades to the NBI protocol.

3. National Cybersecurity Agency (NCSA)

  • Role: Responsible for the security of the NBI as a Critical Information Infrastructure (CII). The NCSA will monitor the network via the National Security Operations Center (NSOC) and coordinate incident response.

4. Regulatory Sandbox

  • The NDGA will administer a Blockchain Regulatory Sandbox. This allows startups and enterprises to test innovative blockchain solutions (e.g., tokenized assets, smart insurance) in a controlled environment with relaxed regulatory requirements for a limited period (12-18 months). This fosters innovation while protecting consumers.

Legal Position on Crypto-Assets

It is crucial to clarify that this policy does not legalize or regulate speculative cryptocurrencies (like Bitcoin) or Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs).

  • Bangladesh Bank Jurisdiction: The regulation of virtual currencies, payments, and forex trading remains the exclusive domain of Bangladesh Bank.

  • BSEC Jurisdiction: The Bangladesh Securities and Exchange Commission (BSEC) retains authority over security tokens and digital asset investment schemes.

  • Focus: This policy is strictly focused on enterprise and government blockchain applications for service delivery, data integrity, and administrative efficiency.

Implementation Roadmap (2026-2030)

  • Phase 1 (Foundation - 2026): Establish the NDGA and NBTC. Deploy the initial NBI validator nodes at the National Data Center. Launch pilot projects for Digital Identity and Academic Credentials.

  • Phase 2 (Scale - 2027-2028): Expand the validator network to key ministries (Land, Health, Education). Integrate NBI with NRDEX. Launch the "Property Chain" pilot. Open the Regulatory Sandbox.

  • Phase 3 (Consolidation - 2029-2030): Achieve national coverage for land records and educational verification. Implement cross-chain interoperability with international trade networks (e.g., for cross-border logistics).

Conclusion

The National Blockchain Policy 2026 represents a paradigm shift from "trust in people" to "trust in code." By building a sovereign, privacy-preserving, and immutable infrastructure, Bangladesh is laying the rails for a transparent, corruption-free, and highly efficient digital future. This initiative will not only secure citizen data but also significantly reduce the cost of doing business, enhancing Bangladesh's position in the global digital economy.

5.2.6 National Green ICT Policy

The National Green ICT Policy 2026 establishes the governance framework to ensure that Bangladesh's digital transformation advances economic development without compromising environmental sustainability. As the government expands its digital footprint—through data centers, cloud infrastructure, and millions of connected devices—energy consumption and electronic waste are projected to rise exponentially. This policy operationalizes a "Sustainability-by-Design" approach, mandating energy efficiency, renewable integration, and circular economy practices across the public sector.

Vision and Strategic Objectives

Vision: A sustainable, secure, and interoperable Bangladesh where ICT systems deliver public value with minimal environmental harm, and where electronic waste is managed through circular economy principles.

Strategic Objectives (2026–2030):

  1. Decarbonize Digital Infrastructure: Achieve 30% renewable energy sourcing for public ICT infrastructure by 2028, scaling to 50% by 2041.

  2. Institutionalize Green Procurement: Transition from "lowest price" to Lifecycle Costing (LCC) for all major ICT procurements, ensuring durability and energy efficiency are valued over cheap, short-lived hardware.

  3. Build National E-waste Infrastructure: Establish a formal Take-back and Treatment system that integrates the informal sector while enforcing environmental standards.

  4. Secure IT Asset Disposition (ITAD): Ensure that end-of-life government devices are sanitized of sensitive citizen data before disposal, aligning environmental goals with data privacy (PDPO) requirements.

Key Policy Requirements

A. Green Data Centers & Cloud Infrastructure Government data centers are major energy consumers. To curb this, the policy mandates:

  • PUE Limits & Reporting: All government data centers must establish baseline Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) measurements by 2026 and achieve a 15% efficiency improvement by 2030.

  • Thermal Optimization: Adoption of ASHRAE TC 9.9 thermal guidelines, allowing wider temperature ranges to reduce cooling loads (which often account for 40% of total energy).

  • Renewable Integration: Mandatory disclosure of renewable energy procurement methods. The National Data Center (NDC) aims for ISO 50001 energy management certification by 2028.



B. Universal Charging Standard (The "One Cable" Rule) To tackle the 3,000-4,000 tonnes of e-waste generated annually from discarded chargers and cables (from ~35 million mobile imports), Bangladesh will align with international standards (IEC 62680 / ITU-T L.1000).

  • Mandate: All government-procured mobile devices must use USB Type-C with USB Power Delivery (USB-PD) by 2027. Laptops must transition by 2028 where technically feasible.

  • Unbundling: Procurement preferences will be given to "Charger Unbundled" options where compatible chargers are already available, reducing redundant accessory acquisition.

C. Green Public Procurement (GPP) The government will use its purchasing power to drive market transformation.

  • Lifecycle Costing (LCC): For ICT procurements exceeding BDT 50 Lakh, agencies must use LCC methodology, evaluating not just the purchase price but also operating energy costs and end-of-life disposal costs.

  • Sustainability Weighting: Tender evaluations shall allocate a minimum 10% weighting to environmental criteria (e.g., ENERGY STAR/EPEAT certification, repairability, extended warranty).

D. Secure IT Asset Disposition (ITAD) Bridging the gap between Green ICT and Data Privacy:

  • Data Sanitization: Before any government device (laptop, server, drive) enters the e-waste stream, it must undergo data sanitization aligned with NIST SP 800-88 guidelines.

  • Verification: Agencies must maintain a "Chain of Custody" document for retired assets. Devices that stored Restricted/Confidential data (under NDGO) require enhanced sanitization verification or physical destruction of storage media to prevent data leakage.

Institutional Governance

Implementation is overseen by a multi-tier governance model to ensure cross-sector coordination:

  1. National Green ICT Steering Committee (NGICESC): The apex body chaired by the Secretary, ICT Division, with co-chair from the Ministry of Environment (MoEFCC). It sets targets and resolves jurisdictional issues.

  2. Green ICT Implementation Unit (GIIU): A specialized unit within the ICT Division responsible for technical guidelines, the National Green ICT Dashboard, and monitoring PUE compliance.

  3. E-waste Regulatory Directorate: A designated wing within the Department of Environment (DoE) responsible for licensing treatment facilities and enforcing Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR).

E-Waste Management & Just Transition

Bangladesh generates ~500,000 tonnes of e-waste annually, handled largely by the informal sector under hazardous conditions. The policy promotes a "Just Transition":

  • Integration of Informal Sector: Rather than banning informal collectors, the policy establishes "Authorized Aggregator" models. Informal workers are trained, equipped with safety gear, and licensed to feed collected waste into formal recycling centers.

  • Target: Integrate 500 informal workers into formal operations by 2030, ensuring decent work standards and safety.

Implementation Roadmap (2026-2030)

Phase

Timeline

Key Actions

Foundation

2026

Publish Green ICT Procurement Guidelines; Establish GIIU; Baseline PUE for NDC.

Integration

2027

Mandatory USB-C for Govt Mobile Devices; Launch National Green ICT Dashboard; Enforce ITAD certification.

Scale

2028

30% Renewable Energy in Public ICT; Mandatory LCC for tenders >50 Lakh; Formalize 200 informal e-waste collectors.

Maturity

2030

15% PUE improvement across all govt data centers; Full circular economy integration for ICT assets.

Conclusion

The National Green ICT Policy ensures that Bangladesh’s digital growth is decoupled from environmental degradation. By standardizing ports, enforcing energy efficiency, and securing the disposal of digital assets, the government sets a precedent for the private sector, driving the nation toward a low-carbon, circular digital economy.

5.2.7 National Semiconductor Policy

Bangladesh’s Semiconductor Policy (2026–2030) establishes a national framework of binding directives, minimum technical requirements, and institutional coordination mechanisms to develop a credible semiconductor ecosystem—prioritising fabless design, verification/DFT services, packaging & reliability engineering, and OSAT as realistic entry points for 2026–2030, while deferring wafer fabrication until ecosystem maturity is proven.

Vision and Policy Mandate

Vision: To establish Bangladesh as a competitive, trusted, and sustainable participant in the global semiconductor value chain by 2030, leveraging Fabless Design, OSAT, and Trusted Electronics as primary growth engines.

Strategic Objectives: The policy mandates the achievement of the following national targets by 2030:

  • Export Sovereignty: Generate $1 Billion+ in annual export revenue.

  • Human Capital: Train and certify 10,000+ engineers under the BASICS framework.

  • Industrial Capability: Operationalize at least 5 Design Centers and 2 OSAT facilities.

  • Sustainability: Enforce 30% renewable energy adoption across all semiconductor facilities.

Institutional Governance Architecture

To ensure coordinated implementation, the policy establishes a robust regulatory structure:

  1. National Semiconductor Council: The apex decision-making body chaired by the Secretary, ICT Division. The Council is empowered to approve incentives, designate semiconductor zones, and coordinate cross-agency security protocols.

  2. Semiconductor Intellectual Property Office (SIPO): A specialized regulatory office established to protect Industrial IP (Topographies, Layout Designs). SIPO provides the legal certainty required for multinational corporations to offshore sensitive R&D to Bangladesh.

  3. Green ICT Implementation Unit (GIIU): Mandated to enforce Green Foundry Guidelines, ensuring rigorous water stewardship and chemical management compliance.

Core Policy Directives

A. The "Fabless First" Industrial Strategy The policy explicitly prioritizes high-value, lower-capital entry points over immediate wafer fabrication.

  • Fabless Design: Entities focusing on RTL design, Verification, and IP Block creation shall receive priority fiscal support.

  • OSAT (Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly & Test): The policy designates OSAT as a "High-Priority Industry," enabling duty-free import of testing equipment and specialized machinery.

  • Trusted Electronics: Manufacturing of secure modules (SIMs, Smart Cards, Biometric Sensors) for the National Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) is mandated to reduce reliance on non-sovereign supply chains.

B. Workforce Development: The BASICS Mandate The Bangladesh Skills Initiative for Chip Systems (BASICS) is established as the national workforce development program.

  • Curriculum Reform: Universities receiving public grants must modernize curricula to include VLSI, microelectronics, and material science.

  • Shared EDA Infrastructure: The policy mandates the creation of a "National EDA Cloud," providing students and startups with subsidized access to industry-standard tools (Cadence, Synopsys, Siemens).

C. Investment Framework: The 50:50 Co-Investment Model To de-risk the sector and ensure market discipline, the policy enforces a strict co-investment structure:

  • Public Capital: Allocated strictly for "Common Goods"—workforce training, shared labs (Clean Rooms), and R&D grants.

  • Private/FDI Capital: Required for commercial operations, equipment procurement, and scaling. This ensures that public funds are leveraged to catalyze, not replace, private investment.

Strategic Pillars and Security Protocols

1. Intellectual Property Protection Recognizing IP as the currency of the semiconductor industry, the policy extends legal protection to Layout Designs (Topographies) and trade secrets. SIPO is authorized to prosecute IP infringement with expedited judicial processes.

2. Security by Design All semiconductor facilities designated as Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) under the CSO must implement:

  • Zero-Trust Architecture: Network segmentation to protect design IP.

  • Secure Access Controls: Biometric authentication for sensitive zones, compliant with PDPO privacy standards.

3. Sustainability Directives Facilities must adhere to the National Green ICT Policy:

  • Renewable Energy: A roadmap to 100% renewable energy adoption by 2040.

  • Circular Economy: Mandatory recycling protocols for e-waste and chemical byproducts.

Implementation Roadmap

Phase

Timeline

Strategic Directive

Foundation

2026

Establish National Semiconductor Council and SIPO. Launch BASICS pilot.

Activation

2027

Operationalize SHQTC for hardware testing. Secure first anchor investment for Design Centers.

Acceleration

2028-29

Commission first OSAT facility. Achieve international certification for national labs.

Consolidation

2030

Full ecosystem operational. Feasibility study for Wafer Fabrication initiated.

Conclusion

The National Semiconductor Policy 2026 transforms the semiconductor sector from an aspirational goal into a regulated, strategic industry. By enforcing the BEAR framework (Biotech, Electronics, AI, Robotics) and adhering to strict governance standards, Bangladesh secures its technological sovereignty and economic resilience for the decades ahead.

5.2.8 Cross-Border Digital Trade Policy

The Cross-Border Digital Trade Policy aims to simplify and secure the import and export of goods and services through digital platforms while aligning Bangladesh's trade practices with global standards (WTO, WCO, UNESCAP). It emphasizes creating opportunities for small and medium entrepreneurs, expanding international market access, and ensuring safe, transparent, and efficient cross-border trade.

Industry Context and Challenges

Recent findings from the software and IT-enabled services (ITES) industry highlight critical bottlenecks that this policy seeks to address. Currently, many export-oriented software firms face significant hurdles in paying for essential foreign services—such as cloud hosting (AWS, Azure), software licenses, and digital marketing—due to restrictive outward remittance caps.

  • Operational Expense Gap: Industry data suggests that a growing software firm often needs to reinvest 30-40% of its revenue into foreign services to maintain operations and growth. However, current regulations limit outward remittance for such expenses (e.g., often capped at $30,000-$40,000 annually per card), which is insufficient for scaling companies.

  • Revenue Retention Abroad: To bypass these limits and avoid service disruptions, many companies are forced to retain a portion of their export earnings in foreign jurisdictions or use informal channels, leading to a discrepancy between actual export performance and officially recorded revenue repatriated to Bangladesh.

  • Double Taxation Issues: The lack of streamlined tax treaties often results in double taxation for digital services, further disincentivizing formal cross-border transactions.

Key Priorities

  • Trade Facilitation: Streamlined rules for cross-border B2B and B2C trade, simplified customs processes, dedicated logistics tracking, and digital trade zones near ports.

  • Payments and Regulation: Internationally connected payment systems, escrow services, and policy support for secure settlements under the Foreign Exchange Control Act. Specifically, this includes revising outward remittance limits for bona fide digital exporters to cover legitimate business expenses like cloud infrastructure and marketing.

  • Tax and Compliance: Clear VAT, tariff, and reporting guidelines, with APIs linking platforms and customs. Mechanisms to avoid double taxation on digital services will be prioritized.

  • Consumer Protection: Strengthened dispute resolution, after-sales service requirements, and strict enforcement of intellectual property laws.

  • Capacity Building: Training programs, institutional support for entrepreneurs, and promotional initiatives to brand Bangladeshi products globally.

  • Data and Monitoring: Systems for trade data collection, risk analysis, and interoperability to inform policy.

Vision and Strategic Objectives

Vision: To position Bangladesh as a competitive, trusted, and resilient hub for cross-border digital trade by enabling seamless payments, frictionless trade documentation, and secure data flows.

Strategic Objectives (2026–2030):

  • Export Growth: Increase IT/ITES exports from USD 1.5 billion to USD 5 billion by 2030.

  • Paperless Trade: Reduce average trade documentation processing time by 50%.

  • Financial Integration: Enable international payment gateway access for qualified exporters and freelancers.

  • Trust & Compliance: Achieve Trust Mark certification for at least 500 digital exporters.

Core Policy Pillars

A. Modernizing Foreign Exchange Regulations Current foreign exchange rules, designed for traditional goods trade, stifle digital exporters. The policy introduces three critical reforms:

  1. Revenue-Linked Foreign Expense Caps: Replacing the flat annual remittance cap (e.g., $40,000) with tiered limits based on export turnover. This allows growing firms to pay for essential cloud infrastructure, software licenses, and digital marketing required to scale global operations.

  2. Foreign-Currency Retention Windows: Permitting exporters to hold a portion of earnings in foreign currency accounts (or offshore for defined periods) to hedge against exchange rate volatility and meet operational expenses without forced conversion losses.

  3. Simplified Freelancer Settlement: Streamlining compliance for low-value export receipts, allowing platform-verified invoices as sufficient proof for inward remittance, reducing the administrative burden on micro-exporters.

B. Trade Facilitation & Paperless Customs To accelerate cross-border e-commerce, the policy mandates:

  • Paperless Trade: Recognition of secure electronic signatures and digital records for customs processing, aligning with the UNESCAP Cross-Border Paperless Trade Agreement.

  • Digital Trade Lanes: Fast-track customs clearance for low-risk, low-value e-commerce shipments, supporting the growth of direct-to-consumer (D2C) exports.

  • API Integration: Direct integration between major e-commerce platforms and customs systems for automated duty calculation and declaration.

C. Cross-Border Data Flows & Safe Corridors Recognizing that data is the lifeblood of digital trade, the policy establishes a balanced approach:

  • "Data Free Flow with Trust": Allowing cross-border data transfer for legitimate commercial purposes (e.g., BPO operations, SaaS delivery) while enforcing privacy safeguards.

  • Safe Corridors: Designating approved data transfer channels for priority markets (e.g., EU, USA) where data protection standards are equivalent, facilitating BPO and ITES contracts without complex individual approvals.

D. Trust Services & Certification To build the "Brand Bangladesh" in the global digital market:

  • Trust Mark Certification: A national certification scheme signaling that an exporter meets international standards for privacy (PDPO), security (ISO 27001), and service quality. This tiered system (Baseline, Assured, High Assurance) helps Bangladeshi firms prove their credibility to foreign clients.

  • Platform Accountability: Clear guidelines for cross-border e-commerce platforms regarding consumer protection, dispute resolution, and returns management.

Addressing Structural Constraints

The policy directly tackles long-standing industry grievances:

  • Overseas Subsidiaries: Extending the right to establish overseas subsidiaries to all IT/ITES exporters meeting revenue thresholds, removing the arbitrary age limit that previously restricted established firms from expanding globally.

  • Incentive Rationalization: Simplifying the documentation required to claim the IT/ITES export cash incentive, ensuring that genuine digital exports are not misclassified as general remittances due to bureaucratic hurdles.

Institutional Governance

Implementation is coordinated by the Ministry of Commerce, supported by:

  • Bangladesh Bank: For payment and foreign exchange reforms.

  • NBR/Customs: For paperless trade and incentive administration.

  • ICT Division: For technical standards, Trust Mark certification, and exporter capacity building.

Implementation Roadmap (2026-2030)

Phase

Timeline

Key Reform Actions

Foundation

2026

Issue circulars for Revenue-Linked Expense Caps and Freelancer Settlement. Launch Trust Mark consultation.

Expansion

2027-28

Deploy National Digital Trade Gateway. Establish Safe Corridors for key markets. Full e-KYC integration.

Maturity

2029-30

Achieve mutual recognition of electronic trade documents with trading partners. Full paperless trade capability.

Conclusion

The Cross-Border Digital Trade Policy transforms the regulatory environment from a barrier into an enabler. By aligning foreign exchange rules with the realities of the digital economy and digitizing trade facilitation, Bangladesh empowers its entrepreneurs to compete on a level playing field in the global market.

The policy introduces a central coordination committee and expert bodies to oversee implementation, risk management, and global alignment, ensuring Bangladesh becomes a competitive player in cross-border digital commerce.





5.2.9 Digital Skills and Workforce Development Policy

Bangladesh's digital economy is expanding rapidly, but workforce skills remain unevenly distributed across regions, education levels, and sectors. Existing initiatives are fragmented and lack a legal framework to ensure continuity, accountability, and alignment with market demand. The National Digital Skills and Workforce Development Policy 2026 establishes a unified national framework to build the human capital required for Bangladesh's digital transformation. It addresses the critical "Skills Mismatch" that currently constrains the growth of the ICT sector, ensuring that workforce development is demand-driven, inclusive, and aligned with global standards. The policy shifts from fragmented, project-based training to a permanent, institutionally embedded capacity-building model.

Vision and Strategic Objectives

Vision: A digitally capable Bangladesh where every citizen possesses the skills to participate safely and productively in the digital economy, and where a world-class ICT workforce drives innovation, exports, and public service excellence.

Strategic Objectives (2026–2030):

  • Universal Literacy: Train 20 million students in foundational digital literacy and coding.

  • Workforce Readiness: Certify 1 million youth in market-ready freelancing and vocational digital skills.

  • Advanced Specialization: Produce 100,000 graduates with advanced competencies in AI, Data Science, Cybersecurity, and Cloud Computing.

  • Public Sector Capacity: Train 50,000 government officials in digital governance and AI ethics.

  • Inclusion: Ensure at least 40% female participation in all publicly funded training programs.

The 5-Track Skills Highway

The policy organizes skills development into five distinct tracks targeting specific population segments:

Track 1: Foundational Skills (Schools & Early Learning)

  • Target: Primary and secondary students (ages 6–16).

  • Focus: Integrating digital literacy, online safety, and basic coding/computational thinking into the national school curriculum. Content will be Bangla-first to ensure accessibility.

Track 2: Vocational & Youth Employment (TVET & Freelancers)

  • Target: School leavers, unemployed youth (16–25), and madrasa students.

  • Focus: Market-ready skills for immediate employment (graphic design, web dev, digital marketing).

  • Madrasa Track: Specialized training in Arabic/Farsi/Bangla localization and content moderation to target the GCC outsourcing market.

Track 3: Higher Education & Advanced Tech (Deep Tech)

  • Target: University students.

  • Focus: "Deep Tech" competencies—AI, Blockchain, Semiconductors, Cybersecurity.

  • Mechanism: Establishing Innovation Accelerators at major universities (BUET, DU, etc.) and mandating 6-month industry internships.

Track 4: Lifelong Learning & Workforce Reskilling

  • Target: Existing workforce, SME owners, returning migrants, women at home.

  • Focus: Reskilling for the digital economy—e-commerce management for SMEs, digital remittance tools for migrants, and micro-work platforms for home-based female workers.

Track 5: Public Sector Leadership

  • Target: Civil servants, judiciary, law enforcement.

  • Focus: Competencies in Digital Governance, Data Privacy (PDPO), Cybersecurity Hygiene, and AI Ethics.

Institutional Architecture & Reform

The policy mandates a clear division of labor to prevent duplication:

  1. Policy Authority: ICT Division sets the strategic direction and funding priorities.

  2. Standards Body: National Skills Development Authority (NSDA) is the sole authority for setting competency standards, accrediting training providers, and managing the National Skills Registry.

  3. Grassroots Delivery: Department of ICT (DoICT) is reformed into the primary training delivery arm, utilizing its network of Upazila and District offices to deliver foundational and intermediate training nationwide.

  4. Advanced Certification: Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC) and BKIICT focus on advanced, specialized certifications and industry partnerships.

Key Enablers

  • National Skills Registry: A centralized database tracking all verified certifications linked to the OneID system, allowing employers to instantly verify a candidate's credentials.

  • Freelancer.gov.bd: A unified platform for freelancer registration, training enrollment, and the issuance of the Freelancer ID Card, which facilitates banking and social protection access.

  • Green Digital Skills: Embedding sustainability competencies (energy efficiency, e-waste management) across all tracks to support the Green ICT Policy.

Financing & Accountability

  • Outcome-Based Funding: Public funding for training providers will be linked to verified outcomes (certification rates, employment placement) rather than just enrollment numbers.

  • Industry Co-Investment: Encouraging private sector participation in curriculum design and funding for specialized tracks (e.g., vendor-specific certifications like AWS, Cisco).

Implementation Roadmap (2026-2030)

Phase

Timeline

Key Actions

Foundation

2026

Publish National Digital Skills Taxonomy; Launch National Skills Registry; Pilot Madrasa Track.

Scale

2027-28

Roll out training via DoICT Upazila Hubs; Integration with Freelancer.gov.bd; Green Skills modules mandatory.

Maturity

2029-30

Full nationwide coverage; International mutual recognition of NSDA certifications; 1M freelancers active.

Conclusion

The Digital Skills Framework moves Bangladesh from ad-hoc training to a structured, lifecycle-based human capital development model. By aligning training with industry needs and global standards, it ensures that the "Demographic Dividend" is translated into a "Digital Dividend," powering the nation's economic growth.

5.3 Regulatory Frameworks

Beyond strategic policies, Bangladesh's digital transformation requires sector-specific regulatory frameworks addressing emerging digital applications. The following frameworks establish governance structures for sectors with significant growth potential and consumer protection requirements.

5.3.1 National Digital Social Protection Framework

The Digital Social Protection Framework establishes systems for implementing Digital Social Protection Delivery Systems (DSPDS) in Bangladesh, transforming how social safety net benefits reach vulnerable populations.

Vision: Ensure that every eligible Bangladeshi can access social protection benefits with dignity, efficiency, and respect for their rights and well-being.

Key Components: Dynamic Social Registry (dSR) enabling on-demand registration; Beneficiary Operations Management Systems (BOMS) for programme-specific operations; Integrated Beneficiary Registry (IBR) for cross-programme coordination; Multi-Programme Multi-Provider Payments Platform progressing toward G2P 4.0 architecture.

Guiding Principles: Human-centred design; dynamic inclusion enabling registration when needed; interoperability by design; collect-once, use-many with governance; privacy by design; explainability and due process; resilience and continuity.

Indicator

2026 Target

2030 Target

Beneficiaries in dynamic registry

10 million

40 million

Programmes on unified payment platform

20

100+

Digital payment coverage

60%

95%

Grievance resolution within 30 days

70%

90%

Exclusion error rate

< 10%

< 5%

5.3.2 National Electronic Games Industry Regulatory Framework

The Electronic Games Industry Framework establishes comprehensive governance for Bangladesh to participate competitively in the global games ecosystem while protecting citizens from potential harms. The global games market exceeds USD 200 billion annually, presenting significant export and employment opportunities.

Vision: Position Bangladesh as a competitive participant in the global electronic games ecosystem through domestic development, export-oriented production, and robust consumer protection.

Key Provisions: Bangladesh Game Certification Board (BGCB) for age rating and content classification; mandatory probability disclosure for randomised monetisation (loot boxes); dark pattern prohibitions protecting consumers from manipulative design; parental controls and spending limits for minor protection; Game Process Outsourcing (GPO) export incentives.

Consumer Protection: Fundamental consumer rights including informed purchase decisions, access to support, and data portability. Prohibition of predatory monetisation targeting minors. Age assurance requirements proportionate to content risk.

5.3.3 National E-Waste Management Framework

The E-Waste Management Framework establishes comprehensive regulatory, operational, and technical requirements for managing electronic waste in Bangladesh, implementing Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) principles that align incentives with environmental outcomes.

Strategic Linkage: This Framework operates as a companion document to the National Green ICT Policy, together forming an integrated approach to lifecycle sustainability in the ICT sector.

Extended Producer Responsibility: Producers bear financial responsibility for end-of-life management of products they place on market, implemented through Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) pooling resources for collection and treatment infrastructure.

IT Asset Disposition (ITAD): Government organisations shall dispose of IT assets only through NDGA-accredited ITAD service providers meeting both data security (aligned with CSO/PDPO) and environmental standards. Data sanitisation must achieve NIST 800-88 equivalent standards.

Just Transition: Formalisation of the informal e-waste sector protecting existing livelihoods while improving working conditions, targeting integration of 500 informal workers into formal operations by 2030.

2028 Target

2030 Target
