5.13 Chapter Summary

This chapter outlined nine strategic policies and three regulatory frameworks forming the foundation of Bangladesh's digital sovereignty and innovation ecosystem. Together, these instruments:

  • Establish Ethical Principles: Ensure digital sovereignty through vendor neutrality, source code ownership, and AI ethics with risk-based oversight.

  • Modernize Infrastructure & Trade: Enable drone usage as a shared public resource, facilitate cross-border digital trade aligned with international standards, and promote cloud sovereignty.

  • Build Trust & Security: Mandate open source for transparency, enforce secure coding practices, and implement blockchain for immutable public records.

  • Drive Sustainability & Skills: Institutionalize green ICT practices to reduce carbon footprints and build a future-ready workforce through inclusive digital skills development.

  • Foster Industrial Growth: Create a strategic roadmap for the semiconductor industry, positioning Bangladesh in the global deep-tech value chain.

These policies collectively ensure Bangladesh's digital transformation is technologically advanced, secure, sustainable, inclusive, and aligned with global best practices while preserving national interests.





---

End of Chapter 5





6. Institutional reform

6.0 Introduction

Bangladesh's digital transformation cannot succeed without fundamental reform of the institutions responsible for driving and sustaining change. The current institutional landscape within the ICT Division and its affiliated agencies reflects legacy structures that were designed for an earlier era of standalone projects and siloed operations. As Bangladesh moves toward an integrated Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) ecosystem aligned with the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDA), institutional mandates, capacities, and coordination mechanisms must be modernized.

This chapter outlines comprehensive reforms across five key institutions: the Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC), the Department of ICT (DoICT), the National Cybersecurity Agency (NCSA), Access to Information (a2i), and the flagship ICT projects requiring redesign. Each reform agenda is justified by identified gaps, aligned with the overall strategy vision, and supported by clear Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to measure progress.



6.1 Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC) Restructuring

6.1.1 Rationale for Reform

The Bangladesh Computer Council serves as the apex technical body for government ICT initiatives. However, its current operational model is characterized by fragmented project execution, inadequate human resource development programs, and limited regional presence. As Bangladesh transitions toward a knowledge-driven digital economy, BCC must evolve from a project-executing agency into a strategic enabler of national digital infrastructure, workforce development, and data governance.

6.1.2 Digital Infrastructure Backbone

National Service Bus (NSB) Integration: BCC will strengthen the National Service Bus in full integration with the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDA). This ensures interoperability across all government systems, compliance with DPI standards, and secure integration of Digital Identity systems. The NSB will serve as the middleware backbone enabling ministries and agencies to share data through standardized APIs without duplicating infrastructure.

Info-Sarker-3 Network Upgrade: The Info-Sarker network, which connects government offices nationwide down to the union level, is undergoing a comprehensive reform to enhance resilience, redundancy, and nationwide coverage. While the network serves as the country's critical digital artery, historical limitations in monitoring and bandwidth allocation are being addressed through the deployment of SNMP-ready infrastructure and modern Network Management Systems (NMS). These technical upgrades are critical to preventing data manipulation and ensuring real-time visibility into network utilization.

Strategic PPP Contract Reform (The February 2026 Amendment): In a landmark move to protect national interests, the Interim Government successfully concluded a six-month renegotiation of the Public-Private Partnership (PPP) agreement for Info-Sarker-3 on February 4, 2026. This reform fundamentally restructures the relationship between the Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC) and the private project companies (SPVs formed by Summit Communications Limited and Fiber@Home Limited), transitioning from a restrictive, fixed-revenue model to a transparent, progressive, and state-friendly framework.

  • Financial Restructuring (The Slab-Based Model): The previous contract limited the government's revenue share to a flat 10%, regardless of the project's financial success. This static model failed to reflect the massive public investment (approx. Tk 1,200 crore) and allowed private operators to maximize profits while the state realized near-zero returns due to opaque reporting. The revised contract introduces a Progressive Revenue Sharing Model based on monthly gross revenue slabs:

    • Tier 1: For monthly revenue up to BDT 5 Crore, the government share is increased to 15%.

    • Tier 2: For revenue between BDT 5 Crore and 10 Crore, the share rises to 20%.

    • Tier 3: For revenue exceeding BDT 10 Crore, the government claims 30%.

    • Additionally, the revenue share from active devices at District and Upazila levels has been increased from 10% to 15%, ensuring the state benefits proportionally from network utilization.

  • Unlocking National Assets (Dark Fiber & Fiber Bank): Previously, the state-built fiber infrastructure was contractually "locked" within the project, preventing broader national use. The reform explicitly authorizes the leasing of unused "Dark Fiber" cores to Mobile Network Operators (MNOs), ISPs, and the newly established National Fiber Bank (NFB).

    • Strategic Reservation: The new agreement mandates the reservation of specific fiber cores for future government bandwidth expansion, emergency national use, and connecting priority public projects, ensuring that national sovereignty over the infrastructure is never compromised.

    • Investment Security: A clear legal provision has been added ensuring that the operationalization of the National Fiber Bank cannot be used as grounds by private partners to cancel or unilaterally modify the PPP contract, thereby securing long-term stability for both state assets and private investors.

This structural overhaul transforms Info-Sarker-3 from a fiscal liability into a self-sustaining national asset, ensuring that the project contributes equitably to the public exchequer while facilitating affordable broadband expansion.

6.1.3 Human Resource Development

Frontier Technology Training Programs: BCC will launch comprehensive training and certification programs covering AI, blockchain, IoT, cloud computing, and quantum computing fundamentals. These programs will be developed in collaboration with international universities and global certification bodies to ensure recognition and relevance in the global ICT market.

6.1.4 Decentralization & Regional Empowerment

BCC's regional offices will be empowered as innovation hubs and service delivery centers, providing localized digital solutions, training, and monitoring of ICT service quality. Their mandate will expand to include certification, skill-building, and entrepreneurship support to strengthen grassroots-level digital ecosystems. This decentralization is essential for achieving the vision of inclusive digital transformation that reaches all divisions of Bangladesh.

6.1.5 National Data & Documentation Ecosystem

National Document Wallet / Digital Locker: BCC will establish a secure digital storage and authentication system for citizen documents. This Digital Locker will integrate with the National ID system and enable citizens to access verified copies of their certificates, licenses, and official documents from any location.

Government Source Code Repository: To ensure government ownership of mission-critical software and reduce vendor lock-in, BCC will create and manage a centralized Government Source Code Repository. This repository will enforce compliance with national digital architecture guidelines, cybersecurity standards, and data protection protocols.

National Data Analytics Platform (NDAP): BCC will build a National Data Analytics Platform to support evidence-based policymaking and sectoral planning. This platform will aggregate anonymized data from across government systems to enable predictive analytics, trend identification, and resource optimization.

6.1.6 Enterprise & Governance Readiness

Government Resource Planning (GRP): BCC will ensure nationwide adoption of Government Resource Planning across all ministries and agencies, enabling unified financial, HR, and asset management systems. This standardization is critical for reducing duplication, improving accountability, and enabling cross-ministry coordination.

Interoperability Standards: BCC will develop and enforce policies and standards promoting interoperability, data sovereignty, and efficiency in all government digital systems. These standards will align with NDGA guidelines and international best practices.

6.1.6 Strategic Infrastructure & Resource Optimization

BCC is undertaking a critical "Hard Reset" of the National Data Center (NDC) to address legacy resource constraints and establish a sovereign cloud backbone.

  • Resource Optimization: Immediate deployment of 10 new computing nodes/servers into the NDC cloud platform, alongside a rigorous audit to reclaim unused resources from client institutions. This optimization has resolved initial capacity crises.

  • Infrastructure Modernization: A comprehensive review of hardware/software lifecycles is underway. The "National Data Center Capacity Augmentation" project is being formulated to replace obsolete platforms and meet future demand.

  • Cloud Sovereignty: Transitioning from reliance on foreign cloud providers to a "Sovereign Cloud" model. BCC is leading the "Secure Cloud" initiative to host all critical government data domestically, ensuring data privacy and national security.

  • Disaster Recovery (DR): Upgrading the Jashore Disaster Recovery Center with active-active replication capabilities to ensure 99.99% uptime for critical national services like NID and Passport.

6.1.7 Cyber Defense & Security Operations

BCC is operationalizing the National Security Operations Center (NSOC) to move from reactive defense to proactive threat intelligence.

  • 24/7 Monitoring: Full-scale monitoring of Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) has commenced.

  • Open Source Security Tools: Deployment of cost-effective, open-source security visibility tools (SIEM/SOAR) to detect and mitigate threats in real-time without expensive proprietary vendor lock-in.

  • Rohingya Database Handover: Secure transfer of the Rohingya database management to the Ministry of Home Affairs to streamline national security operations.

6.1.8 Emerging Technology & Innovation Initiatives

BCC is evolving from a support agency to an R&D powerhouse, leveraging frontier technologies for public good.

  • National GPU Cloud: Repurposing unused GPU resources from Hi-Tech Parks into a centralized "National GPU Cluster." This facility will be accessible to researchers and startups for AI model training and high-performance computing.

  • AI-Driven Solutions:

    • "Kagoj.AI": Development of an AI-powered document analysis tool to automate government file processing.

    • "July Font": Creation of a standardized, open-source Bangla font to ensure digital inclusivity.

  • Battery-Rickshaw Management System: Successful piloting of a digital registration system for battery-operated rickshaws in Dhaka North/South City Corporations, registering over 23,900 drivers to bring order to urban transport.

  • Unified Ticketing System: Developing a single interoperable ticketing platform integrating Railway, BRTC buses, and Biman Bangladesh Airlines to facilitate seamless multimodal travel.

6.1.9 Institutional Capacity & Governance

  • Recruitment Reform: Overhauling recruitment rules to allow lateral entry of industry experts (CTOs, Architects) into BCC, breaking the bureaucratic ceiling for technical talent.

  • "July Corner": Establishing a dedicated memorial corner and training programs for the "July Warriors" to honor the spirit of the student-led revolution.



6.1.10 Infrastructure Expansion

National Data Center (NDC) Expansion: BCC will coordinate the allocation of suitable land in Dhaka Metro for NDC expansion, ensuring scalability for future cloud, AI, and big data needs. Current capacity constraints in BCC's Nutanix and Huawei data centers will be addressed through this expansion.

Green ICT Practices: All data center upgrades will incorporate green ICT practices including renewable energy integration, efficient cooling systems, and carbon-neutral operations. Bangladesh commits to ensuring at least 30% renewable energy sourcing in public sector ICT infrastructure by 2028.

6.2 Department of ICT (DoICT) Reform

6.2.1 Rationale for Reform

The Department of ICT maintains the most extensive grassroots presence of any ICT institution, with officers stationed at every district and upazila. However, this network has been underutilized for systematic digital capacity building. The reform aims to transform DoICT into the primary training and capacity-building arm of the ICT Division, ensuring that digital literacy and skills development reach every corner of Bangladesh.

6.2.2 Strategic Initiatives & Progress Updates

To achieve this transformation, DoICT has executed a series of structural and operational reforms ("Hard Reset"):

1. Specialization via Subject-Matter Pools

  • Action: Moving away from generalist roles, DoICT has restructured its technical workforce into 04 Specialized Pools based on expertise:

    1. Cyber Security Pool

    2. Software Development Pool

    3. Operation & Maintenance Pool

    4. Human Resource Development (HRD) Pool

  • Impact: This ensures that the right officer is deployed for the right task—e.g., a cyber-expert handling security audits, while a developer manages software projects. This specialization is now central to all postings, deputations, and training decisions. This initiative fosters a coordinated and supportive work environment at both policy and implementation levels, significantly increasing efficiency and accountability.

2. Field-Level Digital Governance (D-Nothi & Portals)

  • Action: Upazila-level ICT Officers have been formally designated as "Office Admins" for the D-Nothi (Digital Filing) system and National Portal management.

  • Progress: They are now responsible for overseeing the updating of 25,000+ government web portals and ensuring the smooth operation of digital filing in local administration. This includes regular updates to portals and monitoring of digital file management.

  • Impact: This decentralization has drastically reduced administrative delays and ensured accountability at the grassroots level. It has increased the speed and efficiency of administrative activities through digital file management.

3. Cyber Security Helpdesks

  • Action: In collaboration with the National Cybersecurity Agency (NCSA), DoICT has established "Cyber Security Helpdesks" at District and Upazila levels based on an MOU signed on November 09, 2025.

  • Function: These helpdesks provide immediate support to citizens and local government offices for cyber incidents (hacking, harassment, fraud) and implement standard operating procedures (SOPs) for digital safety prepared by NCSA.

  • Impact: Taking cyber defense from the capital to the community. This ensures faster incident detection, reporting, and management, increasing public awareness about cybersecurity.

4. Freelancer & Entrepreneur Support

  • Action: Creation of a dynamic database of freelancers and IT entrepreneurs at the district level. DoICT is organizing mentorship programs, workshops, and "Freelancer Summits" to connect local talent with global markets. The National Freelancers Registration Platform (freelancers.gov.bd) has been launched (January 13, 2026), and ID cards are being issued (3708 cards issued as of Jan 25, 2026).

  • Synergy: Integration with the Freelancer ID system to ensure legitimate professionals receive banking and social recognition. This includes synergy with BHTPA to provide co-working spaces or incubation facilities for LEDP-trained freelancers and entrepreneurs in Software Technology Parks.

  • LEDP Project: The proposed Learning, Earning & Entrepreneurship Development (LEDP) project (2026-2028) aims to train 1,07,320 youth in ICT and freelancing to create entrepreneurship and income opportunities.

5. Administrative Strengthening

  • Action: Recruitment process for 497 Office Support Staff positions has been completed to strengthen the administrative structure, especially at the field level. Appointment letters have been sent.

  • Impact: Addressing long-standing manpower shortages in field offices, increasing the speed and efficiency of administrative activities.

6. Surokkha Platform & Office Management

  • Surokkha: The surokkha.gov.bd platform is live, enabling vaccination card downloads for compliance in foreign employment.

  • Smart Office Management System (SOMS): Implementation of SOMS (soms.gov.bd) across DoICT and other ICT Division agencies. New modules for transfer applications, meeting nominations, and lab/office inspections have been developed. Training has been provided to officials.

7. Specialized Training for Officials

  • Action: Providing specialized training to officials on Cyber Security (VAPT), Freelancing ToT (Graphics Design & Digital Marketing with AI), and Operation & Maintenance.

  • Progress: 120 officials trained in Cyber Security (VAPT), 100 in SOMS, and 100 planned for Operation & Maintenance.

6.2.3 Accelerating the EDC Project (2nd Revised DPP)

DoICT is the implementing agency for the Establishing Digital Connectivity (EDC) project. Following the cancellation of the Chinese loan component due to high interest rates, the project has been restructured (2nd Revised DPP approved by PIC on Jan 25, 2026) to focus on verifiable outputs:

  • Connectivity: Providing 1,09,244 broadband connections to end-users (schools, colleges, land offices, etc.) nationwide.

  • Infrastructure: Construction of 400 Upazila Seva & ICT Training Centers.

    • Status: 100 Centers were inaugurated on January 26, 2026 by the Special Assistant to the Chief Advisor. Construction is ongoing in 189 upazilas and is expected to be completed within the next 6 months. Tenders are being processed for the remaining 97 (24 invited, rest in process).

  • Specialized Labs: Establishment of 20 High-Tech Labs (17 VLSI & AI, 1 HPC, 1 PKI, 1 Forensic) to build deep-tech capacity.

  • ICT Tower: Construction of a 12-story Information Technology Building.

  • Network Monitoring System (NMS): A Central NMS is being procured to monitor all connections in real-time. Already, 49,000 existing connections have been integrated into the monitoring framework to stop billing fraud. A technical committee has been formed to oversee this.

  • School of Future & Digital Labs:

    • Python Training: 27,000 students from School of Future institutions trained in Python programming.

    • Robotics Corners: 300 Robotics Corners established in School of Future institutions with drones, VR headsets, 3D printers, etc.

    • LMS Training: 8,300 teachers from Digital Lab institutions trained on Learning Management Systems.

    • Frontier Technology ToT: Ongoing ToT for 1200 teachers on Frontier Technologies.

6.2.4 Expanded Training Mandate

Selected ICT-related courses will be standardized and approved by the National Skills Development Authority (NSDA), ensuring national recognition and quality assurance. DoICT officers at district and upazila levels will deliver these programs in schools, colleges, and madrasas, reaching populations currently excluded from formal ICT training pathways.

Advanced certifications from global providers including Microsoft, AWS, and CISCO will be integrated gradually. DoICT will facilitate partnerships with these global certifying bodies to ensure that Bangladeshi citizens can obtain internationally recognized credentials without traveling abroad or paying prohibitive fees.

6.2.5 Institutional Strengthening

Grassroots Capacity Building: DoICT will be formally designated as the primary grassroots training and capacity-building institution of the ICT Division. This mandate clarifies roles and prevents duplication with other agencies.

Staff Development: Continuous capacity-building will be provided for existing DoICT staff to ensure they can deliver modern curricula effectively. Training of Trainers (ToT) programs will be mandatory for all field officers.

Training Resource Hubs: Training Resource Hubs will be established at divisional and district levels to support curriculum delivery, provide digital content, and enable teacher upskilling.

In-House Technical Cadre: DoICT will actively cultivate a specialized pool of technical experts within its own ranks to reduce dependency on external vendors. Resource people and officers with technical aptitude will be trained and deployed as software developers, system administrators, and cybersecurity experts. This internal workforce will handle routine software maintenance, minor feature developments, and first-line cyber defense, ensuring faster response times and significantly lowering long-term operational costs previously spent on vendor support contracts.

6.2.6 Curriculum Innovation & Local Integration

DoICT will introduce a tiered learning model to meet learners at their current level and provide clear progression pathways:

Foundation Level: Digital literacy for students and community members covering basic computer operations, internet safety, and online services.

Intermediate Level: E-commerce, freelancing, coding fundamentals, and data entry skills to enable economic participation in the digital economy.

Advanced Level: Cybersecurity, AI basics, cloud computing, and IoT fundamentals for future workforce readiness and specialized career paths.

All curricula will be available in Bangla with accessibility features for rural and marginalized communities. Content will be optimized for low-bandwidth environments common in remote areas.

6.2.7 Future Roadmap & Strategic Priorities

To sustain this momentum, DoICT is focusing on:

  • NMS Implementation: Completing the procurement of the Central NMS to ensure 100% uptime visibility for all government internet connections and ensuring service quality.

  • School of Future (SoF): Shifting focus from mere equipment supply to Sustainable Maintenance and continuous Training of Trainers (ToT) for teachers in Robotics, IoT, and Frontier Technologies to make the program more dynamic.

  • Cloud Sovereignty: Migrating all local government applications to the National Data Center (NDC) cloud infrastructure to ensure data sovereignty and security.

  • Alternative Financing: Securing funds to complete the remaining EDC components (Upazila Centers, Specialized Labs) through alternative financing following the exit of foreign loan partners (China).

  • Skill Development Synergy: Coordinating training modules of the EDC project with the Directorate's own training programs.

6.2.8 Monitoring & Accountability

National Digital Learning Dashboard: DoICT will implement a real-time dashboard tracking enrollment, completion, and job-placement outcomes across all training programs. This transparency enables evidence-based program improvement and accountability.

Performance-Based Incentives: DoICT trainers and officers will receive performance-based incentives tied to verified training outcomes, including learner certification rates and employment outcomes.

6.2.9 Digital Service Integration

Beyond training, DoICT offices will be equipped to support local digital service delivery including assistance with e-services, online applications, and grievance systems. Every DoICT office will function as a community digital help desk, ensuring that citizens who face barriers to online services can receive in-person support.

6.2.10 Public-Private Partnerships

DoICT will establish partnerships with tech companies, industry associations, and freelancing platforms to align training with actual market demand. Internship and apprenticeship programs developed through industry collaboration will provide practical experience and employment pathways for program graduates.

6.2.11 Sustainability & Future-Readiness

DoICT reforms will be integrated into the national digital vision as a permanent institution, not a time-bound project. Blended learning models combining offline instruction with online platforms and mobile-based microlearning will ensure scalability and resilience against disruptions.

6.3 National Cybersecurity Agency (NCSA) Reform

6.3.1 Rationale for Reform

As Bangladesh's digital footprint expands through increased e-governance, digital payments, and online services, the attack surface for cyber threats grows proportionally. The National Cybersecurity Agency must evolve from a reactive incident-response entity to a proactive defender capable of anticipating threats, protecting critical infrastructure, and building national cyber resilience. This reform is essential for the effective implementation of the Cyber Safety Ordinance, 2025.

6.3.2 Strategic Initiatives & Progress Updates

1. Legislative and Administrative Reformation

To restore public trust and ensure a transparent legal framework, the NCSA has executed significant legislative overhauls:

  • Repeal and Replacement: The controversial "Cyber Security Act, 2023" was repealed. Following extensive stakeholder consultations, the new "Cyber Security Ordinance, 2025" was gazetted on May 21, 2025.

  • Regulatory Framework:

    • Rules 2026: The "Cyber Security Rules, 2026" have been drafted following workshops (Jan 19–20, 2026) and are being processed for approval.

    • Strategy 2026-30: The "Cyber Security Strategy 2026-30" draft is ready for stakeholder consultation (Target: March 2026).

  • Administrative Status: On September 21, 2025, the NCSA was officially gazetted as an "Attached Department" of the ICT Division to streamline administrative decision-making.

  • Recruitment: Recruitment rules have been sent to the Public Service Commission (PSC), and 70 temporary revenue-sector posts have been approved to build a dedicated workforce (Target: June 2026).

2. Infrastructure and Technical Capacity Building

The agency is establishing state-of-the-art infrastructure to monitor and neutralize threats in real-time:

  • National Security Operation Center (NSOC): Tender evaluation is ongoing to establish the NSOC, which will serve as the central nerve center for nationwide threat monitoring (Target: June 2026).

  • NCERT Enhancement: A project is underway to significantly upgrade the National Computer Emergency Response Team (NCERT), including the establishment of a National Digital Forensic Lab and the procurement of advanced OSINT and Fact-Checking tools.

  • Digital Forensic Standardization: To ensure international standards (ISO/IEC 17025), a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for accrediting forensic labs has been drafted. A technical team comprising experts from BUET, Dhaka University, and MIST is being formed to assess labs operated by CID, PBI, and ACC.

3. Operational Monitoring and Incident Response

The NCSA has operationalized rigorous monitoring mechanisms to protect national assets:

  • CIRRS Deployment: The Cyber Incident Reporting & Response System (CIRRS) has been developed to centralize complaint handling and reporting. It is currently under testing at SQTC (Target: Jan 2026).

  • Threat Intelligence: In FY 2025, the agency (via BGD e-GOV CIRT) issued:

    • 283 Cyber Threat Intelligence Reports.

    • 147 National Data Center Netflow Alerts.

    • 23 Cyber Sensor Reports regarding risks to Bangladesh Bank and BCC.

    • 16 Security Advisories and 3 Threat Landscape reports.

  • Audits: IT Audits for three (3) critical systems have been completed, with three more ongoing.

4. Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) Protection

Recognizing the vulnerability of strategic assets, the NCSA has intensified its focus on CIIs:

  • Assessment Framework: As per the decision of the National Cyber Security Council's first meeting (held on Dec 28, 2025, chaired by the Chief Adviser), a framework for assessing the cyber maturity of government and private institutions has been finalized. Automated assessment software is scheduled for release by January 31, 2026.

  • CII List Update: The list of CIIs is being updated under Section 16(5) of the Ordinance, with the Directorate of Posts being processed for inclusion.

  • Maturity Assessment: A "Cyber Maturity Model Assessment" has been conducted for all existing CIIs, and five dedicated teams have been formed to handle incident responses within these institutions.

5. Content Control and Financial Sector Security

A zero-tolerance policy has been adopted against digital financial crime and online gambling:

  • Online Gambling: The agency has referred 1,266 web portals, 194 social media links, and 14,435 Bank/MFS accounts to BTRC/law enforcement for blocking. Notably, a legal notice to ESPNcricinfo successfully halted the broadcasting of betting advertisements targeting Bangladesh.

  • Disinformation Control: 147 links related to rumors, fake news, and ethnic violence were processed for removal.

  • Financial Sector: A seminar on "Cybersecurity in Financial Sector of Bangladesh" was held in August 2025 to align banking stakeholders with the new Ordinance.

6. Awareness and Decentralization

To build a "Cyber Hygiene" culture, operations are being expanded beyond the capital:

  • Student Awareness: Sessions were held at 16 universities (3,000+ students) and 40 Upazilas (800+ students) to train the youth on cyber safety.

  • Field Operations: An MoU has been signed with the Department of ICT (DoICT) to establish Cyber Security Helpdesks in District and Upazila ICT offices, ensuring service delivery at the grassroots level.

  • Professional Training:

    • Law Enforcement: ~240 officials trained on Advanced Cybersecurity, Fact-Checking, and Sentiment Analysis over two fiscal years.

    • Govt Officials: ~140 officials trained on cyber safety protocols.

6.3.3 National Security Operations Center (NSOC)

World Bank Funding: A $5 million proposal has been submitted to the World Bank through the EDGE project for financial assistance to establish the National Security Operations Center (NSOC). This funding will enable procurement of advanced monitoring equipment, threat intelligence platforms, and forensic laboratory capabilities.

Full Deployment with CIIs: After initial setup, NSOC will be fully deployed with all Critical Information Infrastructures (CIIs). The center will provide 24/7 monitoring, incident response coordination, and threat intelligence sharing.

Enhanced Capabilities: NSOC enhancement will include digital forensics laboratory, threat intelligence platform, and centralized incident reporting system. This integrated approach significantly enhances national cyber readiness and enables rapid response to emerging threats.

6.3.4 Strategic Framework Development

Bangladesh Cybersecurity Strategy 2026-2030: NCSA will prepare and finalize a comprehensive national cybersecurity strategy serving as the framework for building a resilient and secure digital ecosystem. This strategy will guide effective implementation of mandates under the Cyber Safety Ordinance, 2025.

Cyber Safety Policy: As a supplementary document to the Ordinance, NCSA will formulate a Cyber Safety Policy providing clearer, actionable guidance for public and private sectors to maintain secure digital operations.

Updated Guidelines and SOPs: NCSA will update and formulate relevant cybersecurity guidelines including revision of the Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Guideline (2022), Digital Forensics Lab Guideline (2023), and development of new guidelines based on current needs and international standards.





6.3.5 Capacity Building & Awareness

Specialized Training for Law Enforcement: NCSA will design and deliver technical training programs tailored to cybercrime investigation, digital forensics, and legal handling of cyber incidents. Judiciary and law enforcement agencies will receive specialized instruction ensuring effective enforcement of the Cyber Safety Ordinance.

Public Awareness Campaigns: NCSA will launch targeted campaigns for government, business, and citizen sectors focusing on digital hygiene, social engineering awareness, and safe online behavior. Content will be delivered through multimedia in both Bengali and English for maximum reach.

Cybersecurity Skills Development: NCSA will support national-level cybersecurity certification tracks and encourage academic-industry-government collaboration. Target: 20,000 certified cybersecurity professionals by 2027, scaling to 50,000 by 2030.

6.3.6 Proactive Threat Monitoring & Victim Support

Proactive Cyber Threat Intelligence: NCSA will implement active threat hunting capabilities to identify evolving cybercrimes (e.g., deepfake fraud, ransomware trends) before they scale. This includes monitoring dark web forums for data leaks and analyzing malware trends targeting Bangladesh.

Protection for Vulnerable Communities: Dedicated units will focus on the cyber safety of women, children, and marginalized groups, addressing issues like non-consensual image sharing ("revenge porn"), sextortion, and cyberbullying with high priority and confidentiality.

Victim Assistance & Legal Navigation: NCSA will establish a dedicated Cyber Victim Support Cell. This unit will not only provide technical remediation advice but also guide victims through the legal process, helping them understand their rights under the Cyber Safety Ordinance and assisting them in filing actionable reports with law enforcement. This ensures that the gap between a "technical incident" and "legal justice" is bridged for the average citizen.

Strategic Challenges and Directives (2026-2030)

While significant progress has been made, the following emerging challenges are identified for strategic review within the digital transformation strategy:

  1. Ransomware & Phishing: Persistent threats to government and financial archives.

  2. Institutional Inequality: The gap in cyber resilience between different government agencies creates weak links.

  3. AI & Deepfakes: The rapid evolution of AI-driven threats requires dynamic countermeasures.

Future Directives: To address these, the NCSA will implement:

  • Automated Cyber Audits: Full deployment of the assessment software to ensure regular, automated audits of all CIIs.

  • Cyber Resilience Hub: Establishing a robust "Incident Response Network" for rapid post-attack recovery.

  • International Cooperation: Strengthening data sharing and legal cooperation with global cybersecurity bodies to tackle cross-border cybercrime.

  • ISO Accreditation: Ensuring all forensic labs achieve international accreditation for legal validity.

6.3.7 Sustainable Operations Model

While NSOC Phase 1 is supported under World Bank funding, NCSA will establish a sustainable operation model for post-deployment operations. This model incorporates skilled personnel from both government and private sectors for continuous operations, ensuring the center remains effective beyond the initial project period.

6.4 Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority (BHTPA) Reform

6.4.1 Rationale for Reform

The Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority (BHTPA) was established in 2010 with the visionary mandate to transition the nation toward a knowledge-based economy through specialized infrastructure, investment-friendly policies, and human capital development. However, a comprehensive diagnostic review reveals that over the past 16 years, the authority has largely failed to meet its core objectives due to systemic mismanagement, shortsighted policy-making, unplanned project execution, and deep-seated corruption by syndicates of unskilled officials.

Instead of functioning as a dynamic ecosystem enabler, BHTPA drifted into becoming a mere civil construction agency.

  • Infrastructure Decay & Developer Negligence: The flagship Kaliakoir Hi-Tech Park (355 acres) exemplifies this failure. Despite spending BDT 14 crore (2010-13) and BDT 400 crore (2013-2021) on basic utilities (roads, sewerage, power, gas, fiber), the private developers—Summit Technopolis and Bangladesh Technocity (Fiber@Home)—failed to build the required superstructure on their 130-acre lease. Critical security infrastructure like CCTV and surveillance systems remains absent. The park's potential has been stifled by incompetent and dishonest project directors who prioritized spending over functionality.

  • "Ghost Parks" & Political Planning: Hi-tech parks and Software Technology Parks (STPs) were frequently established in remote, non-viable locations based on political patronage rather than economic logic. These sites lack fiber connectivity, university proximity, or a talent pool, resulting in "Ghost Parks"—empty buildings in rural areas with zero industry uptake.

  • Land Speculation: Prime industrial plots were allocated to politically connected conglomerates with no track record in tech, leading to land hoarding. Genuine investors faced artificial scarcity and bureaucratic harassment, while valuable land lay fallow.

  • Politically Motivated Planning: High-tech parks and Software Technology Parks (STPs) have frequently been established in remote, non-viable locations based on political considerations rather than economic feasibility, fiber connectivity, or talent availability. This has resulted in "Ghost Parks"—empty buildings in rural areas with zero industry uptake.

  • Infrastructure Corruption & Waste: Massive public resources have been wasted on substandard construction and luxury amenities in administrative buildings, while critical industrial utilities (stable power, high-speed redundant internet, waste treatment) remain inadequate.

  • Land Speculation: Industrial plots in prime parks (e.g., Kaliakoir) were allocated to politically connected conglomerates with no track record in tech, leading to land hoarding. Genuine investors often face artificial scarcity and bureaucratic harassment.

6.4.2 Reform Progress Update & Strategic Directives

As of February 2026, the Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority (BHTPA) has transitioned into an active implementation phase of its reform agenda. Moving beyond planning, the authority is now executing specific operational directives aimed at infrastructure optimization, legal modernization, and administrative transparency.

The following outlines the core pillars of this reform progress:

1. Optimization of Infrastructure and Decentralization

To address underutilization issues, BHTPA has initiated a rigorous "De-scoping and Re-scoping" process for all ongoing projects. The primary objective is to ensure 100% utilization of existing Hi-Tech Parks, Software Technology Parks (STPs), and IT Training & Incubation Centers.

  • Action Taken: Active engagement with investors, associations, and higher education institutions is underway to resolve bottlenecks. Two projects have already been revised, and three are currently under review by the Planning Commission.

  • New Expansion: A major initiative has been launched to establish five (5) new STPs in the satellite townships surrounding Dhaka—specifically Purbachal, Uttara, Mirpur, Mohammadpur, and Badda.

  • Strategic Impact: This move aims to decentralize the IT industry, reducing the pressure on Dhaka’s city center while creating "Decent Jobs" in accessible residential zones.

2. Legislative Modernization (BHTPA Ordinance)

A critical overhaul of the legal framework is in the final stages of approval to streamline authority operations and enhance the investment climate.

  • Current Status: The draft Ordinance has been presented twice to the Advisory Council. Following the second meeting, it is being refined in consultation with the Honorable Finance Advisor regarding VAT and Tax implications.

  • Next Steps: The Secretary of the ICT Division will facilitate the final presentation for approval by December 2025.

  • Expected Outcome: Once enacted, this will clarify BHTPA’s scope of work and permanently establish an investor-friendly regulatory environment.

3. Administrative Restructuring of Jashore Software Technology Park

In a decisive move to improve governance, BHTPA has canceled the contract with the third-party Property Management Company (PMC) for the Sheikh Hasina Software Technology Park, Jashore.

  • Current Status: The park is now operated under BHTPA’s direct management (effective May 13, 2025).

  • Results: This transition has allowed for the rationalization of rent rates and has significantly improved the relationship between the authorities and investors, creating a positive work environment in the South-Western IT hub.

4. Land Recovery and Asset Management at Kaliakoir

To ensure the highest utility of national assets, BHTPA has taken a zero-tolerance approach toward non-performance at the flagship Kaliakoir Hi-Tech Park.

  • Action Taken: Approximately 50 acres of land have been recovered from two developer companies that failed to utilize the allocation effectively. A firm has been appointed to assess the liabilities of these outgoing developers.

  • Future Plan: This recovered land is now being re-allocated to capable investors, specifically targeting high-tech manufacturing industries (including semiconductors and electronics), thereby pivoting Bangladesh toward a hardware manufacturing hub.

5. Establishment of the Institute of Frontier Technology (IFT)

To prepare for the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR), BHTPA has solidified a partnership with the Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology (BUET).

  • Collaboration: An MoU has been signed to establish the Institute of Frontier Technology (IFT) directly on the BUET campus.

  • Infrastructure Plan: The project includes academic and administrative infrastructure at BUET for undergraduate and postgraduate research. Additionally, a specialized center for "Lifelong Learning" is being developed in Shibchar, Madaripur, to offer certification courses and PG diplomas.

  • Timeline: The Revised Development Project Proposal (RDPP) has been submitted, with a Project Evaluation Committee (PEC) meeting scheduled for February 1, 2026.

6. Industry-Academia Collaboration Model

A new operational model has been successfully piloted to bridge the gap between higher education and industry requirements.

  • Pilot Project: An MoU was signed with Begum Rokeya University, Rangpur (BRUR) to manage the Rangpur IT Training & Incubation Center.

  • Status: The center is currently operational under university management (effective November 24, 2025), serving as a blueprint for future university-led park management.

7. Standardization of Operating Procedures (SOPs)

To eliminate ambiguity in operations and maintenance, five specific Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) and Guidelines have been drafted and are currently being finalized:

  • Park Location Selection Guideline 2026.

  • Civil Infrastructure Repair & Maintenance SOP 2026.

  • E/M & Networking Repair & Maintenance SOP 2026.

  • Kaliakoir Hi-Tech Park Operation SOP (Administrative & Investor Duties) 2026.

  • University Innovation Hub Guideline 2026.

6.4.3 Infrastructure Rationalization: The "Viability Audit"

To stop the bleeding of public funds and restore order, the Interim Government has initiated a strict rationalization of all infrastructure assets.

  • Mandatory Feasibility & "De-scoping" Audit: A third-party technical audit is assessing all ongoing projects. Projects found economically unviable are being "De-scoped" or "Re-scoped." For example, the costly "District Level IT/Hi-Tech Park (12 Districts)" project is being radically revised to prevent further waste.

  • Repurposing Non-Viable Assets: Remote, unoccupied STPs will not be abandoned. They are being repurposed into "Sheikh Kamal IT Training & Incubation Centers" or DoICT Technical Training Hubs, transferring management to BCC/DoICT for grassroots skills development.

  • Focus on Economic Corridors: Future investment is being consolidated into high-potential economic corridors (Dhaka, Gazipur, Chattogram, Sylhet, Jashore) and 5 new suburban STPs around Dhaka (Purbachal, Uttara, Mirpur, Mohammadpur, Badda) to create "Decent Jobs" near talent hubs.

6.4.4 Operational Reform: From Bureaucracy to Business

  • Policy Modernization & SOPs: To eliminate arbitrary decision-making, BHTPA is finalizing a new suite of governance instruments for 2026:

    • Park Location Selection Guideline 2026: Ensuring future parks are built only where economically viable.

    • Infrastructure Maintenance SOP 2026: Standardizing civil and electrical maintenance to prevent degradation.

    • Kaliakoir Operation SOP 2026: Clearly defining the administrative and maintenance responsibilities of investors versus the authority.

    • University Innovation Hub Guideline 2026: Formalizing the link between academia and industry.

  • Land Lease "Use-it-or-Lose-it" Policy: A strict audit of allocated plots is underway. Allottees who failed to commence construction within the stipulated contract period face lease cancellation. A new "Performance-Based Allocation Policy" ensures land goes to active tech companies, not real estate speculators.

  • One-Stop Service (OSS) Digitization: The current manual OSS is being fully digitized to integrate with BIDA, NBR, and RJSC. The goal is a "Zero-Visit" investor onboarding experience, transparently delivering licenses, utility connections, and bonded warehouse approvals.

6.4.5 Ecosystem Focus: Soft Infrastructure Over Hard Concrete

Moving beyond concrete, BHTPA is pivoting to "Soft Infrastructure" to create a thriving, innovation-led economy.

  • Institute of Frontier Technology (IFT): In a landmark move, the "Bangladesh Institute of Frontier Technology" project has been fundamentally restructured. Originally conceived as a construction-heavy project, it is now established as a premier academic and research partnership through a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology (BUET), signed in September 2025. This collaboration establishes the IFT directly on the BUET campus, shifting focus from erecting empty buildings to fostering cutting-edge research in Artificial Intelligence (AI), Robotics, and Semiconductor design. This strategic pivot ensures immediate access to top-tier academic talent and research capabilities, accelerating Bangladesh's entry into deep-tech industries.

  • University-Industry Collaboration: BHTPA is actively forging strong linkages between academia and industry to ensure a steady pipeline of skilled talent. A prime example is the operationalization of the Rangpur IT Training & Incubation Center through an MoU with Begum Rokeya University (BRUR) (signed November 24, 2025). This "Triple Helix" model (Government-Academia-Industry) mandates that universities manage incubation centers to produce graduates with market-ready skills directly aligned with industry needs, rather than generic training. This approach is being scaled to other universities to maximize the utilization of established infrastructure.

  • Startup & Investor Connect: To foster a vibrant investment climate, BHTPA has institutionalized regular investor matchmaking sessions. These sessions connect park tenants and incubated startups with domestic and foreign capital, shifting the authority's role from passive "tenant management" to active "business success facilitation." The goal is to create a seamless pathway for startups to access funding, mentorship, and global markets.

  • Innovation Hubs & R&D: The authority is finalizing the "University Innovation Hub Guideline 2026" to standardize the operation of innovation hubs across universities. These hubs will serve as centers for research, prototyping, and commercialization, bridging the gap between academic research and industrial application.

6.4.6 Strategic Impact Summary

These reforms are expected to yield three major national outcomes:

  1. Macro-Economic Contribution: Transparency in infrastructure allocation and the new legal framework will directly boost Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) and strengthen foreign currency reserves.

  2. Brain Drain Prevention: By creating world-class research facilities (like the IFT) and global-standard work environments locally, Bangladesh will retain top-tier engineering talent.

  3. Hardware Ecosystem: The strategic recovery of land at Kaliakoir allows Bangladesh to expand beyond software into hardware and semiconductor manufacturing, diversifying the export basket.

6.5 a2i Transformation: Evolving into a GovTech Agency

6.5.1 Foundation and Achievements

Access to Information (a2i), established in 2006 and later embedded within the ICT Division, has emerged as a pioneering entity in Bangladesh's digital landscape. Through systematic initiatives, a2i has tackled entrenched issues including isolated data systems, incomplete datasets, restricted access for vulnerable populations, and reliance on outdated manual processes.

6.5.2 Key Accomplishments

National Portal: A unified online gateway consolidating information and services from over 35,000 government websites, enabling citizens to access more than 1,000 e-services. Processing times reduced from days to minutes through backend integration and workflow automation.

Union Digital Centers (UDCs): Over 8,500 centers nationwide transforming rural post offices and community spaces into hubs for digital services, skill development, and local entrepreneurship. Serving 30 million users annually and generating thousands of local jobs.

Shared Health Record (SHR): Aggregating patient data to support telemedicine and electronic health records. Enabled 340+ Upazila Health Complexes to deliver over 1 million remote consultations annually.

Teachers' Portal: Digital repository serving 15,000+ schools with educational resources, lesson plans, and collaborative tools promoting blended learning models.

e-Filing System: Digitized administrative processes eliminating 70% of paperwork through automated approvals and document tracking.

MyGov App: 5+ million downloads providing citizen engagement, real-time feedback, service requests, and government notifications.

Impact Metrics: Over 2 billion digital transactions processed, saving citizens approximately 1.5 billion days in administrative tasks, and elevating Bangladesh's UN e-Government ranking from 150th in 2012 to 111th in 2022.

6.5.3 Strategic Initiative Updates & Roadmap

To accelerate the transition to a GovTech agency, a2i is actively executing the following high-priority initiatives:

1. Nagorik Sheba Bangladesh Implementation

  • Current Status: A total of 1,122 Nagorik Sheba Centers have been launched. The platform has integrated over 41 services, and 453 services are available through MyGov. More than 2,000 entrepreneurs nationwide have been trained.

  • Target: Convert all Digital Centers into Nagorik Sheba Centers by March 2026.

  • Impact: Citizens can access end-to-end online services from a single, convenient location, ensuring "zero-visit" delivery for verified services.

2. Digital Toll (D-Toll) Management System

  • Current Status: Launched on Padma Bridge on September 15, 2025. Over 4,000 vehicles have used the system seamlessly. Seven financial institutions are integrated.

  • Roadmap: Implementation on Jamuna Bridge by February 1, 2026; Meghna-Gomti Bridge by February 5, 2026; and subsequently across all bridges, tunnels (Karnaphuli), and elevated expressways under the Bridge Authority and Roads Division.

  • Impact: Non-stop vehicle passage in D-Toll lanes, significantly reducing travel time and congestion.

3. E-Apostille System

  • Current Status: The system is ready for inauguration.

  • Impact: Digitizes the verification of documents (certificates, affidavits) for students and professionals going abroad, eliminating the need for physical visits to ministries and foreign missions.

4. ekPay Transformation (PLC Formation)

  • Current Status: The draft Memorandum and Articles of Association (MoA & AoA) for establishing ekPay as a Public Limited Company (PLC) are under review by the ICT Division.

  • Roadmap: Formation of the company by December 2026.

  • Impact: Establishes a sustainable, autonomous financial ecosystem for government payments, reducing dependency on external funding.

5. Smart D-Nothi & AI Integration

  • Current Status: D-Nothi is currently active in 12,600+ offices with over 140,000 users.

  • Roadmap:

    • Full implementation in all government offices by December 2026.

    • Integration of AI-assisted file processing to speed up decision-making.

    • Introduction of a "Tracker" system for real-time file monitoring.

  • Impact: Complete elimination of paper-based filing (Paperless Office) and drastic reduction in administrative delays.

6. Innovation Ecosystem & Policy

  • National Innovation Policy: Finalize by December 2026 to provide a legal and financial framework for innovation.

  • Innovation Repository: A unified platform to protect intellectual property (IP) and support commercialization for public and private innovations.

  • Challenge Fund: Launching an "Innovation Challenge Fund" to support 30 new citizen-centric innovations by October 2026.

7. Citizen Service Workbook (Standardization)

  • Initiative: Development of a "Citizen Service Workbook" to standardize service delivery parameters across all ministries.

  • Objective: Mandate adherence to specific service standards to overcome institutional inertia and resistance to manual-to-digital migration.

6.5.4 Operational Challenges & Mitigation Strategies

To ensure the success of these initiatives, a2i will address critical challenges:

  • Cybersecurity: Implement mandatory Quarterly Security Audits and establish a dedicated Cyber Incident Response Team (CIRT) for a2i platforms.

  • Financial Compliance: Form a specialized Fintech Compliance Team to navigate Bangladesh Bank regulations for the ekPay PLC formation.

  • Infrastructure: Leverage BDCCL’s high-performance cloud infrastructure to handle massive traffic and data loads, ensuring uninterrupted service delivery.

6.5.5 Transformation Roadmap to GovTech Agency

Transforming a2i into a full-fledged GovTech agency requires institutionalizing its role as a hub for digital innovation and cross-governmental coordination. This evolution mirrors global exemplars including Singapore's GovTech Agency, Estonia's X-Road system, and the UK's Government Digital Service (GDS).

Structural Reforms: a2i should gain semi-autonomous status within the ICT Division with independent budget and authority to enforce digital standards across ministries. Dedicated divisions for emerging technologies, data governance, and ethical AI oversight will be established, supported by multi-stakeholder committees.



Scalable DPI Framework: Development of integrated Digital Public Infrastructure incorporating modular components for digital identity, payments, and data sharing. Inclusive design principles will ensure accessibility through multilingual interfaces and low-bandwidth optimizations.

Policy Alignment: Comprehensive GovTech policy framework incorporating robust data protection measures, ethical AI guidelines, and PPP incentives to facilitate collaborations with tech firms.

Monitoring & Evaluation: Advanced monitoring systems with KPIs including service availability rates exceeding 99%, user satisfaction scores above 80%, and quantifiable cost reductions. Real-time analytics dashboards will enable proactive adjustments.

6.5.6 Engineering Talent Strategy

To build sophisticated digital systems, a2i must adopt specialized hiring practices aiming to expand the national ICT workforce to 7-8 million by 2030:

Software Engineers (40%): Experts in cloud computing, microservices, and scalable architectures.

Data Engineers and AI Specialists (30%): Professionals skilled in big data processing and machine learning with ethical AI training.

Cybersecurity Engineers (20%): Specialists in advanced threat detection and zero-trust frameworks.

DevOps Engineers (10%): Individuals proficient in automation tools and containerization.

Hiring will emphasize diversity with 40% representation of women and underrepresented groups, skill-based recruitment through coding challenges and hackathons, continuous upskilling through mandatory certifications, and competitive compensation packages.

6.5.7 Public Procurement Reform for Software

Bangladesh's existing Public Procurement Rules (PPR) often prioritize cost over quality, resulting in vendor dependencies and integration issues. Reforms will include:

PPR Reforms: Transition to balanced evaluation where technical merits (innovation, scalability) account for at least 40% of criteria. Mandatory clauses for open APIs and data interoperability.

Agile Procurement: Segmenting large projects into phased deliverables, favoring open-source solutions and cloud-based deployments with mandatory third-party security audits.

Enhanced e-GP: AI-assisted bid analysis for fairness, provisions for joint ventures, and streamlined approvals. Target: pilot reforms in 2025, full rollout by 2026.

6.5.8 Emerging Technology Adoption

AI-powered chatbots for 24/7 citizen queries; cloud-native architecture for auto-scaling during peak demand

6.5.9 Interoperability Leadership

a2i is ideally suited to lead Bangladesh's interoperability agenda through:

General Strategy: Champion unified standards for data formats and APIs, establishing governance bodies for data exchanges. Enable 'once-only' data submission reducing redundancy.

Financial Sector: Drive platforms like Binimoy for cross-provider transactions, incorporating blockchain for transparent payments linking mobile wallets with banking systems.

Health Sector: Facilitate integrated pipelines connecting shared records with geo-located facilities, using AI for disease pattern analytics and resource allocation.

Education Sector: Link student profiles with national IDs for automated enrollment and personalized learning paths.

Timeline: Expanded pilots in 2025, full sectoral integration by 2027, potentially yielding efficiencies comparable to Estonia's annual savings equivalent to 2% of GDP.
