6.6 Data Center Infrastructure Reform (NDC/BDCCL)

6.6.1 Current Challenges

Bangladesh's growing demand for digital services has pushed existing government data centers to full capacity. The Bangladesh Computer Council's (BCC) Nutanix and Huawei data centers face critical constraints in memory, storage, and computing power. Without strategic reform, these limitations will impede the scaling of e-governance, fintech, and digital entrepreneurship services.

Key Challenges Identified:

  • Infrastructure Obsolescence: A significant portion of the Native Cloud environment was running on legacy hardware that had reached end-of-life status. These assets were consuming excessive power and rack space without delivering commensurate computational value, driving up operational costs (OpEx) while offering poor performance.

  • Cooling & Power Inefficiency: The data center faced challenges with cooling optimization, leading to a high Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) ratio (around 1.8). The lack of modern containment strategies meant that energy was being wasted on cooling non-critical or empty rack spaces.

  • Vendor Lock-in & Financial Bleeding: The previous Oracle Cloud contract was financially unsustainable, with the project consuming $23.93 million in costs while generating less than $6 million in revenue. The terms effectively trapped BDCCL in a "Use-it-or-Lose-it" credit model without ownership of the hardware, forcing the government to pay duties on assets it did not own.

  • Underutilized Capacity: Despite high demand for cloud services in the country, BDCCL's pricing models and service portfolio were not competitive with private sector or international providers, leading to low occupancy rates.

6.6.2 Reform Measures

  1. Integration of BDCCL and NDC: The Bangladesh Data Centre Company Limited (BDCCL) and the National Data Centre (NDC) will be integrated for capacity and resource optimization, eliminating duplication and enabling economies of scale.



  1. Capacity Expansion: Expansion plans are being pursued to significantly increase storage and computing power. Land allocation in Dhaka Metro for NDC expansion will ensure scalability for future cloud, AI, and big data needs.

  2. Service Model Transformation: A strategic shift from an infrastructure-only model to a product-oriented service platform, enabling value-added services for freelancers, the gaming industry, and the wider digital services ecosystem.

  3. Disaster Recovery Infrastructure: A national disaster recovery infrastructure expansion plan to safeguard against data loss, ensure business continuity, and strengthen cyber resilience.

  4. Green ICT Practices: Upgrade data centers with renewable energy, efficient cooling systems, and carbon-neutral operations aligned with the National Green ICT Policy.

6.6.3 Strategic Initiatives & Progress Updates

To transform BDCCL from a passive colocation facility into a dynamic "National Cloud Service Provider," a rigorous reform agenda ("Hard Reset") is being executed.

1. Infrastructure Optimization & Decommissioning

  • Action: A complete audit of the Native Cloud environment was conducted to identify "zombie servers" and obsolete hardware.

  • Progress: Decommissioning of legacy hardware is in the final stages (Target: January 2026). This includes the migration of critical services for the Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority (BHTPA) and DNS systems to optimized platforms.

  • Impact: This initiative significantly reduces electricity consumption, frees up valuable rack space for high-density modern computing, and lowers the overall carbon footprint of the facility.

2. New Cloud Service Expansion (Revenue Sharing Model)

  • Strategy: Recognizing that BDCCL cannot solely rely on government capex for rapid expansion, a Revenue Sharing Model has been adopted to attract private investment and technical expertise.

  • Progress: An Expression of Interest (EOI) was floated for a Joint Venture to deploy a new "Sovereign Cloud." As of Jan 20, 2026, 5 out of 7 shortlisted firms have submitted detailed proposals. Evaluation is currently underway.

  • Objective: To deploy a state-of-the-art cloud platform capable of hosting critical government and private sector workloads, reducing reliance on foreign hyperscalers (AWS, Azure) and saving foreign currency.

3. Tariff Rationalization & Market Competitiveness

  • Action: To make BDCCL competitive against private operators, the 40th Board Meeting approved a strategic reduction in Co-Location service charges.

  • Progress: The new tariff structure is effective from January 2026. Existing contracts with clients are being amended to reflect these lower rates.

  • Impact: This move aims to attract a broader client base, including private sector enterprises, fintechs, and ISPs, thereby increasing overall revenue through higher volume utilization.

4. Strategic Contract Renegotiation (Oracle Cloud)

  • Context: Rectifying the financially unbalanced Oracle contract was a top priority.

  • Reform Actions: High-level renegotiations have been initiated with the following demands:

    • Duty Reimbursement: Oracle must reimburse the $3.68 million in import duties paid by BDCCL for hardware owned by Oracle.

    • Credit Rollover: Transitioning from a rigid annual forfeiture model to a flexible system where unused credits carry forward to subsequent years.

    • Market Expansion: Amending the contract to allow BDCCL to sell Oracle Cloud services to the Private Sector, monetizing the idle capacity.

    • Term Extension: Extending the service period from 3 to 5 years to allow sufficient time for market uptake and ROI generation.

5. Hybrid Cloud & Future Architecture

  • Roadmap: BDCCL is developing a Hybrid Cloud architecture. This involves creating a strictly isolated "Private Cloud" (potentially air-gapped) for highly sensitive government data (National Security, NID, Health) and a "Public Cloud" partition for general citizen services and commercial use. This tiered approach balances supreme security with scalability.

6.6.4 Industry-Academia Collaboration

  • Talent Pipeline: Addressing the shortage of skilled Data Center Engineers, BDCCL is launching joint training programs with top engineering universities. These programs will focus on Data Center Operations (DCO), Cloud Architecture, and Green IT, creating a specialized workforce to support the national digital infrastructure.

6.6.5 Expected Impact & Vision

  • Data Sovereignty: By repatriating critical national data from foreign servers to BDCCL, Bangladesh strengthens its national security and ensures compliance with the Data Localization mandates of the NDGO.

  • Economic Efficiency: A successful Sovereign Cloud will save millions in foreign currency currently paid to international providers. Lower hosting costs will also lower the barrier to entry for local startups.

  • Innovation Enabler: Affordable, sovereign cloud resources (including the planned GPU cluster) will empower local AI researchers and deep-tech startups to innovate without the high infrastructure costs associated with global providers.

6.6.6 BDCCL Reform: Oracle Cloud Contract Negotiation

The Bangladesh Data Center Company Limited (BDCCL) entered into a strategic agreement with Oracle for the Dedicated Region Cloud@Customer (DRCC) service to enhance national cloud sovereignty. However, a comprehensive review by the Interim Government in 2025 revealed critical imbalances in the contractual terms that rendered the project financially unsustainable.

An operational audit indicated that the project was consuming approximately USD 23.93 million in costs while generating less than USD 6 million in revenue. The existing contract contained provisions that deviated from international best practices, placing disproportionate financial burdens on the state while restricting market flexibility.

Key Issues Identified

  • Unfair Financial Liability: Despite Oracle retaining ownership of the hardware, BDCCL was designated as the 'Importer of Record,' forcing the government to bear an additional USD 3.68 million in import duties and taxes—a burden typically borne by the asset owner.

  • Restrictive Credit Model: The "Annual Universal Credit (AUC)" structure (USD 3M, 6M, and 9M scaling over 3 years) functioned as a rigid capacity charge with no rollover option. Unused credits were forfeited annually, pushing BDCCL toward significant losses due to initial underutilization.

  • Market Restrictions: The contract limited DRCC usage strictly to government entities, preventing BDCCL from selling excess capacity to the private sector, thereby capping potential revenue.

  • Sovereignty Risks: The agreement was governed by English Law with arbitration in London, and Oracle retained unilateral rights to remove hardware if unused for six months, posing a risk to national data infrastructure stability.

Strategic Reform Measures To rectify these issues and ensure the project's viability, the government has initiated a high-level renegotiation with Oracle, mandated by the Special Assistant to the Chief Advisor. The reform agenda focuses on ten critical amendments to restore balance:

Financial Restructuring

  • Duty Reimbursement: Demand for Oracle to assume ownership liabilities, including the reimbursement of the USD 3.68 million in taxes paid by BDCCL.

  • Credit Rollover & Optimization: Transitioning from a "Use-it-or-Lose-it" model to a flexible credit system where unused funds carry forward, protecting public money from forfeiture.

  • Service Extension: Proposing a term extension from 3 to 5 years with a rationalized total value (e.g., USD 6 million inclusive of taxes), aligning costs with actual market uptake.

Market Liberalization

  • Private Sector Access: Amending the "Usage Rights" clause to allow BDCCL to extend sovereign cloud services to private sector clients (e.g., banks, MNCs), subject to security compliance. This is essential to utilize the idle capacity and generate revenue to cover operational costs.

Legal & Operational Sovereignty

  • Governing Law: Shifting the legal framework to the Laws of Bangladesh, with arbitration seated in Dhaka under the Bangladesh International Arbitration Centre (BIAC), ensuring national legal sovereignty.

  • Asset Control: Revoking Oracle’s unilateral right to remove hardware. Any infrastructure changes must now require BDCCL’s explicit written consent to prevent service disruption.

  • Data Retention: Extending the post-termination data retrieval period beyond the restrictive 60-day window to a mutually agreed timeframe, safeguarding critical national data.

Outcome of Renegotiation Efforts

To rectify these issues, the government initiated high-level renegotiations aimed at financial restructuring, market liberalization, and legal sovereignty (shifting arbitration to Dhaka). However, these negotiations have effectively reached an impasse. The vendor remained unwilling to align the contract with the financial realities of the local market or the sovereignty requirements of the state. Consequently, continuing the project under the current terms would constitute a significant and recurring loss of public funds.


The Way Forward: Exit Strategy and Mitigation In light of the failed negotiations, BDCCL is triggering a Strategic Exit and Rationalization Plan to protect state assets and minimize fiscal impact. The reform agenda now focuses on four immediate actions:

1. Phased Decommissioning of Hardware

  • Capacity Downsizing: BDCCL will immediately halt the intake of new hardware capacity under the "scaling" clauses of the contract.

  • Asset Return: A formal process will be initiated to decommission and return unused Oracle hardware racks currently occupying space in the National Data Center. This aims to stop the accrual of maintenance and capacity charges for infrastructure that is effectively idle.

2. Sovereign Cloud Provisioning & Migration

  • Workload Migration: A "Lift and Shift" roadmap is being executed to migrate existing government workloads from the Oracle DRCC environment to BDCCL’s alternative sovereign infrastructure or open-standard private clouds (e.g., OpenStack-based solutions).

  • Service Continuity: Ensure zero downtime for critical government services during the migration phase by maintaining a minimum viable footprint until the transition is complete.

3. Financial Recovery & Dispute Resolution

  • Duty Reclamation: The government will pursue legal and administrative channels to reclaim or adjust the USD 3.68 million paid in import duties for assets that are being returned to the vendor.

  • Contract Termination: Legal notice will be served to terminate the agreement based on "Force Majeure" or "Economic Hardship" clauses, shifting dispute resolution to a localized framework to minimize exit penalties.

4. Policy Correction: Open Standards Mandate

  • Vendor Lock-in Prevention: Moving forward, BDCCL should refrain from signing proprietary, closed-loop cloud contracts. Future procurement must prioritize open-source, interoperable cloud technologies to ensure that the state never again faces data hostage situations or rigid capacity payments.

6.7 Software and Hardware Quality Testing and Certification

Software and Hardware Quality Testing and Certification (SHQTC) Center, accredited by BAB, is a national authority responsible for the mandatory testing, certification, and compliance validation of all government-developed software, hardware, and digital systems. This strategic initiative is designed to enhance the security, reliability, and interoperability of public sector digital services, while also promoting industry-wide quality assurance practices.

The SHQTC Center will play a pivotal role in the reform agenda, certifying the functionality, security, and performance of all ICT solutions procured or developed by government entities. No digital system will be operationalized without undergoing formal certification, ensuring full compliance with national digital architecture standards, cybersecurity frameworks, and data protection policies. To foster a secure, transparent, and efficient digital public service ecosystem, SHQTC will also maintain a centralized Government Software Repository, supporting software reuse, interoperability, and quality standardization across ministries and agencies.

6.7.1 Strategic Empowerment and Institutional Strengthening

To ensure SHQTC can effectively execute its mandate, the government will implement a comprehensive empowerment strategy:

  • Operational Autonomy & Promotion: SHQTC will operate as an autonomous body with the authority to enforce quality standards. A clear career progression path and promotion policy will be established for technical staff to retain top talent and ensure institutional memory.

  • Dedicated Financial Codes: The Ministry of Finance will introduce specific economic codes in the national budget for "Software and Hardware Quality Testing." This ensures that every digital project has a mandatory, ring-fenced budget allocation for quality assurance, preventing funds from being diverted to other operational costs.

  • Increased Funding & Staffing: A significant budget increase will be allocated to recruit specialized engineers, security analysts, and compliance auditors. Staffing levels will be scaled up to meet the growing demand of certifying all government digital assets.

  • Capacity Building: Continuous professional development programs will be institutionalized, offering certifications in advanced testing methodologies (e.g., ISTQB, CISA) and emerging technologies to keep the workforce at the cutting edge.

6.7.2 Dedicated SHQTC R&D Lab

A state-of-the-art Research and Development (R&D) Lab will be established within SHQTC. This facility will focus on:

  • Automated Testing Frameworks: Developing AI-driven tools for rapid vulnerability scanning and code quality analysis.

  • Hardware Stress Testing: Simulating extreme environmental conditions to ensure hardware resilience in diverse Bangladeshi climates.

  • Security Research: Proactively identifying zero-day vulnerabilities in locally used software stacks.

  • Interoperability Standards: creating testbeds to validate API integrations against the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDA).

6.7.3 Market Expansion

In addition to public sector services, the SHQTC will extend its certification services to private sector ICT firms on a voluntary basis. This will enable local software and hardware developers to secure official certification for their products, enhancing credibility, market trust, and competitiveness. By opening these services to the private sector, Bangladesh positions itself to raise national ICT standards and establish itself as a regional hub for trusted, secure, and certified digital solutions.

6.7.4 Key Responsibilities Reform of SHQTC

  • Mandatory Testing: Certification for all government-developed and procured ICT systems.

  • Voluntary Certification: Optional, standards-based certification services for private software and hardware firms.

  • Repository Management: Managing a Government Software Repository for certified and reusable software assets.

  • Compliance Enforcement: Ensuring adherence to national digital interoperability, cybersecurity, and data governance frameworks.

  • Lifecycle Assurance: Delivery of structured quality assurance, security, and performance testing throughout system development life cycles.

6.7.5 Implementation Roadmap

  • Mid 2026: Certification authority operations to commence.

  • September 2026: Phase 1 launch of the Government Software Repository.

  • 2026 onwards: Optional certification services available for private ICT firms.

  • By 2030: Full certification compliance expected for all government ICT systems.

To successfully deliver on its mandate, a dedicated capacity development program will be introduced, focusing on secure software development, cybersecurity testing, compliance auditing, and interoperability management. This will be supported by collaborative partnerships with universities, R&D centers, and international digital governance experts to ensure adoption of international best practices and to strengthen institutional capability.

6.8 Reform of the Office of the Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)

6.8.1 Background and Context

The Office of the Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA) is the national trust anchor for electronic signatures and public key infrastructure (PKI) in Bangladesh. To enable end-to-end paperless citizen and government services, the CCA must evolve from a primarily oversight and compliance function into a modern trust-service regulator and platform operator. This requires legal and policy modernization; resilient 24/7 trust infrastructure (Root CA, OCSP/CRL, signature verification); stronger audits and forensics; a developer-first integration layer (APIs, SDKs, client tools); and systematic cross-ministry adoption that reduces service silos.

Electronic signatures and PKI are foundational enablers for secure digital transactions, providing identity binding, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation for documents and online workflows. In a government service-delivery context, they enable 

  • (i) legally enforceable digital approvals; 

  • (ii) tamper-evident records; 

  • (iii) auditable decision trails; and 

  • (iv) faster, less corruptible processes.

The CCA functions as the apex authority that regulates and supervises licensed Certifying Authorities (CAs) and operates national Root CA trust services. Future reform functions include expanding scope beyond licensing into ecosystem enablement, e.g., e-sign API specifications for online e-KYC, interoperability guidance for certificates, and capacity-building programs.

6.8.2 Current Scope of Work and Services

The CCA currently provides or coordinates the following functions:

  1. Regulation and supervision of licensed Certifying Authorities (CAs), including review and update of CA Certificate Policy (CP) and Certificate Practice Statement (CPS).

  2. Operation and maintenance of national Root CA trust services and trust governance artifacts (Root CA CP/CPS).

  3. 24/7 validation services: Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP), Certificate Revocation List (CRL), and public digital signature verification services.

  4. Support services for subscriber verification, including National Identity Card (NID) verification support for CA operations.

  5. Development and periodic update of sector-wide instruments (e.g., PKI Auditing Guideline, CA Licensing Guideline, PKI Enabled Application Guideline, Digital Signature Certificate Interoperability Guidelines, and e-KYC / e-Sign API specifications).

  6. Ecosystem development and capacity building: trainings, workshops, PKI summits/hackathons/quizzes, and recognition programs for digital signature adopters.

Operational Trust Services

CCA operates public-facing services that underpin nationwide verification and trust. These should be treated as national critical digital infrastructure and managed with explicit SLAs:

  • OCSP service (real-time certificate status).

  • CRL publication service (revocation list distribution).

  • Public digital signature verification service (document signature checking).

6.8.3 Recent Accomplishments

Strategic Initiatives & Progress Updates

CCA is executing a comprehensive roadmap to integrate digital signatures across all pillars of the government.

1. e-GP System Integration (Transparency in Procurement)

  • Action: The Bangladesh e-Government Procurement (e-GP) Guidelines (Revised), 2025 (enacted March 12, 2025) now mandates the use of digital signatures at various stages of the tender process.

  • Progress: Technical integration is ongoing to ensure full compliance.

  • Target: Complete integration by June 2026.

  • Impact: This will eliminate procurement fraud, ensure non-repudiation of bids, and guarantee 100% transparency in public spending.

2. Land Service Modernization (Secure Property Rights)

  • Action: The Ministry of Land decided (August 18, 2025) to introduce digital signatures for critical citizen documents: Record of Rights (Khatian), Duplicate Carbon Receipts (DCR), and Dakhila (Rent Receipts).

  • Progress: CCA is providing technical support for API integration.

  • Target: Live deployment by June 2026.

  • Impact: This will prevent the forgery of land documents, reduce litigation, and massively increase the legal reliability of online land services.

3. Smart D-Nothi & Office Digitization

  • Current Status: Digital signatures are currently used for outgoing letters (Patra Jari) in 30 offices. A Proof of Concept (POC) has been completed for signing internal Note Sheets.

  • Roadmap: Full-scale implementation of Note Sheet signing across all ministries by June 2026.

  • Impact: Achieving a truly "Paperless Secretariat" where file approval workflows are 100% digital and legally secure.

4. BNDX Platform Integration (Interoperability)

  • Action: To secure the National Data Exchange Layer, CCA has approved a specialized Certificate Profile for the BNDX (Bangladesh National Digital Exchange) platform.

  • Status: Completed (January 2026).

  • Impact: Ensures that all cross-agency data exchanges (e.g., NID to Passport) are cryptographically signed and trusted.

Other notable accomplishments include:

  • Expansion of Digital Signatures: Digital signatures were introduced in 20 organizations over the past year, including the National Board of Revenue (NBR), Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Bangladesh Cricket Board, Mutual Trust Bank, Eastern Bank Limited, and BRAC Bank PLC.

  • Service Launch: Launched the Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) service for the Root CA system.

  • Continuous Availability: Providing 24/7 Certificate Revocation List (CRL) services via crl.cca.gov.bd.

  • Verification Services: Maintaining the 'Digital Signature Verification' service (digisigchecker.cca.gov.bd) and providing 24/7 'National ID Verification' services to CA institutions.

  • Regulatory Framework: Formulated the Office of the Controller of Certifying Authorities (Employee) Recruitment Rules, 2025.

  • Guidelines & Policies: Updated the Digital Certificate Interoperability Guideline, PKI Enabled Application Guideline, Bangladesh Root CA Certificate Policy (CP), and Root CA Certificate Practice Statement (CPS).

  • CA Oversight: Reviewed and updated the CP and CPS of various Certifying Authorities, along with PKI Auditing and CA Licensing guidelines.

  • Training & Engagement: Provided PKI training and distributed e-Signs to 50 university students and teachers on the occasion of July Revolution Day.

  • Events: Organized the PKI Summit 2025, PKI Hackathon 2025, and PKI Online Quiz 2025.

  • Recognition: Honored the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, RJSC, BRAC Bank PLC, and Robi Axiata Limited as the best users of digital signatures.

  • Institutional Strengthening: Submitted a manpower proposal to the Finance Division for the digital signature implementation project.

  • Future Planning: Developed the DPP for a feasibility study project titled "Electronic Signature Subscriber Verification Ecosystem, Strengthening the Office of the CCA, and Electronic Record Repository."

  • Capacity Building: Trained 173 government officials across 5 institutions and organized workshops/seminars in 8 institutions regarding digital signatures over the last year.

6.8.4 Current Challenges and Structural Gaps

Despite progress, the ecosystem remains constrained by structural issues that limit nationwide, citizen-facing scale-up:

  • Siloed ownership of government services across ministries: workflows remain paper-based or hybrid, and electronic signature usage is not consistently mandated in priority e-services.

  • Absence of standardized integration patterns: agencies often lack reusable toolkits (signature validation middleware, timestamping, long-term validation, role-based signing policies) and developer enablement for quick onboarding.

  • Infrastructure modernization needs: Root CA platform and cryptographic modules require continuous upgrades; OCSP/CRL throughput and latency must scale for higher transaction volumes and stricter availability targets.

  • Assurance maturity gaps: independent audits, continuous compliance monitoring, and forensic readiness must be strengthened to protect national trust infrastructure and investigate signature-related cybercrime.

  • International recognition barriers: without meeting major root store inclusion and audit transparency requirements, cross-border interoperability and wider private-sector adoption remain limited.

  • Human resource and empowerment constraints: the reform proposal highlights gaps in grade/authority, staffing structure, and administrative autonomy expected for 24/7 national services.

  • Sustainable financing: recurring OPEX for secure infrastructure, audits, and 24/7 operations requires dedicated budget heads and clear cost recovery mechanisms for certain services.

  • Identity privacy and verification risks: evolving NID verification posture requires consent-based, data-minimizing verification mechanisms in e-KYC and signing flows.

6.8.5 Reform Vision, Objectives, and Design Principles

Reform Vision: Establish the CCA as a modern, empowered national Trust Services Authority that enables secure, legally enforceable, paperless transactions for government, citizens, and businesses.

Reform Objectives:

  • Scale digital signature usage across priority government workflows (procurement, finance, land, education, health) and progressively expand to citizen-facing services.

  • Integrate high-assurance digital signature capabilities with the Citizen ID ecosystem (NID/OneID), enabling remote signing and consent-based identity verification.

  • Modernize Root CA infrastructure and operational processes to deliver 24/7 services hosted on government cloud infrastructure with appropriate disaster recovery.

  • Strengthen audit, compliance, incident response, and digital forensics capabilities aligned with international best practices.

  • Create a developer-first integration layer: standard APIs, SDKs, and reference implementations to reduce onboarding friction for agencies and vendors.

  • Progressively achieve international trust and cross-border recognition through conformance, audit transparency, and alignment with global root store policies.

Design Principles:

  • Security-by-design and privacy-by-design; least-privilege access; strong key management and HSM governance.

  • Interoperability as a policy: standards-based profiles and conformance testing.

  • Operational resilience: 24/7 monitoring, defined SLAs, high availability (HA), and disaster recovery (DR).

  • Modular enablement: reusable components and APIs usable across sector systems.

  • Measurable outcomes: KPI-driven management and transparent reporting.

6.8.6 Roadmap and Priority Actions

1. "Online Fraud Prevention" Project (Secured Repository)

  • Initiative: A new project titled "Expansion of Digital Signatures for Cyber Security to Prevent Online Fraud" was approved in January 2026.

  • Core Component: Establishment of a Secured Electronic Repository to store and verify all digital certificates and signatures centrally.

  • Goal: To achieve International Recognition (WebTrust Seal) for Bangladesh's Root CA, allowing Bangladeshi digital signatures to be recognized globally (e.g., by Adobe, Microsoft).

  • Target: Architecture design completion by November 2026.

2. Judicial Integration (e-Judiciary)

  • Vision: Extending digital signatures to the judiciary to enable digitally signed court verdicts and bail orders, reducing tampering and delays.

Institutional Synergy & Challenges

To succeed, CCA is building synergies with other agencies:

  • With BDCCL: Hosting the new Secured Electronic Repository at the Tier-4 National Data Center for maximum security.

  • With NCSA: Establishing a real-time Threat Intelligence Sharing mechanism to detect and prevent digital signature forgery.

  • With DoICT: Launching grassroots awareness campaigns through Upazila-level ICT officers.

Near-term outputs (12–24 months)

  • Upgradation of the administrative status of the Controller of the CCA to Grade-2. This elevation is critical for ensuring the CCA has the requisite authority to lead inter-ministerial coordination and enforce compliance across the whole-of-government.

  • Procurement integration roadmap with BPPA/e-GP for tender and contract signing.

  • iBAS++ integration design and piloting across key approval stages (bills, budgets, funds).

  • Land document signing pilots (Namjari, Khatian, and related services) with training and implementation support.

  • Academic and health record pilots: digitally-signed certificates/transcripts; EMR/e-prescription authenticity and non-repudiation.

  • Onboard at least 10 additional priority government agencies with customized adoption plans.

  • Root CA system upgrade planning and execution (hardware, software, cryptographic modules) and CP/CPS modernization.

  • CRL/OCSP performance uplift: increased capacity and improved validation speed for real-time use cases.

  • To overcome procurement hurdles, the Ministry of Finance will introduce a specific finance code for "Digital Signature & PKI Services" in the national budget. This reform will allow government offices to allocate dedicated funds for acquiring digital signatures, tokens, and related services, eliminating the ambiguity that currently hinders adoption.

  • Quarterly third-party cyber security audits of Root CA; strengthening of digital forensics lab operations and SOPs.

  • Nationwide awareness program (≥20 seminars), user guidelines publication, and quarterly high-level stakeholder review meetings.

  • Collaborate with the Ministry of Finance to open specific Economic Revenue Codes for electronic signature services. This will ensure a sustainable, recurring budget for 24/7 Root CA system upgrades, WebTrust audits, and the maintenance of national critical trust infrastructure, reducing dependency on ad-hoc project funding.

  • The CCA will actively pursue inclusion in the global trusted stores of Mozilla, Microsoft, and Adobe through rigorous WebTrust audits. This is essential for the international acceptance of Bangladeshi digital certificates and to facilitate Cross-Border Mutual Recognition of digital signatures for international trade and legal cooperation.

Medium-term outcomes (2028–2030)

  • Citizen ID-linked signing capability available as a national service, supporting citizen-facing digital approvals and transactions (remote signing with consent-based verification).

  • Standard trust-service components (signing gateway, verification middleware, timestamping, long-term validation) reusable across sector systems.

  • Government cloud-hosted trust infrastructure with HA/DR, integrated SOC/SRE operations, and mature incident handling.

  • International trust milestones: completed audits and transparency requirements aligned with major root store policies; phased mutual recognition pathways initiated.

  • Continuous policy modernization: adoption of new cryptographic practices and readiness for post-quantum cryptography transition planning.

  • Partner with the Ministry of Education to integrate Digital Signature and PKI concepts into the national ICT curriculum. This will build a foundational 'culture of trust' and ensure a steady pipeline of domestic PKI experts.

  • Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) Readiness: Establish a dedicated research and development cell within the CCA to explore Post-Quantum Cryptography and its implications for national security. Budgetary priority will be given to PKI-related innovation ideas to ensure Bangladesh’s digital trust infrastructure remains resilient against emerging computing threats.

6.8.7 KPI Framework (Outcome and Service KPIs)

The KPI framework should balance (i) adoption outcomes, (ii) service reliability, (iii) assurance and compliance, and (iv) ecosystem enablement. Indicative KPIs and target levels are proposed below; baselines should be confirmed at program inception.

KPI Category

KPI

Target (Indicative)

Notes

Adoption

New ministries/agencies onboarded (production e-sign workflows)

≥10 per year

Includes customized adoption plans

Adoption

≥1 major workflow/sector live by 2027; scale by 2030

≥99.9% monthly uptime

Operations

Defined CRL update SLA met

Revocation safety

Operations

Target after baseline (e.g., <300ms)

User experience

Quarterly (Root CA) + annual (ecosystem)

≥1,000/year (role-based)

Milestones completed per roadmap

International credibility





6.9 Summary: Integrated Institutional Framework

The institutional reforms outlined in this chapter form an interconnected framework where each agency plays a distinct yet complementary role in achieving Bangladesh's digital transformation objectives:

BCC (Bangladesh Computer Council) functions as the primary standards and infrastructure body, managing critical assets like the National Data Center and National Digital Architecture (BNDA) while ensuring technical standardization across government systems.

DoICT (Department of ICT) serves as the grassroots implementation arm, extending digital services and literacy to the upazila level through its field offices, technical support for local administration, and connectivity maintenance.

NCSA (National Cyber Security Agency) anchors the nation's digital defense, operating the National Security Operations Center (NSOC), conducting critical infrastructure audits, and enforcing the Cyber Safety Ordinance to protect citizen data.

a2i (Aspire to Innovate) operates as the central GovTech innovation hub, designing citizen-centric services, simplifying public service delivery (SPS), and driving the "Zero-Visit" mandate through policy advocacy and pilot initiatives.

BHTPA (Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority) acts as the engine for industry growth, managing Hi-Tech Parks and Software Technology Parks to attract foreign direct investment (FDI) and foster a thriving startup ecosystem.

CCA (Controller of Certifying Authorities) establishes the foundation for digital trust, regulating electronic signatures (CA) and ensuring the legal validity of digital transactions essential for the smart economy.

BDCCL (Bangladesh Data Center Company Ltd) ensures data sovereignty and secure hosting, managing the Tier-IV National Data Center to provide cloud services and colocation for critical government and private sector data.

SHQTC (Software and Hardware Quality Testing Center) provides the essential quality assurance layer, offering standardized testing and certification for government software and hardware procurement to ensure reliability, security, and interoperability.



6.9.1 Coordination Mechanisms

Successful implementation requires coordination mechanisms including quarterly inter-agency coordination meetings, shared KPI dashboards for transparency, joint capacity building programs, and unified standards enforcement through NDGA. The ICT Division will maintain oversight responsibility with clear escalation pathways for resolving inter-institutional challenges.

6.9.2 Success Factors

The reforms will succeed when: government systems achieve full interoperability through the National Service Bus; every citizen can access digital skills training within their district; cyber threats are detected and responded to within hours; public services achieve 99%+ availability with 80%+ user satisfaction; and ICT projects consistently deliver verified outcomes within budget.

6.9.3 Institutional Reform Summary

Institution

Primary Role

Key 2030 Target

Integration Point

BCC

Digital infrastructure backbone & certification

100% NSB integration

PPR reform



Through these coordinated institutional reforms, Bangladesh will establish the governance foundations necessary to achieve its vision of becoming a digitally empowered, innovative, and inclusive society by 2030.



---

End of Chapter 6



7. Project reform

7.1 Introduction and Reform Imperative

Bangladesh's digital transformation journey has been marked by significant ICT project investments over the past decade. However, independent reviews, audit findings, and operational assessments have revealed systemic governance, financial, and technical challenges across several flagship projects. These deficiencies have resulted in cost overruns, suboptimal infrastructure deployment, vendor dependencies, and inadequate accountability mechanisms.

The National Digital Transformation Strategy 2025-2030 recognizes that achieving a digitally empowered Bangladesh requires not only new initiatives but also comprehensive reform of existing projects. This chapter outlines the reform framework for flagship ICT projects, addressing identified weaknesses while establishing scalable models for future digital infrastructure development. The overarching objective is to restore trust, ensure value-for-money, and align all projects with NDGA-led interoperability and cybersecurity standards.

7.2 Expanding Digital Connectivity (EDC) Project Reform

7.2.1 Background and Identified Issues

The Expanding Digital Connectivity (EDC) project, envisioned as a cornerstone for rural digital access, was found to be riddled with systemic malpractice that compromised its integrity and financial viability. Initial audits and independent reviews by the Interim Government uncovered a pattern of falsified reporting and funding mismanagement by service providers and project officials.

  1. Loan Uncertainty & Component Cancellation: Due to complications with the Chinese loan agreement, major components like the Digital Village (Scope-7) and CRVS Software (Scope-6, BDT 502.09 Crore) were suspended. The Revised Development Project Proposal (RDPP) is now restructuring the project to be completed primarily with GoB funds (BDT 2545.15 Crore).

  2. Financial Irregularities: Independent reviews revealed inflated connection costs averaging nearly three times international benchmarks and questionable billing practices.

  3. Implementation & Reporting Fraud: Audit findings flagged unrealistic reporting (e.g., claims of 765+ connections/day) and fake reporting by contractors without third-party verification.

  4. Technical Non-Compliance: Deployed network devices were not SNMP-ready, and the Network Management System (NMS) was non-functional, leaving infrastructure unmonitored and vulnerable.

  5. Falsified NMS Reporting: Contractors were found to be submitting fabricated reports regarding network uptime and connectivity status. The Network Management Systems (NMS), which are critical for real-time monitoring, were often non-functional or manipulated to show "active" connections for sites that were physically offline or non-existent. This deception allowed providers to claim payments for services never rendered.

  6. Funding Mismanagement: Significant portions of the project budget were allocated to non-essential components or overpriced procurement contracts. For instance, the Civil Registration and Vital Statistics (CRVS) software component was allocated BDT 502.09 crore, heavily reliant on a loan agreement with the Chinese government that never materialized. Despite this, administrative overheads continued to drain public resources.

  7. Unrealistic Targets: The project reported installing over 765 connections per day—a logistical impossibility given the available manpower and equipment—raising red flags about the authenticity of the entire deployment data.

Reform Actions & Restructured Scope

To rescue the project and protect public funds, a comprehensive reform plan was executed, focusing on strict verification, component rationalization, and rebranding for relevance.

The reform plan re-aligns the project to ensure verifiable connectivity and practical skill development, while formally acknowledging the cancellation of unviable components.

1. Expanded End-User Connectivity (Revised Scope-1) In addition to the original targets, the project is expanding its reach to include critical public service points. The 1,09,244 broadband connections will now specifically include:

  • District Level Courts under the Ministry of Law.

  • All Post Offices under the Postal Department.

  • Bangladesh Diabetic Association centers.

  • National University Affiliated Colleges.

  • Textile Department Institutes.

  • Compliance: A functional Network Monitoring System (NMS) software is now a mandatory procurement deliverable to monitor these connections in real-time.

2. Upazila Seva & ICT Training Centers (Revised Scope-2) Instead of generic labs, the project will construct 400 "Upazila Seva & ICT Training Centers" via vertical extension of existing Upazila administration buildings (through LGED deposit work). These centers will serve as local hubs for:

  • Advanced IT skill courses for Youth and Women.

  • Startup idea incubation and mentorship.

  • Specialized training for government officials on e-filing, cybersecurity, and digital governance.

3. Establishment of Specialized Labs (Revised Scope-3) To boost national R&D capacity, the project will establish targeted high-tech labs in universities and the ICT Bhaban:

  • VLSI & AI Labs (17 Units): Setup in 17 government/private universities and ICT Bhaban to train students in IC design, FPGA, Machine Learning, and Generative AI, directly supporting the semiconductor engineer pipeline.

  • HPC Cloud GPU-Based Lab (1 Unit): A High-Performance Computing cloud lab at ICT Bhaban (initially hosted at the National Data Center) to provide multi-tenant supercomputing access for university researchers and industry stakeholders.

  • Digital Forensic Lab (1 Unit): Established at DoICT to handle computer, mobile, and network forensics, supporting law enforcement in cybercrime investigations across jurisdictions.

  • PKI Lab (1 Unit): A Public Key Infrastructure lab at DoICT to support the Certification Authority, developing software for Digital Certificate Management and E-Sign systems linked to NID.

4. ICT Tower (Tathya Projukti Bhaban) Construction The project includes the construction of a 12-storied ICT Tower (on a 21-story foundation) at Purbachal. This facility will house the Digital Leadership Academy, specialized research units, the Network Operations Center (NOC) for nationwide government network monitoring, and the DoICT head office.

5. Financial Rationalization and Component Cancellation

  • Saving Public Money: The reform successfully identified and cancelled non-viable components, saving hundreds of crores in public funds. The CRVS Software component (BDT 502.09 crore) and the Digital Village initiative (BDT 251.16 crore) were scrapped due to the failure of the Chinese G2G loan agreement and lack of feasibility. These funds were reallocated or returned to the exchequer.

  • Cost Re-baselining: The cost per connection was re-evaluated against international benchmarks. Inflated procurement costs for IT equipment were slashed, and a new "value-for-money" standard was enforced for all future tenders.

6. Technical Verification and Accountability

  • End-User Connectivity Verification: A new Network Monitoring System was introduced to replace the compromised legacy tools. This system mandates real-time, tamper-proof reporting of bandwidth usage. The definition of "connectivity" was shifted from simple physical installation to verified active usage, ensuring that payments are only released for functional connections.

  • Third-Party Audits: To counter falsified reporting, a mandatory third-party verification mechanism was established. Independent technical auditors now physically inspect random sites to validate the data submitted by contractors before any bill is processed.

7. Rebranding and Strategic Realignment

  • Joy Set Center to "Smart Service & Employment Training Centre": The project component previously named "Joy Set Center" was rebranded to "Smart Service & Employment Training Centre" to better reflect its core objective of youth employment and skill development, removing political branding.

  • Sheikh Russel Digital Lab Reform: Similarly, the "Sheikh Russel Digital Lab" initiative was restructured. Instead of merely supplying hardware that often went unused, the focus shifted to establishing 20 Specialized Labs (VLSI, AI, Robotics, Cyber Security) at universities to drive high-value skills, rather than generic computer literacy.

Impact of Reform: These decisive actions have transformed the EDC project from a source of financial leakage into a transparent infrastructure initiative. By stopping payments for fake connections and cancelling unfeasible components, the government has saved significant public capital while ensuring that the digital connectivity actually reaches the intended rural beneficiaries. The project now operates under a "Zero Tolerance" policy for data manipulation, setting a new standard for accountability in public ICT procurement.





7.2.3 Expected Outcomes

  • Transparent, corruption-free project implementation with full audit trails

  • Functioning, monitored, and secure digital infrastructure across all deployment sites

  • Greater taxpayer value with reduced cost per connection aligned to international benchmarks

  • Scalable model for future connectivity projects with embedded accountability mechanisms

7.2.4 Key Performance Indicators

KPI

Baseline (2024)

Target (2027)

Cost per connection vs. benchmark

3x international avg.

≤1.2x international avg.

Third-party verification rate

0%

100%

SNMP-compliant devices deployed

<30%

100%

NMS operational coverage

Non-functional

100% real-time monitoring

Payment release without verification

>BDT 99 crore

Zero tolerance



7.3 Enhancing Digital Government and Economy (EDGE) Project Reform

7.3.1 Project Overview

The EDGE Project, supported by the World Bank, represents a significant investment in Bangladesh's digital government infrastructure and economic enablement. The project encompasses multiple components including cybersecurity capacity building, digital service delivery enhancement, and foundational system upgrades.

7.3.2 Strategic Infrastructure Overhaul

The Critical Capacity Crisis At the onset of the Interim Government, a diagnostic review of the Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC) revealed a critical bottleneck in the National Data Center (NDC). Resource utilization had reached dangerous levels—with CPU occupancy at ~82% and storage at ~78%. This "capacity crunch" posed an immediate threat to digital governance continuity, as expanding data center capacity via traditional procurement is typically a complex, time-consuming process.

Historically, World Bank-funded projects in this sector have suffered from slow implementation cycles (averaging 7 years for completion) and low fund utilization (averaging ~18%), often leaving significant allocations unused. With the EDGE project approaching the end of its lifecycle, addressing this crisis through standard procedures was deemed nearly impossible.

The Reform Intervention Recognizing the urgency, the government engaged in rapid high-level negotiations with the World Bank and relevant ministries to restructure specific components of the EDGE project. The objective was threefold: immediate capacity enhancement, service simplification, and qualitative modernization. Through agile project management and the dedicated efforts of BCC as the implementing agency, a comprehensive infrastructure overhaul was executed within strict timelines—a feat previously considered unachievable in the public sector.

Modernization of Monitoring and Security

The review identified severe lapses in the legacy monitoring framework. Virtual Machines (VMs) often lacked active monitoring agents, meaning service failures went undetected until user complaints were filed, leading to prolonged diagnoses and service interruptions. Furthermore, the absence of robust uplink monitoring meant that capacity crunches caused by DDoS attacks or BGP protocol anomalies could not be proactively detected.

Under the EDGE reform, these vulnerabilities have been addressed. BCC has deployed a proactive, real-time monitoring architecture that ensures comprehensive visibility from the network layer to the application layer, significantly enhancing service reliability and security posture.

Deployment of Nutanix Cloud Platform (NCP)

To break the cycle of fragmented infrastructure, the project successfully launched the Nutanix Cloud Platform (NCP). This Hybrid Multi-Cloud infrastructure integrates computation, storage, virtualization, and networking into a single unified stack.

  • Impact: Government institutions can now seamlessly manage applications and data across on-premise private clouds, edge locations, and public clouds, ensuring flexibility and scalability.

Establishing Platform as a Service (PaaS) & Microservices

A critical barrier to Bangladesh’s digital evolution has been the "Silo Culture" of government data centers. Most existing systems lack essential features like Disaster Recovery (DR), standardized Cyber Security, or DevOps-friendly environments. Integration with the National Data Center was historically difficult, resulting in a near-zero adoption of Microservices Architecture in public services.

To align with global standards of cost optimization, performance, and scalability, the EDGE project has deployed a Platform as a Service (PaaS) layer.

  • Technology: A CNCF-certified Kubernetes platform.

  • Capabilities: Features Source-to-Image (S2I), built-in CI/CD pipelines, and seamless GitOps integration.

  • DevSecOps: The platform includes integrated container registries with vulnerability scanning, ensuring security at build, deploy, and run stages. It supports High Availability (HA), Disaster Recovery (DR), and provides built-in storage services for block, object, and file types.

Enabling Legal Compliance for Critical Information Infrastructure (CII)

The deployed NCP and PaaS infrastructures act as the technical backbone for enforcing three landmark regulations: the Personal Data Protection Ordinance 2025, the National Data Governance Ordinance 2025, and the Cyber Safety Ordinance 2025.

  • CII Protection: For the first time, the 35 agencies designated as Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) have a platform capable of meeting strict compliance guidelines—a milestone for national digital sovereignty.

  • Data Classification: The infrastructure supports the 4-tier data classification model, facilitating secure cross-border trade and alignment with global data protection standards.

  • Cloud First Policy: This ecosystem enables a transition to "Cloud Native" capacity, Containerized Clouds, and CDNs, paving the way for a citizen-friendly digital ecosystem. Negotiations have also been initiated with major traffic providers like Meta to establish local data centers, aimed at reducing internet costs and improving latency.

Resource Optimization and the National GPU Cloud

Amidst a global chip supply crisis and currency devaluation, the project focused heavily on resource optimization.

  • CPU Optimization: Through technical re-engineering, BCC successfully reclaimed and added over 1,000 vCPUs to the national pool.

  • National GPU Cloud: Leveraging underutilized assets from the BDSET project, the Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority (BHTPA) and BCC have integrated 20+ NVIDIA Volta Architecture Tensor Core GPUs into a sharable Cloud Resource Management Platform.

    • Capacity: Delivers ~2,240 teraFLOPS of Deep Learning capacity, equivalent to the computational power of over 900 CPUs.

    • Access: This is the first public sector GPU cloud open to academia, the gaming industry, and researchers for High-Performance Computing (HPC) tasks such as Generative AI, Threat Intelligence, and Geo-science modeling.

This infrastructure reform is the engine powering the shift from fragmented portals to a true Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI).

  • The Connectivity Highway: The government is constructing a digital highway based on interoperability (mandated by NDGO 2025), centralized source code repositories (BCC/GitHub), and enhanced DevOps capacity at a2i.

  • One ID & Data Reliability: Addressing the crisis of low-accuracy identity data (errors in names, DOBs across agencies), the reform prioritizes the "One ID" foundation. By moving towards Blockchain or Federated Learning-based data management, the system ensures that citizen data is updated automatically across all 17-42 connected departments, eliminating the harassment of repetitive corrections.

7.3.3 National Security Operations Center (N-SOC) Establishment

A $5 million proposal has been submitted to the World Bank through the EDGE project for financial assistance to establish the National Security Operations Center (N-SOC). This critical infrastructure will serve as the nerve center for national cybersecurity monitoring and incident response.

Key N-SOC Components:

  • 24/7 security monitoring capabilities for Critical Information Infrastructure (CII)

  • Digital forensics laboratory for cybercrime investigation support

  • Threat intelligence platform for proactive defense

  • Integration with N-CERT for coordinated incident response

7.3.4 Revitalizing the Research and Innovation Centre (RIC)

The Research and Innovation Centre (RIC) initiative was originally conceived as the "brain" of the EDGE project; designed to establish 10 cutting-edge R&D hubs across public universities to foster Industry-Academia collaboration. However, a diagnostic review by the Interim Government revealed severe planning mismanagement and financial irregularities that had rendered the initiative largely ineffective.

Instead of functioning as incubators for "Deep Tech" and national problem-solving, the RICs had drifted into administrative lethargy. A significant portion of the funds allocated for research was diverted towards non-essential "soft" activities such as lavish opening ceremonies, repetitive seminars, foreign tours, and general training programs; rather than actual hardware procurement, prototyping, or patent development. A review of the RIC projects to disburse the rest of the funds found severe irregularities in project selection and evaluation mechanisms both from the government and the donor organization’s end. Promising projects suffered from poor procurement planning, hindering successful implementation.
Consequently, despite heavy investment, the initiative failed to produce tangible intellectual property or commercially viable solutions, leading to critical observations from development partners regarding the "misuse of public funds." At the same time, the project planning did not incorporate any scope for professional expertise in identifying potential IP and negotiating IP and revenue share with grantees.

The Reform Intervention: Strategic Realignment To salvage this vital component, ICTD has reformed the RIC operational framework. The focus has been shifted from "Event Management" to "Product Development."

Financial Discipline and Allocation Reform

  • Strict "R&D First" Allocation: The budget structure has been overhauled to cap "administrative and event" expenses at a strict minimum. 80% of RIC grants are now mandatorily allocated to direct research costs—including lab equipment (GPUs, specialized sensors), prototyping materials, and researcher stipends.

  • Audit and Accountability: A retrospective audit has been initiated to investigate previous funding anomalies. Going forward, a "Milestone-Based Tranche System" has been introduced. Funds are no longer released as a lump sum; they are unlocked only upon the verification of tangible research outputs (e.g., successful prototype, published peer-reviewed paper, or industry partnership agreement).

Planning and Governance Restructuring

  • Centralized Problem Bank: Previously, research topics were chosen arbitrarily, leading to disconnected and low-impact projects. The reform introduces a a curated list of challenges sourced from industry and government agencies (e.g., AI for traffic management, agricultural IoT, Bangla NLP). RICs will now select projects solely from this strategic list to ensure national relevance.

  • Industry-Led Steering Committees: The governance structure has been democratized. The academic-heavy committees have been reconstituted to include industry CTOs and Deep-Tech founders. This ensures that research proposals are vetted for commercial viability and market readiness, bridging the gap between university labs and the industrial ecosystem.

Operational Focus: From General IT to Deep Tech

  • Specialized Clusters: To prevent duplication of effort, each of the 10 RICs should be focused towards becoming a specific domain based on their faculty strength (e.g., BUET for Robotics, SUST for AI/NLP, KUET for Mechatronics). This specialization ensures focused resource utilization and fosters domain expertise.

  • Intellectual Property (IP) Mandate: The reform will mandate that RICs must target Patent Filing and University Spin-offs as their primary KPIs. The success of a center is now measured not by the number of students trained, but by the number of startups incubated and patents registered.

These corrective measures are rapidly transforming RICs from passive "training centers" into active Innovation Engines. By stopping the leakage of funds into non-productive channels and enforcing a rigorous, output-oriented planning matrix, the EDGE project ensures that the RICs finally deliver on their promise of creating a self-reliant, knowledge-based economy.

7.3.5 Reform Priorities

  1. Sustainable Operation Model: Establish a sustainable operation model post-World Bank support, incorporating skilled personnel from both government and private sectors for continuous operations.

  2. Capacity Building Integration: Align with national cybersecurity certification tracks and encourage academic-industry-government collaboration to ensure an ongoing supply of skilled cybersecurity professionals.

  3. Technology Transfer: Ensure that financing models prioritize capacity transfer clauses so that local agencies retain skills and systems after donor project completion.

7.4 Digital Economy and Innovation Ecosystem Development (DEIED) Reform

7.4.1 Strategic Context

The Digital Entrepreneurship and Innovation Ecosystem Development (DEIED) project serves as the primary "soft-infrastructure" engine of the Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority (BHTPA). While physical parks provide the shell, DEIED provides the soul—nurturing human capital, fostering startup growth, and creating a decentralized innovation network across eight divisions.

However, previous iterations of the project suffered from disconnected planning, where equipment was procured without user consultation, and startup funding decisions were driven by bureaucratic procedure rather than market viability. The reformed strategy shifts DEIED from a procurement-heavy initiative to a Data-Driven Ecosystem Enabler, anchored by a robust digital backbone and industry-led governance.

7.4.2 Reform Focus Areas & Key Components

The reform focuses on optimizing four key pillars: Participatory Innovation Hubs, The Integrated Digital Backbone (LMS/MIS), Lifecycle Accelerator Support, and Expert-Led Governance.



A. University Innovation Hubs (UIHP): Optimization & Resource Sharing

Objective: Transform university campuses into active "Co-Creation Spaces" rather than just equipment storage rooms.

Reform Action: The operationalization of UIHPs in public and private universities will now follow a "Demand-Driven" model to avoid the "Ghost Lab" syndrome (where expensive equipment gathers dust).

  • Participatory Needs Assessment: Before equipping a hub, a mandatory consultation phase involving Student Bodies and Faculty Researchers must be conducted. This ensures that the fabrication labs (FabLabs) are stocked with tools relevant to that university’s specific research strengths (e.g., IoT sensors for an Agritech-focused university vs. GPU clusters for an AI-focused one).

  • Resource Rationalization (Shared Economy Model): To prevent duplication of expenditure, hubs will operate on a "Shared Resource" policy. Expensive assets (e.g., high-end 3D metal printers, PCB assembly lines) will be cataloged centrally. A student from University A can book time on a machine at University B via the central management system, maximizing asset utilization.

  • Active Mentorship: Hubs will not just be physical spaces but will house "Entrepreneurs-in-Residence"—industry leaders who provide direct guidance to student teams, bridging the gap between academic theory and commercial reality.

B. The Digital Backbone: UIHP Integrated LMS/MIS

Objective: Replace manual, fragmented tracking with a unified Learning Management System (LMS) and Management Information System (MIS) that tracks a startup’s journey from ideation to unicorn status.

Reform Action: Implementation of the UIHP LMS/MIS, a secure, role-based platform that acts as the operating system for the national innovation ecosystem.

1. Lifecycle Tracking & Stage-Gated Support:

  • The system will digitize the entire startup lifecycle: Application → Ideation → Prototyping → Pre-Seed Grant → Seed Grant → Commercialization.

  • It utilizes "Stage-Gate" workflows where funding is released only upon the verification of milestones (e.g., prototype completion), replacing arbitrary disbursements.

2. Intellectual Property (IP) & Data Protection:

  • RBAC & PDPO Compliance: To protect sensitive startup ideas and research data, the platform implements strict Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).

  • Privacy First: Aligned with the Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO), the system manages consent for data sharing. Access to intellectual property (IP) is restricted to authorized mentors and jury members only, with comprehensive audit logs to prevent IP theft.

3. Advanced Mentoring & Learning Analytics:

  • Mentor Matching: The system uses domain tagging to match student teams with the right industry mentors (e.g., matching a fintech team with a banking CTO).

  • Learning Analytics: An AI-driven dashboard tracks student progress, engagement, and curriculum effectiveness, allowing BHTPA to measure the quality of innovation, not just the quantity of participants.

4. National Innovation Repository:

  • The system feeds into a Central Digital Hub, creating an institutional memory of all projects. This prevents "reinventing the wheel" by allowing new cohorts to learn from the case studies and technical outcomes of previous teams (where IP sharing is permitted).

C. Structured Accelerator Programs: The "Unicorn Pipeline"

Objective: Provide continuous support for startups between phases, ensuring they survive the "Valley of Death."

Reform Action: Moving beyond generic training, the project will implement targeted tracks with continuous support:

  • Women-Led Digital Enterprise Accelerator: Dedicated support for female founders to increase gender diversity, featuring specific mentorship on overcoming systemic funding barriers.

  • Deep-Tech & Growth Tracks: Specialized support for high-potential ventures (Series A readiness). The LMS/MIS will flag high-performing startups for "Fast-Track" support, connecting them directly with Venture Capitalists even after they graduate from the university program.

  • Post-Graduation Tracking: The system will continue to monitor alumni startups for 2–5 years post-graduation, tracking revenue, job creation, and IP filings to measure long-term economic impact.

D. Expert-Led Governance & Triple-Helix Coordination

Objective: Remove bureaucratic bottlenecks in funding selection and policy planning.

Reform Action: Radical restructuring of the decision-making committees.

  • Decentralized Selection Committees: Startup selection and funding decisions will no longer be dominated by generalist bureaucrats. Selection committees must comprise 70% Industry Specialists, including:

    • Active Venture Capitalists (VCs).

    • Domain Experts (e.g., Doctors for Healthtech, Engineers for Robotics).

    • Successful Founders.

  • Triple-Helix Coordination Council: A standing body representing Industry, Academia, and Regulators will meet quarterly to align DEIED activities with market needs. This ensures that:

    • Academia produces relevant research.

    • Industry provides the problem statements and funding.

    • Regulators (Central Bank, BTRC) clear policy hurdles for new innovations (Sandboxes).

7.4.3 Strategic Impact

By combining participatory planning, a secure digital backbone (LMS/MIS), and expert-led governance, the reformed DEIED project will transition from a grant-giving mechanism to a true National Innovation Engine, capable of producing sustainable, investable, and globally competitive startups.

7.5 Institute of Frontier Technology (IFT) Establishment (Formerly BIFT)

7.5.1 Strategic Context

The Institute of Frontier Technology (IFT) represents a strategic evolution of the project formerly known as the Bangladesh Institute of Frontier Technology (BIFT). Shifting from a standalone infrastructure project, IFT is now being established as a premier academic and research institution through a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) between the Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority (BHTPA) and BUET, signed on September 10, 2025.

7.5.2 Reform Focus Areas

A. Strategic Partnership with BUET

  • Model: The institute will operate under a "Double Affiliation" model where BUET faculty members serve as directors and adjunct faculty, ensuring academic rigor and global standards.

  • Governance: governed by a high-level Board of Governors (BoG) chaired by the Vice Chancellor of BUET, ensuring autonomy and agility.

B. Dual-Campus Structure To balance academic depth with regional development, IFT will operate across two campuses:

  1. BUET Campus, Dhaka: Focusing on high-end research, postgraduate education, and deep-tech innovation.

  2. Shibchar, Madaripur: Serving as an extended campus for specialized training, large-scale infrastructure, and applied technology labs.

C. Core Mandate & Facilities

  • Frontier Technologies: Dedicated research wings for AI, Robotics, Quantum Computing, and Semiconductor design.

  • Zero-Waste Campus: The infrastructure will be designed as a green, carbon-neutral campus featuring at least four advanced innovation labs and space for eight resident startups.

  • Sustainability: A self-sustaining operational model will be developed, leveraging revenue from specialized training, patents, and industry collaborations.



D. Governance Structure

To ensure autonomy and academic rigor, the IFT will be governed by a 14-member Board of Governors (BoG) chaired by the Vice Chancellor of BUET. The Board includes representatives from UGC, BHTPA, line ministries, and industry experts.
+2

E. Sustainability Model

BHTPA will provide capital funding for construction and labs , as well as operating budgets for the initial two years. A sustainable operational framework, including revenue sharing from training and patents, will be developed jointly.

7.6 Enhancement of Bangla Language in ICT (EBLICT) Reform

7.6.1 Background and Context

The “Enhancement of Bangla Language in ICT through Research & Development (EBLICT)” initiative is a national R&D and service delivery programme anchored in Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC) under the ICT Division. Its core mandate is to strengthen Bangla language enablement across government and citizen-facing digital services by developing reusable language technologies (NLP, speech, and translation), building high-quality datasets, and operationalizing these assets as platforms (e.g., bangla.gov.bd) and public-facing tools.

The attached brochures illustrate how EBLICT has already moved beyond research prototypes to deploy multiple production services such as Bangla machine translation, spell/grammar correction (“সঠিক”), sentiment analysis, a Bangla virtual assistant, and speech-to-text/text-to-speech. The diverse services offered positions the programme as a foundational layer for Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) and service transformation.

In 2025, the government also launched “kagoj.ai”, a Bangla-centric AI platform aimed at accelerating official documentation and digitization workflows through Bangla OCR and writing assistance, with integrated speech capabilities. This expands the national product portfolio and creates a clear opportunity to rationalize, standardize, and scale Bangla language AI services under a unified operating model.

7.6.2 Current Status and Accomplishments

The EBLICT project has made significant strides in digitizing the Bangla language and creating foundational NLP tools. Recent launches, including the Kagoj.ai platform, demonstrate the project's evolving capability to deliver consumer-facing AI products.

Key Achievements:

  • Integrated Service Platform (bangla.gov.bd): A centralized portal hosting AI and language technologies, including a corpus platform, resource repository, and developer control dashboard.

  • Kagoj.ai: A recently launched, state-of-the-art AI platform dedicated to document processing. It features advanced OCR (Optical Character Recognition) for digitization, text-to-speech, and speech-to-text capabilities, serving as a critical tool for digitizing government archives and legal documents.

  • Bangla Machine Translator: A neural machine translation system capable of translating between Bangla and 10 major world languages (including English, Arabic, French, Spanish, and Japanese), facilitating cross-border communication and commerce.

  • Essential NLP Tools: Development of 16 core software components, including:

    • ‘Dhwani’ (IPA Converter): For phonetic standardization.

    • Screen Reader: Enhancing digital accessibility for the visually impaired.

    • Sentiment Analysis: Tools for analyzing public opinion and social media data.

    • Bangla Spell & Grammar Checker: Ensuring linguistic accuracy in digital content.

  • Crowdsourcing Platform: A dedicated initiative (collection.bangla.gov.bd) to collect diverse linguistic data from the public to train robust machine learning models.

Kagoj.ai (Bangla AI platform)
