kagoj.ai

Govt officials, enterprises, citizens

Bangla AI platform for writing, official documentation, and OCR-based digitization.



7.6.3 Rationale for Reform and Current Challenges

Despite these successes, the project operates within the constraints of a development project cycle, which poses risks to long-term sustainability and scalability.

  1. Sustainability Risk: As a "project," EBLICT faces funding cycles that threaten the 24/7 availability and maintenance of critical APIs and services once the project timeline concludes.

  2. Closed Ecosystem: Currently, many tools operate as web-based interfaces or closed APIs. While many datasets have been made public and some services have public facing APIs, and There is a lack of open access to model weights and raw datasets, which stifles innovation in the private sector and academia.

  3. Technological Shift: The global AI landscape has shifted towards Large Language Models (LLMs), Vision Language Models (VLM), and edge computing. The current infrastructure needs to evolve from basic NLP tools to advanced, multimodal Generative AI systems.

  4. Developer Experience: The lack of standardized, production-ready Client Tools (SDKs) and public APIs limits the integration of these technologies into third-party applications by startups and software firms.

  5. Fragmented delivery: multiple tools/endpoints without a single, versioned API catalogue and developer onboarding path.

  6. Production reliability and capacity: limited 24/7 operations, monitoring, and incident response for citizen-scale workloads.

  7. Compute constraints for modern models: GPU availability, cost, and scheduling; difficulty supporting large models, VLM, and speech at scale.

  8. Data governance and licensing: ensuring datasets are auditable, rights-cleared, and privacy-preserving while still usable for training and open release.

  9. Quality evaluation gaps: Benchmark regimes across MT, STT, TTS, OCR, and LLM-style tasks; missing acceptance criteria for releases.

  10. Talent and research pipeline: sustaining top-tier publications and conference participation requires structured research time, partnerships, and incentives.

  11. Interoperability: weak integration patterns with other DPI layers and with government cloud hosting standards.

7.6.4 Reform vision and strategic objective

The reform goal is to evolve EBLICT from a collection of useful tools into a unified national platform for Bangla language AI—operated with production-grade reliability, aligned to government digital architecture, and designed for rapid reuse by ministries, private innovators, and academia.

Strategic objectives:

  • API-first platformization: expose each core capability (MT, OCR, STT, TTS, spell/grammar, sentiment, assistant) through documented, versioned APIs and SDKs.

  • Open assets where feasible: publish model weights (including modular weights for large models), training/evaluation datasets, and documentation under clear licenses and governance.

  • Next-generation capability development: establish VLM (vision-language) R&D for OCR+understanding, form understanding, and multimodal assistants.

  • Sustainable data flywheel: deploy a crowdsourced data collection and labeling toolchain (parser–pause tagger, human-in-the-loop review) to continuously improve models.

  • Operational excellence: transition selected services to 24/7 operation with defined SLAs, hosted on government cloud infrastructure (NDC and/or national cloud options).

  • Advanced efficiency: prune and compress models to reduce inference cost and enable edge deployments (offline/low-bandwidth use cases).

  • Global research visibility: increase submissions to top AI/NLP/VLM venues and Q1 journal publications; strengthen international collaborations.

7.6.5 Strategic Future Outlooks

To address these challenges and position Bangladesh as a leader in AI R&D, the EBLICT initiative will undergo a comprehensive reform focusing on eight strategic pillars:

1. API-First Architecture and Client Tools

The reform will transition EBLICT from a portal-based service to an API-first platform. All core technologies (OCR, TTS, Translation, Kagoj.ai services) will be exposed via standardized, high-performance APIs.

  • Action: Release official Client SDKs (Software Development Kits) for Python, JavaScript, and mobile platforms (iOS/Android) to allow developers to integrate Bangla AI capabilities into their apps seamlessly.

2. Open Science Initiative: Public Model Weights and Datasets

Moving away from a "black box" approach, the project will embrace Open Science principles.

  • Action: Publish pre-trained model weights (e.g., BERT, GPT-style models, translation engines) and anonymized datasets on national repositories and global hubs like HuggingFace.

  • Modular Release: Release large models in modular structures to allow researchers to fine-tune specific components without high compute costs.

3. Vision Language Models (VLM) Development

The next frontier of AI involves understanding images and text together.

  • Action: Develop native VLMs capable of describing images in Bangla, performing visual question answering (VQA), and extracting information from unstructured documents (OCR in the wild). This is critical for accessibility and digitizing archives.

4. Advanced Crowdsourcing Tools

To build high-quality datasets for prosody (speech rhythm) and advanced grammar, simple text collection is insufficient.

  • Action: Develop sophisticated annotation tools, specifically a Parser-Pause Tagger, to collect granular data on speech pauses and syntactic parsing. This will significantly improve the naturalness of TTS engines and the accuracy of grammar checkers.

5. Institutional Merger with BCC for 24/7 Availability

To ensure the permanence of these digital assets, the EBLICT project will be institutionalized.

  • Action: Merge the project’s infrastructure and personnel into the Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC) as a permanent "National Language Technology Wing."

  • Service Level: Transition to government cloud hosting with guaranteed 24/7 uptime, redundant architecture, and dedicated DevOps support to serve critical national applications (e.g., judicial systems, emergency services).

6. Robotics and IoT in Native Languages

AI must move from the cloud to the physical world.

  • Action: Research and develop lightweight models for Robotics and Internet of Things (IoT) devices that support voice commands and interaction in Bangla and the ethnic languages of Bangladesh. This ensures that smart home devices, agricultural IoT, and service robots remain accessible to the entire population.

7. Model Pruning and Edge AI

To reduce inference costs and enable AI on low-cost smartphones, model efficiency is paramount.

  • Action: Establish a dedicated R&D track for Model Pruning, Quantization, and Distillation. The goal is to create "TinyML" versions of Bangla models that run entirely on-device, preserving privacy and reducing latency.

8. Research Excellence and Global Presence

The reform aims to elevate Bangladesh’s status in the global AI research community.

  • Action: Shift focus from software development to scientific contribution. Mandate and incentivize the publication of research findings in AAA-ranked conferences (ACL, NeurIPS, ICLR, CVPR) and Q1 journals. This peer-reviewed validation ensures our technology meets global state-of-the-art standards.

7.6.6 Reform KPI Framework (2025-2030)

<100 GB

1 TB High-Quality Text/Audio

10 TB Multimodal Data

7.6.7 Expected Outcomes

By 2030, the reformed EBLICT initiative will transform Bangla into a "first-class citizen" in the AI world. The transition to an API-first, open-science model will spawn a local ecosystem of AI startups building on top of national models. The institutional merger with BCC will guarantee that the digital voice of Bangladesh remains active, resilient, and accessible to future generations, preserving our linguistic heritage while powering our digital future.

7.7 Public Procurement Reform for ICT Projects

7.7.1 Role of PPA and PPR in Digital Transformation

The Public Procurement Act (PPA) 2006 and the Public Procurement Rules (PPR) 2008 laid the legal and institutional foundation for transparent and technology-driven procurement. The implementation of the Electronic Government Procurement (e-GP) system represents one of the most significant milestones achieved under this legal framework.

e-GP System Achievements (as of April 2025):

Metric

Value

Agencies on e-GP

1,483

Procuring Entities (PEs)

12,975

Registered Tenderers

130,640

Tenders Invited

935,576

Contract Awards

704,404

Total Tender Value

USD 113.55 Billion

Total Contract Value

USD 72.80 Billion

Estimated Savings

USD 1.1 Billion



7.7.2 Current Challenges in e-GP Implementation

  • Digital Literacy Gap: Many tenderers, contractors, and consultants lack digital equipment and skills for effective participation.

  • Capacity Constraints: Procuring entities, especially at local levels, lack trained personnel to operate the system effectively.

  • Connectivity Issues: Contractors in rural areas face internet access challenges, limiting participation in digital procurement.

  • System Performance: Users experience slow response times during peak usage and limited mobile device support.

  • Regulatory Gaps: Current legal framework does not fully address cloud hosting, digital signatures, and e-document validation requirements.

  • Underutilized Data: Extensive procurement data is not adequately used for performance monitoring, analytics, or risk mitigation.

7.7.3 PPR Reforms for Software Procurement

Bangladesh's existing Public Procurement Rules often prioritize cost over quality, resulting in suboptimal outcomes including vendor dependencies and integration issues. Drawing from global best practices including the UK's agile procurement frameworks, the EU's value-driven evaluations, and Australia's digital marketplaces, the following reforms are recommended:

  1. Balanced Evaluation Model: Transition to evaluation criteria where technical merits, including innovation and long-term scalability, account for at least 40% of assessment, while mandating clauses for open APIs and data interoperability to prevent vendor lock-in.

PPR Reform

  1. Agile Procurement Practices: Introduce agile methodologies by segmenting large projects into phased deliverables, favoring open-source solutions and cloud-based deployments with mandatory third-party security audits.

  2. Enhanced Tender Processes: Upgrade the e-GP platform with AI-assisted bid analysis for fairness, incorporate provisions for joint ventures with private entities, and streamline approvals to reduce procurement timelines.

  3. Citizen-Centric Outcome Requirements: Revise procurement rules to require mandatory user testing with specific groups (women, rural residents, persons with disabilities), service-level KPIs (completion rate, user satisfaction, processing time), and clear government ownership of data and systems.

7.7.4 Implementation Timeline

Pilot reforms will launch in 2025 for select projects, achieving full rollout by 2026 with ongoing evaluations. The recent amendment (4th May 2025) of Section 65 of the PPA made it mandatory for all Procuring Entities to use the e-GP portal where applicable, establishing the legal foundation for comprehensive digital procurement reform.

7.8 Consolidated Project Reform KPI Framework

The following framework establishes measurable indicators to track the progress and impact of project reforms across all flagship ICT initiatives:

Reform Area

KPI

2025 Target

2030 Target

EDC Project

Third-party verification compliance

100%

100%

EDC Project

Cost per connection vs. benchmark

≤1.5x

≤1.1x

EDGE Project

N-SOC operational status

Phase 1 deployed

Full operation

EDGE Project

CII coverage by N-SOC

50%

100%

NDC/BDCCL

Data center capacity utilization

Optimized

Scalable platform

NDC/BDCCL

Disaster recovery readiness

Plan approved

Fully operational

PPR Reform

Agile procurement pilots

5 projects

All ICT projects

PPR Reform

Technical merit weight in evaluation

≥40%

≥40%

Overall

Projects with NDGA compliance

50%

100%



7.9 Strategic Project Planning & Design Improvement

7.9.1 The Crisis of Poor Planning

Historically, ICT projects in Bangladesh have suffered from a "Plan-as-you-go" approach, leading to severe disconnects between objectives and outcomes. Projects are often designed in silos by individual ministries without a holistic view of the national digital architecture. This has resulted in:

  • Duplication of Expenditure: Multiple agencies procuring identical software (ERPs, Inventory Systems) or infrastructure, wasting hundreds of crores in public funds.

  • Disconnect from Reality: Projects designed by generalist bureaucrats or retired officials often lack technical depth, leading to procurement specifications that are either obsolete or vendor-locked from day one.

  • Implementation Delays: The absence of mapped-out phases means critical dependencies (e.g., waiting for fiber optics before buying servers) are missed, causing years of delay and cost overruns.

7.9.2 Reform Framework

The "Design-First" Mandate To rectify this, the government is instituting a rigorous Project Design & Planning Framework that must be cleared before any Development Project Proposal (DPP) is approved.

A. Expert-Led Planning Committees

  • Domain Expertise Over Political Appointments: Project steering committees must now include at least 40% technical domain experts (industry CTOs, solution architects, academic researchers) rather than relying solely on generalist officers or retired bureaucrats.

  • Role of the Chief Architect: No large-scale digital project (>50 Crore) will be approved without a technical vetting by the National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) to ensure compliance with the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDA).

B. Elimination of Silos & Duplication

  • The "Check-Before-Build" Rule: Before procuring new software, agencies must certify that no similar asset exists in the National Source Code Repository. Reusing existing government code is now mandatory where feasible.

  • Unified Feasibility Studies: For infrastructure projects, a centralized feasibility study by the Planning Commission’s digital wing will replace fragmented agency-level studies, ensuring a sector-wide view of requirements.

C. Coordination with Development Partners

  • Donor Harmonization: A new Digital Development Coordination Cell (DDCC) will be established to synchronize efforts with the World Bank, ADB, JICA, and other partners. This ensures that foreign-funded projects do not overlap but rather complement national priorities (e.g., one donor funds the backbone, another funds the last-mile access).

  • Data-Driven Policy Making: Project planning must be based on real-time data from the National Digital Dashboard, ensuring that investments target actual gaps (e.g., regions with low internet penetration) rather than politically selected constituencies.

7.9.3 Impact

This reform shifts the focus from "spending budget" to "delivering value." By ensuring that projects are professionally designed, technically vetted, and strategically aligned before a single Taka is spent, the government aims to eliminate the "Digital Waste" that has plagued previous administrations and ensure that public money truly benefits the citizen.



7.10 Accountability and Monitoring Framework

7.10.1 Governance Structure

Effective project reform requires robust accountability mechanisms and continuous monitoring. The following governance structure ensures oversight and timely intervention:

  1. Project Reform Steering Committee: A high-level committee chaired by the Secretary of ICT Division with representation from BCC, a2i, NCSA, and relevant ministries to provide strategic oversight and resolve inter-agency issues.

  2. Independent Technical Review Board: External experts from academia and industry to conduct quarterly technical assessments and provide recommendations for course corrections.

  3. Real-Time Monitoring Dashboard: A National Digital Dashboard consolidating data from all flagship projects, enabling real-time progress monitoring accessible to policymakers and stakeholders.

  4. Public Accountability Mechanisms: Annual Digital Transformation Scorecards published to benchmark Bangladesh against regional and global peers, with citizen feedback mechanisms including surveys, grievance redressal portals, and social media analytics.



7.10.2 Enforcement Mechanisms

  • Performance reviews linked to budgetary allocations for ministries and agencies

  • Divisional investigations under PPR for identified irregularities with clear penalty provisions

  • Mandatory disclosure of project status, expenditure, and deviation reports

  • Integration of reform compliance into annual civil service performance evaluations

7.11 Conclusion and Future Outlook

Project reform is not merely a corrective measure but a strategic imperative for Bangladesh's digital transformation. The reforms outlined in this chapter address systemic weaknesses while establishing governance frameworks that will prevent recurrence of past challenges. By re-baselining costs, enforcing technical compliance, mandating third-party verification, and aligning all projects with NDGA standards, Bangladesh is building a foundation for sustainable, transparent, and citizen-centric digital infrastructure.

The success of these reforms will be measured not only by improved project outcomes but also by enhanced public trust in government ICT initiatives. As Bangladesh progresses toward its vision of becoming a regional digital leader by 2030, the lessons learned from project reform will inform future initiatives, ensuring that every public investment in digital infrastructure delivers maximum value to citizens and contributes to inclusive national development.

The integration of reformed projects with the broader Digital Public Infrastructure ecosystem will enable Bangladesh to achieve its ambitious targets: $5 billion in ICT exports, 7-8 million ICT professionals, and a position among the top 15 nations in the UN E-Government Development Index. These outcomes depend on maintaining reform momentum, institutionalizing accountability mechanisms, and continuously adapting to technological and governance challenges.

---

End of Chapter 7

8: Startup Ecosystem and Innovation

8.1 Introduction

Over the last decade, Bangladesh's startup ecosystem has shown promising growth, driven by digital adoption, a young population, and government-led innovation initiatives. The ecosystem has evolved from fewer than 100 companies to a dynamic landscape where digital startups across fintech, e-commerce, logistics, healthtech, and education are addressing real-world challenges and creating employment opportunities. Success stories like bKash—which attracted a US$250 million investment from SoftBank—have demonstrated that Bangladeshi startups can achieve venture-scale growth and global recognition.

However, the ecosystem remains fragmented, with multiple government agencies working in silos, overlapping mandates, and inconsistent policy execution. Access to early-stage finance remains constrained, links between universities and industry are weak, and regulatory bottlenecks impede growth. Compared to regional peers like India and Vietnam, Bangladesh's venture capital flows remain small, and startups face high barriers to scaling both domestically and internationally.

To address these structural inefficiencies and transform the digital economy into a driver of inclusive growth and exports, this chapter proposes the establishment of a Startup Authority / Commission—a centralized, empowered body responsible for policy formulation, implementation, coordination, enforcement, and approval of new initiatives under the government system. The Authority will act as the single coordinating backbone for the startup ecosystem, overseeing fund-of-funds (FoF), VC and accelerator enablement, incubation, infrastructure, R&D, government service digitization, regulatory facilitation, and innovation infrastructure such as makers labs.

8.2 Current Ecosystem Landscape

8.2.1 Ecosystem Strengths

Despite coordination challenges, Bangladesh's startup ecosystem has several inherent strengths that provide a foundation for accelerated growth:

Government Commitment to Innovation: Dedicated programs such as innovation funds, iDEA, ICT innovation grants, and digitization initiatives signal strong policy intent. State-backed venture capital initiatives like Startup Bangladesh Limited (SBL), established in March 2020 with a US$65 million flagship fund, provide early market confidence and demonstrate government commitment to the startup sector.

Growing Entrepreneurial Talent Pool: Bangladesh has a large youth demographic with increasing exposure to technology, freelancing, and digital entrepreneurship. According to the Oxford Internet Institute's Online Labour Index, Bangladesh accounts for approximately 15% of global freelance labour supply, ranking in the top three countries globally. BASIS estimates over 650,000 active freelancers, with more than 500,000 registered on international platforms generating approximately US$400 million annually in foreign exchange. This freelance ecosystem feeds directly into the startup talent pipeline.

Emerging Investment Landscape: The presence of global and local VCs, angel networks, and co-investment mechanisms has grown steadily. Initial success stories—bKash, Pathao, ShopUp, Chaldal, ShareTrip—have created proof-of-concept for venture-scale businesses, attracting international investor attention to the Bangladesh market.

Digital Public Infrastructure Foundation: National ID systems, mobile financial services (MFS), and expanding broadband coverage provide a strong base for civic tech and govtech startups. With over 100 million mobile financial service users and growing internet penetration, Bangladesh offers a substantial addressable market for digital solutions.

8.2.2 Critical Challenges

Despite progress, startups continue to face systemic barriers that constrain ecosystem growth:

Fragmented Institutional Landscape: Multiple ministries and agencies operate independently, leading to duplication, delays, and policy inconsistency. iDEA, SBL, BHTPA, a2i, and various ministry-level initiatives often work in silos without coordinated strategy or shared objectives. This fragmentation creates confusion for entrepreneurs and investors alike, and results in inefficient allocation of public resources.

Weak Regulatory Navigation: Startups struggle to understand and comply with regulations across sectors—finance, health, telecom, logistics. There is no single-window mechanism for approvals or guidance. A fintech startup, for instance, may need to navigate Bangladesh Bank regulations, ICT Division policies, and sector-specific compliance requirements without coordinated support. This regulatory friction diverts entrepreneurial energy from innovation to bureaucratic navigation.

Inefficient Capital Deployment: Public innovation funds often focus on grants rather than market-linked outcomes. While grants serve a purpose in early ideation, they do not build the discipline required for venture-scale growth. Additionally, there is limited capacity building for VCs, accelerators, and angels to operate sustainably. The absence of a vibrant domestic VC ecosystem means Bangladesh lacks the institutional investor base to fuel early-stage growth—unlike India and Vietnam where local VCs and corporates anchor early funding rounds.

Limited R&D and Commercialization Pathways: Research funding is largely disconnected from startup commercialization pathways. Universities produce research papers, but these rarely convert into commercial products, patents, or startups. The weak linkage between academia, industry, and startups represents a significant missed opportunity for technology transfer and innovation-driven economic growth.

Governance Deficit: Allegations of inefficiency and lack of transparency reduce ecosystem trust. Without performance-based accountability mechanisms, it is difficult to assess which interventions work and which resources are being deployed effectively. This governance gap undermines both domestic and international investor confidence.

Limited Exit Pathways: Rare successful exits prevent the recycling of capital and experience. Without exits, investor confidence remains low and the founder-to-founder investment cycle—critical for ecosystem maturation—cannot develop. The absence of a startup-friendly IPO framework at the Dhaka Stock Exchange further constrains exit options.

Geographic Concentration: Innovation activity remains concentrated in Dhaka and, to a lesser extent, Chattogram. Regional entrepreneurs face limited access to investors, mentors, co-working spaces, and support infrastructure. This concentration not only limits inclusive growth but also leaves regional market opportunities underexplored.

8.3 Strategic Initiatives Updates

A. Investment, Funding & Exit Strategy

1. Direct Startup Investment SBL has accelerated capital deployment to fuel high-potential ventures.

  • Progress: A total of BDT 17.15 Crore has been invested in five key startups: 10 Minute School, Shikho, Chaldal, Bookshunari, and i-Farmer.

  • Pipeline: Follow-on investment for one startup is currently in process, while two new investment proposals are pending Board approval.

2. Launch of "Fund of Funds" (FoF) To leverage private capital, SBL is operationalizing a "Fund of Funds" structure.

  • Status: Policy documents are finalized. BDT 50 Crore has been received from the Finance Division, with a request for an additional BDT 25 Crore submitted.

  • Implementation: A "Legal Due Diligence Partner" has been onboarded to ensure compliance. A Request for Expressions of Interest (EOI) for fund managers is ready for release.

3. Portfolio Exit Milestone Demonstrating the maturity of the ecosystem, SBL is executing its first exit.

  • Status: Exit negotiations with one portfolio startup began in December 2025 and have reached the final stage.

  • Target: An official exit announcement is expected by the first week of February 2026.

B. Policy Formulation & Governance

4. National Startup Policy SBL is leading the formulation of a unified national policy to streamline the startup landscape.

  • Timeline: The drafting committee held its first meeting on January 6, 2026. Two additional meetings and a workshop are scheduled for Feb-March.

  • Goal: The final draft will be submitted to the ICT Division by April 2026.

5. Formation of "Startup Board" To facilitate public listing and exit routes, SBL is working with the capital market regulator.

  • Action: A Concept Note has been submitted to the Bangladesh Securities and Exchange Commission (BSEC) to form a dedicated "Startup Board."

  • Objective: This board will provide strategic direction, ensure transparent management, and protect investor interests in the public market.

C. Portfolio Value Creation & Monitoring

6. Portfolio Monitoring & Impact Measurement Monitoring has been strengthened to provide "Smart Capital."

  • Value Addition: SBL is actively assisting startups with fundraising guidance, business development, and client acquisition.

  • Impact Framework: An integrated Impact Measurement & Monitoring (IMM) framework is being developed to quantify socio-economic contributions. This framework will be incorporated into SBL’s future Investment Guidelines.

  • Global Exposure: "Startup Bangladesh Portfolio Night" was organized to connect portfolio companies directly with foreign investors.

7. Communication & Branding

  • Activity: Continuous social media promotion and branding activities are being conducted to highlight the achievements of SBL, its portfolio startups, and the broader national ecosystem.

D. Ecosystem Infrastructure & Enablement

8. Ecosystem Data & Knowledge Infrastructure SBL is building the digital backbone for the ecosystem.

  • Startup Universe: A comprehensive database/dashboard has been created mapping over 1,250 active startups.

  • Expansion: Work is ongoing to develop an "Organization Universe" and "People Universe" to map the entire network of stakeholders. The initial dashboard is live and undergoing further development.

9. Advisory, Partnership & Enablement SBL is acting as a strategic advisor to key national and international partners.

  • Partnerships: Advisory and enablement support provided to Stanford Seed, Accelerating Bangladesh, EDGE RIC, NSU Startup Next, Build Bangladesh, and Needle Incubator.

  • Strategic Support: SBL provided design support for Business Incubation Centers (BIC), structured the "Narayanganj Business Innovation Summit," and advised on the "National Startup Platform."

10. Local & International Events

  • Executed: SBL successfully hosted "Bangladesh Startup Connect 2025", "Youth Startup Summit 2025", and two international webinars between March and April 2025 to foster networking and knowledge exchange.

E. Global Connectivity & Capacity Building

11. Global Membership & Integration To internationalize the ecosystem, SBL is building bridges with global venture hubs.

  • Action: SBL has initiated membership with the Singapore Venture & Private Capital Association (SVCA).

  • Benefits: This membership provides access to a global network of Fund Managers and Limited Partners (LPs), as well as discounted access to capacity-building training and roadshows in Singapore.

12. New Strategic Programs Three new strategic initiatives have been launched to address specific ecosystem needs:

  • Impact Measurement & Management (IMM): To track sustainable development goals.

  • SBL NRB Connect Program: To engage Non-Resident Bangladeshi experts and investors.

  • SBL Young Ambassador Program: To develop future leaders and campus advocates for the startup ecosystem.

13. Team Development Preparing the SBL team for the new "Fund of Funds" mandate.

  • Training: Initiatives have been taken to equip the team with advanced investment skills. This includes FMVA certification from the CFI Institute and weekly internal training sessions to ensure the team is ready to manage larger, more complex capital structures.

8.4 The Case for a Startup Authority / Commission

The challenges outlined above share a common root cause: the absence of centralized coordination, clear mandate allocation, and performance-based governance. Individual agencies—iDEA, SBL, BHTPA, a2i—have achieved results within their domains, but the ecosystem as a whole suffers from fragmentation, overlap, and inconsistent policy execution.

To address this, a Startup Authority / Commission is proposed as a structural shift from fragmented program management to coordinated ecosystem governance. This body would be responsible for:

  • Policy Formulation: Developing coherent, ecosystem-wide policies that align capital deployment, infrastructure provision, regulatory reform, and talent development

  • Implementation Oversight: Ensuring that policies translate into action across all stakeholder agencies

  • Coordination: Serving as the single backbone connecting all ecosystem players—startups, investors, incubators, universities, regulators, and government agencies

  • Enforcement: Establishing and monitoring performance metrics, ensuring accountability, and course-correcting where necessary

  • Approval Authority: Streamlining approvals for new initiatives, funding mechanisms, and regulatory relaxations

Critically, this model does not replace existing agencies. Rather, it redefines their roles, improves accountability, and ensures that public resources translate into scalable, sustainable startups. Each agency retains operational responsibility in its area of expertise, but operates within a coordinated framework with clear mandates and performance expectations.

8.4.1 International Benchmarks

The proposed Startup Authority draws on international best practices:

  • Startup India (India): A flagship initiative providing a single-window clearance system, tax exemptions, simplified compliance, and fund of funds mechanism that has catalyzed one of the world's fastest-growing startup ecosystems

  • Enterprise Singapore: Combines trade promotion, enterprise development, and startup support under one agency with clear KPIs and accountability mechanisms

  • Startup Chile: Government-backed accelerator program that attracted global startups through equity-free funding and visa facilitation, demonstrating how coordinated policy can position a country as an innovation hub

  • UK's British Business Bank: Operates fund of funds and co-investment programs (including EIS/SEIS tax incentives) that have mobilized billions in private capital for startups

These examples demonstrate that coordinated, empowered institutions with clear mandates can significantly accelerate ecosystem growth while leveraging—rather than crowding out—private capital.

8.5 Central Repository for Innovation, Startup, and Progress (CRISP)

8.5.1 Context: The Need for a Unified Data Backbone

Bangladesh’s startup ecosystem has witnessed exponential growth, yet it faces a critical challenge: information asymmetry. Currently, data regarding startups, investors, and support programs is fragmented across siloed databases, making it difficult for policymakers to track growth, for investors to discover investable assets, and for startups to access consolidated resources. To address this, the government will launch CRISP (Central Repository for Innovation, Startup, and Progress)—a dynamic, single-source-of-truth platform designed to map and monitor the entire innovation landscape.

8.5.2 Core Objectives and Features

CRISP will serve as the national digital backbone for the startup economy, focusing on three primary objectives:

  • Unified Ecosystem Registry: Creating a comprehensive, verified directory of all active stakeholders, including startups, investors (VCs, Angels), incubators, accelerators, and mentors.

  • Data-Driven Policymaking: providing real-time analytics and dashboards to the government (ICT Division, BHTPA) to monitor sector performance, employment generation, and funding inflows, enabling evidence-based policy interventions.

  • Investment Facilitation: Functioning as a discovery platform where global and local investors can filter startups by sector, stage, and revenue, thereby reducing due diligence friction and accelerating capital deployment.

8.5.3 Functional Architecture

CRISP will be built on a secure, interoperable architecture aligned with the National Digital Architecture (BNDA):

  • Automated Verification: Integration with NID and RJSC (Registrar of Joint Stock Companies) APIs to ensure that all registered entities are authentic and compliant.

  • Dynamic Profiles: Startups will maintain "live" profiles showcasing their traction, fundraising history, and impact metrics, which can be used as a "Digital Passport" to apply for government grants or tax incentives.

  • Interoperability: The platform will feature open APIs to connect with private sector platforms and international ecosystem trackers, ensuring Bangladesh's data is visible globally.

8.5.4 Strategic Impact

By establishing CRISP, Bangladesh aims to eliminate the "invisible startup" problem. The platform will ensure that support mechanisms—such as grants, tax holidays, and mentorship—reach deserving ventures transparently. Furthermore, it will market the Bangladesh startup ecosystem to the world by presenting a transparent, validated, and growing pipeline of innovation.

8.6 Proposed Institutional Architecture

The following framework assigns clear mandates to each stakeholder within the startup ecosystem, with the Startup Authority / Commission serving as the central coordinating body. This architecture ensures that each agency focuses on its core competency while operating within an integrated, accountable system.

Function

Execution Body

Key Mandate

Policy & Oversight

Startup Authority / Commission

Centralized policy formulation, approval, enforcement, and coordination across all ecosystem stakeholders. Sets ecosystem-wide KPIs and monitors performance.

Fund of Funds & VC Enablement

Startup Bangladesh Limited

Developing a market-driven Fund of Funds framework that deploys capital to venture capital firms. Institutionalizes VC Enabler functions to build long-term, profitable fund management capabilities in the domestic market.

Growth Level Capital

Startup Bangladesh Limited

Investment or co-investment in seed, pre-Series A, and Series A rounds. Focus on trust building among ecosystem players, investors, and stakeholders through transparent, market-aligned investment practices.

Accelerator & Angel Enablement

SBL or new organization

Establishing mechanisms to deploy accelerating capital with enabler support. Co-designing inclusive programs including marketplace creation, investment readiness training, and digital academy development to bridge the missing middle.

Incubation

iDEA

Adopting an enablement-first approach by empowering private incubators, accelerators, and venture studios. Fostering a private-sector-driven early-stage support ecosystem rather than government-run incubation.

Innovation Infrastructure

BHTPA

Developing a nationally coordinated infrastructure framework. Institutionalizing Makers Labs to catalyze hardware, deep-tech, and applied innovation. Managing Hi-Tech Park facilities for startup co-location.

R&D Enhancement

ICTD Innovation Fund

Market-aligned R&D funding linked to startups and commercialization pathways. Strengthening university-industry linkages for technology transfer.

Government Services Digitization

a2i

Developing a central design lab (ICTD Playground) as a cross-ministerial innovation platform. Engaging startups in solution design and delivery for national challenges.

Single Point Regulatory Services

All agencies, hosted by BHTPA

Central Regulatory Service Centers with all relevant ministries under one roof for streamlined approvals, compliance guidance, and regulatory navigation.

8.6.1 Rationale for Role Allocation

Each allocation reflects comparative advantage and lessons learned:

SBL for Fund of Funds: As Bangladesh's only government-sponsored VC fund, SBL has the mandate and initial capital base to anchor a Fund of Funds mechanism. Rather than SBL directly picking startup winners—which requires specialized expertise better found in private markets—SBL will select and fund professional VC managers who specialize in startup evaluation, portfolio management, and value addition. This approach leverages market efficiency while deploying government capital to catalyze private investment.

Fund of Funds (FoF) Structure

  • Initial FoF Size: BDT 500 Crore, with flexibility to scale further based on performance and ecosystem needs

  • Fund Life: 30 years, with option to transition to an evergreen fund thereafter

  • Investment Focus: Direct investments in partnership funds established with local and global VC fund managers mandated to invest in Bangladesh

  • Ecosystem Support: Portion allocated for government-approved incubators, accelerators, and challenger funds

  • Sector Approach: Stage and sector-agnostic, prioritizing high-growth sectors including financial services, e-commerce, logistics, healthcare, education, and agriculture

  • ESG Integration: Environmental, Social, and Governance principles embedded to ensure sustainable, long-term value creation





FoF Strategic Objectives

  1. Enhance mobilization of foreign capital and promote foreign market access for Bangladesh-focused funds

  2. Stimulate local funding to advance domestic VCs and attract funding from local limited partners (LPs) including corporates, family offices, and high-net-worth individuals

  3. Drive sustainable development by contributing toward impact goals aligned with national priorities

This represents a fundamental paradigm shift: instead of SBL directly picking startup winners (which requires expertise better found in private markets), SBL will select and fund professional VC managers who specialize in startup evaluation, portfolio management, and value addition. This approach leverages market efficiency while deploying government capital to catalyze private investment.

SBL Recent Investment Activity (September 2024 - January 2025)

During the transition period, SBL invested BDT 11.10 crore across nine startups, supporting both established platforms (Ten Minute School, Sheba Platform, ShareTrip) and early-stage ventures (ParentsCare for elderly healthcare, Dubotech Digital for underwater robotics, Sokrio Technologies for distribution management). This portfolio demonstrates SBL's focus on ventures addressing real-world challenges across healthcare, logistics, education, and technology sectors.

iDEA for Incubation Enablement: iDEA has built infrastructure (co-working spaces, labs) and networks through years of operation. Its future role should shift from direct grant disbursement to enabling a private-sector-driven incubation ecosystem. This means empowering private incubators, accelerators, and venture studios through training, certification, and performance-linked support—rather than competing with them.

BHTPA for Infrastructure: Bangladesh Hi-Tech Park Authority already manages physical infrastructure across multiple locations. Extending this mandate to include Makers Labs, Central Regulatory Service Centers, and regional innovation hubs leverages existing capabilities and real estate assets.

a2i for Govtech: a2i has demonstrated capability in digital service delivery and citizen-centric design through initiatives like the National Portal (over 2 billion transactions processed), Union Digital Centers (8,500+ centers serving 30 million users yearly), and various e-governance platforms. The ICTD Playground concept positions a2i as the bridge between government challenges and startup solutions.



8.7 Core Strategic Principle

The government's reformed approach is anchored in a fundamental principle: sustainable ecosystem growth requires private sector dynamism, with government creating the enabling environment rather than attempting to pick winners directly. This represents a strategic shift from uncoordinated funding to facilitation. To address structural inefficiencies, the government will shift from a fragmented approach to a coordinated model where specific agencies are empowered to deploy capital and resources directly. Unlike previous strategies that limited government participation to facilitation, this framework mandates active state participation in deploying catalytic capital to bridge market gaps.

8.7.1 The Facilitation-Led Model

Under the facilitation-led model, government capital is most effective when used to:

• De-risk private investment through Fund of Funds and co-investment mechanisms, making it attractive for professional VCs to enter the Bangladesh market

• Build ecosystem infrastructure—co-working spaces, labs, regulatory service centers—that private markets may underprovide due to coordination failures or public goods characteristics

• Create market-enabling policies through tax incentives (EIS/SEIS models), regulatory sandboxes, and streamlined approvals that reduce friction for private actors

• Provide training and capacity building for entrepreneurs, investors, and support organizations to strengthen the ecosystem's human capital



This approach recognizes that the ecosystem needs freedom to operate, compete, and fail—essential elements of entrepreneurial learning that cannot be engineered through government programs alone.

8.7.2 Capital Mobilization Architecture

While the ecosystem matures, the government will play a critical dual role:

Direct Investment: Deploying "Growth Level Capital" (Seed to Series A) via Startup Bangladesh Limited to bridge funding gaps where private risk appetite is low .

Ecosystem Activation: Using a Fund of Funds (FoF) structure to de-risk and attract professional venture capital firms to the Bangladesh market.

The reformed capital mobilization framework operates through five interconnected layers:

Layer

Mechanism

Government Role

Expected Impact

Fund of Funds

SBL deploys capital to professional VC fund managers

Capital provider; strategy oversight; impact alignment

Catalyze US$300-500M in VC fund formation by 2030

Angel & Accelerator Enablement

Tax incentives and co-investment programs

Policy design via NBR; compliance monitoring

10x increase in domestic angel investment

Debt Financing

Concessional lending through commercial banks

Regulatory framework; refinancing support

US$100M+ in startup-accessible debt capital

Infrastructure

Co-working spaces, labs, Hi-Tech Park facilities

Direct provision; subsidized access

12 regional hubs by 2030

Regulatory Enablement

Sandboxes, single-window clearance, tax holidays

Policy design; enforcement

50% reduction in compliance burden



8.8 Innovation Infrastructure Framework

Physical infrastructure forms the backbone of startup ecosystem development. While capital is essential, entrepreneurs also need access to facilities, equipment, networks, and regulatory support. BHTPA will lead the development of a nationally coordinated innovation infrastructure framework with three core components:

8.8.1 Makers Labs

Makers Labs address a critical gap in Bangladesh's startup ecosystem: the absence of facilities for hardware, deep-tech, and applied innovation. While software startups can operate from any location with internet access, hardware and deep-tech ventures require specialized equipment and facilities that are prohibitively expensive for individual startups.

• Specialized equipment for prototyping: 3D printers, CNC machines, electronics workbenches, testing facilities

• Technical mentorship and industry partnerships for commercialization pathways

• Shared infrastructure model reducing capital requirements for hardware startups

• Integration with university engineering programs for talent pipeline development

Target: Establish Makers Labs in 6 locations by 2028, co-located with Hi-Tech Parks and university engineering faculties.

8.8.2 Regional Innovation Hubs

To address geographic concentration, sector-focused regional innovation hubs will be established across all divisions. Each hub will specialize based on regional economic strengths and market opportunities:

Location

Sector Focus

Rationale

Target Launch

Proximity to financial institutions, largest consumer market, talent concentration

2029

8.8.3 Central Regulatory Service Centers

A single-window mechanism for startup approvals will be established through Central Regulatory Service Centers hosted by BHTPA. These centers will co-locate representatives from key regulatory bodies:

• Bangladesh Bank (fintech, payments, lending)

• BTRC (telecom, spectrum, digital services)

• DGDA (healthtech, pharmaceuticals)

• RJSC (company registration, compliance)

• NBR (tax registration, incentives)

• BIDA (foreign investment, work permits)

The goal is to reduce regulatory navigation time by 50% and provide startups with clear, consistent guidance on compliance requirements. Initial pilots will launch at Dhaka and Chattogram Hi-Tech Parks in 2025, with expansion to all regional hubs by 2028.

8.9 ICTD Playground: Govtech Innovation Platform

a2i will develop a central design lab—the ICTD Playground—serving as a cross-ministerial innovation platform. This initiative recognizes that government itself is both a massive market for digital solutions and a source of complex challenges that can drive innovation.

8.9.1 Core Functions

• Challenge Definition: Work with ministries to define specific problems suitable for startup solutions—from agricultural extension to healthcare delivery to urban mobility

• Sandbox Environment: Provide safe spaces for startups to test solutions with real government data and processes without full compliance burden

• Pilot Programs: Run structured pilots with successful startups, measuring impact and refining solutions before scale

• Procurement Pathways: Establish streamlined procurement mechanisms for scaling proven solutions into government service delivery

8.9.2 Startup Engagement Model

The ICTD Playground will operate through a structured engagement process:

• Open Innovation Challenges: Quarterly challenge announcements with defined problem statements, success criteria, and pilot budgets

• Technical Support: API access to government systems, data sharing agreements (PDPO-compliant), technical documentation

• Mentorship: Pairing startups with government domain experts who understand operational realities

• Scaled Procurement: Successful pilots graduate to framework agreements enabling multi-ministry deployment

This model addresses a common complaint from govtech startups: that the government is interested in their solutions but lacks mechanisms to actually buy them. By creating a structured pathway from challenge to pilot to procurement, the ICTD Playground can unlock significant startup revenue while improving government service delivery.

8.10 Strategic Objectives and KPI Framework

8.10.1 Strategic Objectives

#

Strategic Objective

Rationale

1

Position Bangladesh among top 50 global startup ecosystems by 2030

Currently unranked in top 50; requires coordinated improvement across funding, talent, market access, and infrastructure dimensions

2

Grow annual investment to US$1B with 30% domestic sources

Current local investment is negligible; healthy ecosystems require balanced domestic and foreign capital

3

Enable 5,000 startups with 30% women-led

Scale and inclusion targets; women's participation currently below 15%

4

Establish 10-12 regional innovation hubs

Address geographic concentration; unlock regional market opportunities

5

Facilitate 10+ IPOs/major exits

Exit pathways essential for capital recycling and investor confidence

6

Build 15+ active domestic VC fund managers

Professional VC ecosystem required for sustainable early-stage funding

7

Operationalize Startup Authority with clear governance

Institutional foundation for coordinated ecosystem development

8.11 Implementation Roadmap

Phase 1: Foundation (2026-2027)

Establish institutional architecture and launch initial programs:

• Establish Startup Authority / Commission with governance framework, mandate, and staffing

• Redefine agency roles through formal mandate letters: SBL for FoF/VC enablement, iDEA for incubation enablement, BHTPA for infrastructure, a2i for govtech

• Launch Fund of Funds mechanism with US$33 million initial deployment; issue call for VC fund manager applications

• Establish first two Central Regulatory Service Centers (Dhaka, Chattogram)

• Launch ICTD Playground pilot with 5 challenge areas

• Open first regional innovation hubs (Dhaka fintech, Chattogram healthtech)

• Establish first Makers Lab at BHTPA Kaliakair

• Design and launch angel investor tax incentive scheme modeled on UK EIS/SEIS

• Launch NRB Connect program to engage diaspora investors and mentors

Phase 2: Scaling (2027-2028)

Expand programs based on Phase 1 learnings:

• Expand regional innovation hubs to 6-8 locations with sector specializations

• Scale Fund of Funds deployment; onboard 8-10 professional VC fund managers

• Launch DSE Startup Board with relaxed listing requirements for tech companies

• Expand Central Regulatory Service Centers to all regional hubs

• Scale ICTD Playground to 20+ challenge areas with procurement pathways

• Achieve US$600 million annual investment with 25%+ domestic participation

• Enable 4-6 IPOs/exits demonstrating viable exit pathways

• Launch women entrepreneurship targeted fund and mentorship program

• Establish regulatory sandboxes for fintech, healthtech, edtech sectors

Phase 3: Maturation (2029-2030)

Achieve scale and ecosystem self-sustainability:

• Complete 10-12 innovation hubs covering all divisions

• Achieve US$1 billion annual investment target with 30% domestic participation

• Reach 5,000 active startups with 30% women-led

• Enable 10+ IPOs/major exits, establishing DSE as a startup-friendly exchange

• Position Bangladesh in Top 50 global startup ecosystems

• Achieve 15+ active domestic VC fund managers with combined AUM exceeding US$500 million

• Full integration of ICTD Playground with government procurement systems

• University-industry linkages producing 300 joint R&D projects with 120 commercialized outputs

• Makers Labs operational in 6 locations catalyzing hardware and deep-tech innovation





8.12 Expected Outcomes by 2030

Successful implementation of this strategic framework will deliver transformational outcomes across multiple dimensions:

Institutional Outcomes

• Operational Startup Authority / Commission with clear mandate, accountability mechanisms, and cross-agency coordination

• Streamlined regulatory environment through Central Regulatory Service Centers with 50% reduction in compliance burden

• Performance-based governance with published KPIs and regular public reporting

Investment Outcomes

• Annual US$1 billion investment inflows with 30% from domestic sources (up from negligible local participation)

• At least 15 active domestic VC fund managers with combined AUM exceeding US$500 million

• 10+ successful IPOs and major exits, establishing Dhaka Stock Exchange as startup-friendly

• Functional angel investor tax incentive regime catalyzing 10x increase in angel investment

Startup and Innovation Outcomes

• Vibrant ecosystem of 5,000 active startups with 30% led by women entrepreneurs

• Over 500,000 direct and indirect jobs created through startup ecosystem growth

• Strong university-industry linkages with 300 joint R&D projects and 120 commercialized patents/products

• 100,000 entrepreneurs trained through iDEA and partner programs

Infrastructure Outcomes

• 10-12 regional innovation hubs across all divisions with sector specializations

• 6 operational Makers Labs catalyzing hardware and deep-tech innovation

• ICTD Playground integrated with government procurement, enabling startup-delivered public services

National Positioning

• Bangladesh ranked among Top 50 global startup ecosystems

• Recognized regional hub for fintech, agritech, and healthtech innovation

• Globally recognized Bangladesh startup brand contributing to ICT exports, jobs, and national innovation capacity

8.13 Conclusion

The proposed Startup Authority / Commission represents a structural shift from fragmented program management to coordinated ecosystem governance. By centralizing policy, aligning capital with capability, enabling market-driven innovation, and simplifying regulatory engagement, the Authority can significantly accelerate startup growth and economic impact.

This model does not replace existing agencies; rather, it redefines their roles, improves accountability, and ensures that public resources translate into scalable, sustainable startups. SBL focuses on what it does best—capital deployment through market mechanisms. iDEA focuses on what it does best—enabling the broader incubation ecosystem. BHTPA focuses on infrastructure. a2i focuses on govtech. Each agency operates with clear mandate and accountability, coordinated by the Authority.

The core principle of 'Fund Less, Facilitate More' acknowledges that government money can prime the pump, but it cannot replace market forces. Through the Fund of Funds mechanism, VC enablement, infrastructure provision, and regulatory simplification, the government can catalyze private capital without crowding it out.

With the right governance framework, transparency, and performance metrics, the Startup Authority can become the cornerstone of Bangladesh's transition to an innovation-led economy. By 2030, Bangladesh can transform from an ecosystem constrained by fragmentation and capital scarcity to one where domestic investors, professional VCs, and government facilitation work together to nurture world-class startups. The foundations are being laid today.

— End of Chapter 8 —





9. Capacity building and training

Building human capital is the cornerstone of any successful digital transformation. Bangladesh's journey toward becoming a digitally empowered, innovative, and inclusive society hinges critically on developing a workforce capable of creating, implementing, managing, and continuously improving digital systems and services. This chapter presents a comprehensive framework for capacity building and training that addresses the diverse needs of citizens, civil servants, industry professionals, and educators while learning from past initiatives and charting an ambitious roadmap for the future.

9.1 Context and Rationale: The Imperative for Digital Skills Development

Bangladesh's digital economy is expanding rapidly, with ICT exports exceeding US$1.5 billion and a freelancing sector generating approximately US$400 million annually. According to the Oxford Internet Institute's Online Labour Index, Bangladesh accounts for approximately 15% of global freelance labour supply, ranking among the top three countries globally. BASIS estimates over 650,000 active freelancers, with more than 500,000 registered on international platforms.

However, this growth has been constrained by critical capacity gaps. Workforce skills remain unevenly distributed across regions, education levels, and sectors. Existing training initiatives have been fragmented, lacking a unified legal framework to ensure continuity, accountability, and alignment with market demand. The Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) demands proficiency in technologies such as artificial intelligence, blockchain, IoT, cloud computing, and data analytics — skills that remain scarce in Bangladesh's labour market.

Several structural challenges persist: curricula at educational institutions lag behind industry demand; research rarely converts into commercial products; government policies are designed centrally without continuous feedback from industry and academia; and public sector officials often lack the digital competencies required to implement and manage modern e-government systems. Addressing these challenges requires a multi-track approach that targets different population segments with tailored interventions.

9.2 Review of Past Initiatives: What Has Been Done

9.2.1 Leveraging ICT for Growth, Employment and Governance (LICT) Project

The LICT Project (2013-2019), implemented by Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC) with US$102.52 million World Bank funding, represents the most significant ICT capacity building initiative to date. The project successfully trained 33,564 individuals — including 10,450 women (31%) — in marketable ICT skills. Training was delivered in two tracks: 10,000 received Top-Up IT training for the IT services segment, while 20,000 received Foundation Skills training for the IT-enabled services (ITES) segment.

The project's approach was notable for its quality assurance mechanisms: pre-assessment and post-assessment of trainees were conducted by the National Association of Software Services Companies (NASSCOM), content was certified by George Washington University (USA), and a Training of Trainers (ToT) pool was established by internationally reputed trainers. Post-training surveys indicated that 90% of trained individuals obtained employment, contributing to the growth of IT/ITES industry employment from 12,000 jobs in 2013 to 47,000 jobs by 2019.

The LICT project also supported institutional capacity by assisting 66% of government ministries and divisions to introduce and maintain at least two or more e-government services, contributing to Bangladesh's improvement in the UN E-Government Development Index from 150th position in 2012 to 115th by 2018.



LICT Project Metrics

Achievement

Project Duration

2013-2019 (Extended)

Total Funding

US$102.52 million (World Bank)

Total Trained

33,564 individuals

Women Trained

10,450 (31%)

IT Sector Employment Growth

12,000 (2013) to 47,000 (2019)

Employment Rate Post-Training

90%

E-Gov Development Index Improvement

150th (2012) to 115th (2018)



9.2.2 Enhancing Digital Government and Economy (EDGE) Project

Building on LICT's success, the EDGE Project was initiated with US$300 million World Bank funding to continue digital transformation efforts. The project focuses on creating an enabling environment for digital government and the digital economy through multiple components including IT workforce development, digital infrastructure enhancement, policy reform, and research support.

Under Component 1, EDGE has conducted multifaceted digital skills training programs, training over 51,584 students — including 43% women — in foundational digital skills across more than 25 trades. In January 2024, the ICT Division signed MOUs with 28 public universities to provide digital skills training to 80,000 students over two years. The training is structured in three tiers: 50,000 at foundational level covering basic digital skills; 20,000 at intermediate level addressing more complex technical competencies; and 10,000 at advanced level focusing on frontier technologies including AI, robotics, and blockchain.

The EDGE project has also established partnerships with industry through its Hire & Train (HAT) program, which links training directly to employment guarantees — beneficiary companies must provide employment to at least 80% of trainees for a minimum of six months. The project supports the Digital Leadership Academy (DLA) and Center for 4IR to strengthen institutional capacity for emerging technology adoption.

9.2.3 Bangladesh Korea Institute of Information and Communication Technology (BKIICT)

BKIICT, established in 2005 with technical and financial assistance from the Korean International Cooperation Agency (KOICA), operates as an international-standard ICT training institute under Bangladesh Computer Council. Located at BCC Bhaban, Sher-e-Bangla Nagar, BKIICT offers a comprehensive range of courses including software development, web development, mobile app development, data science, networking, cybersecurity, digital marketing, graphic design, animation, and video editing.

BKIICT has served as a model for specialized ICT training delivery in Bangladesh, demonstrating the value of international partnerships in building advanced technical training capacity. The institute continues to evolve its curriculum to address emerging technology needs and market demands.

9.2.4 Bangladesh-Korea Technical Training Centres (BKTTC)

The Bangladesh-Korea Technical Training Centres represent a long-standing collaboration in technical and vocational education. BKTTC Dhaka, originally established in 1942 as a training center for World War II veterans, was modernized with KOICA funding and renamed in 2012-2013. Similarly, BKTTC Chattogram (established 1962) was remodeled and upgraded with modern equipment and machinery.

These centers operate under the Bureau of Manpower Employment and Training (BMET) and provide a range of services including one-year Skills Certificate courses across six trades, modular courses in 24 trades, pedagogical training for trainers (NTVQF Level 4), mandatory pre-departure orientation for overseas-bound workers, and job placement support. The curriculum is updated by Korea University of Technology (KUT), with trainers receiving professional development through KUT, JOCV, JICA, ILO, and KOICA programs.

9.2.5 Skills and Training Enhancement Project (STEP)

The World Bank-funded STEP project focused on strengthening technical and vocational education and training (TVET) institutions. The project supported 30 polytechnics with implementation grants of up to US$1 million each, and engaged 50 training providers for short-course delivery. Performance-based incentives rewarded institutions achieving strong pass rates, teacher training outcomes, and graduate employment placement.

STEP supported the development of market-aligned competency standards through Industry Skills Councils (ISCs), including the ICT ISC. The project contributed to increased enrollment in polytechnics and higher employability of graduates, though challenges remained in coordination between ISCs and the National Skills Development Authority (NSDA).

9.2.6 Union Digital Centers (UDCs) and a2i Capacity Building

The Access to Information (a2i) program has rolled out over 8,500 Union Digital Centers nationwide, transforming rural post offices and community spaces into hubs for digital services, skill development, and local entrepreneurship. These centers provide on-demand access to government services while offering training programs with strong emphasis on women and youth empowerment.

Through initiatives like the Teachers' Portal and multimedia classroom setups in over 15,000 schools, a2i has revolutionized teaching by providing digital resources, lesson plans, and collaborative tools. The Digital Service Accelerator initiative has further expanded service digitization while incorporating user feedback loops for continuous improvement.

9.3 Lessons Learned and Programs Discontinued

The review of past initiatives reveals several critical lessons and identifies programs that were discontinued or scaled back due to various challenges:

9.3.1 Key Success Factors from Past Programs

  • Quality Assurance Mechanisms: Programs with international certification (NASSCOM, George Washington University) and standardized content achieved higher employment outcomes.

  • Industry Linkages: Direct partnerships with IT/ITES companies and guaranteed employment clauses (HAT model) significantly improved training-to-employment conversion rates.

  • Training of Trainers (ToT): Investment in building local training capacity ensured sustainability beyond project timelines.

  • Women's Inclusion: Targeted outreach and supportive environments enabled significant female participation (31-43% in major programs).

  • Performance-Based Incentives: Linking institutional funding to outcomes (pass rates, employment placement) drove quality improvements.



9.3.2 Challenges and Discontinued Approaches

  • Fragmented Coordination: Multiple ministries and agencies operating training programs without unified standards or credential recognition led to duplication and inefficiency.

  • Curriculum-Industry Mismatch: Static curricula that failed to keep pace with rapidly evolving technology requirements produced graduates with outdated skills.

  • Teacher Training Gaps: Many ICT training activities focused primarily on computer literacy rather than enabling teachers to integrate ICT effectively into teaching and learning.

  • Sustainability Challenges: Project-based interventions without institutional embedding often lost momentum after donor funding ended.

  • Urban-Rural Divide: Training infrastructure remained concentrated in metropolitan areas, limiting access for rural populations.

  • ISC Coordination Gaps: Industry Skills Councils faced challenges in institutional capacity and coordination with NSDC, limiting their effectiveness in curriculum alignment.



9.3.3 Rationale for Reform

These lessons inform the strategic shift toward a more integrated, institutionally embedded, and outcome-focused approach to capacity building. The reformed strategy addresses sustainability through permanent institutional mandates, quality through standardized frameworks and international partnerships, relevance through industry-academia collaboration mechanisms, and inclusion through targeted interventions for underserved populations.

9.4 The Reformed Capacity Building Framework

The National Digital Transformation Strategy introduces a comprehensive, lifecycle-based approach to capacity building that addresses the diverse needs of Bangladesh's population while building sustainable institutional capacity. The framework operates across five interconnected tracks targeting different population segments.

9.4.1 Track 1: Foundational Skills (Schools and Early Learning)

Target Group: Primary and secondary students (ages 6-16)

The foundational track aims to embed digital literacy into the national education curriculum, ensuring that every child develops core competencies in safe device use, online safety, and basic problem-solving through Bangla-first digital tools. The curriculum integrates coding and computational thinking from an early age, while introducing awareness of freelancing and e-commerce concepts.

Strategic Interventions:

  • Digital Classrooms Nationwide: Equip schools with smart boards, tablets, and internet access integrated into a National Education Cloud.

  • E-Learning Platforms: Expand Bangla-friendly e-learning portals with offline access for rural students.

  • Accessible Digital Content: Scale Multimedia Talking Books and interactive learning tools for students with disabilities.

  • AI-Enabled Assessment: Deploy AI to track student performance, reduce dropout risks, and provide adaptive learning paths.

  • Teacher Training: Mandatory ICT pedagogy certification for teachers; peer learning communities to sustain skills.

Table 9.1: KPI Framework for Foundational Digital Skills (2025-2030)



9.4.2 Track 2: Vocational and Youth Employment Skills

Target Group: School leavers, madrasa students, unemployed youth (ages 16-25)

This track focuses on developing market-ready skills for immediate employment in the digital economy. It includes two specialized pathways: the General Youth Track through the National Outsourcing & Freelancing Accelerator, and the Madrasa-Focused Arabic/Farsi Employment Track that leverages the linguistic advantages of madrasa graduates for Middle Eastern markets.

A. National Outsourcing & Freelancing Accelerator (General Youth Track):

  • Digital Foundations: ICT literacy, online safety, productivity tools, client-facing English.

  • Freelancing Skills: Graphic design, web and app development, digital marketing, transcription, QA, data entry, e-commerce management.

  • Emerging Skills: AI data labeling, cloud operations, cybersecurity micro-tasks, fintech back-office, content moderation.

  • Platform Skills: Onboarding for Upwork, Fiverr, Freelancer, Amazon MTurk, Remotasks, Clickworker.

  • Placement & Market Linkage: Bootcamps, mentorship networks, and verified talent pools with BASIS, BACCO, and global platforms.



B. Madrasa-Focused Arabic/Farsi Employment Track:

Bangladesh's madrasas educate over 2.75 million students in the Alia stream (2023 BANBEIS data), with millions more in the Qawmi stream. Many madrasa learners are proficient in Arabic and Farsi, providing a natural advantage for Middle Eastern outsourcing markets including customer support, transcription, and content moderation.

  • Target Niches: Arabic transcription, subtitling, cataloguing, customer support, content moderation, EN↔AR localization.

  • Curriculum: Modern Arabic, workplace English, freelancing toolsets, specialized software.

  • Partners: Alia/Qawmi boards including BEFAQ, GCC BPO firms, freelancing platforms for Arabic talent pools.

9.4.3 Track 3: Higher Education and Advanced Tech Skills

Target Group: University and college students

This track prepares Bangladesh's future technology leaders through advanced training in frontier technologies, close university-industry collaboration, and establishment of Innovation Accelerators at major universities including BUET, DU, CUET, KUET, and RUET.

Focus Areas:

  • AI, data science, cloud computing, cybersecurity, blockchain, fintech, and semiconductors.

  • University-industry collaboration for R&D and internships with minimum 6 months industry attachment.

  • Innovation Accelerators hosted within Hi-Tech Parks with access to labs, co-working spaces, seed grants, and mentorship.

  • Joint R&D Grant Facility co-financed by government, private sector, and donors focusing on AI, semiconductors, digital health, fintech, and agriculture tech.
