9.4.4 Track 4: Lifelong Learning and Workforce Reskilling

Target Group: Existing workforce, SME owners, returning migrants, women at home

This track addresses the continuous learning needs of the existing workforce, ensuring that professionals remain competitive as technology evolves.

Focus Areas:

  • Short online bootcamps and micro-credentials through platforms like Udemy, Coursera, and local portals.

  • SME digital adoption training covering finance, logistics, and e-commerce.

  • Women's reskilling programmes for home-based freelancing and e-commerce.

  • Migrant workers training in digital remittances and GCC outsourcing jobs.

KPI 2030: 500,000 workers reskilled; women's participation in freelancing to reach 40%.

9.4.5 Track 5: Public Sector and Leadership Skills

Target Group: Civil servants, judiciary, law enforcement

Digital transformation of government services requires a public sector workforce equipped with modern digital competencies. This track addresses the unique training needs of government officials at all levels.

Focus Areas:

  • Cybersecurity, AI governance, digital ethics, and policy/regulatory skills.

  • Judicial and law enforcement training for cybercrime investigation, forensics, and digital evidence handling.

  • Senior leadership training on digital economy policymaking.

  • Data stewardship and AI ethics training through NDGA.

KPI 2030: 50,000 officials trained in digital governance and cybersecurity.

9.5 Institutional Reforms for Capacity Building

9.5.1 Department of ICT (DoICT) as Grassroots Training Arm

DoICT will be transformed into the primary grassroots training and capacity-building arm of the ICT Division, with expanded mandate and strengthened institutional capacity.

Expanded Training Mandate:

  • Selected ICT-related courses (digital literacy, coding fundamentals, office productivity tools, cybersecurity hygiene) standardized and approved by NSDA.

  • DoICT officers at district and upazila levels to deliver programs in schools, colleges, and madrasas.

  • Advanced certifications (Microsoft, AWS, CISCO) integrated gradually with DoICT facilitating partnerships with global certifying bodies.



Curriculum Innovation and Local Integration:

  • Foundation: Digital literacy for students and community members.

  • Intermediate: E-commerce, freelancing, coding, and data entry.

  • Advanced: Cybersecurity, AI, cloud, and IoT basics for future workforce readiness.

  • Local language content and accessibility for rural and marginalized communities.



Monitoring and Accountability:

  • National Digital Learning Dashboard to track enrollment, completion, and job-placement outcomes.

  • Performance-based incentives for DoICT trainers/officers tied to training outcomes.

  • DoICT offices equipped to serve as community digital help desks.





9.5.3 National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) Capacity Building Division

NDGA will establish a dedicated Coordination & Capacity Building Division responsible for training government officials in data governance, interoperability, and AI ethics.

Key Responsibilities:

  • Train 5,000 officials in data stewardship and AI ethics by 2030.

  • Publish open data handbooks and interoperability toolkits.

  • Coordinate sectoral data offices across ministries.

  • Conduct awareness programs on data protection and citizen rights under PDPO.

9.6 Digital Skills and Workforce Development Framework

A comprehensive legal and policy framework will underpin the capacity building strategy, providing statutory foundation for training standards, accreditation, and industry participation.

Key Provisions:

  • National Skills Registry: Central database of trained professionals accessible to employers and agencies.

  • Accreditation Framework: Legal recognition of digital skills certificates, micro-credentials, and global vendor certifications.

  • Public Funding Commitment: Annual budgetary allocation to skills development through NSDA and ICT Division.

  • Inclusion Mandate: At least 40% of training beneficiaries must be women and marginalized groups.

  • Industry Participation: Private sector co-investment and curriculum advisory roles mandated by law.



9.6.1 Bangladesh Digital Skills Roadmap:



1. Foundational Skills (Schools & Early Learning)

  • Target Group: Primary and secondary students (ages 6–16).

  • Focus Areas:

    • Digital literacy (safe device use, online safety, Bangla-first digital tools).

    • Coding and problem-solving basics in school curriculum.

    • Exposure to freelancing/e-commerce concepts (early awareness).

  • KPI 2030: 20 million students trained in digital literacy and coding basics.



2. Vocational & Youth Employment Skills (TVET, Madrasa, Freelancers)

  • Target Group: School leavers, madrasa students, unemployed youth (16–25).

  • Focus Areas:

    • Market-ready freelancing skills: design, coding, e-commerce, transcription, data entry.

    • Specialized Madrasa Track: Arabic/Bangla/Farsi-based services (transcription, content moderation, customer support for GCC markets).

    • ICT-enabled vocational trades (repair, IT support, digital agriculture tools).

    • Certification programs with NSDA, BASIS, and global partners (AWS, Cisco, Microsoft).

  • KPI 2030: 1 million youth certified; 150,000 madrasa learners integrated into freelancing workforce.



3. Higher Education & Advanced Tech Skills

  • Target Group: University and college students.

  • Focus Areas:

    • AI, data science, cloud, cybersecurity, blockchain, fintech, semiconductors.

    • University–industry collaboration for R&D and internships.

    • Establish Innovation Accelerators at major universities (BUET, DU, CUET, KUET, RUET).

  • KPI 2030: 100,000 graduates with advanced ICT/AI/data qualifications.



4. Lifelong Learning & Workforce Reskilling

  • Target Group: Existing workforce, SME owners, returning migrants, women at home.

  • Focus Areas:

    • Short online bootcamps and micro-credentials (Udemy, Coursera, local platforms).

    • SME digital adoption training (finance, logistics, e-commerce).

    • Women’s reskilling programmes for home-based freelancing and e-commerce.

    • Migrant workers trained in digital remittances, GCC outsourcing jobs.

  • KPI 2030: 500,000 workers reskilled; women’s participation in freelancing to reach 40%.



5. Public Sector & Leadership Skills

  • Target Group: Civil servants, judiciary, law enforcement.

  • Focus Areas:

    • Cybersecurity, AI governance, digital ethics, policy and regulatory skills.

    • Judicial/law enforcement training for cybercrime, forensics, and digital evidence handling.

    • Senior leadership training on digital economy policymaking.

  • KPI 2030: 50,000 officials trained in digital governance and cybersecurity.



Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • 2 million citizens trained across all lifecycle stages.

  • 1 million active freelancers, including 150,000 madrasa students.

  • 100,000 advanced ICT graduates ready for high-value global markets.

  • 500,000 reskilled workers (SMEs, women, migrants).

  • Stronger public sector capacity in digital governance and cybersecurity.







9.7 Freelancer.gov.bd:

The freelancer.gov.bd platform creates an integrated national infrastructure that:

Strategic Need

Platform Response

Workforce Visibility

National Freelancer Registry with unique ID system enabling real-time tracking of 1M+ freelancers

Credential Trust

Verified digital certificates linked to NID through NDGA interoperability standards

Training Coordination

Single enrollment point connecting to DoICT, BCC, NSDA, and global certification partners

Financial Integration

Remittance tracking, tax compliance, and integration with banking/MFS ecosystem

Market Linkage

Direct connection with BASIS, BACCO, global platforms, and GCC BPO firms

9.7.1 Freelancer.gov.bd Platform Architecture

3.1 Platform Vision

Freelancer.gov.bd will serve as the national digital gateway for Bangladesh’s freelancing workforce, providing a single, authoritative platform where freelancers can register, access training, earn certifications, receive the Freelancer ID Card, track their earnings, and connect with domestic and international opportunities.

3.2 Core Platform Modules

Module 1: National Freelancer Registry

The foundational module establishing Bangladesh’s first comprehensive database of digital workers:

Component

Description

Registration Gateway

Online registration with NID verification through oneID/NDGA integration, supporting both web and mobile access with Bangla-first interface design

Profile Management

Comprehensive skill profiles including completed trainings, certifications, portfolio links, platform accounts (Upwork, Fiverr, etc.), and earning history

Unique Freelancer ID

System-generated unique identifier linked to NID for permanent tracking across all training programs, certifications, and earnings

Verification API

Public API enabling employers and platforms to verify freelancer credentials and certification status in real-time

Analytics Dashboard

Administrative interface providing real-time statistics on registered freelancers, active earners, geographic distribution, and skill coverage



Module 2: Training & Certification Hub

A unified gateway connecting freelancers with all available training programs:

Component

Description

Course Catalog

Searchable database of all available courses from DoICT, BCC, a2i, NSDA-accredited providers, and global certification partners

Enrollment System

Single enrollment point with automatic eligibility checking, slot allocation, and waitlist management

Learning Pathways

Structured progression tracks from Foundation (digital literacy) to Intermediate (freelancing skills) to Advanced (AI, cloud, cybersecurity)

Certification Tracker

Digital wallet for all earned certificates including NSDA credentials, global vendor certifications (Microsoft, AWS, Cisco), and platform-specific badges

Progress Dashboard

Individual learning analytics showing completion rates, skill gaps, and recommended next courses



Module 3: Earnings & Payment Integration

Financial tracking and support module addressing payment challenges:

Component

Description

Earnings Tracker

Voluntary self-reporting of earnings from all platforms with aggregated dashboard showing lifetime earnings, monthly trends, and platform breakdown

Remittance Guide

Updated information on legal remittance channels, bank partnerships, and MFS options with comparative cost analysis

Tax Compliance

Integration with NBR API Connectivity Hub for simplified tax registration and compliance reporting for freelance income

Bank Integration

Partnerships with scheduled banks for preferential freelancer accounts with reduced foreign exchange conversion fees



Module 4: Market Linkage & Opportunities

Connecting freelancers with work opportunities:

Component

Description

Job Board

Curated opportunities from verified employers, government projects, and enterprise outsourcing contracts specifically seeking Bangladeshi talent

Platform Onboarding

Step-by-step guides and assisted registration for Upwork, Fiverr, Freelancer, Amazon MTurk, Remotasks, Clickworker, and other global platforms

Talent Pools

Verified talent directories accessible to BASIS members, BACCO companies, and international clients seeking specialized skills

GCC Connect

Dedicated section for Arabic/Farsi-skilled freelancers connecting with Middle Eastern BPO firms and outsourcing contracts

Mentorship Network

Connection with successful freelancers for guidance, bootcamps, and career support through industry partnerships



9.7.2 Freelancer ID Card Initiative

Concept and Purpose

The Freelancer ID Card represents a groundbreaking initiative to formally recognize freelancing as a legitimate profession and provide digital workers with an official credential that facilitates access to services, builds credibility, and enables government support.

Unlike traditional employment identification, the Freelancer ID is specifically designed to address the unique circumstances of gig economy workers who operate independently across multiple platforms and clients.





Card Features and Specifications

Feature

Description

Physical Card

Standard ISO/IEC 7810 ID-1 format smart card with embedded chip containing digital certificates and NID linkage

Digital Card

Mobile-accessible digital version through freelancer.gov.bd app with QR code for instant verification

Unique ID Number

Permanent identifier format: BD-FLR-XXXXXXXX linked to NID and National Skills Registry

Skill Endorsements

Digital attestation of verified skills and certifications with expiry dates and issuing authority

Earning Tier

Voluntary badge system showing lifetime earning ranges: Bronze (<$5K), Silver ($5K-25K), Gold ($25K-100K), Platinum (>$100K)

Verification API

Real-time verification endpoint for employers/platforms to confirm ID validity and credentials



Eligibility Criteria

To receive the Freelancer ID Card, applicants must meet the following requirements:

Requirement

Specification

Age

Minimum 18 years of age with valid NID

Registration

Active registration on freelancer.gov.bd with verified NID linkage

Training

Completion of at least one NSDA-accredited or government-recognized digital skills course

Platform Activity

Active profile on at least one freelancing platform OR documented freelance work history

Code of Conduct

Agreement to National Freelancer Code of Conduct covering ethical work practices and quality standards



Benefits of Freelancer ID Card



For Freelancers

Benefit

Description

Official Recognition

Government-backed credential establishing freelancing as a recognized profession

Banking Access

Simplified opening of business accounts with partner banks; serves as income proof for loans

Tax Benefits

Streamlined tax registration and potential future incentives for compliant freelancers

Social Protection

Foundation for future enrollment in social security and health insurance schemes

Client Trust

Verifiable credential increasing credibility with international clients and platforms

Government Services

Priority access to government digital services, subsidized co-working spaces, and training programs



For the Government

Benefit

Description

Workforce Visibility

Accurate data on digital workforce size, skills, earnings, and geographic distribution

Policy Planning

Evidence-based inputs for skills development policy, training investment, and export strategy

Revenue Tracking

Better capture of freelance export earnings data for national accounts and forex planning

Formalization

Pathway for bringing informal digital workers into the formal economy

Training Program Integration

9.8 Government-Industry-Academia Collaboration Framework

A structured triple helix collaboration framework will ensure that capacity building efforts are aligned with market demand and leverage the strengths of all stakeholders.

9.8.1 National Council for Digital Innovation (NCDI)

  • Chaired by ICT Division, co-chaired by BASIS/BACCO, with Vice-Chancellors of major universities.

  • Meets quarterly to align on skills, R&D, and industry challenges.

  • Provides recommendations to NDGA, BCC, and Planning Commission.



9.8.2 Curriculum and Talent Alignment

  • Industry advisory boards embedded into universities.

  • Annual curriculum review aligned with ICT export market trends.

  • Internship/apprenticeship pipeline: minimum 6 months industry attachment for ICT students.



9.8.3 Applied Research and Commercialization

  • Incentives for faculty to co-publish and co-patent with industry.

  • Innovation vouchers for SMEs to access university labs.

  • National Startup Incubation Challenge linking student startups to venture funds.





Governance & Institutional Setup

  • National Council for Digital Innovation (NCDI)

    • Chaired by ICT Division, co-chaired by BASIS/BACCO, with Vice-Chancellors of major universities.

    • Meets quarterly to align on skills, R&D, and industry challenges.

    • Provides recommendations to NDGA, BCC, and Planning Commission.

  • University–Industry Innovation Accelerators

    • Hubs established at BUET, DU, CUET, KUET, RUET, and leading private universities.

    • Hosted within Hi-Tech Parks, giving access to labs, co-working, seed grants, and mentorship.

  • Joint R&D Grant Facility

    • Co-financed by government, private sector, and donors.

    • Grants focused on AI, semiconductors, digital health, fintech, and agriculture tech.

    • Outcomes tracked: prototypes, patents, startups.

  • Align Skills with Market Demand: Ensure curricula, certifications, and training programs respond to real-time industry needs.

  1. Accelerate Research to Market: Translate academic R&D into products, patents, and startups.

  2. Build Innovation Hubs Nationwide: Foster university–industry accelerators in every region, beyond Dhaka and Chattogram.

  3. Facilitate Policy Feedback Loops: Formalize government–industry–academia councils for co-creation of strategies.

  4. Create Talent & Startup Pipelines: Leverage students and researchers as a source of entrepreneurship and innovation.

Key Interventions

1. Curriculum & Talent Alignment

  • Industry advisory boards embedded into universities.

  • Annual curriculum review aligned with ICT export market trends.

  • Internship/apprenticeship pipeline: minimum 6 months industry attachment for ICT students.

2. Applied Research & Commercialization

  • Incentives for faculty to co-publish and co-patent with industry.

  • Innovation vouchers for SMEs to access university labs.

  • National Startup Incubation Challenge linking student startups to venture funds.

3. Innovation Hubs & Regional Balance

  • 12 hubs by 2030 across all divisions.

  • Sector-focused hubs: fintech (Dhaka), agritech (Rajshahi), healthtech (Chattogram), manufacturing IoT (Gazipur), semiconductors (Khulna).

  • Annual National Digital Innovation Expo to showcase outcomes.

4. Policy Co-Creation

  • Government agencies to co-develop digital standards with universities and industry.

  • Regulatory sandboxes tested in collaboration with academia.

  • Real-time feedback loop: research insights → policy pilots → scaled solutions.

Expected Outcomes by 2030

  • 300 joint R&D projects with at least 120 commercialized outputs.

9.9 International Partnerships and Knowledge Transfer

International cooperation will be leveraged strategically to build sustainable domestic capacity while ensuring that partnerships are mutually beneficial rather than donor-driven.

Key Principles:

  • All major ICT projects with foreign partners will include mandatory knowledge transfer programs, on-site training, and certification of Bangladeshi officials.

  • Bilateral programs will focus on co-innovation rather than simple aid, including joint pilots in 5G, health tech, and smart agriculture.

  • Joint R&D projects with universities abroad will feed into the Government-Industry-Academia collaboration framework.

  • Engagement with E-Government Academy (EGA), ITU, and OECD networks for benchmarking and standards localization.

9.10 Expected Outcomes by 2030

The comprehensive capacity building framework is designed to achieve transformative outcomes across all population segments:

9.10.1 Aggregate Training Outcomes

  • 2 million citizens trained across all lifecycle stages.

  • 1 million active freelancers, including 150,000 madrasa students.

  • 100,000 advanced ICT graduates ready for high-value global markets.

  • 500,000 reskilled workers (SMEs, women, migrants).

  • 50,000 public sector officials trained in digital governance and cybersecurity.

  • 500,000+ teachers ICT-trained with 25 million students engaged in digital learning.



9.10.2 Economic Impact

  • US$1 billion+ annual freelance export earnings.

  • Bangladesh positioned as #1 freelancing workforce hub globally.

  • Women's participation in freelancing increased from 20% to 40%.

  • 90% of ICT graduates considered industry-ready.



9.10.3 Institutional Outcomes

  • 12 innovation hubs connected to universities, industry, and hi-tech parks across all divisions.

  • 300 joint R&D projects with at least 120 commercialized outputs.

  • 75,000 students gaining direct industry placements.

  • 10,000+ Bangladeshi officials trained in frontier technologies through international partnerships.

  • Bangladesh recognized as a regional leader in government-industry-academia collaboration.

The capacity building and training reforms outlined in this chapter represent a paradigm shift from fragmented, project-based interventions to a comprehensive, institutionally embedded, and outcome-focused approach. By addressing the diverse needs of different population segments through tailored tracks, building sustainable institutional capacity, and leveraging international partnerships for knowledge transfer, Bangladesh will develop the human capital foundation essential for realizing its vision of becoming a digitally empowered, innovative, and inclusive society by 2030.

---

End of Chapter 9





10. Strategic and governance reforms

10.1 Introduction: The Imperative for Strategic Governance Reform

Bangladesh's digital transformation journey requires more than technological adoption—it demands fundamental changes in how government institutions coordinate, make decisions, and deliver services to citizens. The National Digital Transformation Strategy recognizes that sustainable digital progress is impossible without parallel reforms in governance structures, inter-agency coordination, funding mechanisms, and accountability frameworks. This chapter outlines the comprehensive strategic and governance reforms necessary to ensure the successful implementation of Bangladesh's digital vision through 2030 and beyond.

The current state of governance in Bangladesh's ICT ecosystem presents significant challenges. Fragmented digital assets across ministries, siloed decision-making processes, overlapping mandates, and insufficient coordination mechanisms have historically undermined digital initiatives. Major data repositories—including the National ID (NID), Birth and Death Registration System (BDRIS), and National Board of Revenue (NBR) records—lack secure interoperable mechanisms. This fragmentation has resulted in redundant infrastructure, higher operational costs, poor coordination among government agencies, and citizen data vulnerabilities. The reforms outlined in this chapter address these systemic challenges through institutional restructuring, enhanced coordination frameworks, sustainable financing models, and robust monitoring mechanisms.



10.2 Coordination Framework

Effective implementation of the National Digital Transformation Strategy requires a robust coordination framework that aligns diverse institutions, minimizes duplication, and ensures timely delivery of reforms. Bangladesh's digital ecosystem spans ministries, regulators, state-owned enterprises, and private sector actors. Without structured inter-agency mechanisms, overlapping mandates and siloed decision-making risk undermining progress.

10.2.1 Three-Tier Coordination Structure

The proposed coordination framework consists of three interlinked tiers, each serving distinct but complementary functions in ensuring cohesive digital governance:

Tier 1: National Digital Transformation Council (NDTC)

The National Digital Transformation Council serves as the apex strategic oversight body, providing high-level policy direction and ensuring alignment with national development priorities like Sustainable Development Goals. The Council is chaired at the highest level of government with membership including key ministers, secretaries, and heads of regulatory bodies. Its responsibilities include approving major policy frameworks, resolving inter-ministerial conflicts, and ensuring political commitment to digital transformation objectives. The NDTC convenes quarterly to review progress and provide strategic guidance.

Tier 2: Inter-Agency Taskforces and Committees

Issue-specific taskforces form the operational backbone of the coordination framework. These include dedicated taskforces for Cybersecurity, Digital Public Infrastructure, AI & Data Governance, E-Commerce and Logistics, Land Automation, and Digital Financial Services. Each taskforce is chaired by the relevant line ministry with co-chairing by the ICT Division to ensure technical consistency. Taskforces are empowered to draft policies, oversee implementation pilots, and coordinate resource sharing across agencies. This structure ensures that specialized expertise is brought to bear on sector-specific challenges while maintaining alignment with overall digital transformation objectives.

Tier 3: Implementation and Monitoring Secretariat

A permanent secretariat under the ICT Division serves as the central coordination hub, responsible for day-to-day coordination and monitoring activities. The secretariat's core functions include convening taskforces, tracking progress through a National Digital Dashboard, and providing quarterly reports to the NDTC. It ensures accountability through transparent KPIs, citizen feedback loops, and cross-agency performance reviews. The secretariat also serves as the primary interface with development partners, coordinating technical assistance and funding flows.

10.2.2 National Digital Transformation Taskforce (NDTT)

To ensure effective implementation of the strategy's bold goals, the government will establish a National Digital Transformation Taskforce (NDTT). This taskforce will include representatives from the ICT Division, Bangladesh Computer Council (BCC), Bangladesh Bank, the Ministry of Finance, the National Board of Revenue (NBR), the Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission (BTRC), the Cabinet Division, and development partners such as the World Bank, JICA, the EU, and UNDP. The inclusion of development partners ensures access to global expertise, financing, and best practices while maintaining national ownership of the transformation agenda.

The NDTT operates under a clear mandate to break down institutional silos, harmonize digital initiatives across ministries, and ensure that all government agencies feel ownership of the transformation process. Unlike previous ICT Division-led initiatives, the NDTT's inclusive structure ensures that digital transformation is not viewed as the responsibility of a single ministry but as a whole-of-government priority. Biannual progress reports will track key performance indicators through global benchmarking and public feedback.

Coordination Framework Structure

Tier

Body

Chair

Frequency

Primary Functions

1

National Digital Transformation Council (NDTC)

Prime Minister / Chief Adviser

Quarterly

Strategic oversight, policy approval, conflict resolution

2

Inter-Agency Taskforces

Relevant Line Ministry + ICT Division

Monthly

Policy drafting, pilot oversight, resource coordination

3

Implementation Secretariat

ICT Division

Continuous

Daily coordination, monitoring, reporting, partner liaison



10.3 Public-Private Partnership (PPP) Model

The success of Bangladesh's digital transformation requires mobilizing both public and private resources. While the government provides the regulatory framework, strategic direction, and foundational infrastructure, the private sector contributes innovation, investment, and operational efficiency. A structured Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model will therefore be central to accelerating priority digital projects.

10.3.1 Strategic Principles of the PPP Framework

Under this model, the government will identify flagship initiatives—such as 5G rollout, National Fibre Bank, Digital Public Infrastructure, and Semiconductor Roadmap—that require joint investment. The framework ensures transparent procurement and risk-sharing mechanisms, creating a predictable environment for private investors. Innovation sandboxes and pilot projects will enable startups and enterprises to co-develop solutions with government agencies. Blended financing through a mix of public funds, concessional loans, foreign direct investment (FDI), and private capital will accelerate implementation.

By leveraging PPPs, Bangladesh can fast-track implementation timelines, reduce fiscal burdens, and ensure that cutting-edge global expertise is embedded in national digital systems. This collaborative approach aligns with the strategy's vision of building a resilient, citizen-centric, and innovation-driven digital ecosystem. Key principles guiding PPP engagements include value for money, risk allocation to the party best able to manage it, transparency in procurement and contracting, and accountability through clearly defined performance metrics.

10.3.2 Priority Sectors for PPP Engagement

Sector

Initiative

Government Role

Private Sector Role

Expected Investment

Telecommunications

5G Network Rollout

Spectrum allocation, regulatory framework

Network deployment, operations

Finance

Regulatory oversight, interoperability standards

Platform development, operations

$200 million



10.4 Role of Public Procurement Act and Rules in Digital Transformation

Bangladesh's journey toward a more efficient and accountable public sector has been strongly supported by digital transformation. A key enabler in this process has been the Public Procurement Act (PPA) 2006 and the Public Procurement Rules (PPR) 2008, which laid the legal and institutional foundation for transparent and technology-driven procurement. The implementation of the Electronic Government Procurement (e-GP) system is one of the most significant milestones achieved under this legal framework, contributing to increased efficiency, reduced corruption, and improved service delivery across public sector entities.

10.4.1 Role of PPA 2006 and PPR 2008 in Digital Transformation

The PPA and PPR provided a forward-thinking legal environment that facilitated the digitalization of procurement operations. Key contributions include:

Legal Authority for e-GP: A recent amendment (4th May 2025) of Section 65 of the PPA made it mandatory for all Procuring Entities (PEs) to use the e-GP portal where applicable.

Process Standardization: The Act and Rules introduced standardized procedures, formats, and thresholds, which were critical to designing an automated, auditable procurement workflow.

Support for ICT Sector Engagement: The legal framework allowed for the procurement of IT-enabled services, facilitating the development of national digital platforms and systems through engagement with local ICT firms.

Decision Capacity for Software Specifications: Procuring Authorities should have the decision capacity to define software technology specifications to ensure appropriate technical requirements are established for digital procurement.

Through these contributions, the procurement law not only ensured transparency and competitiveness but also actively supported Bangladesh's broader digital service delivery goals.

10.4.2 e-GP Maturity Status (as of 12 April 2025)

Indicator

Value

Agencies

1,483

PEs in e-GP

12,975

Registered Tenderers

130,640

Tender Invited

935,576

Contract Awards

704,404

Tender Value

USD 113.55 Billion

Contract Value

USD 72.80 Billion

Registered Banks

53



10.4.3 Impact of e-GP

The e-GP system has delivered significant measurable improvements:

Publication of 100% of procurement notices and contract award information has been achieved. Average procurement lead time (from invitation to contract signing) decreased from 86.7 days in FY12 to 53 days in FY23. The percentage share of awarded bids within the original bid validity period increased from 10% in FY07 to 99% in FY23. The percentage share of rejected bids decreased from 8% in FY12 to 3% in FY19. An estimated USD 1.1 billion in savings has been realized. Improved market access and bidding environment compared to manual bidding has been established. An estimated 497 million km of travel distance reduction and decrease of 153,559 tons of CO2 emission has been achieved, along with savings of 1,053 million pages of paper.

10.4.4 Current Challenges in e-GP Implementation

Digital Literacy Issues: Not all tenderers, contractors, and consultants are digitally equipped to effectively use the e-GP system.

Capacity Constraints: Many procuring entities lack trained and experienced personnel to operate the system effectively, especially at the local level.

Connectivity and Access Issues: Contractors in rural areas often face challenges related to internet access, limiting their participation.

System Performance Limitations: Users continue to experience slow response times, technical issues during peak usage, and limited support for mobile devices.

Regulatory Gaps: The current legal framework does not fully reflect the operational needs of digital procurement, such as cloud hosting, digital signatures, and e-document validation.

Limited Use of Procurement Data: While the e-GP system captures extensive data, it is not being adequately used for performance monitoring, analytics, or risk mitigation through IMED and other oversight bodies.

10.4.5 Strategic Recommendations

Legal and Regulatory Update:

Amend the PPA and PPR to explicitly incorporate provisions for e-signatures and secure electronic records, incorporate provisions for Agile procurement practices for software development, enable cloud-based platforms and third-party integrations, and establish simplified procedures for procuring digital services.

Capacity Development:

Scale up mandatory training and certification for procurement practitioners, including a focus on digital procurement tools and ethical compliance. Establish dedicated procurement support cells in key ministries and local government bodies.

System Enhancement and User Support:

Upgrade the infrastructure of the e-GP system to ensure better performance and scalability. Improve the user interface with mobile-responsive design and multilingual access. Strengthen technical support through helpdesks and online troubleshooting tools.

Performance Monitoring and Analytics:

Deploy real-time dashboards and automated performance indicators for procurement oversight bodies including IMED. Utilize procurement data to detect red flags, ensure competition, and improve value-for-money outcomes.

10.5 National Computing Infrastructure: Centralized GPU Clusters

As Bangladesh accelerates its transition into the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR), access to High-Performance Computing (HPC) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) resources is no longer a luxury but a necessity for academic and industrial innovation. To maximize resource utilization and ensure equitable access, the government will adopt a Centralized GPU Cluster model for University Innovation Hubs, moving away from fragmented, siloed procurement.

10.5.1 The Strategic Pivot: Centralization vs. Fragmentation

Traditionally, computing resources have been procured in silos, with individual universities establishing small, isolated server rooms. This decentralized approach has led to significant inefficiencies:

  • Underutilization: Academic GPU servers in isolated environments typically see less than 40% average utilization, wasting valuable computational potential.

  • Operational Inefficiency: Managing ten separate small clusters requires ten times the cooling, power, and maintenance overhead (OPEX), alongside a duplication of skilled system administrator roles.

  • Inequity: Smaller universities with lower budgets are left behind, creating a "digital divide" within the academic ecosystem.

The Reform Directive: Bangladesh will establish a National GPU Cluster hosted in a central Tier-III/IV data center (e.g., BDREN, BUET, or National Data Center). This consolidated infrastructure will serve all University Innovation Hubs via the high-speed BDREN optical backbone.

10.5.2 Technical Architecture & Governance

The centralized architecture is designed to offer "elastic scaling"—allowing researchers to access massive compute power when needed, which would be impossible with local hardware limits.

Core Infrastructure:

  • Hosting: Centralized facility with optimized cooling and power redundancy.

  • Compute Nodes: High-density Multi-GPU servers (utilizing enterprise-grade GPUs like A100/H100 equivalents).

  • Connectivity: Accessed remotely via the Bangladesh Research and Education Network (BDREN), ensuring low-latency data transfer (99%+ uptime).

Workload Management: To manage demand fairly across institutions, the cluster will utilize industry-standard schedulers:

  • Slurm: For batch processing of heavy research jobs (e.g., training Large Language Models, genomic sequencing).

  • Kubernetes + GPU Operator: For containerized, interactive AI/ML development environments.

Security & Isolation: Data sovereignty and security are paramount. The system will employ Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and Namespaces to ensure per-project isolation. Sensitive research data remains protected, accessible only to authorized users despite the shared infrastructure.

10.5.3 The "Hybrid Model": Balancing Efficiency with Autonomy

To address the pedagogical need for "hands-on" hardware access, a Hybrid Model will be implemented:

  1. The Central Core (Heavy Lifting): The bulk of the investment will go into the central cluster for heavy model training, scalable workloads, and collaborative research.

  2. The Edge (Teaching & Tinkering): Innovation Hubs at universities will retain 1-2 edge GPU units locally.

    • Purpose: These units are strictly for teaching basic system administration, robotics interfacing, and offline inference tasks.

    • Outcome: Students gain physical hardware experience without compromising the efficiency of national-scale computing.

10.5.4 Strategic Benefits

Adopting this centralized approach aligns with global best practices (e.g., Japan's Fugaku, US National Research Cloud) and delivers four key advantages:

  1. Cost Efficiency: Bulk procurement and consolidated maintenance significantly reduce Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).

  2. Academic Equity: A student at a remote university gains access to the same high-end compute power as a researcher at a leading engineering university.

  3. Future-Proofing: Upgrades are managed centrally, eliminating the "hardware obsolescence" cycle for individual campuses.

  4. Collaboration: A shared software stack fosters cross-institutional research, preventing the emergence of "software silos."

By unifying our computing power, Bangladesh avoids the risk of falling behind due to fragmentation. This infrastructure will serve as the engine for the next generation of AI researchers, ensuring sustainable and scalable innovation for complete digital transformation.



10.6 Funding and Financial Mechanisms

The long-term success of Bangladesh's digital transformation will depend on the availability of sustainable and diversified financing mechanisms. Given the scale of planned reforms and infrastructure projects, traditional budget allocations alone will not be sufficient. A blended financial strategy is therefore essential, combining national resources, foreign capital, and innovative funding instruments.

10.6.1 Non-Resident Bangladeshi (NRB) Involvement

Mobilizing the global Bangladeshi diaspora represents a strategic opportunity for investment in digital infrastructure and startups. NRB-focused bonds, venture funds, and digital innovation platforms will channel remittances into productive, high-impact projects. The diaspora community possesses not only financial resources but also technical expertise, global market connections, and entrepreneurial experience that can accelerate Bangladesh's digital transformation. Specific mechanisms include Digital Infrastructure Bonds targeting NRB investors, Diaspora Venture Funds focused on technology startups, and Digital Innovation Platforms enabling direct investment in approved projects.

10.6.2 Foreign Loans and Development Assistance

Concessional loans from multilateral institutions and bilateral partners will finance large-scale infrastructure such as the National Fibre Bank, 5G rollout, and Smart Governance systems. Partnerships with global development agencies including the World Bank, Asian Development Bank, JICA, KOICA, EU, and UNDP will bring technical expertise, policy benchmarking, and risk-sharing support. Bangladesh has already secured significant commitments through the World Bank's EDGE project and other initiatives, providing a foundation for continued engagement.

10.6.3 Operational Cost Management

A sustainability model will be introduced for flagship digital projects, where cost recovery is achieved through service fees, transaction charges, and shared savings. Ministries and agencies will adopt performance-based budgeting, ensuring efficiency and value-for-money. Public-private revenue-sharing models will further reduce the government's fiscal burden while maintaining affordability for citizens. Key mechanisms include user fee structures for premium digital services, transaction-based cost recovery for payment systems, and efficiency gain sharing between government and private operators.

10.7 IT Export Strategy & Action Plan:

The Information Technology (IT) sector has emerged as a priority engine for Bangladesh’s future export growth. However, despite significant progress, IT export performance remains below potential due to structural gaps in international market access, talent development, and branding. To position Bangladesh as a global IT powerhouse, targeted reforms and enabling policies must be adopted.



10.7.1 Problem Statement

Despite rising global demand for software and IT/ITS services, Bangladeshi exporters increasingly:

  • register offices/entities abroad to operate normally in target markets,


  • retain export earnings offshore for extended periods to preserve operational solvency,


  • route IT/ITS earnings through remittance channels instead of formal IT/ITS export mechanisms.


These practices reduce formal export figures, distort policy data and incentives, create “policy blind spots,” and constrain sectoral scaling—particularly for SaaS/product-led growth and globally distributed delivery models.

10.7.2 Key Structural Constraints to Address

Inadequate foreign-currency flexibility for export operations

IT/ITS exporters routinely incur legitimate foreign expenses, including:

  • cloud infrastructure and SaaS subscriptions,


  • international marketing and client acquisition,


  • foreign consultants/technical professionals,


  • cross-border licensing, compliance, and certification costs.

However, outward remittance for business expenses is capped (e.g., USD 40,000 annually) irrespective of firm scale—constraining normal operations for firms earning both USD 100,000 and USD 1 million per year.

Exchange-rate risk and forced value loss

Rapid depreciation of the Taka creates real losses for firms that repatriate early and later need to pay foreign expenses. This rationally encourages offshore retention of earnings to avoid operational disruption.

Mid-level management deficit and founder bottlenecks

Many firms remain founder-dependent for routine decisions, limiting speed, scalability, and export delivery maturity. The ecosystem lacks structured pipelines for mid-level managerial talent (critical for scaling from small teams to export enterprises). (A solution addressing this has been under development by Adnan Dewan/SBL.)

Unequal access to overseas subsidiary formation

Existing provisions enabling certain startups to open overseas subsidiaries (including limited setup remittance such as USD 10,000 with post-notification) do not extend equitably to older registered firms—even if they became export-ready later. Registration age is treated as a maturity proxy, which does not reflect sector realities.

Misclassification of IT/ITS exports as remittance

IT/ITS exports are eligible for a cash incentive (e.g., 3%), while remittances receive a comparable benefit (e.g., 2.5%) with significantly simpler procedures. Due to documentation burdens and approvals, many freelancers and firms remit as “remittance” rather than IT/ITS export—undercounting export volumes and weakening sector statistics.

10.7.3 Strategic Priorities and Policy Actions

Policy Support, FX Modernization & Market Access

1.1 Overseas subsidiaries and branches (global footprint enablement)

  • Establish a clear regulatory framework enabling Bangladeshi IT/ITS companies to set up foreign branches/subsidiaries across all firm ages, with risk-based safeguards and transparent reporting.

  • Standardize allowable purposes (sales offices, customer success, compliance representation, overseas hiring, local contracting) and simplify post-notification workflows.

1.2 Revenue-linked foreign expense caps (replace flat ceilings)

  • Replace a flat annual outward remittance cap (e.g., USD 40,000) with tiered limits linked to export turnover to reflect export-driven cost structures.
    Illustrative model (policy to finalize by regulator):

  • Tier 1: Export turnover up to USD 250k → allowable FX business expense cap up to USD 75k (or up to X% of turnover)

  • Tier 2: USD 250k–1M → cap up to USD 200k (or up to X% of turnover)

  • Tier 3: > USD 1M → cap up to USD 500k (or up to X% of turnover)

  • Apply enhanced AML/CFT and audit requirements progressively by tier (risk-based regulation).

1.3 Foreign-currency retention windows and onshore FC accounts

  • Permit exporters to retain foreign earnings for defined operational purposes before mandatory conversion (e.g., 90–180 days, renewable based on documented pipeline).

  • Alternatively (or additionally), allow exporters to hold earnings in foreign currencies in local banks (special FC retention accounts) to reduce offshore retention incentives while preserving solvency.

1.4 International payment gateways

  • Ensure full operational integration of global payment platforms (e.g., PayPal/Stripe or equivalent rails) to facilitate seamless cross-border transactions and reduce informal routing.

1.5 Outward FDI allowance (controlled)

  • Permit controlled outward FDI by IT/ITS firms to accelerate market entry, acquisitions, and overseas delivery capability—subject to export performance thresholds and reporting.

1.6 Tax and cash incentives (predictable, digitized, performance-linked)

  • Sustain and enhance export-linked benefits (e.g., restore cash incentive from 6% toward 10% if policy permits), digitize application workflows, and publish predictable tax exemption/withholding frameworks.

  • Implement fast-track incentive processing for compliant firms with verified export receipts.

1.7 Innovation funding aligned to export opportunities

  • Expand industry–academia collaboration grants for export-relevant R&D (AI services, cybersecurity, healthtech, fintech, govtech, supply-chain tech), with clear pathways from prototype to international commercialization.

Marketing, Branding & Global Positioning

2.1 Unified “Bangladesh Tech” global presence

  • Participate in major global events (GITEX, VivaTech, Web Summit, Japan IT Week, etc.) with a unified Bangladesh Tech Pavilion supported by a national brand kit, deal-room, and pitch roster.

2.2 Bangladesh IT Summits abroad

  • Organize annual IT summits in priority markets (USA, UK, Japan, UAE, Germany), designed as B2B/B2G deal accelerators (buyers, integrators, venture funds, compliance advisors, diaspora CTO networks).

2.3 Embassy engagement and ICT trade desks

  • Embed ICT trade support within embassies; designate honorary ICT consuls and formalize matchmaking and investor outreach KPIs per mission.

2.4 International media and analyst relations

  • Launch a structured global branding campaign to place Bangladeshi IT success stories in global platforms and analyst ecosystems (industry publications, research notes, rankings, and market reports), supported by a consistent narrative: quality delivery, cost competitiveness, security compliance, and scaling maturity.

Talent Development & Leadership Growth

3.1 Hire & Train (HAT) expansion

  • Scale the EDGE project’s HAT program to produce industry-ready professionals with placement-linked outcomes.

3.2 Mid-level management capacity programs (to break founder bottlenecks)

  • Create structured pathways for product managers, delivery managers, engineering managers, QA leads, customer success leads, and finance/ops managers.

  • Link participation and subsidies to export-growth benchmarks and scaling milestones.

  • Integrate ecosystem solutions being developed by SBL leadership initiatives (including the work referenced under Adnan Dewan/SBL).

3.3 Leadership development

  • Strengthen CXO and ACMP programs to develop globally competent leadership and export-ready governance practices (contracts, compliance, pricing, risk management).

3.4 Global certification support

  • Provide reimbursement or voucher-based support for internationally recognized certifications (e.g., Microsoft/AWS/Cisco), tied to employment retention and export delivery needs.

3.5 Retention & return incentives

  • Adopt milestone-based support to retain local talent and enable NRB professionals to contribute (remote mentorship, short-term placements, co-founding support), with clear deliverables and accountability.

Export Growth Pathways

4.1 Scaling SMEs (3–5 to 50+ employees)

  • Provide structured scaling support: export readiness diagnostics, sales enablement, legal/compliance toolkits, offshore partner matching, and tiered FX facilitation.

4.2 Expanding mid-large firms

  • Enable global scaling through export-linked tax incentives, R&D rebates, and facilitation for overseas operations and payments.



4.3 Boosting SaaS & product exports

  • Offer targeted support for product-led growth: international market access programs, compliance and data protection readiness, marketplace onboarding, and customer acquisition co-funding.

4.4 BPO transformation

  • Move BPO up the value chain via AI-driven service innovation (automation, multilingual support, analytics services) and next-gen outsourcing models.

Strategic Learnings & Global Best Practices

  • JETRO model (Japan): international offices, market facilitation, and brand-building support through coordinated government-industry structures.

DANIDA-style milestone funding (Denmark): performance-linked funding tranches (e.g., USD 100k–800k equivalents) tied to verified export outcomes, accelerating scaling while protecting public funds.

10.8 Monitoring and Evaluation Framework

A robust Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) Framework is essential to ensure that the National Digital Transformation Strategy delivers measurable results, maintains accountability, and adapts to emerging challenges. The framework will provide government, private sector, and citizens with transparent insights into progress while enabling timely course corrections.

10.8.1 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

A national KPI matrix will be developed, aligning with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Indicators will cover areas such as digital service adoption, infrastructure expansion, cybersecurity resilience, ICT sector growth, and citizen satisfaction. The KPI framework is designed to track both output metrics (what is being delivered) and outcome metrics (what impact is being achieved), ensuring a comprehensive view of transformation progress.

Category

Key Indicators

Baseline (2024)

Target (2030)

Digital Services

Government services digitized end-to-end

30%

95%

Infrastructure

Mobile towers fiber-connected

25%

100%

10.0

Top 15

Interoperability

Systems integrated via BNDA

20%

100%

Data Governance

Agencies PDPO-compliant

0%

100%

10.8.2 Digital Dashboard for Real-Time Tracking

A National Digital Transformation Dashboard will consolidate data from ministries and agencies, enabling real-time progress monitoring. Dashboards will be accessible to policymakers and select stakeholders, with periodic public reports to strengthen transparency. The dashboard will provide visualization of KPI trends, early warning alerts for underperforming initiatives, comparative benchmarking against regional peers, and integration with citizen feedback systems.

10.8.3 Independent Audits and Third-Party Evaluation

External assessments by academic institutions, industry experts, and civil society will validate progress and provide recommendations. Annual Digital Transformation Scorecards will be published to benchmark Bangladesh against regional and global peers including Vietnam, India, and Malaysia. Independent evaluations will focus on value-for-money assessments, technical compliance verification, user experience analysis, and impact assessment on citizen outcomes.

10.8.4 Accountability and Feedback Loops

Ministries and agencies will be held accountable through performance reviews linked to budgetary allocations. Citizen feedback mechanisms—including surveys, grievance redressal portals, and social media analytics—will help evaluate the effectiveness and inclusivity of services. Through this framework, Bangladesh will establish a culture of evidence-based policymaking, ensuring that digital transformation is not only implemented but also continuously refined to maximize impact.



10.9 International Partnerships

Bangladesh's digital transformation requires more than domestic reforms—it must be anchored in sustainable, mutually beneficial international partnerships. Such collaborations should not only bring finance but also ensure technology transfer, institutional strengthening, and skills development so that local capacity grows and dependency is reduced over time.

10.9.1 Development Partners

The World Bank, UNDP, EU and others will support e-governance, cybersecurity, and digital inclusion initiatives. Financing models will prioritize capacity transfer clauses, ensuring that after donor projects end, local agencies retain skills and systems. Digital inclusion projects will include measurable targets for women, rural communities, and persons with disabilities, not just infrastructure rollout. Key ongoing partnerships include the World Bank's EDGE Project supporting digital government and economy, the EU's Effective E-Governance program enhancing public service delivery, and UNDP's technical assistance for policy development and South-South cooperation.

10.9.2 Bilateral Cooperation

Partners like JICA and KOICA will go beyond infrastructure loans to co-develop innovation ecosystems, including research labs and skills academies. Agreements will mandate local content requirements, so a minimum share of hardware/software is sourced or assembled in Bangladesh. Bilateral programs will focus on co-innovation rather than simple aid—for example, joint pilots in 5G, health tech, and smart agriculture. This approach ensures that Bangladesh builds indigenous capability while benefiting from partner expertise.

10.9.3 Global Platforms and Benchmarking

Engagement with the E-Government Academy (EGA), ITU, and OECD networks will help Bangladesh benchmark against peers. Instead of adopting 'copy-paste' solutions, global models will be localized through NDGA for cultural, linguistic, and socio-economic fit. Participation will ensure Bangladesh contributes to global digital norms (AI ethics, cross-border data flows), giving the country a voice in shaping standards. Bangladesh will actively participate in WTO e-commerce negotiations, ITU digital standards development, and AI ethics frameworks.

10.9.4 Technology and Knowledge Transfer

All major ICT projects with foreign partners will include mandatory knowledge transfer programs, on-site training, and certification of Bangladeshi officials. Partnerships will incentivize the growth of local startups, ensuring they are included in procurement and pilot programs. Joint R&D projects with universities abroad will feed into Bangladesh's Government–Industry–Academia collaboration framework, building a long-term skills pipeline.

10.10 Bangladesh in the Global Digital Economy

Bangladesh's ICT exports are growing steadily, exceeding US$1.5 billion, mainly in software, BPO, and freelancing. However, integration into regional platforms (BIMSTEC, SAARC, ASEAN) and participation in global digital governance remain limited. To sustain growth and credibility, Bangladesh must evolve from a cost-driven outsourcing hub to a trusted and globally integrated digital partner.

10.10.1 Strategic Objectives

The global positioning strategy aims to position Bangladesh as a regional digital trade hub by 2030, secure cross-border agreements on data flows, digital trade, and payment systems, actively participate in global forums on AI ethics, data protection, and open-source governance, leverage the diaspora tech community to channel investment, skills, and market access, and build Bangladesh's global reputation as a secure and resilient digital service provider.

10.10.2 Key Interventions

Digital Trade and Regional Cooperation

Bangladesh will negotiate digital trade provisions in FTAs and regional agreements (BIMSTEC, SAARC, ASEAN, GCC), establish cross-border e-commerce corridors with South Asia and the Middle East, and develop regional digital payment systems to reduce transaction costs for freelancers and SMEs.

Global Standards and Governance

The strategy calls for aligning national regulations with global standards (GDPR, ISO, UN Open Source Principles), actively participating in WTO e-commerce negotiations, ITU digital standards, and AI ethics frameworks, and positioning NDGA as Bangladesh's representation node in global data governance forums.

Diaspora Tech Talent and Investment

Key initiatives include launching a Bangladesh Tech Diaspora Network to attract mentorship and venture investment, creating a Reverse Brain Drain programme offering incentives for returning ICT professionals, and facilitating diaspora-led venture funds targeting startups in fintech, healthtech, and agritech.

Bangladesh as a Trusted Digital Hub

The government will market Bangladesh's cybersecurity, IP protection, and service reliability globally, establish 'Trusted Bangladesh' branding for ICT exports similar to global 'Made in...' labels, and build partnerships with GCC and ASEAN countries to position Bangladesh as a preferred outsourcing hub.

EXPANDING BANGLADESH’S DIGITAL INFLUENCE



10.11 Phased Implementation Roadmap for Strategic Governance Reforms

The strategic and governance reforms outlined in this chapter will be implemented through a carefully phased approach over six years (2025-2030). This phased structure ensures that foundational reforms are established before more complex initiatives are undertaken, and that lessons learned in early phases inform subsequent activities.

10.11.1 Phase 1: Foundation Building (2025-2026)

The first phase focuses on establishing the institutional foundations for digital transformation governance. Priority activities include establishing the National Digital Transformation Taskforce (NDTT) with full membership and operational mandate, operationalizing the Implementation and Monitoring Secretariat under ICT Division, launching the National Digital Dashboard for real-time monitoring, establishing inter-agency taskforces for priority areas (Cybersecurity, DPI, AI & Data Governance), developing and adopting the initial KPI framework aligned with SDGs, and initiating first-round PPP negotiations for 5G and National Fibre Bank.

10.11.2 Phase 2: Scaling and Optimization (2027-2028)

The second phase focuses on scaling successful pilots and optimizing coordination mechanisms. Key activities include full operationalization of all inter-agency taskforces with clear deliverables, launching performance-based budgeting across all participating ministries, expanding PPP portfolio to include DPI, semiconductor roadmap, and Universal Payment Gateway, establishing diaspora engagement mechanisms including Digital Infrastructure Bonds and Venture Funds, implementing capacity transfer clauses in all major donor-funded projects, and achieving 70% of international projects with knowledge transfer and local content requirements.

10.11.3 Phase 3: Consolidation and Global Integration (2029-2030)

The final phase focuses on consolidating gains and achieving global recognition. Priority activities include achieving 100% compliance with coordination framework requirements across government, reaching US$10 billion annual ICT exports, securing Bangladesh's position in UN E-Government Index Top 15, signing 6+ regional digital trade agreements, training 10,000+ officials in frontier technologies, engaging 1,000+ local startups in international pilot projects, and achieving active participation in at least 10 global digital governance forums.

Reform Area

10.12 Expected Outcomes by 2030

The successful implementation of the strategic and governance reforms outlined in this chapter will fundamentally transform how Bangladesh approaches digital development. By 2030, the following outcomes are expected:

Governance Transformation

The three-tier coordination framework will eliminate institutional silos and ensure coherent digital strategy execution across government. All ministries will operate under unified digital standards with clear accountability mechanisms. Performance-based budgeting will ensure efficient resource allocation and value-for-money in all digital investments.

Public-Private Collaboration

Mature PPP mechanisms will have mobilized over $4 billion in private sector investment in digital infrastructure. Innovation sandboxes and pilot programs will have created sustainable pathways for technology adoption. Risk-sharing frameworks will have reduced government fiscal burden while accelerating implementation timelines.

Sustainable Financing

Diversified funding sources—including government budget, development partners, NRB investment, private sector FDI, and PPP co-investment—will have mobilized over $11 billion for digital transformation. Self-sustaining revenue models for flagship digital services will reduce dependency on external financing.

Evidence-Based Governance

The National Digital Dashboard will provide real-time visibility into transformation progress. Annual Digital Transformation Scorecards will benchmark Bangladesh against global peers. Citizen feedback mechanisms will ensure that digital services remain responsive to user needs.

Global Integration

All international projects will include built-in knowledge transfer and sustainability clauses. Over 10,000 Bangladeshi officials and professionals will have been trained in frontier technologies. 1,000 local startups will be integrated into international pilot projects, creating export-ready innovation. Bangladesh will have become an active voice in at least 10 global digital forums, shaping international digital governance.

Economic Impact

ICT exports will reach US$10 billion annually. Bangladesh will be a signatory to at least 6 regional digital trade agreements. Diaspora will contribute US$500 million+ annually in investment and mentorship. Bangladesh will be recognized among the Top 20 trusted outsourcing destinations globally.

10.13 Risk Management and Mitigation Strategies

The ambitious reforms outlined in this chapter carry inherent risks that must be proactively managed. A comprehensive risk management framework will be integrated into the governance structure to identify, assess, and mitigate potential challenges.

Risk Category

Specific Risks

Likelihood

Impact

Mitigation Strategies

Institutional

Resistance to coordination, turf protection

High

High

Clear mandates, incentive alignment, leadership commitment

Political

Policy discontinuity, changing priorities

Medium

High

Cross-party consensus, institutional embedding

Financial

Funding shortfalls, budget constraints

Medium

High

Diversified funding, PPP expansion, cost recovery

Technical

Integration failures, security breaches

Medium

High

Robust architecture, cybersecurity investment

Capacity

Skills gaps, brain drain

High

Medium

Training programs, diaspora engagement, retention incentives

External

Global economic shocks, partner withdrawal

Low

High

Contingency planning, diversified partnerships



10.14 Conclusion

The strategic and governance reforms outlined in this chapter represent a fundamental shift in how Bangladesh approaches digital transformation. By establishing robust coordination mechanisms, sustainable financing models, transparent accountability frameworks, and strategic international partnerships, Bangladesh positions itself not merely as a consumer of digital technology but as a contributor to global digital governance.

The three-tier coordination structure—comprising the National Digital Transformation Council, Inter-Agency Taskforces, and the Implementation Secretariat—ensures vertical and horizontal integration across all levels of government. The National Digital Transformation Taskforce brings together key ministries, regulators, and development partners under a unified mandate, breaking down the institutional silos that have historically undermined digital initiatives.

The blended financing strategy—combining government resources, diaspora investment, development partner support, and private sector capital—provides the sustainable funding base necessary for long-term success. Performance-based budgeting and cost-recovery mechanisms ensure fiscal sustainability while maintaining service affordability.

The monitoring and evaluation framework, anchored by the National Digital Dashboard and regular third-party assessments, creates a culture of evidence-based governance. Transparent KPIs, citizen feedback mechanisms, and global benchmarking ensure continuous improvement and accountability.

International partnerships structured around knowledge transfer, local content requirements, and co-innovation ensure that Bangladesh builds indigenous capacity while benefiting from global expertise. The goal is not dependency but partnership—positioning Bangladesh as a contributor to global digital norms and standards.

By 2030, these reforms will have transformed Bangladesh into a regional digital leader—not through technological adoption alone, but through the governance innovations that make sustainable digital development possible. This chapter's reforms are not merely administrative adjustments; they are the foundation upon which Bangladesh's digital future will be built.



---

End of Chapter 10



11. Strategic roadmap to 2030 and beyond

11.1 Introduction

The National Digital Transformation Strategy 2025-2030 represents Bangladesh's most comprehensive effort to transition from a technology consumer to a regional digital leader. This roadmap consolidates all reforms, initiatives, and projects outlined in the preceding chapters into a unified, phased implementation plan. The roadmap is designed to ensure that each initiative builds upon the foundations laid by earlier reforms, creating a cohesive and self-reinforcing digital ecosystem.

The implementation timeline spans six years, organized into three primary phases, with provisions for longer-term initiatives extending to 2035 and 2040 where foundational infrastructure investments require extended development cycles. Each phase has been calibrated to balance immediate priorities with sustainable, long-term growth objectives.

11.2 Strategic Phases Overview

The roadmap is structured around three core implementation phases, each with distinct objectives that progressively build national digital capacity:

Phase

Timeline

Strategic Focus

Phase 1: Foundation

2025-2026

Legal frameworks, institutional reform, data governance establishment, core infrastructure deployment

Phase 2: Scale & Optimize

2027-2028

Nationwide rollout, infrastructure expansion, sectoral integration, private sector engagement

Phase 3: Smart Governance

2029-2030

AI-driven governance, DPI consolidation, global positioning, predictive services

Phase 4: Long-term Vision

2030+

Emerging technology leadership, regional hub status, sustainable digital sovereignty

11.3 Phase 1: Foundation (2025-2026)

Phase 1 establishes the legal, institutional, and technical foundations for Bangladesh's digital transformation. This phase prioritizes governance frameworks, regulatory compliance, and critical infrastructure deployment.

11.3.1 Legal and Governance Framework

Legal & Regulatory Foundations (2025-2026)

Key Actions

Target Outcomes

Enact NDGA Rules and Regulations under NDGO 2025

2025-2026

Establish NDGA Secretariat and Policy Board

2025-2026

Implement PDPO 2025 compliance framework

2025-2026

Deploy Cyber Safety Ordinance enforcement mechanisms

2025-2026

Appoint Chief Data Officers across 10-20 ministries

End 2026

Begin PDPO compliance audits government-wide

Q4 2026

11.6.3 Deep-Tech Identity (BEAR)

Bangladesh's long-term deep-tech identity will be anchored in the BEAR framework:

  • Biotech: Health tech innovations, genomics, and precision medicine

  • Electronics: Semiconductor design, IoT manufacturing, and smart devices

  • AI: Ethical AI governance, NLP for Bangla, and predictive systems

  • Robotics: Industrial automation, agricultural robotics, and smart manufacturing
